## 4.1 Parse Tree Construction for `id + id * id`

The diagram below shows the unique parse tree under the precedence-enforcing grammar. The grouping $(id + (id * id))$ is dictated by the grammar structure: $E \rightarrow E + T$ forces $+T$ to be the right operand of $+$, and $T \rightarrow T * F$ forces $id * id$ to be grouped.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootE["E"]
    nodeE1["E"]
    nodePlus1["+"]
    nodeT1["T"]
    nodeT2["T"]
    nodeMult["*"]
    nodeF1["F"]
    nodeF2["F"]
    nodeF3["F"]
    leaf1["id"]
    leaf2["id"]
    leaf3["id"]

    rootE --> nodeE1
    rootE --> nodePlus1
    rootE --> nodeT1
    nodeT1 --> nodeT2
    nodeT1 --> nodeMult
    nodeT1 --> nodeF3
    nodeT2 --> nodeF2
    nodeF2 --> leaf2
    nodeMult --> leaf3
    nodeE1 --> nodeF1
    nodeF1 --> leaf1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The leaves left-to-right read: `id + id * id` ✓. The tree structure is unique under this grammar — there is exactly one parse tree for this string.

Using the **ambiguous** grammar $E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid id$, the same string has two distinct parse trees.

## 4.3 LL(1) Predictive Parser Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    InputBuffer["Input Buffer: id + id * id $"]
    Stack["Stack: $ S"]
    ParsingTable["Predictive Parsing Table M A a"]
    Output["Output: Production Steps"]
    Driver["LL Driver Algorithm"]

    Driver -->|Read top of stack X| Stack
    Driver -->|Read current input a| InputBuffer
    Driver -->|Consult M X a| ParsingTable
    ParsingTable -->|Production A to alpha| Output
    Output -->|Apply production| Stack
    Driver -->|Advance pointer| InputBuffer
```

**The LL(1) driver algorithm in pseudocode:**

```
push($);
push(S);
ip = first input symbol;
repeat:
    X = top of stack;
    if X == ip == $:
        break (ACCEPT)
    else if X is a terminal:
        pop X; advance ip
    else if M[X, ip] is an error entry:
        error recovery
    else if M[X, ip] = X -> Y1 Y2 ... Yk:
        pop X;
        push Yk ... Y2 Y1  (right-to-left)
        output production X -> Y1 ... Yk
```

---

## 4.4 Shift-Reduce Parser Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    InputTape["Input Tape: tokens remaining"]
    Stack2["Parse Stack: handles accumulated"]
    HandleDetector["Handle Detection Engine"]
    ActionTable["ACTION Table"]
    GotoTable["GOTO Table"]
    Reducer["Reduction Module: A to beta"]

    InputTape -->|Next token| HandleDetector
    Stack2 -->|Top symbols| HandleDetector
    HandleDetector -->|shift| Stack2
    HandleDetector -->|Consult ACTION s a| ActionTable
    ActionTable -->|reduce A to beta| GotoTable
    GotoTable -->|New state| Stack2
    Reducer -->|Pop RHS push LHS| Stack2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** In an LL(1) parser, the parsing decision is **what production to expand next**. In an LR(1) parser, the decision is **when to shift vs. when to reduce**, and with **which production**. LR(1) is more powerful because it can defer decisions until enough context is seen.

---

## 4.5 Recursive Descent Call Tree

For the grammar $E \rightarrow T E'$, $E' \rightarrow + T E' \mid \varepsilon$, $T \rightarrow F T'$, $T' \rightarrow * F T' \mid \varepsilon$, $F \rightarrow (E) \mid id$:

```mermaid
graph TD
    parseE["parseE"]
    parseEprime["parseEprime"]
    parseT["parseT"]
    parseTprime["parseTprime"]
    parseF["parseF"]
    matchLparen["matchLeftParen"]
    matchRparen["matchRightParen"]
    matchPlus["matchPlus"]
    matchTimes["matchTimes"]
    matchId["matchId"]

    parseE --> parseT
    parseE --> parseEprime
    parseT --> parseF
    parseT --> parseTprime
    parseF --> matchLparen
    parseF --> matchId
    parseEprime --> matchPlus
    parseEprime --> parseT
    parseTprime --> matchTimes
    parseTprime --> parseF
    matchLparen --> parseE
    matchLparen --> matchRparen
```

> [!NOTE]
> This call graph mirrors the parse tree structure. The recursive functions "unwind" as the parse tree is traversed, producing a depth-first, left-to-right analysis of the input — the essence of top-down parsing.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a parse tree. How is a parse tree different from a derivation?**

**Model Answer:**

A **parse tree** is a graphical representation of the syntactic structure of a string derived from a Context-Free Grammar. It is a rooted, ordered tree where:
- The **root** is labeled with the start symbol $S$.
- Each **interior node** is labeled with a non-terminal.
- Each **leaf node** is labeled with a terminal or $\varepsilon$.
- The **children of a node** (read left-to-right) form a production of the grammar.

**Difference from derivation:**
- A **derivation** is a *linear sequence* of sentential forms obtained by repeatedly applying production rules. It records the *order* of rule applications.
- A **parse tree** records the *hierarchical structure* of the derivation, *ignoring* the order in which non-terminals were replaced.
- One parse tree may correspond to **multiple derivations** (leftmost, rightmost, or mixed) but produces only **one string** (the yield of the leaves).
- Conversely, **two distinct parse trees** for the same string indicate the grammar is **ambiguous**.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is an ambiguous grammar? Explain with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

A Context-Free Grammar $G$ is said to be **ambiguous** if there exists at least one string $w \in L(G)$ that has **more than one parse tree** (equivalently, more than one distinct leftmost derivation).

**Example:** Consider the grammar:
$$E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid id$$

The string $w = id + id * id$ has two parse trees:
1. $(id + id) * id$ — grouping addition first.
2. $id + (id * id)$ — grouping multiplication first.

This means the grammar is **ambiguous** and would cause a compiler to generate different code for the same expression. The grammar can be made unambiguous by introducing precedence levels:
$$E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$$
$$T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$$
$$F \rightarrow (E) \mid id$$

Now $w = id + id * id$ has a unique parse tree corresponding to $id + (id * id)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** For the following grammar, compute the **FIRST** and **FOLLOW** sets for all non-terminals. **(7 Marks)**

$$S \rightarrow a A B \mid b$$
$$A \rightarrow a A \mid \varepsilon$$
$$B \rightarrow b B \mid \varepsilon$$

**(b)** Construct the **LL(1) predictive parsing table** for the above grammar. Is the grammar LL(1)? Justify your answer. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

### Part (a): FIRST and FOLLOW Sets

**Computing FIRST sets:**

**For terminal symbols:** $\text{FIRST}(a) = \{a\}$, $\text{FIRST}(b) = \{b\}$.

**For non-terminal $A$:**
- $A \rightarrow aA$: $\text{FIRST}(aA) = \{a\}$ → add $a$ to $\text{FIRST}(A)$.
- $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$: add $\varepsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(A)$.

$$\text{FIRST}(A) = \{a, \varepsilon\}$$

**For non-terminal $B$:**
- $B \rightarrow bB$: $\text{FIRST}(bB) = \{b\}$ → add $b$ to $\text{FIRST}(B)$.
- $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$: add $\varepsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(B)$.

$$\text{FIRST}(B) = \{b, \varepsilon\}$$

**For start symbol $S$:**
- $S \rightarrow aAB$: $\text{FIRST}(aAB) = \{a\}$ (since $a$ is terminal) → add $a$ to $\text{FIRST}(S)$.
- $S \rightarrow b$: $\text{FIRST}(b) = \{b\}$ → add $b$ to $\text{FIRST}(S)$.

$$\text{FIRST}(S) = \{a, b\}$$

**Computing FOLLOW sets:**

**For $S$:** $S$ is the start symbol, so:

$$\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$$

**For $A$:** $A$ appears in $S \rightarrow aAB$ (followed by $B$), and $S \rightarrow aA$ alone (followed by end).
- Since $A$ is followed by $B$, add $\text{FIRST}(B) \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{b\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$.
- Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(B)$, also add $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$.

$$\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{b, \$\}$$

**For $B$:** $B$ appears at the end of $S \rightarrow aAB$ with no symbol following.
- Add $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.

$$\text{FOLLOW}(B) = \{\$\}$$

### Part (b): Predictive Parsing Table

For each production, we determine table entries:

| Production | FIRST | Adds to Table |
|------------|-------|---------------|
| $S \rightarrow aAB$ | $\{a\}$ | $M[S, a] = S \rightarrow aAB$ |
| $S \rightarrow b$ | $\{b\}$ | $M[S, b] = S \rightarrow b$ |
| $A \rightarrow aA$ | $\{a\}$ | $M[A, a] = A \rightarrow aA$ |
| $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon \in$ FIRST, FOLLOW$(A) = \{b, \$\}$ | $M[A, b] = M[A, \$] = A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| $B \rightarrow bB$ | $\{b\}$ | $M[B, b] = B \rightarrow bB$ |
| $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon \in$ FIRST, FOLLOW$(B) = \{\$\}$ | $M[B, \$] = B \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |

**Predictive Parsing Table:**

| | $a$ | $b$ | $\$$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S$ | $S \rightarrow aAB$ | $S \rightarrow b$ | |
| $A$ | $A \rightarrow aA$ | $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| $B$ | | $B \rightarrow bB$ | $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |

**Conclusion:** The table has **at most one production per cell**. Therefore, the grammar **is LL(1)** ✓

**Justification:** For every non-terminal $A$ with multiple productions:
- $A$: $\text{FIRST}(aA) = \{a\}$ and (since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\varepsilon)$) $\text{FIRST}(\varepsilon) = \{\varepsilon\}$. The first sets $\{a\}$ and $\varepsilon$ do not overlap. $\text{FIRST}(aA) \cap \text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{a\} \cap \{b, \$\} = \emptyset$. ✓
- $B$: $\text{FIRST}(bB) = \{b\}$ and (since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\varepsilon)$) $\text{FIRST}(\varepsilon) = \{\varepsilon\}$. $\text{FIRST}(bB) \cap \text{FOLLOW}(B) = \{b\} \cap \{\$\} = \emptyset$. ✓
- $S$: $\text{FIRST}(aAB) = \{a\}$ and $\text{FIRST}(b) = \{b\}$. They are disjoint. ✓

All conditions for LL(1) are satisfied.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Consider the grammar:
$$E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$$
$$T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$$
$$F \rightarrow (E) \mid id$$

Construct the **rightmost derivation** for the input $id + id * id$ and draw the corresponding **parse tree**. Show how the tree is constructed step-by-step. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **recursive descent parsing** with its advantages and limitations. Show the recursive descent parsing procedures for the above grammar. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

### Part (a): Rightmost Derivation and Parse Tree

**Step-by-step rightmost derivation:**

| Step | Sentential Form | Production Applied | Position |
|------|-----------------|--------------------|----------|
| 1 | $E$ | (start) | — |
| 2 | $E + T$ | $E \rightarrow E + T$ | Leftmost $E$ |
| 3 | $E + T * F$ | $T \rightarrow T * F$ | Rightmost $T$ |
| 4 | $E + T * id$ | $F \rightarrow id$ | Rightmost $F$ |
| 5 | $E + F * id$ | $T \rightarrow F$ | Rightmost $T$ |
| 6 | $E + id * id$ | $F \rightarrow id$ | Rightmost $F$ |
| 7 | $T + id * id$ | $E \rightarrow T$ | Leftmost $E$ |
| 8 | $F + id * id$ | $T \rightarrow F$ | $T$ |
| 9 | $id + id * id$ | $F \rightarrow id$ | $F$ |

**Parse tree construction (top-down, expanding in same order as RMD):**

The root is $E$. Apply the productions in the order above, building the tree:

```
                    E
                  / | \
                 E  +  T
                 |    /|\
                 T   T * F
                 |   |   |
                 F   F   id
                 |   |
                 F   id
                 |
                id
```

Wait, this is incorrect. Let me redraw the actual tree:

```
E (root)
├── E (left subtree, eventually becomes "id")
│   └── T
│       └── F
│           └── id
├── +  (terminal leaf)
└── T (right subtree, eventually becomes "id * id")
    ├── T
    │   └── F
    │       └── id
    ├── *  (terminal leaf)
    └── F
        └── id
```

**Verification:** Reading the leaves left-to-right: `id + id * id` ✓. The grouping $id + (id * id)$ correctly reflects the standard precedence of multiplication over addition.

### Part (b): Recursive Descent Parsing

**Definition:** A recursive descent parser is a top-down parser that uses a set of **recursive procedures** to recognize the input. Each non-terminal in the grammar has an associated procedure. The parser works by attempting to match the input string by expanding the start symbol using these procedures.

**Advantages:**
1. **Conceptual simplicity** — easy to understand and implement.
2. **Modular structure** — each non-terminal becomes a separate function, aiding maintenance.
3. **No table construction** required (in the simple backtracking version).
4. **Easy to extend** with semantic actions for code generation.

**Limitations:**
1. **Cannot handle left-recursive grammars** — leads to infinite recursion (e.g., $E \rightarrow E + T$ causes `parseE` to call `parseE` immediately).
2. **Backtracking** can be expensive (exponential worst case).
3. **Difficult to generate automatically** from arbitrary grammars.
4. **Error recovery is complex** compared to table-driven parsers.
5. **Cannot handle all context-free grammars** — only LL(k) grammars work without backtracking.

**Recursive Descent Procedures for the Grammar:**

```
procedure parseE()
    parseT()
    parseEprime()

procedure parseEprime()
    if lookahead == '+':
        match('+')
        parseT()
        parseEprime()
    // else: epsilon production, do nothing

procedure parseT()
    parseF()
    parseTprime()

procedure parseTprime()
    if lookahead == '*':
        match('*')
        parseF()
        parseTprime()
    // else: epsilon production, do nothing

procedure parseF()
    if lookahead == '(':
        match('(')
        parseE()
        match(')')
    else if lookahead == 'id':
        match('id')
    else:
        error("Unexpected token")
```

**Key Observation:** The grammar $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$ is **left-recursive**. Recursive descent parsers cannot directly handle this — we must first eliminate left recursion by transforming it to:
$$E \rightarrow T E'$$
$$E' \rightarrow + T E' \mid \varepsilon$$

The procedures above are written for this **left-factored, non-left-recursive** form. The original grammar requires left recursion elimination before recursive descent parsing is feasible.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** write a recursive descent parser for a left-recursive grammar directly — it will loop infinitely. Always eliminate left recursion first.
> 2. **Do not** forget to apply left factoring when the grammar has common prefixes. Otherwise, the parser cannot decide which production to choose without backtracking.
> 3. In FIRST/FOLLOW computation, **always include** the $\varepsilon$ handling correctly. Missing $\varepsilon$ propagation is the #1 cause of wrong tables in KTU exams.
> 4. For the parsing table, **double-check** that $M[A, b]$ and $M[A, c]$ entries (where $b, c$ are in FOLLOW(A)) are filled for productions with $\varepsilon \in$ FIRST($\alpha$).
> 5. **Always verify** LL(1) by checking that the table has at most one entry per cell, NOT by merely computing FIRST/FOLLOW.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Parse Tree** = rooted tree; root = start symbol; interior = non-terminals; leaves = terminals (yield = derived string).
- **Derivation** = sequence of sentential forms via production applications; **Leftmost** replaces leftmost non-terminal; **Rightmost** replaces rightmost.
- **Ambiguity** = a grammar with at least one string having ≥ 2 parse trees. Disambiguate via precedence, associativity, or grammar rewriting.
- **Inherently ambiguous** = no unambiguous grammar exists for that language. Decidability of ambiguity is **undecidable** in general.
- **Top-down parsing** = starts from $S$, builds tree root-to-leaves. Subtypes: Recursive Descent (with/without backtracking) and Predictive (LL(1)).
- **Bottom-up parsing** = starts from input, reduces to $S$. Subtypes: Shift-Reduce, LR(0), SLR(1), LALR(1), CLR(1).
- **FIRST(α)** = set of terminals that can begin strings derived from α. Algorithm: apply rules iteratively until no change.
- **FOLLOW(A)** = set of terminals that can immediately follow A in any sentential form. Always includes $\$$ for the start symbol.
- **LL(1) condition** = at most one production per cell in the predictive parsing table. Equivalent to disjoint FIRST sets and FOLLOW disjointness for nullable productions.
- **Recursive Descent** = one procedure per non-terminal. Must eliminate **left recursion** and apply **left factoring** first.
- **Left recursion elimination**: $A \rightarrow A\alpha \mid \beta$ becomes $A \rightarrow \beta A'$ and $A' \rightarrow \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$.
- **Left factoring**: $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2$ becomes $A \rightarrow \alpha A'$ and $A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2$.
- **Shift-Reduce parsing** uses a stack. **Shift** = push next input. **Reduce** = pop RHS of handle, push LHS. **Accept** when stack = $S$ and input = $\$$.
- **Handle** = substring matching RHS of a production, whose reduction represents one step of RMD in reverse.
- **LR(1) parsing** = bottom-up, right-to-left (in reverse), 1-symbol lookahead. More powerful than LL(1) — can handle left recursion and a wider class of grammars.
- **LALR(1)** is used by **Yacc/Bison** — merges CLR(1) states with identical cores; widely deployed in real compilers.
- **Time complexity** of LL(1) and LR(1) parsing: $O(n)$ where $n$ = input length. Space: $O(n)$ for stack.
- **Real-world tools**: ANTLR (LL(\*)), Yacc/Bison (LALR(1)), JavaCC (LL(k)), Parsec (combinator parsers in Haskell), PEG (parsing expression grammars).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Practice drawing parse trees meticulously, with every internal node and leaf labeled. In LL(1) problems, always show the predictive parsing table with all cells filled. For shift-reduce, trace step-by-step with explicit actions (shift/reduce/accept) and the production used in each reduction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ambiguity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ambiguity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Ambiguity (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Ambiguous Grammar):**
> A **context-free grammar (CFG)** $G$ is said to be **ambiguous** if there exists at least one string $w \in L(G)$ that has **two or more distinct parse trees** (also equivalently, **two or more distinct leftmost derivations**, or **two or more distinct rightmost derivations**).
> Conversely, a grammar is **unambiguous** if **every** string in its language has **exactly one parse tree**.

Formally, a CFG $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is ambiguous if:
$$\exists w \in T^* : w \text{ has at least two different parse trees in } G$$

The three equivalences that examiners love to test are:

| Condition | Statement |
|---|---|
| Multiple Parse Trees | $\exists\, w$ with $\geq 2$ derivation trees |
| Multiple Leftmost Derivations | $\exists\, w$ with $\geq 2$ leftmost derivations $S \Rightarrow^*_{lm} w$ |
| Multiple Rightmost Derivations | $\exists\, w$ with $\geq 2$ rightmost derivations $S \Rightarrow^*_{rm} w$ |

> [!NOTE]
> All three conditions are mathematically equivalent. If you prove any one, the other two follow automatically. KTU examiners frequently expect students to state this equivalence explicitly.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Recipe Problem"

Imagine you are following a recipe book to bake a cake. If the same final cake can be produced by following **two completely different step-by-step instruction sequences** in the recipe, the recipe book is **ambiguous**. A good cookbook (an unambiguous grammar) must lead to exactly one unique step sequence for every dish. In compilers, this "recipe" is the grammar, and the "step sequence" is the parse tree. If the parser can construct the parse tree in more than one way, the meaning (semantics) attached to the program becomes non-deterministic — which is fatal for code generation.

## 1.3 Why Ambiguity Matters in Compilers

> [!WARNING]
> KTU board examinations treat ambiguity as a **high-priority topic** because it directly impacts:
> 1. **Determinism of Parsing** — ambiguous grammars break top-down (LL) and bottom-up (LR) parsers.
> 2. **Operator Semantics** — without resolving ambiguity, `a + b * c` may be evaluated as `(a+b)*c` instead of `a+(b*c)`.
> 3. **Code Generation** — different parse trees may lead to different machine code outputs for the same source program.

## 1.4 Key Terms and Their Precise Meanings

- **Inherent Ambiguity**: A language $L$ is inherently ambiguous if **every** grammar that generates $L$ is ambiguous (i.e., no unambiguous grammar exists for $L$).
- **Dangling Else Ambiguity**: A specific structural ambiguity in `if-then-else` statements across most programming languages.
- **Associativity Ambiguity**: When the same operator used in a chain can be grouped either left-to-right or right-to-left.
- **Precedence Ambiguity**: When two different operators appear in an expression and it is unclear which one binds tighter.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Mathematical Conditions for Ambiguity (Expanded)

A grammar $G$ is ambiguous **iff** there exists a string $w$ such that **at least one** of the following holds:

1. **Condition A — Multiple Parse Trees:**  
   $w$ admits $\geq 2$ distinct derivation trees rooted at $S$.

2. **Condition B — Multiple Leftmost Derivations:**  
   There exist two distinct sequences of leftmost derivation steps:  
   $S \Rightarrow_{lm} \alpha_1 \Rightarrow_{lm} \alpha_2 \Rightarrow^*_{lm} w$ and $S \Rightarrow_{lm} \beta_1 \Rightarrow_{lm} \beta_2 \Rightarrow^*_{lm} w$ with at least one differing step.

3. **Condition C — Multiple Rightmost Derivations:**  
   Analogous to Condition B but with rightmost replacements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Proof Sketch of Equivalence:** Every parse tree corresponds to exactly one leftmost derivation (read leaves left-to-right) and exactly one rightmost derivation (read leaves right-to-left). The tree's shape uniquely determines the order of rule applications, and vice versa.

## 2.2 The Decision Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Undecidability):** It is **undecidable** in general whether an arbitrary context-free grammar is ambiguous. That is, there is no algorithm that, given any CFG $G$, always halts and correctly answers "ambiguous" or "unambiguous."
> However, for **specific subclasses** (LL, LR, LALR), ambiguity is decidable via parser-generator diagnostics.

**Engineering Implication:** We cannot write a universal "ambiguity checker" program. Instead, we must **analyze each grammar manually** or use **parser-generator tools** (like YACC/Bison) that report **shift/reduce** and **reduce/reduce conflicts** — practical proxies for ambiguity.

## 2.3 Classical Ambiguity Scenarios (KTU Favourites)

### 2.3.1 Arithmetic Expression Ambiguity

Consider the grammar:
$$E \rightarrow E + E \;\vert\; E * E \;\vert\; (E) \;\vert\; id$$

**Demonstration:** The string `id + id * id` admits two parse trees:
- **Tree 1:** `id + (id * id)` — correct precedence
- **Tree 2:** `(id + id) * id` — incorrect precedence

### 2.3.2 Dangling Else Ambiguity

Consider the grammar:
$$
\text{stmt} \rightarrow \text{if } expr \text{ then } stmt \;\vert\; \text{if } expr \text{ then } stmt \text{ else } stmt \;\vert\; \text{other}
$$

**Demonstration:** For the string `if E1 then if E2 then S1 else S2`, the `else` clause may legally attach to either the inner `if` or the outer `if`.

### 2.3.3 Associativity Ambiguity

For string `id - id - id` with grammar $E \rightarrow E - E \;\vert\; id$:
- **Left-associative parse:** $(id - id) - id$
- **Right-associative parse:** $id - (id - id)$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formal Statement | Detection Method |
|---|---|---|
| Grammar Ambiguity | $\exists w \in L(G)$ with $\geq 2$ parse trees | Construct two distinct parse trees for the **same** string |
| Inherent Language Ambiguity | $\forall G'$ with $L(G') = L(G)$, $G'$ is ambiguous | Generally proven by pumping-lemma-style arguments |
| Dangling Else | `if-then-else` matched ambiguously | Convention: match `else` with **nearest unmatched** `then` |
| Left-Recursion | $A \Rightarrow^+ A\alpha$ | Direct or indirect; often correlated with ambiguity |
| Operator Precedence | Multiplicative binds tighter than additive | Encoded via grammar layering (Expression → Term → Factor) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Modern compiler tools (LLVM, GCC, ANTLR, Bison) automatically detect ambiguity via **conflict reports**. The YACC directive `%left`, `%right`, and `%nonassoc` are practical tools to enforce associativity and precedence, thereby resolving ambiguity **at parser-generation time**.

## 2.5 Strategies to Resolve Ambiguity

1. **Rewrite the Grammar:** Introduce intermediate non-terminals to enforce precedence and associativity.
2. **Use Parser Directives:** YACC-style precedence and associativity declarations.
3. **Disambiguate Semantics:** Add language-specific conventions (e.g., the "nearest `then`" rule for dangling else).
4. **Use Unambiguous Subclasses:** Restrict to LL(k) or LR(k) grammars that are provably unambiguous.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Proving Ambiguity for Arithmetic Expressions

**Given Grammar:**
$$G: E \rightarrow E + E \;\vert\; E * E \;\vert\; (E) \;\vert\; id$$

**Claim:** $G$ is ambiguous because $w = id + id + id$ has two distinct leftmost derivations.

### Derivation 1 (Left-Associative Grouping: $(id + id) + id$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\Rightarrow E + E \\
  &\Rightarrow id + E \\
  &\Rightarrow id + E + E \\
  &\Rightarrow id + id + E \\
  &\Rightarrow id + id + id
\end{aligned}
$$

### Derivation 2 (Right-Associative Grouping: $id + (id + id)$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\Rightarrow E + E \\
  &\Rightarrow E + E + E \\
  &\Rightarrow id + E + E \\
  &\Rightarrow id + id + E \\
  &\Rightarrow id + id + id
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:** The first step is identical ($E \Rightarrow E+E$), but at the **second step**, Derivation 1 replaces the **left** $E$ with $id$, while Derivation 2 replaces the **right** $E$ with $E+E$. The two sequences differ at this step. Both terminate at the same string $id + id + id$, proving $G$ is ambiguous.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU valuation tip: Examiners award **full marks** only when students construct **two distinct parse trees** OR **two distinct leftmost derivations** for the same string. Showing only one tree/derivation gets partial credit.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Removing Ambiguity from Arithmetic Grammar

**Given (Ambiguous):**
$$E \rightarrow E + E \;\vert\; E * E \;\vert\; (E) \;\vert\; id$$

**Step 1 — Establish Precedence Hierarchy:**  
`*` must bind tighter than `+`. Create a layered grammar with three non-terminals, one for each precedence level.

**Step 2 — Establish Associativity:**  
For left-associativity, the recursive production must have the new non-terminal on the **left** side of the binary operator.

**Step 3 — Construct Unambiguous Grammar:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\rightarrow E + T \;\vert\; T \\
T &\rightarrow T * F \;\vert\; F \\
F &\rightarrow (E) \;\vert\; id
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of Unambiguity for $w = id + id * id$:**

- The only parse tree possible is `id + (id * id)`, because:
  - $*$ can only be generated from $T \rightarrow T * F$.
  - $+$ can only be generated from $E \rightarrow E + T$.
  - This forces `$*$` to be evaluated at the lower (deeper) tree level, ensuring higher precedence.

**Verification of Unambiguity for $w = id - id - id$ (with $-$ added):**

- Adding $E \rightarrow E - T \;\vert\; T$ enforces **left-associativity** because the left recursive structure $E - T$ cannot be re-associated into $id - (id - id)$.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Dangling Else Resolution

**Original (Ambiguous) Grammar:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{stmt} &\rightarrow \text{if } expr \text{ then } stmt \\
            &\;\vert\; \text{if } expr \text{ then } stmt \text{ else } stmt \\
            &\;\vert\; \text{other}
\end{aligned}
$$

**String exhibiting ambiguity:**
$w = \text{if } E_1 \text{ then if } E_2 \text{ then } S_1 \text{ else } S_2$

### Resolution via Convention (No Grammar Change)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention:** "Match each `else` with the **nearest preceding unmatched `then`**." This is the rule adopted by C, C++, Java, Python, and most mainstream languages.

Under this convention, $w$ is parsed as:
$$\text{if } E_1 \text{ then } [\text{if } E_2 \text{ then } S_1 \text{ else } S_2]$$

### Resolution via Grammar Rewriting (Unambiguous Version)

Introduce a new non-terminal `matched_stmt` to enforce pairing:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{stmt} &\rightarrow \text{matched\_stmt} \;\vert\; \text{unmatched\_stmt} \\
\text{matched\_stmt} &\rightarrow \text{if } expr \text{ then } \text{matched\_stmt} \text{ else } \text{matched\_stmt} \\
                  &\;\vert\; \text{other} \\
\text{unmatched\_stmt} &\rightarrow \text{if } expr \text{ then } stmt \\
                      &\;\vert\; \text{if } expr \text{ then } \text{matched\_stmt} \text{ else } \text{unmatched\_stmt}
\end{aligned}
$$

This forces every `if` matched to an `else` to be syntactically completed within `matched_stmt`, eliminating the ambiguity.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Ambiguous Grammar | Unambiguous Grammar |
|---|---|---|
| Parse Trees per String | $\geq 1$ (possibly many) | Exactly 1 |
| Parser Compatibility | Breaks LL, LR, LALR | Compatible with all deterministic parsers |
| Semantic Determinism | Non-deterministic | Deterministic |
| Left Recursion | Often present | Often present (acceptable for bottom-up) |
| YACC Conflicts | Reports `shift/reduce` | Reports zero conflicts |
| Example | $E \rightarrow E+E \;\vert\; E*E \;\vert\; id$ | $E \rightarrow E+T \;\vert\; T$; $T \rightarrow T*F \;\vert\; F$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Two Parse Trees for the Same String (Proving Ambiguity)

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the **two distinct parse trees** for the string `id + id + id` under the ambiguous grammar $E \rightarrow E + E \;\vert\; id$.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["E"] --> B1["E"]
    A1 --> C1["+"]
    A1 --> D1["E"]
    B1 --> E1["id"]
    D1 --> F1["E"]
    D1 --> G1["+"]
    D1 --> H1["E"]
    F1 --> I1["id"]
    H1 --> J1["id"]
    style A1 fill:#ffcccc
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    A2["E"] --> B2["E"]
    A2 --> C2["+"]
    A2 --> D2["E"]
    B2 --> E2["E"]
    B2 --> F2["+"]
    B2 --> G2["E"]
    D2 --> H2["id"]
    E2 --> I2["id"]
    G2 --> J2["id"]
    style A2 fill:#ccffcc
```

**Reading the diagrams:**
- **Tree 1 (red root):** Groups as $(id + id) + id$ — **left-associative**.
- **Tree 2 (green root):** Groups as $id + (id + id)$ — **right-associative**.
- Both produce the **same final string** but **different tree structures** → **ambiguity confirmed**.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Ambiguity Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Source Grammar G"] --> S1["Identify Ambiguous String w"]
    S1 --> S2{"Two or More Parse Trees?"}
    S2 -- "No" --> S3["Grammar is Unambiguous"]
    S2 -- "Yes" --> S4["Classify Ambiguity Type"]
    S4 --> S5["Precedence Ambiguity"]
    S4 --> S6["Associativity Ambiguity"]
    S4 --> S7["Dangling Else Ambiguity"]
    S5 --> R1["Apply Layered Non-Terminals"]
    S6 --> R2["Enforce Left or Right Associativity"]
    S7 --> R3["Apply Nearest-Then Rule OR Grammar Rewriting"]
    R1 --> V1["Verify with Parser Generator"]
    R2 --> V1
    R3 --> V1
    V1 --> END["Unambiguous Grammar G Prime"]
    style S0 fill:#e6f3ff
    style END fill:#d4edda
```

## 4.3 Parse Tree for Unambiguous Arithmetic Grammar

The following diagram shows the **unique** parse tree for `id + id * id` under the unambiguous layered grammar:

```mermaid
graph TD
    U0["E"] --> U1["E"]
    U0 --> U2["+"]
    U0 --> U3["T"]
    U1 --> U4["T"]
    U4 --> U5["F"]
    U5 --> U6["id"]
    U3 --> U7["T"]
    U3 --> U8["*"]
    U3 --> U9["F"]
    U7 --> U10["F"]
    U10 --> U11["id"]
    U9 --> U12["id"]
    style U0 fill:#cce5ff
```

**Interpretation:** The `*` operator sits at a **deeper level** in the tree than `+`, which corresponds to **higher binding precedence** in the generated assembly/machine code.

## 4.4 Dangling Else — Two Parse Trees Visualized

```mermaid
graph TD
    D1["stmt"] --> D2["if E1 then"]
    D1 --> D3["stmt"]
    D3 --> D4["if E2 then"]
    D3 --> D5["S1"]
    D3 --> D6["else"]
    D3 --> D7["S2"]
    style D1 fill:#fff3cd
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    E1["stmt"] --> E2["if E1 then"]
    E1 --> E3["stmt"]
    E1 --> E4["else"]
    E1 --> E5["S2"]
    E3 --> E6["if E2 then"]
    E3 --> E7["S1"]
    style E1 fill:#f8d7da
```

The first tree attaches `else S2` to the **inner** `if` (nearest-then rule). The second tree attaches it to the **outer** `if`. Both are valid under the original grammar, demonstrating the dangling-else ambiguity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define an ambiguous grammar. Show that the grammar $S \rightarrow SS \;\vert\; a$ is ambiguous.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** A CFG is ambiguous if there exists a string with two or more distinct parse trees (or equivalently, two or more leftmost derivations).

**Proof of Ambiguity (2 Marks):** Consider the string $w = aaa$. Two distinct leftmost derivations are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Derivation 1: } S &\Rightarrow SS \Rightarrow aS \Rightarrow aSS \Rightarrow aaS \Rightarrow aaa \\
\text{Derivation 2: } S &\Rightarrow SS \Rightarrow SSa \Rightarrow Sa \Rightarrow SSa \Rightarrow aaa
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — correcting Derivation 2 carefully:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Derivation 2: } S &\Rightarrow SS \Rightarrow SSS \Rightarrow aSS \Rightarrow aaS \Rightarrow aaa
\end{aligned}
$$

Since two distinct leftmost derivations exist for the same string, the grammar is ambiguous. ∎

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: State two ways to resolve ambiguity in a grammar. Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Operator Precedence and Associativity Declaration (1 Mark):** Rewrite the grammar to enforce a layered structure, e.g., $E \rightarrow E + T \;\vert\; T$ and $T \rightarrow T * F \;\vert\; F$.

2. **Use of "Nearest `then`" Convention for Dangling Else (1 Mark):** Adopt the rule that each `else` binds to the nearest unmatched `then`.

3. **Example (1 Mark):** The grammar $E \rightarrow E + E \;\vert\; id$ is ambiguous for $id + id + id$, but the equivalent grammar $E \rightarrow E + id \;\vert\; id$ is unambiguous (forces left-associativity).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Consider the following grammar:**
$$E \rightarrow E + E \;\vert\; E * E \;\vert\; (E) \;\vert\; id$$

**(a) [7 Marks]** Prove that the grammar is ambiguous by constructing two distinct parse trees for the string `id + id * id`.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Rewrite the grammar to be unambiguous. Show the unique parse tree for `id + id * id` under the new grammar and justify the precedence and associativity rules encoded.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Proving Ambiguity (7 Marks):**

**Parse Tree 1** (Precedence: `*` binds tighter than `+`):

```mermaid
graph TD
    PA0["E"] --> PA1["E"]
    PA0 --> PA2["+"]
    PA0 --> PA3["E"]
    PA1 --> PA4["id"]
    PA3 --> PA5["E"]
    PA3 --> PA6["*"]
    PA3 --> PA7["E"]
    PA5 --> PA8["id"]
    PA7 --> PA9["id"]
    style PA0 fill:#ffe6e6
```

This tree groups as $id + (id * id)$.

**Parse Tree 2** (Incorrect precedence: `+` binds tighter):

```mermaid
graph TD
    PB0["E"] --> PB1["E"]
    PB0 --> PB2["*"]
    PB0 --> PB3["E"]
    PB1 --> PB4["E"]
    PB1 --> PB5["+"]
    PB1 --> PB6["E"]
    PB4 --> PB7["id"]
    PB6 --> PB8["id"]
    PB3 --> PB9["id"]
    style PB0 fill:#e6ffe6
```

This tree groups as $(id + id) * id$.

Since both trees derive the same string `id + id * id`, the grammar is **ambiguous**. ∎ **(7 Marks — 3 for Tree 1, 3 for Tree 2, 1 for conclusion)**

**Part (b) — Unambiguous Rewrite (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Identify precedence: `*` > `+`.

**Step 2:** Identify associativity: Both `+` and `*` are **left-associative** in standard mathematics.

**Step 3:** Construct the layered grammar (3 levels of non-terminals):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\rightarrow E + T \;\vert\; T \\
T &\rightarrow T * F \;\vert\; F \\
F &\rightarrow (E) \;\vert\; id
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Justification of Rules:**
- The recursive production $E \rightarrow E + T$ forces `+` to be **left-associative** (the left subtree must be a full expression $E$, ensuring left grouping).
- The non-terminal layering $E \supset T \supset F$ forces `*` to be evaluated **before** `+` (multiplication appears deeper in the tree).

**Step 5 — Unique Parse Tree for `id + id * id`:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q0["E"] --> Q1["E"]
    Q0 --> Q2["+"]
    Q0 --> Q3["T"]
    Q1 --> Q4["T"]
    Q4 --> Q5["F"]
    Q5 --> Q6["id"]
    Q3 --> Q7["T"]
    Q3 --> Q8["*"]
    Q3 --> Q9["F"]
    Q7 --> Q10["F"]
    Q10 --> Q11["id"]
    Q9 --> Q12["id"]
    style Q0 fill:#cce5ff
```

This tree uniquely groups as $id + (id * id)$, with `*` at a deeper level than `+`, confirming correct precedence. ∎ **(7 Marks — 2 for grammar, 2 for justification, 3 for parse tree)**

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Consider the following grammar for `if-then-else` statements:**
$$
\text{stmt} \rightarrow \text{if } e \text{ then } stmt \;\vert\; \text{if } e \text{ then } stmt \text{ else } stmt \;\vert\; s
$$

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the dangling-else ambiguity using the string `if e1 then if e2 then s1 else s2`. Construct two distinct parse trees.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Resolve the ambiguity by (i) stating the standard "nearest-then" convention, and (ii) providing an unambiguous grammar that enforces it.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Dangling Else Ambiguity (7 Marks):**

The string $w = \text{if } e_1 \text{ then if } e_2 \text{ then } s_1 \text{ else } s_2$ has two valid parse trees because the single `else` can legitimately attach to either the inner `if` (governing $e_2$) or the outer `if` (governing $e_1$).

**Parse Tree 1** — `else` attaches to **inner** `if`:

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1A["stmt"] --> R1B["if e1 then"]
    R1A --> R1C["stmt"]
    R1C --> R1D["if e2 then"]
    R1C --> R1E["s1"]
    R1C --> R1F["else"]
    R1C --> R1G["s2"]
    style R1A fill:#fff3cd
```

**Parse Tree 2** — `else` attaches to **outer** `if`:

```mermaid
graph TD
    R2A["stmt"] --> R2B["if e1 then"]
    R2A --> R2C["stmt"]
    R2A --> R2D["else"]
    R2A --> R2E["s2"]
    R2C --> R2F["if e2 then"]
    R2C --> R2G["s1"]
    style R2A fill:#f8d7da
```

Two distinct parse trees for the same string prove the grammar is ambiguous. ∎ **(7 Marks — 2 for explanation, 3 for Tree 1, 2 for Tree 2)**

**Part (b) — Resolution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Standard Convention (2 Marks):**  
> **Rule:** Each `else` is matched with the **nearest preceding unmatched `then`**. This is the rule used by C, C++, Java, Python, Pascal, and Ada.

Under this rule, $w$ is parsed as: $\text{if } e_1 \text{ then } [\text{if } e_2 \text{ then } s_1 \text{ else } s_2]$, i.e., `else` binds to the **inner** `if`.

**(ii) Unambiguous Grammar (5 Marks):**

We split `stmt` into two categories: those whose `if` is **matched** (has an `else`) and those whose `if` is **unmatched** (no `else`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{stmt} &\rightarrow \text{matched\_stmt} \;\vert\; \text{unmatched\_stmt} \\
\text{matched\_stmt} &\rightarrow \text{if } e \text{ then } \text{matched\_stmt} \text{ else } \text{matched\_stmt} \\
                   &\;\vert\; s \\
\text{unmatched\_stmt} &\rightarrow \text{if } e \text{ then } stmt \\
                      &\;\vert\; \text{if } e \text{ then } \text{matched\_stmt} \text{ else } \text{unmatched\_stmt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Justification:**
- `matched_stmt` represents an `if` that **always** has a corresponding `else`.
- The second production of `unmatched_stmt` allows an `else` to follow a matched inner `if` and attach to an outer unmatched `if` — this is the **only** way an `else` can appear, and it is **forced** to attach to the nearest unmatched `then`. **(3 Marks)**

The unique parse tree for $w$ under this grammar matches the "nearest-then" rule. ∎

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often provide **only one** parse tree in Part (a) and conclude "ambiguous." This earns partial credit. You **must** provide **two** distinct trees OR **two** distinct leftmost derivations.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** In Part (b), students rewrite the grammar but **forget to verify** with a sample string. KTU examiners explicitly test whether the new grammar is actually unambiguous — always parse a test string.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing **inherent ambiguity** (a property of the **language**) with **grammar ambiguity** (a property of a **specific grammar**). A language may have both ambiguous and unambiguous grammars.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting the equivalence of the three conditions (parse trees ↔ leftmost ↔ rightmost derivations). Examiners award bonus marks when this is stated explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Ambiguity Definition:** A grammar is ambiguous if any string has $\geq 2$ parse trees.
- [x] **Triple Equivalence:** Multiple parse trees $\iff$ multiple leftmost derivations $\iff$ multiple rightmost derivations.
- [x] **Undecidability:** Detecting ambiguity for an arbitrary CFG is **undecidable** in general.
- [x] **Inherent Ambiguity:** A language where **every** generating grammar is ambiguous (e.g., $\{a^n b^n c^m \mid n = m\} \cup \{a^n b^m c^m \mid n = m\}$).
- [x] **Dangling Else:** The classic `if-then-else` ambiguity; resolved by the "nearest-then" rule or by grammar splitting.
- [x] **Arithmetic Expression Ambiguity:** Resolved by layered non-terminals: $E \rightarrow E + T \;\vert\; T$; $T \rightarrow T * F \;\vert\; F$; $F \rightarrow (E) \;\vert\; id$.
- [x] **Left-Associativity Encoding:** Place the recursive non-terminal on the **left** side of the binary operator in the production.
- [x] **Precedence Encoding:** Operators of **lower precedence** appear in **higher-level** non-terminals; higher precedence appears in lower levels.
- [x] **Practical Detection:** YACC/Bison report **shift/reduce** and **reduce/reduce** conflicts as proxies for ambiguity.
- [x] **Examiner's Mantra:** Always show **two complete parse trees** OR **two complete leftmost derivations** for the **same** string.
- [x] **Semantics Impact:** Ambiguity leads to non-deterministic code generation — every expression must have **exactly one** valid evaluation order.
- [x] **Real-World Languages:** C, C++, Java, Python, and Ada all use the "nearest-then" rule for dangling else.
- [x] **Grammar Restrictions:** LL(k), LR(k), and LALR(k) grammars are **unambiguous by construction**.
- [x] **Conflict Resolution in YACC:** Use `%left`, `%right`, and `%nonassoc` directives to enforce associativity and precedence declaratively.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recursive Descent Parsing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Recursive Descent Parsing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Recursive Descent Parsing (RDP)** is a **top-down parsing technique** in which a set of **mutually recursive procedures** (or functions) is constructed — **one procedure for each non-terminal** of the grammar. The parser begins with the procedure corresponding to the start symbol and attempts to recognize the input string by recursively expanding non-terminals using the productions of the grammar.

A Recursive Descent Parser is formally classified as an **LL(k) parser** (Left-to-right scan, Leftmost derivation, k symbols of lookahead). For practical and tractable implementations in KTU syllabus scope, we use **LL(1)** — i.e., **one symbol of lookahead** is sufficient to choose the correct production.

There are two variants:
1. **Recursive Descent Parsing with Backtracking** — may require multiple attempts to match a production; inefficient and exponential worst-case complexity.
2. **Predictive Parsing (Recursive Descent without Backtracking)** — uses FIRST/FOLLOW sets to unambiguously select a production in **O(n)** time. This is the KTU-examined variant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** A grammar is suitable for **Predictive / Recursive Descent Parsing** **iff** it is **LL(1)**, which requires the grammar to be:
> - (a) **Free of Left Recursion**, and
> - (b) **Left Factored**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Reader" Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef following a complex recipe book**. The recipe says things like:

> *"A Sandwich needs 2 Bread slices AND Filling. A Filling can be Cheese OR Paneer OR Veggies..."*

You walk through the recipe **line by line**. At every step, you look at the **next ingredient on your counter** (your **lookahead**) and decide which sub-recipe to follow next. You never jump back to re-check a previous step — you trust the structure of the recipe.

- Each **recipe section** = a **procedure** for a non-terminal.
- The **next ingredient on the counter** = the **lookahead token** (current input symbol).
- The **decision rule** at each step = the **predictive parsing logic** based on FIRST sets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Analogy Mapping:**
> - **Sandwich → E (Expression)**
> - **Filling → T (Term)**
> - **Bread slice → F (Factor)**
> - **Ingredients on counter → input tokens (id, +, *, (, ))**
> - **Decision rule = "If next token is '+', add bread to left side; if it's 'id', start filling"**

If your recipe book had a rule like *"A Sandwich is a Sandwich + Filling"*, you'd be **stuck forever** (you'd never finish the left side before starting the right). This is exactly **left recursion** — and the recipe book (grammar) must be rewritten first!

---

### 1.3 The Parsing Hierarchy (Position in the KTU Syllabus)

> [!NOTE]
> Recursive Descent Parsing belongs to the family of **Syntax Analyzers**, which sit in the **Front-End** of a compiler. Its input is the **stream of tokens** produced by the **Lexical Analyzer**, and its output is either a **Parse Tree / Derivation** (success) or an **Error** (failure).

**Position in Compiler Pipeline:**
$$
\text{Source Code} \rightarrow \textbf{Lexical Analysis} \rightarrow \text{Tokens} \rightarrow \textbf{Syntax Analysis (Parsing)} \rightarrow \text{Parse Tree}
$$

---

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Notations (KTU Convention)

| Symbol | Meaning | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|
| $G$ | Context-Free Grammar | $G = (V_N, V_T, P, S)$ |
| $V_N$ | Set of Non-Terminals | Uppercase: $E, T, F$ |
| $V_T$ | Set of Terminals | Lowercase: $id, +, *, (, )$ |
| $P$ | Set of Productions | $A \rightarrow \alpha$ |
| $S$ | Start Symbol | Usually $E$ (Expression) |
| $\varepsilon$ | Empty String | Derives nothing |
| $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ | Set of terminals beginning of strings derivable from $\alpha$ | KTU High-Yield |
| $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ | Set of terminals that can immediately follow $A$ in some derivation | KTU High-Yield |

---

### 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Recursive Descent Parsing is **a high-weightage topic in KTU ESE Module 2**. Expected question types include:
> - Constructing **FIRST** and **FOLLOW** sets.
> - Building the **Predictive Parsing Table**.
> - **Eliminating Left Recursion**.
> - **Left Factoring** a grammar.
> - **Tracing a string parse** using the parsing table.
> These are routinely asked as **14-mark Part B** questions in KTU University Exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Workflow of a Recursive Descent Parser

The construction of a Recursive Descent Parser proceeds in **five mandatory steps**. Each step is essential — skipping any one leads to either an incorrect parser or a non-LL(1) grammar that the parser cannot handle.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Five-Step Protocol (KTU Board Standard):**
> 1. **Eliminate Left Recursion** from the grammar.
> 2. **Perform Left Factoring** to resolve common prefixes.
> 3. **Compute FIRST sets** for all non-terminals.
> 4. **Compute FOLLOW sets** for all non-terminals.
> 5. **Construct the Predictive Parsing Table (PPT)** and write recursive procedures.

---

### 2.2 Step 1 — Left Recursion Elimination

A grammar is **left-recursive** if there exists a derivation $A \Rightarrow^+ A\alpha$ for some non-terminal $A$. This causes **infinite recursion** in a top-down parser (the procedure for $A$ calls itself before consuming any input).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Rule (Immediate Left Recursion):** If $A \rightarrow A\alpha_1 \mid A\alpha_2 \mid \dots \mid A\alpha_m \mid \beta_1 \mid \beta_2 \mid \dots \mid \beta_n$ where no $\beta_i$ begins with $A$, then replace with:
>
> $$A \rightarrow \beta_1 A' \mid \beta_2 A' \mid \dots \mid \beta_n A'$$
> $$A' \rightarrow \alpha_1 A' \mid \alpha_2 A' \mid \dots \mid \alpha_m A' \mid \varepsilon$$

**For indirect left recursion** (e.g., $A \rightarrow B\alpha$, $B \rightarrow A\beta$): substitute to bring to immediate form, then eliminate.

---

### 2.3 Step 2 — Left Factoring

When two productions for the same non-terminal share a common prefix, the parser cannot decide which one to apply with just one lookahead.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Rule:** If $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2$, replace with:
> $$A \rightarrow \alpha A'$$
> $$A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2$$

This is repeated until **no two productions for the same non-terminal have a common prefix**.

---

### 2.4 Step 3 — FIRST Set Computation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> $$\text{FIRST}(\alpha) = \{ a \in V_T \mid \alpha \Rightarrow^* a\beta \} \cup \{\varepsilon \mid \alpha \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon\}$$

**Recursive Rules:**
1. If $X$ is a **terminal**, $\text{FIRST}(X) = \{X\}$.
2. If $X \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is a production, add $\varepsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.
3. If $X \rightarrow Y_1 Y_2 \dots Y_k$, then add $\text{FIRST}(Y_1) - \{\varepsilon\}$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$. If $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(Y_1)$, also add $\text{FIRST}(Y_2) - \{\varepsilon\}$, and so on. If all $Y_i$ are nullable, add $\varepsilon$.

---

### 2.5 Step 4 — FOLLOW Set Computation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> $$\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{ a \in V_T \mid S \Rightarrow^* \alpha A a \beta \}$$
> Also, **\$** (end-of-input marker) is in $\text{FOLLOW}(S)$ where $S$ is the start symbol.

**Recursive Rules:**
1. **\$** $\in \text{FOLLOW}(S)$ (start symbol).
2. If $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$, then $\text{FIRST}(\beta) - \{\varepsilon\}$ is in $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.
3. If $A \rightarrow \alpha B$ or $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$ where $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$, then $\text{FOLLOW}(A) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$.

---

### 2.6 Step 5 — Predictive Parsing Table Construction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Algorithm (KTU Board Standard):**
> For each production $A \rightarrow \alpha$:
> 1. For every terminal $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$: add $A \rightarrow \alpha$ to $\text{M}[A, a]$.
> 2. If $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$: for every $b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$, add $A \rightarrow \alpha$ to $\text{M}[A, b]$.
> 3. If after all entries, $\text{M}[A, a]$ is **empty** for any $(A, a)$ pair that can occur, the grammar is **NOT LL(1)** — flag as **error**.

> [!NOTE]
> **LL(1) Condition (Verifying the Grammar):**
> A grammar $G$ is **LL(1) if and only if** whenever $A \rightarrow \alpha \mid \beta$ are two distinct productions:
> $$\text{FIRST}(\alpha) \cap \text{FIRST}(\beta) = \emptyset$$
> AND if $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$, then $\text{FIRST}(\alpha) \cap \text{FOLLOW}(A) = \emptyset$.

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **FIRST of Terminal** | $\text{FIRST}(a) = \{a\}$ | Foundational |
| 2 | **FIRST of Non-Terminal (Concatenation)** | $X \rightarrow Y_1 Y_2 \dots Y_k$ | Foundational |
| 3 | **FOLLOW Start Symbol** | $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(S)$ | **Always** |
| 4 | **FOLLOW (Embedded)** | $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta \Rightarrow \text{FIRST}(\beta) \setminus \{\varepsilon\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$ | Core rule |
| 5 | **FOLLOW (Trailing)** | $A \rightarrow \alpha B$ or $\beta$ nullable $\Rightarrow \text{FOLLOW}(A) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$ | Core rule |
| 6 | **Left Recursion Elimination** | $A \rightarrow A\alpha \mid \beta \Rightarrow A \rightarrow \beta A', \quad A' \rightarrow \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$ | **Mandatory** for RDP |
| 7 | **Left Factoring** | $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2 \Rightarrow A \rightarrow \alpha A', \quad A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2$ | **Mandatory** for RDP |
| 8 | **LL(1) Sufficiency** | For all $A$: $\text{M}[A, a]$ has **at most one** entry | Validation check |
| 9 | **PPT Entry (Non-nullable)** | $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha) \Rightarrow \text{M}[A, a] = A \rightarrow \alpha$ | Entry rule |
| 10 | **PPT Entry (Nullable)** | $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha), \ b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A) \Rightarrow \text{M}[A, b] = A \rightarrow \alpha$ | Entry rule |
| 11 | **Parse Trace Advance** | On terminal match: input pointer advances | Trace step |
| 12 | **Parse Trace Pop / Call** | On non-terminal: corresponding procedure called | Trace step |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Note:** $\varepsilon$ (epsilon) is **never inserted** into a FOLLOW set. FOLLOW sets contain only **terminals** and **\$**. This is a common error students make in exams.

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Recursive Descent Parsers are the **backbone of modern domain-specific languages (DSLs)** and lightweight compilers. Real-world deployments include:

- **SQL parsers** in database engines (e.g., PostgreSQL's parser).
- **JSON / YAML parsers** in configuration loaders.
- **Markup language parsers** (e.g., early HTML parsers, Markdown renderers).
- **Expression evaluators** in spreadsheets (Excel formula engine, Google Sheets).
- **Compiler front-ends** of languages like **Pascal, Java (early versions), and Python's pgen** used Python's own RD parser historically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Recursive Descent over LR?** Recursive Descent Parsers are **easier to implement by hand** (no automatic parser generator like YACC/Bison required), are **highly readable**, and **integrate cleanly with semantic actions** (e.g., AST construction, type-checking). The trade-off is that they handle a **smaller class of grammars** (only LL(1)), so some grammars must be rewritten.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — The "Arithmetic Expression" Grammar

We will use the canonical **KTU textbook example** — the grammar for arithmetic expressions with `+` and `*`:

**Original Grammar (NOT LL(1)):**

$$E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$$
$$T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$$
$$F \rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This grammar is **left-recursive** (e.g., $E \rightarrow E + T$ has $E$ as the leftmost symbol on the RHS), so it **cannot be parsed by recursive descent** in its current form.

---

### 3.2 Step A — Eliminate Left Recursion

Applying the rule to each non-terminal:

**For $E$:** $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$
- $\alpha_1 = +T$, $\beta_1 = T$
- Rewritten: $E \rightarrow T E'$, $\quad E' \rightarrow + T E' \mid \varepsilon$

**For $T$:** $T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$
- $\alpha_1 = *F$, $\beta_1 = F$
- Rewritten: $T \rightarrow F T'$, $\quad T' \rightarrow * F T' \mid \varepsilon$

**For $F$:** No left recursion.

**Resulting Grammar (Left-Recursive-Free):**

$$E \rightarrow T E'$$
$$E' \rightarrow + T E' \mid \varepsilon$$
$$T \rightarrow F T'$$
$$T' \rightarrow * F T' \mid \varepsilon$$
$$F \rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of Equivalence:** The new grammar produces the **exact same language** as the original — only the derivation structure is changed. Every string derivable in the original is also derivable in the new grammar and vice versa.

---

### 3.3 Step B — Left Factoring

Inspect the new grammar:
- $E \rightarrow T E'$ — only one production. No factoring needed.
- $E' \rightarrow + T E' \mid \varepsilon$ — no common prefix. OK.
- $T \rightarrow F T'$ — only one production. OK.
- $T' \rightarrow * F T' \mid \varepsilon$ — no common prefix. OK.
- $F \rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}$ — no common prefix. OK.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** This grammar requires **no left factoring** (its left-factoring is already in canonical form). For grammars like $S \rightarrow \text{if } E \text{ then } S \mid \text{if } E \text{ then } S \text{ else } S$, factoring would be required.

---

### 3.4 Step C — Compute FIRST Sets

Apply the recursive rules systematically.

**Computing $\text{FIRST}(E)$:**
$E \rightarrow T E'$. We need $\text{FIRST}(T)$.
$\text{FIRST}(T) = \text{FIRST}(F)$ (since $T \rightarrow F T'$).
$\text{FIRST}(F)$: Productions are $F \rightarrow (E)$ and $F \rightarrow \text{id}$.
- $\text{FIRST}((E)) = \{\ (\ \}$
- $\text{FIRST}(\text{id}) = \{\ \text{id}\ \}$

So $\text{FIRST}(F) = \{\ (, \ \text{id} \}$.

Therefore $\text{FIRST}(T) = \{\ (, \ \text{id} \}$ and $\text{FIRST}(E) = \{\ (, \ \text{id} \}$.

**Computing $\text{FIRST}(E')$:**
$E' \rightarrow + T E'$: terminal begins, so $\{+\}$.
$E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$: $\varepsilon$ is nullable.

So $\text{FIRST}(E') = \{+, \varepsilon\}$.

**Computing $\text{FIRST}(T')$:**
$T' \rightarrow * F T'$: $\text{FIRST}(*) = \{*\}$.
$T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$.

So $\text{FIRST}(T') = \{*, \varepsilon\}$.

**Final FIRST Sets:**

| Non-Terminal | FIRST Set |
|---|---|
| $E$ | $\{(\ ,\ \text{id}\}$ |
| $E'$ | $\{+, \varepsilon\}$ |
| $T$ | $\{(\ ,\ \text{id}\}$ |
| $T'$ | $\{*, \varepsilon\}$ |
| $F$ | $\{(\ ,\ \text{id}\}$ |

---

### 3.5 Step D — Compute FOLLOW Sets

**Computing $\text{FOLLOW}(E)$:**
$E$ is the start symbol, so $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(E)$.
Where else does $E$ appear on the RHS? In $F \rightarrow (E)$.
In $F \rightarrow (E)$, the symbol after $E$ is $)$, so $\text{FIRST}()) = \{ ) \}$ is added.

So $\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\$\ ,\ ) \}$.

**Computing $\text{FOLLOW}(E')$:**
$E'$ appears at the end of $E \rightarrow T E'$.
So $\text{FOLLOW}(E) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(E')$, i.e., $\{\$,\ )\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(E')$.

So $\text{FOLLOW}(E') = \{\$,\ )\}$.

**Computing $\text{FOLLOW}(T)$:**
$T$ appears in $E \rightarrow T E'$. The next symbol is $E'$, so $\text{FIRST}(E') - \{\varepsilon\} = \{+\}$ is added.
Also, since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(E')$, we add $\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\$,\ )\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(T)$.

So $\text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{+, \$\ ,\ ) \}$.

**Computing $\text{FOLLOW}(T')$:**
$T'$ appears at the end of $T \rightarrow F T'$.
So $\text{FOLLOW}(T) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(T')$, i.e., $\{+, \$\ ,\ )\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(T')$.

So $\text{FOLLOW}(T') = \{+, \$\ ,\ ) \}$.

**Computing $\text{FOLLOW}(F)$:**
$F$ appears in $T \rightarrow F T'$. The next symbol is $T'$, so $\text{FIRST}(T') - \{\varepsilon\} = \{*\}$ is added.
Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(T')$, we add $\text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{+, \$\ ,\ )\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(F)$.

So $\text{FOLLOW}(F) = \{*, +, \$\ ,\ ) \}$.

**Final FOLLOW Sets:**

| Non-Terminal | FOLLOW Set |
|---|---|
| $E$ | $\{\$,\ )\}$ |
| $E'$ | $\{\$,\ )\}$ |
| $T$ | $\{+, \$\ ,\ )\}$ |
| $T'$ | $\{+, \$\ ,\ )\}$ |
| $F$ | $\{*, +, \$\ ,\ )\}$ |

---

### 3.6 Step E — Construct the Predictive Parsing Table

Apply the PPT algorithm to each production:

**Production $E \rightarrow T E'$:** $\text{FIRST}(T E') = \text{FIRST}(T) = \{(\ ,\ \text{id}\}$
- $\text{M}[E,\ ( \ ] = E \rightarrow T E'$
- $\text{M}[E,\ \text{id}\ ] = E \rightarrow T E'$

**Production $E' \rightarrow + T E'$:** $\text{FIRST}(+ T E') = \{+\}$
- $\text{M}[E',\ +] = E' \rightarrow + T E'$

**Production $E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$:** $\text{FIRST}(\varepsilon) = \{\varepsilon\}$, use $\text{FOLLOW}(E') = \{\$,\ )\}$
- $\text{M}[E',\ \$] = E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$
- $\text{M}[E',\ )] = E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$

**Production $T \rightarrow F T'$:** $\text{FIRST}(F T') = \text{FIRST}(F) = \{(\ ,\ \text{id}\}$
- $\text{M}[T,\ ( \ ] = T \rightarrow F T'$
- $\text{M}[T,\ \text{id}\ ] = T \rightarrow F T'$

**Production $T' \rightarrow * F T'$:** $\text{FIRST}(* F T') = \{*\}$
- $\text{M}[T',\ *] = T' \rightarrow * F T'$

**Production $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$:** $\text{FOLLOW}(T') = \{+, \$\ ,\ )\}$
- $\text{M}[T',\ +] = T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$
- $\text{M}[T',\ \$] = T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$
- $\text{M}[T',\ )] = T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$

**Production $F \rightarrow (E)$:** $\text{FIRST}((E)) = \{\ (\ \}$
- $\text{M}[F,\ ( \ ] = F \rightarrow (E)$

**Production $F \rightarrow \text{id}$:** $\text{FIRST}(\text{id}) = \{\ \text{id}\ \}$
- $\text{M}[F,\ \text{id}\ ] = F \rightarrow \text{id}$

**The Predictive Parsing Table:**

| | `(` | `)` | `+` | `*` | `id` | `$` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **$E$** | $E \rightarrow T E'$ | | | | $E \rightarrow T E'$ | |
| **$E'$$** | | $E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $E' \rightarrow + T E'$ | | | $E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| **$T$** | $T \rightarrow F T'$ | | | | $T \rightarrow F T'$ | |
| **$T'$** | | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $T' \rightarrow * F T'$ | | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| **$F$** | $F \rightarrow (E)$ | | | | $F \rightarrow \text{id}$ | |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Check:** The table has **no multiple entries** in any cell. Therefore, the grammar is **LL(1)** ✓ and the predictive parser is valid. (If any cell had two entries, the grammar would NOT be LL(1) and backtracking would be required.)

---

### 3.7 Step F — Parse Trace for the String `id + id * id $`

We now simulate the parser. **Stack** initially contains `$E` (top at left). **Input** is `id + id * id $`. We use the rule: **look at top of stack $X$ and current input $a$:**

- If $X = a = \$$, **accept**.
- If $X = a$ (terminals match), **pop** $X$, **advance** input.
- If $X$ is non-terminal, consult $\text{M}[X, a]$ and **push RHS** (in reverse) onto stack.
- If $\text{M}[X, a]$ is empty, **error**.

| Step | Stack (top→bottom) | Input (left→right) | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `$E` | `id + id * id $` | $\text{M}[E, \text{id}] = E \rightarrow T E'$ |
| 2 | `$E' T` | `id + id * id $` | $\text{M}[T, \text{id}] = T \rightarrow F T'$ |
| 3 | `$E' T' F` | `id + id * id $` | $\text{M}[F, \text{id}] = F \rightarrow \text{id}$ |
| 4 | `$E' T' id` | `id + id * id $` | Match `id`, pop, advance |
| 5 | `$E' T'` | `+ id * id $` | $\text{M}[T', +] = T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| 6 | `$E'` | `+ id * id $` | $\text{M}[E', +] = E' \rightarrow + T E'$ |
| 7 | `$E' T +` | `+ id * id $` | Match `+`, pop, advance |
| 8 | `$E' T` | `id * id $` | $\text{M}[T, \text{id}] = T \rightarrow F T'$ |
| 9 | `$E' T' F` | `id * id $` | $\text{M}[F, \text{id}] = F \rightarrow \text{id}$ |
| 10 | `$E' T' id` | `id * id $` | Match `id`, pop, advance |
| 11 | `$E' T'` | `* id $` | $\text{M}[T', *] = T' \rightarrow * F T'$ |
| 12 | `$E' T' F *` | `* id $` | Match `*`, pop, advance |
| 13 | `$E' T' F` | `id $` | $\text{M}[F, \text{id}] = F \rightarrow \text{id}$ |
| 14 | `$E' T' id` | `id $` | Match `id`, pop, advance |
| 15 | `$E' T'` | `$` | $\text{M}[T', \$] = T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| 16 | `$E'` | `$` | $\text{M}[E', \$] = E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| 17 | `$` | `$` | **ACCEPT ✓** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Parse Trace Verification:** The string `id + id * id` is **successfully accepted**. Notice how the parser correctly respects operator precedence (multiplication binds tighter than addition) **without any backtracking** — purely table-driven.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Recursive Descent Parser

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a Recursive Descent Parser and its interaction with the lexical analyzer and the parser table.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputSource["Input Layer"]
        SRC["Source Code<br/>(Character Stream)"]
        LEX["Lexical Analyzer<br/>(Scanner)"]
        TOK["Token Stream<br/>id, +, *, (, ), $"]
    end

    subgraph ParserCore["Parser Core (Recursive Descent)"]
        PROC_E["Procedure E()<br/>Matches Expressions"]
        PROC_EPRIME["Procedure EPrime()<br/>Handles +T E' or empty"]
        PROC_T["Procedure T()<br/>Matches Terms"]
        PROC_TPRIME["Procedure TPrime()<br/>Handles *F T' or empty"]
        PROC_F["Procedure F()<br/>Matches Factors: id or (E)"]
    end

    subgraph DataLayer["Data Structures"]
        STACK["Call Stack<br/>(Recursive Frames)"]
        PPT["Predictive Parsing Table<br/>M[A, a] lookup"]
        AST["Abstract Syntax Tree<br/>(Output)"]
    end

    subgraph ErrorHandler["Error Subsystem"]
        ERR["Error Reporter<br/>Line + Column"]
    end

    SRC --> LEX
    LEX --> TOK
    TOK --> PROC_E
    PROC_E --> PROC_T
    PROC_T --> PROC_F
    PROC_F -->|"Matches id"| TOK
    PROC_F -->|"Matches (E)"| PROC_E
    PROC_E --> PROC_EPRIME
    PROC_T --> PROC_TPRIME
    PROC_EPRIME -.->|"empty, return"| STACK
    PROC_TPRIME -.->|"empty, return"| STACK
    PROC_E --> PPT
    PROC_T --> PPT
    PROC_F --> PPT
    PROC_EPRIME --> PPT
    PROC_TPRIME --> PPT
    PROC_E --> AST
    PROC_T --> AST
    PROC_F --> AST
    PROC_E -->|"M[X,a] empty"| ERR
    PROC_T -->|"M[X,a] empty"| ERR
    PROC_F -->|"M[X,a] empty"| ERR

    style ParserCore fill:#e3f2fd
    style DataLayer fill:#fff3e0
    style ErrorHandler fill:#ffebee
```

---

### 4.2 Recursive Call Tree (Call Stack Behavior)

This diagram traces the **call stack depth** during parsing of `id + id`. Notice the **LIFO return** behavior — the most recently called procedure returns first.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CALL1["Call: E()"]
    CALL2["Call: T()"]
    CALL3["Call: F()"]
    MATCH1["Match: id<br/>(consume token)"]
    RETURN1["Return from F()"]
    RETURN2["Return from T()"]
    CALL4["Call: EPrime()"]
    CALL5["Call: T()"]
    CALL6["Call: F()"]
    MATCH2["Match: id<br/>(consume token)"]
    RETURN3["Return from F()"]
    RETURN4["Return from T()"]
    RETURN5["Return from EPrime()"]
    RETURN6["Return from E()"]

    CALL1 --> CALL2
    CALL2 --> CALL3
    CALL3 --> MATCH1
    MATCH1 --> RETURN1
    RETURN1 --> RETURN2
    RETURN2 --> CALL4
    CALL4 --> CALL5
    CALL5 --> CALL6
    CALL6 --> MATCH2
    MATCH2 --> RETURN3
    RETURN3 --> RETURN4
    RETURN4 --> RETURN5
    RETURN5 --> RETURN6

    style CALL1 fill:#bbdefb
    style CALL2 fill:#c5e1a5
    style CALL3 fill:#fff59d
    style MATCH1 fill:#ffccbc
    style CALL4 fill:#c5e1a5
    style CALL5 fill:#fff59d
    style MATCH2 fill:#ffccbc
    style RETURN6 fill:#b39ddb
```

---

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Predictive Parsing Logic

This diagram shows the **core decision loop** that drives the parser — equivalent to what is mechanically implemented in the parsing table lookup.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START(["Parser Activated<br/>Stack = $S, Input = w$"])
    CHECK{"Stack Top X<br/>== Input $ ?"}
    ACCEPT(["ACCEPT<br/>String Valid"])
    TERMCHK{"X is a<br/>terminal?"}
    MATCHOP["Pop X from Stack<br/>Advance Input Pointer<br/>(Consume Token)"]
    NONTERM{"X is a<br/>non-terminal?"}
    TABLE{"M[X, a]<br/>has entry?"}
    APPLY["Push RHS of<br/>production on Stack<br/>(reverse order)"]
    SYNC["Error Recovery<br/>Pop or Insert"]
    ERROR(["SYNTAX ERROR<br/>Report and Halt"])

    START --> CHECK
    CHECK -->|"Yes"| ACCEPT
    CHECK -->|"No"| TERMCHK
    TERMCHK -->|"Yes, X == a"| MATCHOP
    TERMCHK -->|"No, mismatch"| ERROR
    TERMCHK -->|"No, X is non-term"| NONTERM
    NONTERM --> TABLE
    TABLE -->|"Entry exists"| APPLY
    TABLE -->|"Empty cell"| SYNC
    APPLY --> CHECK
    MATCHOP --> CHECK
    SYNC --> CHECK

    style START fill:#c8e6c9
    style ACCEPT fill:#a5d6a7
    style ERROR fill:#ef9a9a
```

---

### 4.4 Mapping of Grammar Productions to Procedure Calls

This **architecture flow** visualizes the relationship between the productions of the grammar and the corresponding recursive procedure calls.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Grammar["Grammar Productions"]
        P1["E → T EPrime"]
        P2["EPrime → + T EPrime"]
        P3["EPrime → epsilon"]
        P4["T → F TPrime"]
        P5["TPrime → * F TPrime"]
        P6["TPrime → epsilon"]
        P7["F → ( E )"]
        P8["F → id"]
    end

    subgraph Procedures["Parser Procedures"]
        PROC1["proc E()<br/>Call T()<br/>Call EPrime()"]
        PROC2["proc EPrime()<br/>If next = '+': consume, call T(), call EPrime()<br/>Else: return"]
        PROC3["proc T()<br/>Call F()<br/>Call TPrime()"]
        PROC4["proc TPrime()<br/>If next = '*': consume, call F(), call TPrime()<br/>Else: return"]
        PROC5["proc F()<br/>If next = '(': consume, call E(), expect ')'<br/>If next = 'id': consume<br/>Else: error"]
    end

    P1 --> PROC1
    P2 --> PROC2
    P3 --> PROC2
    P4 --> PROC3
    P5 --> PROC4
    P6 --> PROC4
    P7 --> PROC5
    P8 --> PROC5

    PROC1 -.-> PROC3
    PROC3 -.-> PROC5
    PROC1 -.-> PROC2
    PROC3 -.-> PROC4

    style Grammar fill:#e1f5fe
    style Procedures fill:#f3e5f5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** Each non-terminal $X$ in the grammar maps to **exactly one procedure** $X()$. The body of the procedure is constructed by replacing each non-terminal on the RHS with the corresponding procedure call, and each terminal with a "consume token" action. This is the **mechanical translation** from grammar to code that makes RDPs extremely easy to implement by hand.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define Recursive Descent Parsing. List the conditions a grammar must satisfy to be parsable by Recursive Descent Parsing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Recursive Descent Parsing** is a top-down parsing technique in which a set of mutually recursive procedures is constructed — one procedure for each non-terminal of the grammar. The parser begins with the procedure for the start symbol and attempts to recognize the input by recursively expanding non-terminals based on the productions.

**Conditions for the grammar to be parsable by RDP (Predictive, non-backtracking):**
1. The grammar must be **free of left recursion** (to prevent infinite recursion).
2. The grammar must be **left-factored** (to resolve common-prefix ambiguity).
3. The grammar must be **LL(1)** — for any two productions $A \rightarrow \alpha \mid \beta$, $\text{FIRST}(\alpha) \cap \text{FIRST}(\beta) = \emptyset$ (or, if $\varepsilon$ is involved, the conditions must hold with FOLLOW sets).

**[1 mark — definition; 2 marks — conditions with brief explanation]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is the role of FIRST and FOLLOW sets in predictive parsing? Why is \$ included in FOLLOW of the start symbol?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role of FIRST:**
- FIRST($\alpha$) gives the set of terminals that can begin any string derived from $\alpha$.
- It is used to decide **which production to apply** by looking at the next input symbol.
- For production $A \rightarrow \alpha$, we add $A \rightarrow \alpha$ to the parsing table entry $\text{M}[A, a]$ for every $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$.

**Role of FOLLOW:**
- FOLLOW($A$) gives the set of terminals that can legally appear immediately after $A$ in some sentential form.
- It is used when a production's RHS is **nullable** ($\varepsilon$-derivable): we add $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ to $\text{M}[A, b]$ for every $b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$.

**Why \$ is in FOLLOW($S$):**
- The marker **\$** denotes **end of input**. Since the start symbol $S$ can derive a complete input string, the parser must accept when $S$ has been fully matched and the input has been fully consumed. Therefore, the "end-of-input" marker is always a valid symbol to follow $S$, ensuring the parser can recognize successful termination.

**[1 mark each for FIRST role, FOLLOW role, and \$ explanation]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Convention:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** and typically have an **internal choice** — two alternative questions of equal difficulty. Each question is split into sub-parts, e.g., **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Below we provide the **mandatory alternative pair**.

---

## **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Consider the following grammar for a programming language construct:

$$S \rightarrow A a \mid b A c$$
$$A \rightarrow S b \mid b$$

**Eliminate left recursion from the grammar and compute FIRST and FOLLOW sets for all non-terminals.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct the **Predictive Parsing Table** for the modified grammar. **Is the grammar LL(1)? Justify.** Verify by parsing the string **$b \ b \ b \ a \$$** using the table. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Left Recursion Elimination + FIRST/FOLLOW (7 marks)

**Step 1: Identify left recursion.**

The non-terminal $S$ does not have immediate left recursion ($S \rightarrow Aa \mid bc$, none begin with $S$).

The non-terminal $A$ has productions $A \rightarrow Sb \mid b$. Substituting the RHS of $S$ into $A \rightarrow Sb$:

$$A \rightarrow Aa b \mid b c b \mid b$$

Now $A$ has **immediate left recursion** $A \rightarrow Aab$.

**Step 2: Apply left-recursion elimination rule.**

For $A \rightarrow Aab \mid bcb \mid b$:
- $\alpha_1 = ab$, $\beta_1 = bcb$, $\beta_2 = b$.

Rewritten:
$$A \rightarrow bcb \ A' \mid b \ A'$$
$$A' \rightarrow ab \ A' \mid \varepsilon$$

The complete modified grammar (call it $G'$):
$$S \rightarrow Aa \mid bc$$
$$A \rightarrow bcb A' \mid b A'$$
$$A' \rightarrow ab A' \mid \varepsilon$$

**Step 3: Compute FIRST sets.**

- $\text{FIRST}(S)$: $S \rightarrow Aa$ gives $\text{FIRST}(A)$; $S \rightarrow bc$ gives $\{b\}$. So we need $\text{FIRST}(A)$.
- $\text{FIRST}(A)$: $A \rightarrow bcb A'$ gives $\{b\}$; $A \rightarrow b A'$ gives $\{b\}$. So $\text{FIRST}(A) = \{b\}$.
- $\text{FIRST}(S) = \{b\}$.
- $\text{FIRST}(A')$: $A' \rightarrow ab A'$ gives $\{a\}$; $A' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ gives $\{\varepsilon\}$. So $\text{FIRST}(A') = \{a, \varepsilon\}$.

**Step 4: Compute FOLLOW sets.**

- $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$ (start symbol).
- $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$: $A$ appears in $S \rightarrow Aa$, so $\text{FIRST}(a) = \{a\}$ is added. So $\text{FOLLOW}(A) \supseteq \{a\}$.
- $\text{FOLLOW}(A')$: $A'$ appears at the end of $A \rightarrow bcb A'$ and $A \rightarrow b A'$. So $\text{FOLLOW}(A) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(A')$, i.e., $\{a\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(A')$.

So:
- $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$
- $\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{a\}$
- $\text{FOLLOW}(A') = \{a\}$

| Non-Terminal | FIRST | FOLLOW |
|---|---|---|
| $S$ | $\{b\}$ | $\{\$\}$ |
| $A$ | $\{b\}$ | $\{a\}$ |
| $A'$ | $\{a, \varepsilon\}$ | $\{a\}$ |

**[Distribution: 2 marks for elimination, 2 marks for FIRST, 2 marks for FOLLOW, 1 mark for table presentation]**

---

#### Part (b) — Predictive Parsing Table + LL(1) Check + Parse Trace (7 marks)

**Step 1: Construct the Predictive Parsing Table.**

- $S \rightarrow Aa$: $\text{FIRST}(Aa) = \text{FIRST}(A) = \{b\}$. So $\text{M}[S, b] = S \rightarrow Aa$.
- $S \rightarrow bc$: $\text{FIRST}(bc) = \{b\}$. **Conflict!** — both productions would map to $\text{M}[S, b]$.

> [!WARNING]
> **MULTIPLE ENTRIES DETECTED in $\text{M}[S, b]$: $S \rightarrow Aa$ and $S \rightarrow bc$.**

**The grammar is NOT LL(1)!** Two productions for $S$ have overlapping FIRST sets ($\{b\} \cap \{b\} = \{b\} \neq \emptyset$).

**Step 2: Justification.**

Since the predictive parsing table has at least one cell with multiple entries, the grammar fails the LL(1) condition. To make it suitable for predictive/recursive descent parsing, **left factoring** must be applied.

**Step 3: Left factoring (for completeness / partial credit).**

$S \rightarrow Aa \mid bc$ has no common prefix. **However**, the underlying issue is that $A$ itself can derive strings starting with $b$, so the only way to disambiguate would be to expand $A$ inline first, or to rewrite the grammar more carefully. Since $A$ produces strings starting with $b$ and $A$ is followed by $a$, the combined FIRST of $Aa$ is $\{b\} \cup \{b\} = \{b\}$, while $bc$ is also $\{b\}$. **The grammar is fundamentally not LL(1)** because of this conflict.

**Step 4: Parse trace (best-effort) for `b b b a $`.**

Since the grammar is not LL(1), a **non-predictive RDP with backtracking** would be required. The trace cannot be cleanly given using a single PPT; rather, the parser would try $S \rightarrow Aa$ first, and if it fails, backtrack to $S \rightarrow bc$.

**[Distribution: 2 marks PPT, 2 marks LL(1) justification, 2 marks parse trace/discussion of backtracking, 1 mark for conclusion]**

---

## **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Consider the following grammar:

$$S \rightarrow S + S \mid S S \mid (S) \mid S* \mid \text{id}$$

**Show that this grammar is ambiguous and rewrite it into an unambiguous LL(1) grammar suitable for Recursive Descent Parsing.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the rewritten grammar, compute **FIRST and FOLLOW sets**, construct the **Predictive Parsing Table**, and demonstrate a parse of the string **$\text{id} \ * \ \text{id} + \text{id} \$$** showing that operator precedence is respected. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Ambiguity + Unambiguous Reformulation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Demonstrate ambiguity.**

The string `id + id + id` has two distinct leftmost derivations:

**Derivation 1 (left-associative):**
$$S \Rightarrow S + S \Rightarrow S + S + S \Rightarrow \text{id} + S + S \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} + S \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} + \text{id}$$

**Derivation 2 (right-associative):**
$$S \Rightarrow S + S \Rightarrow S + S + S \Rightarrow S + S + \text{id} \Rightarrow S + \text{id} + \text{id} \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} + \text{id}$$

Since the same string has **two distinct parse trees**, the grammar is **ambiguous**.

**Step 2: Rewrite to remove left recursion and enforce precedence.**
