The standard technique is to introduce non-terminals for each **precedence level** (lower non-terminal = lower precedence, binds later). Following the canonical KTU textbook formulation:

$$E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$$
$$T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$$
$$F \rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}$$

- `$E$` handles `+` (lowest precedence).
- `$T$` handles `*` (higher precedence).
- `$F$` handles primary operands: `id` and parenthesized expressions.

**Step 3: Eliminate left recursion.**

$$E \rightarrow T E'$$
$$E' \rightarrow + T E' \mid \varepsilon$$
$$T \rightarrow F T'$$
$$T' \rightarrow * F T' \mid \varepsilon$$
$$F \rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}$$

This is the **standard unambiguous LL(1) grammar** for arithmetic expressions. (Note: $S \to S*$ is implicitly handled because the structure ensures `*` binds tighter than `+` — $S*$ becomes part of the $F \to \text{id}$ interpretation as $F \to (E) \mid \text{id}$ with $T' \to * F T'$ enforcing that `*` is evaluated within $T$ first.)

**[Distribution: 2 marks ambiguity proof, 3 marks precedence-based rewriting, 2 marks left-recursion elimination]**

---

#### Part (b) — FIRST/FOLLOW + PPT + Parse Trace (7 marks)

**Step 1: FIRST and FOLLOW Sets (already derived in Section 3.4 and 3.5).**

| Non-Terminal | FIRST | FOLLOW |
|---|---|---|
| $E$ | $\{(\ ,\ \text{id}\}$ | $\{\$,\ )\}$ |
| $E'$ | $\{+, \varepsilon\}$ | $\{\$,\ )\}$ |
| $T$ | $\{(\ ,\ \text{id}\}$ | $\{+, \$\ ,\ )\}$ |
| $T'$ | $\{*, \varepsilon\}$ | $\{+, \$\ ,\ )\}$ |
| $F$ | $\{(\ ,\ \text{id}\}$ | $\{*, +, \$\ ,\ )\}$ |

**Step 2: Predictive Parsing Table (already derived in Section 3.6).**

| | `(` | `)` | `+` | `*` | `id` | `$` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **$E$** | $E \rightarrow T E'$ | | | | $E \rightarrow T E'$ | |
| **$E'$$** | | $E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $E' \rightarrow + T E'$ | | | $E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| **$T$** | $T \rightarrow F T'$ | | | | $T \rightarrow F T'$ | |
| **$T'$** | | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $T' \rightarrow * F T'$ | | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| **$F$** | $F \rightarrow (E)$ | | | | $F \rightarrow \text{id}$ | |

**Step 3: Parse Trace for `id * id + id $`.**

| Step | Stack | Input | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `$E` | `id * id + id $` | $E \rightarrow T E'$ |
| 2 | `$E' T` | `id * id + id $` | $T \rightarrow F T'$ |
| 3 | `$E' T' F` | `id * id + id $` | $F \rightarrow \text{id}$, match |
| 4 | `$E' T'` | `* id + id $` | $T' \rightarrow * F T'$ |
| 5 | `$E' T' F *` | `* id + id $` | Match `*` |
| 6 | `$E' T' F` | `id + id $` | $F \rightarrow \text{id}$, match |
| 7 | `$E' T'` | `+ id $` | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| 8 | `$E'` | `+ id $` | $E' \rightarrow + T E'$ |
| 9 | `$E' T +` | `+ id $` | Match `+` |
| 10 | `$E' T` | `id $` | $T \rightarrow F T'$ |
| 11 | `$E' T' F` | `id $` | $F \rightarrow \text{id}$, match |
| 12 | `$E' T'` | `$` | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| 13 | `$E'` | `$` | $E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| 14 | `$` | `$` | **ACCEPT ✓** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Precedence Verification:** Notice that in Step 4, $T' \rightarrow * F T'$ is expanded **before** the `$+$` is consumed (Step 8). This means `id * id` is **grouped first** as the left operand of `+`, exactly matching the precedence rule that `*` binds tighter than `+`. The parse tree would have the `*` deeper in the tree (evaluated first), confirming **precedence is respected**. ✓

**[Distribution: 2 marks FIRST/FOLLOW, 2 marks PPT construction, 3 marks parse trace with precedence justification]**

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> **Pitfall #1: Putting $\varepsilon$ in FOLLOW sets.**
> FOLLOW sets contain only **terminals** and **\$**. $\varepsilon$ is **never** a member of FOLLOW. Students who incorrectly include $\varepsilon$ lose 1 mark per occurrence.
>
> **Pitfall #2: Forgetting the \$ in the parsing table.**
> The **end-of-input marker** must be a column in the predictive parsing table. Productions whose RHS is nullable ($\varepsilon$-derivable) get added to $\text{M}[A, \$]$ for $A$'s where $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$. Missing this column causes incomplete parsing tables.
>
> **Pitfall #3: Confusing FIRST(RHS) computation with FIRST(LHS).**
> For a production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_k$, students must compute $\text{FIRST}(X_1 X_2 \dots X_k)$ — the **first symbol of the RHS string** — not FIRST($A$). Common mistake: copying $\text{FIRST}(A)$ into the table instead of $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$.
>
> **Pitfall #4: Missing left recursion elimination step.**
> If the question provides a left-recursive grammar and the student uses it directly for FIRST/FOLLOW, the resulting parser will **not work**. KTU strictly requires showing the elimination step. Marks are awarded for this step explicitly.
>
> **Pitfall #5: Not verifying LL(1) after table construction.**
> The PPT may have **multiple entries** in a single cell — this means the grammar is **not LL(1)** and the student must state this explicitly. Failing to verify loses 1 mark.
>
> **Pitfall #6: Mixing up FIRST and FOLLOW in nullable cases.**
> When $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, the table entry uses **FOLLOW($A$)**, not FIRST($A$). Students often write FOLLOW($A$) entries into FIRST-derived cells and vice versa. Each such error costs 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist — Recursive Descent Parsing**
>
> **Core Concepts:**
> - [x] Recursive Descent = **top-down** parsing, one procedure per non-terminal.
> - [x] Predictive RD (no backtracking) requires the grammar to be **LL(1)**.
> - [x] Two preconditions: **no left recursion** + **left-factored**.
>
> **FIRST Set Rules (memorize):**
> - [x] Terminal: $\text{FIRST}(a) = \{a\}$.
> - [x] Nullable: $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(X)$ if $X \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon$.
> - [x] Concatenation: $\text{FIRST}(X_1 X_2) = \text{FIRST}(X_1) \cup \text{FIRST}(X_2)$ (if $X_1$ nullable).
>
> **FOLLOW Set Rules (memorize):**
> - [x] Start symbol: $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(S)$.
> - [x] Embedded: $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta \Rightarrow \text{FIRST}(\beta) \setminus \{\varepsilon\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$.
> - [x] Trailing / nullable: $A \rightarrow \alpha B$ or $\beta$ nullable $\Rightarrow \text{FOLLOW}(A) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$.
> - [x] **Never include $\varepsilon$ in FOLLOW.**
>
> **Left Recursion Elimination (memorize the template):**
> - [x] $A \rightarrow A\alpha \mid \beta \Rightarrow A \rightarrow \beta A', \quad A' \rightarrow \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$.
>
> **Left Factoring (memorize the template):**
> - [x] $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2 \Rightarrow A \rightarrow \alpha A', \quad A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2$.
>
> **Predictive Parsing Table:**
> - [x] Cell $\text{M}[A, a]$ filled by every production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ where $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$.
> - [x] If $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$: fill $\text{M}[A, b]$ for all $b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$.
> - [x] **LL(1) check:** no cell should have more than one entry.
>
> **Parse Trace Steps:**
> - [x] Initialize stack = `$S$`, input = `w$`.
> - [x] If stack top = input symbol: **match & advance**.
> - [x] If stack top is non-terminal: **look up PPT, push RHS in reverse**.
> - [x] If stack top = `$` and input = `$`: **ACCEPT**.
> - [x] If PPT cell is empty: **SYNTAX ERROR**.
>
> **Arithmetic Expression Grammar (the KTU standard):**
> - [x] $E \rightarrow T E'$, $E' \rightarrow + T E' \mid \varepsilon$.
> - [x] $T \rightarrow F T'$, $T' \rightarrow * F T' \mid \varepsilon$.
> - [x] $F \rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}$.
>
> **Real-World Anchor:**
> - [x] Used in SQL parsers, JSON/YAML parsers, Pascal/Java compilers, expression evaluators.
> - [x] Trade-off: easy to implement by hand, but handles only LL(1) grammars.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Predictive Parsing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Predictive Parsing** is a specialized form of **top-down syntax analysis** that attempts to construct a parse tree for an input string by scanning the input from **left to right**, deriving the sentential form in the **leftmost derivation** order, and using a deterministic mechanism that requires **only one input symbol of lookahead** to select the correct production rule — without any backtracking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Canonical Form):**
> Predictive Parsing belongs to the class of **LL(k) parsers**, where the first **L** denotes *Left-to-right scan* of input, the second **L** denotes *Leftmost derivation*, and **k** denotes the number of *lookahead symbols* (for KTU Module 2, we exclusively study **k = 1**, i.e., **LL(1) Predictive Parsers**).

A grammar is said to be **LL(1)** if and only if its **predictive parsing table** contains **no multiply-defined entries** (i.e., every cell $M[A, a]$ contains at most one production rule for any non-terminal $A$ and terminal $a$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The GPS Navigation Analogy

Imagine you are driving through an unfamiliar city using a **GPS navigation system**:

| Real-World Element | Predictive Parsing Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Your current road (position) | The top of the **parser stack** |
| The next road sign (lookahead) | The next **input symbol** |
| GPS deciding which turn to take | Consulting the **predictive parsing table** $M[A, a]$ |
| Pre-loaded city map | The **LL(1) grammar** with no ambiguity |
| A wrong turn causing you to backtrack | **Backtracking** (which predictive parsing **eliminates**) |

In a normal GPS without predictive routing, if you took a wrong turn, you would have to reverse and try a different path (this is called *backtracking*, used in *recursive descent parsing*). However, a **predictive GPS** is so well-mapped that it tells you, at every junction, *exactly* which exit to take based on the current road and the next signboard — **no reversal needed**. This deterministic, no-backtracking property is the **defining hallmark** of predictive parsing.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Quantitative Bounds (KTU Board Standard):**
> - **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ where $n$ is the length of the input string.
> - **Space Complexity:** $O(n)$ due to the explicit **parser stack**.
> - **Lookahead Required:** Exactly **1** input symbol for LL(1).
> - **Determinism Guarantee:** Every table cell must be **uniquely defined** (singleton).
> - **Grammar Subclass:** Strict subset of general Context-Free Grammars (CFGs).

## 1.4 Classification of Top-Down Parsers

```
Top-Down Parsing
├── Backtracking Parsing (Recursive Descent with backtracking) — INEFFICIENT
├── Recursive Descent Parsing (No backtracking) — Multi-procedures
└── Predictive Parsing (Table-driven LL(1)) — ★ KTU FOCUS ★
```

The predictive parser is also called a **table-driven parser** because all parsing decisions are encoded in a single 2D matrix called the **Predictive Parsing Table** $M$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Predictive Parsing

For a grammar $G = (V_N, V_T, P, S)$ to admit a valid LL(1) predictive parser, the following three theoretical pillars must be established:

### Pillar 1: FIRST Sets (Foundation Stone)

**Definition:** For any string $\alpha$ of grammar symbols, $FIRST(\alpha)$ is the set of **terminals** that begin the strings derivable from $\alpha$. If $\alpha \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon$, then $\varepsilon \in FIRST(\alpha)$.

**Formal Notation:**
$$FIRST(\alpha) = \{ a \in V_T \cup \{\varepsilon\} \mid \alpha \Rightarrow^* a\beta \text{ for some } \beta \}$$

### Pillar 2: FOLLOW Sets (Lookahead Oracle)

**Definition:** For any non-terminal $A$, $FOLLOW(A)$ is the set of **terminals** that can appear **immediately to the right** of $A$ in some sentential form. If $A$ can be the rightmost symbol, then **\$** (the end-of-input marker) is in $FOLLOW(A)$.

**Formal Notation:**
$$FOLLOW(A) = \{ a \in V_T \cup \{\$\} \mid S \Rightarrow^* \alpha A a \beta \text{ for some } \alpha, \beta \}$$

### Pillar 3: Predictive Parsing Table $M[A, a]$

The matrix $M$ is indexed by **non-terminals** (rows) and **terminals + \$** (columns). Each cell contains **at most one production rule** of the form $A \to \alpha$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Construction Rules for FIRST

**Rule F1 (Terminal):**
$$X \in V_T \implies FIRST(X) = \{X\}$$

**Rule F2 (Epsilon Production):**
$$X \to \varepsilon \in P \implies \varepsilon \in FIRST(X)$$

**Rule F3 (Production Body):**
$$X \to Y_1 Y_2 \ldots Y_k$$
Add $FIRST(Y_1) \setminus \{\varepsilon\}$ to $FIRST(X)$. If $Y_1 \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon$, then also add $FIRST(Y_2) \setminus \{\varepsilon\}$, and so on. If all $Y_i \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon$, then $\varepsilon \in FIRST(X)$.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Construction Rules for FOLLOW

**Rule L1 (Start Symbol):** Put **\$** in $FOLLOW(S)$ where $S$ is the start symbol.

**Rule L2 (Right-side Occurrence):** If $A \to \alpha B \beta$, then everything in $FIRST(\beta)$ **except** $\varepsilon$ is added to $FOLLOW(B)$.

**Rule L3 (Epsilon Derivation):** If $A \to \alpha B$ or $A \to \alpha B \beta$ where $\beta \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon$, then everything in $FOLLOW(A)$ is added to $FOLLOW(B)$.

## 2.4 Predictive Parsing Table Construction Algorithm

**Input:** Grammar $G$ (must be LL(1))
**Output:** Parsing Table $M$

```
For each production A → α in P:
    For each terminal 'a' in FIRST(α):
        Set M[A, a] = A → α
    If ε ∈ FIRST(α):
        For each terminal 'b' in FOLLOW(A):
            Set M[A, b] = A → α
        If $ ∈ FOLLOW(A):
            Set M[A, $] = A → α
```

If any cell is assigned **more than one production**, the grammar is **NOT LL(1)** and predictive parsing fails.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Symbol | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| FIRST of terminal | $FIRST(a) = \{a\}$ | $a \in V_T$ | Always |
| FIRST of production | $FIRST(\alpha) = \bigcup FIRST(X_i)$ until $\varepsilon$ found | $A \to X_1 X_2 \ldots X_k$ | Iterative |
| FOLLOW start | $\$ \in FOLLOW(S)$ | $S$ = start | Mandatory |
| FOLLOW propagation | $FOLLOW(B) \supseteq FIRST(\beta)$ | $A \to \alpha B \beta$ | $\beta \neq \varepsilon$ |
| FOLLOW epsilon chain | $FOLLOW(B) \supseteq FOLLOW(A)$ | $A \to \alpha B \beta, \beta \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon \in FIRST(\beta)$ |
| Table entry (terminal) | $M[A, a] = A \to \alpha$ | $a \in FIRST(\alpha)$ | Standard |
| Table entry (epsilon) | $M[A, b] = A \to \alpha$ | $\varepsilon \in FIRST(\alpha), b \in FOLLOW(A)$ | Special |
| LL(1) validity | $\vert M[A, a] \vert \leq 1$ | All $(A, a)$ pairs | Disjointness |
| Parser time | $O(n)$ | $n = \vert w \vert$ | Linear |
| Stack depth | $\leq \vert w \vert + 1$ | Worst case | Linear |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application Note:**
> Predictive parsers form the **syntactic backbone** of several production compilers and DSLs:
> - **GCC** historically used LALR(1) (a related bottom-up technique), but tools like **ANTLR** generate **ALL(*)** (a generalized LL-based) parsers.
> - **SQL parsers** in databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL) heavily rely on LL(k) parsers.
> - **Lua**, **Python** (in part), and **HTML/XML parsers** use variants of predictive parsing.
> - **IDE syntax checkers** (VS Code, IntelliJ) use LL parsing for real-time error detection.

The determinism of predictive parsers makes them **ideal for real-time systems**, **embedded compilers**, and **high-speed DSL processors** where predictable, linear-time behavior is mandatory.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Example

## 3.1 The Reference Grammar (Standard KTU Exam Pattern)

Consider the following grammar $G$ for arithmetic expressions with left-associative addition and multiplication:

$$E \to T E'$$
$$E' \to + T E' \mid \varepsilon$$
$$T \to F T'$$
$$T' \to * F T' \mid \varepsilon$$
$$F \to ( E ) \mid \text{id}$$

**Objective:** Build the **FIRST sets**, **FOLLOW sets**, and the **Predictive Parsing Table** $M$, then trace the parsing of the input string `id + id * id`.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of FIRST Sets

We apply the **fixed-point algorithm**: keep iterating the rules until no new symbol is added to any set.

### Step F.1: Compute $FIRST(F)$

**Production:** $F \to (E)$
$$FIRST((E)) = \{( \}$$

**Production:** $F \to \text{id}$
$$FIRST(\text{id}) = \{\text{id}\}$$

$$\therefore FIRST(F) = \{(,\ \text{id}\}$$

### Step F.2: Compute $FIRST(T')$

**Production:** $T' \to *FT'$
$$FIRST(*FT') = \{*\}$$

**Production:** $T' \to \varepsilon$
$$\varepsilon \in FIRST(T')$$

$$\therefore FIRST(T') = \{*, \varepsilon\}$$

### Step F.3: Compute $FIRST(T)$

**Production:** $T \to FT'$
Since $FIRST(F) = \{(, \text{id}\}$ and $\varepsilon \notin FIRST(F)$:
$$FIRST(FT') = FIRST(F) = \{(, \text{id}\}$$

$$\therefore FIRST(T) = \{(, \text{id}\}$$

### Step F.4: Compute $FIRST(E')$

**Production:** $E' \to +TE'$
$$FIRST(+TE') = \{+\}$$

**Production:** $E' \to \varepsilon$
$$\varepsilon \in FIRST(E')$$

$$\therefore FIRST(E') = \{+, \varepsilon\}$$

### Step F.5: Compute $FIRST(E)$

**Production:** $E \to TE'$
$$FIRST(TE') = FIRST(T) = \{(, \text{id}\}$$

$$\therefore FIRST(E) = \{(, \text{id}\}$$

**Consolidated FIRST Table:**

| Non-Terminal | FIRST Set |
|---|---|
| $E$ | $\{ (, \text{id} \}$ |
| $E'$ | $\{ +, \varepsilon \}$ |
| $T$ | $\{ (, \text{id} \}$ |
| $T'$ | $\{ *, \varepsilon \}$ |
| $F$ | $\{ (, \text{id} \}$ |

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of FOLLOW Sets

### Step L.1: Initialize

Apply **Rule L1**:
$$FOLLOW(E) = \{\$\}$$

### Step L.2: Process $E \to TE'$

$E'$ appears at the **rightmost position**, so by **Rule L3**:
$$FOLLOW(E') \supseteq FOLLOW(E) = \{\$\}$$

### Step L.3: Process $E' \to +TE'$

The second $E'$ is rightmost, so:
$$FOLLOW(E') \supseteq FOLLOW(E') = \{\$\} \text{ (no new addition)}$$

For $T$: it is followed by $E'$. So everything in $FIRST(E') \setminus \{\varepsilon\}$ goes to $FOLLOW(T)$:
$$FOLLOW(T) \supseteq FIRST(E') \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{+\}$$

Since $\varepsilon \in FIRST(E')$, also add $FOLLOW(E')$:
$$FOLLOW(T) \supseteq FOLLOW(E') = \{\$\}$$

### Step L.4: Process $T \to FT'$

$T'$ is rightmost:
$$FOLLOW(T') \supseteq FOLLOW(T) = \{+, \$\}$$

For $F$: followed by $T'$. Add $FIRST(T') \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{*\}$:
$$FOLLOW(F) \supseteq \{*\}$$

Since $\varepsilon \in FIRST(T')$, also add $FOLLOW(T')$:
$$FOLLOW(F) \supseteq FOLLOW(T') = \{+, \$\}$$

### Step L.5: Process $T' \to *FT'$

Second $T'$ rightmost:
$$FOLLOW(T') \supseteq FOLLOW(T') \text{ (idempotent)}$$

For $F$: followed by $T'$. Add $FIRST(T') \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{*\}$:
$$FOLLOW(F) \supseteq \{*\} \text{ (already present)}$$

### Step L.6: Process $F \to (E)$

$E$ is followed by $)$:
$$FOLLOW(E) \supseteq \{)\}$$

### Step L.7: Fixed-Point Check

Re-iterate until no new symbols appear.

**Final Consolidated FOLLOW Table:**

| Non-Terminal | FOLLOW Set |
|---|---|
| $E$ | $\{ ), \$\}$ |
| $E'$ | $\{ ), \$\}$ |
| $T$ | $\{ +, ), \$\}$ |
| $T'$ | $\{ +, ), \$\}$ |
| $F$ | $\{ *, +, ), \$\}$ |

## 3.4 Predictive Parsing Table Construction

Apply the table-filling rule for every production:

| Production | FIRST / FOLLOW Trigger | Table Entries |
|---|---|---|
| $E \to TE'$ | $FIRST(TE') = \{(, \text{id}\}$ | $M[E, (] = E \to TE'$, $M[E, \text{id}] = E \to TE'$ |
| $E' \to +TE'$ | $FIRST(+TE') = \{+\}$ | $M[E', +] = E' \to +TE'$ |
| $E' \to \varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon \in FIRST(\varepsilon), FOLLOW(E') = \{), \$\}$ | $M[E', )] = E' \to \varepsilon$, $M[E', \$] = E' \to \varepsilon$ |
| $T \to FT'$ | $FIRST(FT') = \{(, \text{id}\}$ | $M[T, (] = T \to FT'$, $M[T, \text{id}] = T \to FT'$ |
| $T' \to *FT'$ | $FIRST(*FT') = \{*\}$ | $M[T', *] = T' \to *FT'$ |
| $T' \to \varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon \in FIRST, FOLLOW(T') = \{+, ), \$\}$ | $M[T', +] = T' \to \varepsilon$, $M[T', )] = T' \to \varepsilon$, $M[T', \$] = T' \to \varepsilon$ |
| $F \to (E)$ | $FIRST((E)) = \{( \}$ | $M[F, (] = F \to (E)$ |
| $F \to \text{id}$ | $FIRST(\text{id}) = \{\text{id}\}$ | $M[F, \text{id}] = F \to \text{id}$ |

### Final Predictive Parsing Table $M$

| | id | + | * | ( | ) | \$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **E** | $E \to TE'$ | | | $E \to TE'$ | | |
| **E'** | | $E' \to +TE'$ | | | $E' \to \varepsilon$ | $E' \to \varepsilon$ |
| **T** | $T \to FT'$ | | | $T \to FT'$ | | |
| **T'** | | $T' \to \varepsilon$ | $T' \to *FT'$ | | $T' \to \varepsilon$ | $T' \to \varepsilon$ |
| **F** | $F \to \text{id}$ | | | $F \to (E)$ | | |

> [!NOTE]
> **LL(1) Validity Check:** Every cell in the table above contains **at most one production**. Therefore, the grammar is **LL(1)** and the predictive parser is valid.

## 3.5 Worked Parsing Trace: Input `id + id * id`

The parser uses a **stack** (initialized with `\$E`) and **input buffer** (terminated by `\$`).

| Step | Stack (top on right) | Input (left unread) | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | \$ E | id + id * id \$ | Pop E, lookahead id → Apply $E \to TE'$ |
| 2 | \$ E' T | id + id * id \$ | Pop T, lookahead id → Apply $T \to FT'$ |
| 3 | \$ E' T' F | id + id * id \$ | Pop F, lookahead id → Apply $F \to \text{id}$ |
| 4 | \$ E' T' id | id + id * id \$ | Match id, advance input |
| 5 | \$ E' T' | + id * id \$ | Pop T', lookahead + → Apply $T' \to \varepsilon$ |
| 6 | \$ E' | + id * id \$ | Pop E', lookahead + → Apply $E' \to +TE'$ |
| 7 | \$ E' T + | + id * id \$ | Match +, advance input |
| 8 | \$ E' T | id * id \$ | Pop T, lookahead id → Apply $T \to FT'$ |
| 9 | \$ E' T' F | id * id \$ | Pop F, lookahead id → Apply $F \to \text{id}$ |
| 10 | \$ E' T' id | id * id \$ | Match id |
| 11 | \$ E' T' | * id \$ | Pop T', lookahead * → Apply $T' \to *FT'$ |
| 12 | \$ E' T' F * | * id \$ | Match * |
| 13 | \$ E' T' F | id \$ | Pop F, lookahead id → Apply $F \to \text{id}$ |
| 14 | \$ E' T' id | id \$ | Match id |
| 15 | \$ E' T' | \$ | Pop T', lookahead \$ → Apply $T' \to \varepsilon$ |
| 16 | \$ E' | \$ | Pop E', lookahead \$ → Apply $E' \to \varepsilon$ |
| 17 | \$ | \$ | **ACCEPT** ✓ |

**Verdict:** The input `id + id * id` is successfully parsed in **17 moves** with the stack finally containing only `\$`, matching the input sentinel.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Predictive Parser Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **runtime architecture** of a table-driven LL(1) predictive parser, showing the interaction between the input buffer, stack, parsing table, and output stream.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IB[INPUT BUFFER]
        I1[a1] --> I2[a2] --> I3[a3] --> I4[ai] --> I5["$"]
    end
    
    subgraph STK[PARSER STACK]
        S1[top symbol X] --> S2[...]
    end
    
    subgraph CTRL[DRIVING ROUTINE - Parsing Algorithm]
        C1{Is X a terminal or $?}
        C2{C2 Compare X with lookahead}
        C3{C3 Look up M X a}
        C4{C4 Error handler}
    end
    
    subgraph PTBL[PREDICTIVE PARSING TABLE M]
        T1[Row indexed by X]
        T2[Column indexed by a]
        T3[Cell M X a gives production X to alpha]
    end
    
    subgraph OUT[OUTPUT - Parse Tree]
        O1[Construction of leftmost derivation]
    end
    
    I1 -.read.-> CTRL
    STK -.pop top X.-> CTRL
    CTRL -- query --> PTBL
    PTBL -- production body --> STK
    CTRL -- emit --> OUT
```

## 4.2 Predictive Parsing Table Construction Algorithm (Flowchart)

This flowchart depicts the **offline construction phase** of the parsing table from the grammar productions and pre-computed FIRST/FOLLOW sets.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Grammar G] --> B[Compute FIRST for all non-terminals]
    B --> C[Compute FOLLOW for all non-terminals]
    C --> D{For each production A to alpha in P}
    D -- iterate --> E{For each terminal a in FIRST alpha}
    E -- yes --> F[Set M A a = A to alpha]
    F --> G{Is epsilon in FIRST alpha?}
    G -- no --> D
    G -- yes --> H{For each terminal b in FOLLOW A}
    H -- iterate --> I[Set M A b = A to alpha]
    I --> J{Is dollar in FOLLOW A?}
    J -- yes --> K[Set M A dollar = A to alpha]
    J -- no --> H
    H -- done --> D
    D -- all processed --> L{Check M for multiple entries}
    L -- conflict --> M[Grammar is NOT LL1: REJECT]
    L -- no conflict --> N[Output Parsing Table M: SUCCESS]
```

## 4.3 Stack Operation Visualization (For Trace in Section 3.5)

This Mermaid **sequence diagram** traces the first five moves of parsing `id + id * id` to demonstrate the **push/pop dynamics** between the stack and input buffer.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Stack
    participant Input as Input Buffer
    participant Table as M[A,a]
    
    Note over Stack,Input: INITIAL STATE
    Stack->>Stack: Push $, E (bottom to top)
    Input->>Input: Buffer: id + id * id $
    
    Note over Stack,Table: MOVE 1
    Stack->>Table: Query M[E, id]
    Table-->>Stack: E -> TE'
    Stack->>Stack: Pop E, Push E', T
    
    Note over Stack,Table: MOVE 2
    Stack->>Table: Query M[T, id]
    Table-->>Stack: T -> FT'
    Stack->>Stack: Pop T, Push T', F
    
    Note over Stack,Table: MOVE 3
    Stack->>Table: Query M[F, id]
    Table-->>Stack: F -> id
    Stack->>Stack: Pop F, Push id
    
    Note over Stack,Table: MOVE 4
    Stack->>Input: Match id (pop both)
    Input->>Input: Advance pointer
```

## 4.4 LL(1) Grammar Decision Tree — Validity Check

This block diagram maps the **logical flow** for verifying whether a given grammar admits an LL(1) predictive parser.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Given Grammar G] --> P1{Left recursion?}
    P1 -- yes --> P2[Eliminate left recursion]
    P1 -- no --> P3{Left factoring needed?}
    P2 --> P3
    P3 -- yes --> P4[Apply left factoring]
    P3 -- no --> P5[Compute FIRST and FOLLOW]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6{Any multiply defined entry in M?}
    P6 -- yes --> P7[NOT LL1 - Reject for predictive parsing]
    P6 -- no --> P8[Grammar is LL1 - Construct Predictive Parser]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Q: Define a predictive parser. State the conditions under which a grammar is LL(1).**

**Model Answer:**

A **predictive parser** is a recursive-descent parser that uses a **predictive parsing table** $M[A, a]$ to select the correct production rule to apply, requiring only **one lookahead symbol** of input. It performs top-down parsing without backtracking and constructs a leftmost derivation.

A grammar is **LL(1)** if and only if the following conditions hold:
1. For every non-terminal $A$ with two distinct productions $A \to \alpha$ and $A \to \beta$:
   $$FIRST(\alpha) \cap FIRST(\beta) = \emptyset$$
2. If $\varepsilon \in FIRST(\beta)$, then:
   $$FIRST(\alpha) \cap FOLLOW(A) = \emptyset$$
3. The resulting parsing table $M$ contains **at most one production per cell**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3-Mark Board Tip:** Always state the **three conditions** explicitly with set notation. Examiners award the 3rd mark only for the conflict-free table condition.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Q: What is the significance of the FOLLOW set in the construction of a predictive parsing table? How is it computed when a non-terminal is followed by a nullable symbol?**

**Model Answer:**

The **FOLLOW set** serves as the **lookahead oracle** for production rules whose right-hand side is **nullable** (i.e., $\varepsilon \in FIRST(\alpha)$). When $A \to \alpha$ has $\varepsilon \in FIRST(\alpha)$, the parser must consult $FOLLOW(A)$ to determine the valid lookahead terminals that permit the application of $A \to \alpha$.

**Computation rule when followed by nullable symbol:**

If $A \to \alpha B \beta$ is a production and $\beta \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon$, then:
$$FOLLOW(B) \supseteq FOLLOW(A)$$

This propagates the end-of-input context of $A$ to $B$, ensuring correct parsing when the body following $B$ can vanish.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Choice A (14 Marks Total)

### Question A — Part (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Consider the following grammar:**
$$S \to aBDh$$
$$B \to bB \mid c$$
$$D \to EF$$
$$E \to \varepsilon \mid d$$
$$F \to \varepsilon \mid e$$
**Compute FIRST and FOLLOW for all non-terminals. Is the grammar LL(1)? Justify.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute FIRST Sets**

$$FIRST(B) = \{b, c\}$$

$$FIRST(E) = \{\varepsilon, d\}$$

$$FIRST(F) = \{\varepsilon, e\}$$

For $D \to EF$: $FIRST(D) = FIRST(E) \cup (FIRST(F) \text{ if } \varepsilon \in FIRST(E))$
$$FIRST(D) = \{\varepsilon, d, e\}$$

For $S \to aBDh$: $FIRST(S) = \{a\}$

| Non-Terminal | FIRST |
|---|---|
| $S$ | $\{a\}$ |
| $B$ | $\{b, c\}$ |
| $D$ | $\{\varepsilon, d, e\}$ |
| $E$ | $\{\varepsilon, d\}$ |
| $F$ | $\{\varepsilon, e\}$ |

**Step 2: Compute FOLLOW Sets**

- $FOLLOW(S) = \{\$\}$ (start symbol)
- $S \to aBDh$: $h$ is a terminal, so for $D$: $FIRST(h) = \{h\}$ enters $FOLLOW(D)$.
- For $B$: $FIRST(D) = \{\varepsilon, d, e\}$. Add $\{d, e\}$ to $FOLLOW(B)$. Since $\varepsilon \in FIRST(D)$, add $FOLLOW(S) = \{\$\}$ to $FOLLOW(B)$.
- $D \to EF$: For $E$, add $FIRST(F) \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{e\}$ to $FOLLOW(E)$. Since $\varepsilon \in FIRST(F)$, add $FOLLOW(D) = \{h, \$\}$ to $FOLLOW(E)$.
- For $F$, add $FOLLOW(D) = \{h, \$\}$ (since $F$ is rightmost).

| Non-Terminal | FOLLOW |
|---|---|
| $S$ | $\{\$\}$ |
| $B$ | $\{d, e, \$\}$ |
| $D$ | $\{h, \$\}$ |
| $E$ | $\{e, h, \$\}$ |
| $F$ | $\{h, \$\}$ |

**Step 3: LL(1) Validity Check**

For $E \to \varepsilon \mid d$: $FIRST(d) \cap FOLLOW(E) = \{d\} \cap \{e, h, \$\} = \emptyset$ ✓

For $F \to \varepsilon \mid e$: $FIRST(e) \cap FOLLOW(F) = \{e\} \cap \{h, \$\} = \emptyset$ ✓

**Verdict:** The grammar **IS LL(1)** since there are no intersections between the FIRST sets of alternatives and between the FIRST set of any alternative and the FOLLOW set of the LHS non-terminal (when the alternative is nullable).

---

### Question A — Part (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Q: Construct the predictive parsing table for the grammar in part (a) and trace the moves of the parser for the input string `abbcdeh$`.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Predictive Parsing Table:**

| | a | b | c | d | e | h | \$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S** | $S \to aBDh$ | | | | | | |
| **B** | | $B \to bB$ | $B \to c$ | | | | |
| **D** | | | | $D \to EF$ | $D \to EF$ | $D \to \varepsilon$ | $D \to \varepsilon$ |
| **E** | | | | $E \to d$ | $E \to \varepsilon$ | $E \to \varepsilon$ | $E \to \varepsilon$ |
| **F** | | | | | $F \to e$ | $F \to \varepsilon$ | $F \to \varepsilon$ |

**Parsing Trace for `abbcdeh$`:**

| Move | Stack | Input | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | \$ S | abbcdeh$ | $M[S,a] = S \to aBDh$ |
| 2 | \$ h D B a | abbcdeh$ | Match a |
| 3 | \$ h D B | bbcdeh$ | $M[B,b] = B \to bB$ |
| 4 | \$ h D B b | bbcdeh$ | Match b |
| 5 | \$ h D B | bcdeh$ | $M[B,b] = B \to bB$ |
| 6 | \$ h D B b | bcdeh$ | Match b |
| 7 | \$ h D B | cdeh$ | $M[B,c] = B \to c$ |
| 8 | \$ h D c | cdeh$ | Match c |
| 9 | \$ h D | deh$ | $M[D,d] = D \to EF$ |
| 10 | \$ h F E | deh$ | $M[E,d] = E \to d$ |
| 11 | \$ h F d | deh$ | Match d |
| 12 | \$ h F | eh$ | $M[F,e] = F \to e$ |
| 13 | \$ h e | eh$ | Match e |
| 14 | \$ h | h$ | Match h |
| 15 | \$ | \$ | **ACCEPT** ✓ |

## 5.3 Part B Question — Choice B (14 Marks Total)

### Question B — Part (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Explain the construction of the predictive parsing table. What happens when a grammar has left recursion? How is it eliminated?**

**Model Answer:**

**Predictive Parsing Table Construction Algorithm:**

```
For each production A → α:
   For each terminal 'a' in FIRST(α):
       M[A, a] = A → α
   If ε ∈ FIRST(α):
       For each terminal 'b' in FOLLOW(A):
           M[A, b] = A → α
       If $ ∈ FOLLOW(A):
           M[A, $] = A → α
```

**Problem with Left Recursion:**

Consider $A \to A\alpha \mid \beta$. Since $FIRST(\beta) \subseteq FIRST(A\alpha) = FIRST(A)$, the table cell $M[A, a]$ would be **multiply defined** for $a \in FIRST(\beta)$. This **destroys LL(1) validity**.

**Elimination of Immediate Left Recursion:**

Replace $A \to A\alpha \mid \beta$ with:
$$A \to \beta A'$$
$$A' \to \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$$

**General Case for Multiple Alternatives:**
$$A \to A\alpha_1 \mid A\alpha_2 \mid \ldots \mid A\alpha_m \mid \beta_1 \mid \beta_2 \mid \ldots \mid \beta_n$$

becomes:
$$A \to \beta_1 A' \mid \beta_2 A' \mid \ldots \mid \beta_n A'$$
$$A' \to \alpha_1 A' \mid \alpha_2 A' \mid \ldots \mid \alpha_m A' \mid \varepsilon$$

**Indirect Left Recursion** ($A \to B\alpha$, $B \to A\beta$) requires **substitution** followed by immediate left recursion elimination.

---

### Question B — Part (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Q: For the grammar below, eliminate left recursion and construct the predictive parsing table:**
$$E \to E + T \mid T$$
$$T \to T * F \mid F$$
$$F \to (E) \mid \text{id}$$

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Eliminate Immediate Left Recursion in $E$**

$$E \to T E'$$
$$E' \to + T E' \mid \varepsilon$$

**Step 2: Eliminate Immediate Left Recursion in $T$**

$$T \to F T'$$
$$T' \to * F T' \mid \varepsilon$$

**Step 3: $F$ has no left recursion, remains unchanged**

$$F \to (E) \mid \text{id}$$

**Step 4: Compute FIRST Sets**

$$FIRST(F) = \{(, \text{id}\}$$
$$FIRST(T') = \{*, \varepsilon\}$$
$$FIRST(T) = \{(, \text{id}\}$$
$$FIRST(E') = \{+, \varepsilon\}$$
$$FIRST(E) = \{(, \text{id}\}$$

**Step 5: Compute FOLLOW Sets**

$$FOLLOW(E) = \{), \$\}$$
$$FOLLOW(E') = \{), \$\}$$
$$FOLLOW(T) = \{+, ), \$\}$$
$$FOLLOW(T') = \{+, ), \$\}$$
$$FOLLOW(F) = \{*, +, ), \$\}$$

**Step 6: Predictive Parsing Table**

| | id | + | * | ( | ) | \$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **E** | $E \to TE'$ | | | $E \to TE'$ | | |
| **E'** | | $E' \to +TE'$ | | | $E' \to \varepsilon$ | $E' \to \varepsilon$ |
| **T** | $T \to FT'$ | | | $T \to FT'$ | | |
| **T'** | | $T' \to \varepsilon$ | $T' \to *FT'$ | | $T' \to \varepsilon$ | $T' \to \varepsilon$ |
| **F** | $F \to \text{id}$ | | | $F \to (E)$ | | |

**LL(1) Validity:** Every cell is **uniquely defined** — the grammar is **LL(1)** after left recursion elimination.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **FIRST of a non-terminal's body is NOT the same as FIRST of the non-terminal alone** when the body has multiple symbols. Many students erroneously copy $FIRST(A)$ for $FIRST(A\alpha)$.
> 2. **Forgetting to add \$ to FOLLOW of the start symbol** costs 1 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Missing the "epsilon in FIRST" case** when filling the table — the most common reason for grammars being wrongly classified as non-LL(1).
> 4. **Not propagating FOLLOW through nullable chains** — always re-iterate the FOLLOW rules until a fixed point is reached.
> 5. **Stack trace errors** — students often forget that the stack grows rightward (top is the rightmost element) and the matched input advances the buffer pointer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Predictive Parsing = Top-Down + 1 Lookahead + No Backtracking + Table-Driven LL(1)**
- **The Predictive Parsing Table $M$** is indexed by **non-terminals (rows)** and **terminals + \$ (columns)**
- **FIRST** contains terminals (and $\varepsilon$) that **begin** derivable strings from a symbol
- **FOLLOW** contains terminals (and \$) that can **immediately follow** a non-terminal in any sentential form
- **Rule for Table Entry:** $M[A, a] = A \to \alpha$ if $a \in FIRST(\alpha)$; if $\varepsilon \in FIRST(\alpha)$, then $a \in FOLLOW(A)$
- **LL(1) Validity Test:** No cell of $M$ should have multiple productions (singleton rule)
- **Left Recursion is FATAL** to predictive parsing — must be eliminated via the standard $A \to A\alpha \mid \beta \implies A \to \beta A', A' \to \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$ transformation
- **Left Factoring** is needed when two productions for the same non-terminal share a common prefix
- **Time Complexity** is $O(n)$ — linear in input length
- **Parser Components:** Input Buffer + Stack + Parsing Table + Driving Routine
- **\$ symbol** must always be added to $FOLLOW(S)$ where $S$ is the start symbol
- **Stack Initialization:** Push `\$` first, then the start symbol $S$ (so $S$ is on top)
- **Acceptance Condition:** Stack contains only `\$` AND input pointer is at `\$`
- **Error Condition:** Either stack top is a terminal that doesn't match lookahead, OR $M[A, a]$ is empty (no entry)
- **Standard Reference Grammar** for KTU exams typically involves arithmetic expressions with $+, *, (, ), \text{id}$ and requires both FIRST/FOLLOW computation AND parsing table construction in a 14-mark question
- **Nullable non-terminals** ($\varepsilon$-deriving) require special treatment: their FIRST contains $\varepsilon$, and they propagate FOLLOW back through their position in production bodies

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LL(1) Grammars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT751 - COMPETER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** LL(1) Grammars

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of LL(1) Grammar

A context-free grammar **G** is said to be an **LL(1) grammar** if and only if its associated **LL(1) parsing table** has no multiply-defined (i.e., conflicting) entries. In other words, a grammar is LL(1) if and only if whenever a non-terminal **A** has two distinct productions **A → α** and **A → β**, the following condition holds:

$$\text{FIRST}(\alpha) \cap \text{FOLLOW}(A) = \emptyset$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LL(1) Decoded**:
> - **First L**: Scan the input string from **L**eft to right.
> - **Second L**: Construct the **L**eftmost derivation of the input.
> - **(1)**: Use exactly **1** (one) symbol of lookahead to decide which production to apply.

This is the most widely used form of **top-down deterministic parsing** in compiler design, and the **LMD (Leftmost Derivation)** trees produced by an LL(1) parser are unique for any given string in the language.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping**:
> This topic maps directly to **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Apply the concepts of lexical analysis and parsing to design a parser for a given grammar."* The cognitive level tested is **Apply (Level 3)** and **Analyze (Level 4)** as per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Reading a Treasure Map

Imagine you are exploring a **maze** (the derivation tree) using a **treasure map** (the grammar). Here is the analogy:

- **L (Left to Right)**: You walk through the maze strictly from the **entrance on the left to the exit on the right**, never backtracking the path you have already explored.
- **L (Leftmost Derivation)**: At every fork, you must always **visit the leftmost unexplored branch first**. You never skip a left branch in favour of a right one.
- **1 (One Lookahead)**: Before choosing a path at any fork, you are allowed to **peek exactly one step ahead**. You cannot see two or more steps. That one peeked symbol is the **lookahead symbol**.

If, with only that single peek, you can **uniquely determine** which path (production) to take at every single fork without ever getting confused, then your map (grammar) qualifies as an **LL(1) map**. The moment you need 2 or more symbols to decide, or two maps give conflicting instructions for the same peek, the grammar fails the LL(1) test.

## 1.3 Why LL(1) Grammars Matter in Compilers

LL(1) grammars are foundational because they:

1. **Enable Recursive Descent Parsing** — One of the simplest parser implementations, where each non-terminal becomes a procedure.
2. **Guarantee Linear Time Parsing** — $O(n)$ parsing time where $n$ is the length of the input token stream.
3. **Allow Predictive Parsing** — The parser never needs to backtrack, because the lookahead is sufficient to predict the production.
4. **Are Used in Real Languages** — The reference grammars of languages like **Java**, **C**, and **Pascal** are often designed (or transformed) into LL(1) form.

> [!TIP]
> Many natural-looking grammars are **NOT** LL(1) due to common-prefix or left-recursion issues. The KTU syllabus expects students to **identify** the conflicts and **transform** them into LL(1)-equivalent grammars using techniques like **left factoring** and **left recursion elimination**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pre-Requisite: FIRST and FOLLOW Sets

To understand LL(1) testing, we must first master the **FIRST** and **FOLLOW** helper sets.

### 2.1.1 FIRST Set

The set **FIRST(α)** is the set of all terminal symbols that can begin any string derivable from the symbol string **α**. If **α** can derive **ε** (epsilon), then **ε** is also included.

**Rules to Compute FIRST(X):**

- **Rule 1**: If $X$ is a terminal, then $\text{FIRST}(X) = \{X\}$.
- **Rule 2**: If $X \to \varepsilon$ is a production, then add $\varepsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.
- **Rule 3**: If $X \to Y_1 Y_2 \dots Y_k$ is a production, then add everything from $\text{FIRST}(Y_i)$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$ as long as $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(Y_{i-1})$ for $i \geq 2$.

### 2.1.2 FOLLOW Set

The set **FOLLOW(A)** is the set of all terminal symbols that can appear **immediately to the right** of the non-terminal **A** in some sentential form. If **A** can be the rightmost symbol of a sentential form, then **\$** (the end-of-input marker) is in FOLLOW(A).

**Rules to Compute FOLLOW(A):**

- **Rule 1**: Place **\$** in $\text{FOLLOW}(S)$ where $S$ is the start symbol.
- **Rule 2**: If there is a production $A \to \alpha B \beta$, then everything in $\text{FIRST}(\beta)$ (except $\varepsilon$) is placed in $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.
- **Rule 3**: If there is a production $A \to \alpha B$ OR $A \to \alpha B \beta$ where $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$, then everything in $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ is in $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.

## 2.2 Conditions for LL(1) Grammar

A grammar is LL(1) if and only if for every non-terminal **A** with productions $A \to \alpha_1 \mid \alpha_2 \mid \dots \mid \alpha_n$, the following **three conditions** are simultaneously satisfied:

1. **No Common Prefix Conflict**: $\text{FIRST}(\alpha_i) \cap \text{FIRST}(\alpha_j) = \emptyset$ for all $i \neq j$.
2. **No Left Factoring Issue**: At most one of the $\alpha_i$ can derive $\varepsilon$.
3. **No FOLLOW-FIRST Overlap**: If $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha_i)$, then $\text{FIRST}(\alpha_j) \cap \text{FOLLOW}(A) = \emptyset$ for all $j \neq i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master Test (Equivalent to above)**:
> For every non-terminal $A$ with two distinct productions $A \to \alpha$ and $A \to \beta$:
> $$\text{FIRST}(\alpha) \cdot \text{FOLLOW}(A) \;\cap\; \text{FIRST}(\beta) \cdot \text{FOLLOW}(A) \;=\; \emptyset$$
> where the dotted operator means "if $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ then also include $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$."

## 2.3 Construction of the LL(1) Parsing Table

The **LL(1) Parsing Table** is a 2D table $M[A, a]$ where rows are non-terminals and columns are terminals (including **\$**). Each cell may contain at most one production.

**Algorithm (Fill M[A, a]):**

For every production $A \to \alpha$ of the grammar:
- For each terminal $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, add $A \to \alpha$ to $M[A, a]$.
- If $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, then for each terminal $b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$, add $A \to \alpha$ to $M[A, b]$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Conflict Alert**: If any cell of $M$ ends up with **more than one production**, the grammar is **NOT LL(1)**. The grammar must be modified.

## 2.4 LL(1) Parsing Algorithm (Table-Driven)

The parser uses an **input buffer**, a **stack**, and the **parsing table** $M$.

```
Initialize:  stack = [S, $]   (S = start symbol on top)
            input buffer = w $
while top of stack ≠ $:
    X = top of stack
    a = current input symbol
    if X == a == $:
        ACCEPT (parsing successful)
    elif X is a terminal:
        if X == a:
            pop X, advance input
        else:
            ERROR
    else:   # X is a non-terminal
        if M[X, a] = X → Y₁Y₂…Yₖ:
            pop X
            push Yₖ, …, Y₂, Y₁    (so Y₁ is on top)
        else:
            ERROR
```

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **FIRST of Terminal** | $\text{FIRST}(a) = \{a\}$ | Direct lookup in lexer |
| 2 | **FIRST of Production** | $\text{FIRST}(\alpha) = \bigcup \text{FIRST}(X_i)$ while $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(X_{i})$ | Top-down parser predictor |
| 3 | **FOLLOW Start** | $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(S)$ | End-of-input sentinel |
| 4 | **FOLLOW Propagate** | $A \to \alpha B \beta \Rightarrow \text{FIRST}(\beta) \setminus \{\varepsilon\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$ | Builds synchronisation sets for error recovery |
| 5 | **FOLLOW Cascade** | If $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$, then $\text{FOLLOW}(A) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$ | Handles epsilon-productions |
| 6 | **LL(1) Test** | $\text{FIRST}(\alpha) \cap \text{FIRST}(\beta) = \emptyset$ for $A \to \alpha \mid \beta$ | Decides grammar validity |
| 7 | **LL(1) Test (with ε)** | If $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, then $\text{FIRST}(\beta) \cap \text{FOLLOW}(A) = \emptyset$ | Decides for nullable rules |
| 8 | **Parser Complexity** | $O(n)$ where $n = \vert w \vert$ | Linear time — production-grade |
| 9 | **LL(k) Strictness** | $\text{LL}(1) \subset \text{LL}(2) \subset \dots \subset \text{LL}(k)$ | Higher $k$ = more expressive, larger table |
| 10 | **Conflict Types** | FIRST/FIRST, FIRST/FOLLOW | Identify non-LL(1) grammars |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical KTU Example: The "Arithmetic Expression" Grammar

Consider the following classical grammar (commonly tested in KTU boards):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\to T E' \\
E' &\to +T E' \mid \varepsilon \\
T &\to F T' \\
T' &\to *F T' \mid \varepsilon \\
F &\to (E) \mid \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 0 — Identify Symbols**:
- Non-terminals: $\{E, E', T, T', F\}$
- Terminals: $\{+, *, (, ), \text{id}, \$\}$

### 3.1.1 Computing FIRST Sets

We compute FIRST iteratively until no change occurs.

**Apply Rule 1** (terminals): $\text{FIRST}(+) = \{+\}$, $\text{FIRST}(*) = \{*\}$, $\text{FIRST}(() = \{\}$, $\text{FIRST}()) = \{\}$, $\text{FIRST}(\text{id}) = \{\text{id}\}$.

**Compute FIRST(F)**:
$F \to (E) \mid \text{id}$.
$$\text{FIRST}(F) = \text{FIRST}(() \cup \text{FIRST}(\text{id}) = \{(\} \cup \{\text{id}\} = \{(\text{, id}\}$$

**Compute FIRST(T)**:
$T \to F T'$. Since $F$ cannot derive $\varepsilon$:
$$\text{FIRST}(T) = \text{FIRST}(F) = \{(\text{, id}\}$$

**Compute FIRST(T')**:
$T' \to *F T' \mid \varepsilon$. So:
$$\text{FIRST}(T') = \{*, \varepsilon\}$$

**Compute FIRST(E)**:
$E \to T E'$. Since $T$ cannot derive $\varepsilon$:
$$\text{FIRST}(E) = \text{FIRST}(T) = \{(\text{, id}\}$$

**Compute FIRST(E')**:
$E' \to +T E' \mid \varepsilon$. So:
$$\text{FIRST}(E') = \{+, \varepsilon\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Summary of FIRST sets**:
> - $\text{FIRST}(E) = \{(, \text{id}\}$
> - $\text{FIRST}(E') = \{+, \varepsilon\}$
> - $\text{FIRST}(T) = \{(, \text{id}\}$
> - $\text{FIRST}(T') = \{*, \varepsilon\}$
> - $\text{FIRST}(F) = \{(, \text{id}\}$

### 3.1.2 Computing FOLLOW Sets

**Apply Rule 1**: $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(E)$ since $E$ is the start symbol.

**Compute FOLLOW(E)**:
From $F \to (E)$, the symbol $E$ is followed by $)$:
$$\$) \in \text{FOLLOW}(E)$$
Therefore: $\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{), \$\}$

**Compute FOLLOW(E')**:
- $E$ is the start, so $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(E)$, and $E' \to \varepsilon$ means we propagate to $E'$? Let's see:
- From $E \to T E'$, $E'$ is followed by whatever follows $E$, so $\text{FOLLOW}(E) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(E')$.
- From $E' \to +T E'$, $E'$ is followed by whatever follows $E'$, which is recursive, plus first of nothing — propagate $\text{FOLLOW}(E')$ to itself.

Thus:
$$\text{FOLLOW}(E') = \text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{), \$\}$$

**Compute FOLLOW(T)**:
- From $E \to T E'$, $T$ is followed by $\text{FIRST}(E') \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{+\}$. So $+\in \text{FOLLOW}(T)$.
- Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(E')$, propagate $\text{FOLLOW}(E)$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(T)$: $\$, ) \in \text{FOLLOW}(T)$.

Therefore: $\text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{+, ), \$\}$.

**Compute FOLLOW(T')**:
- From $T \to F T'$, $T'$ is followed by $\text{FIRST}(E') \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{+\}$. So $+\in \text{FOLLOW}(T')$.
- Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(E')$, propagate $\text{FOLLOW}(T)$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(T')$: $\$, ) \in \text{FOLLOW}(T')$.

Therefore: $\text{FOLLOW}(T') = \{+, ), \$\}$.

**Compute FOLLOW(F)**:
- From $T \to F T'$, $F$ is followed by $\text{FIRST}(T') \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{*\}$. So $* \in \text{FOLLOW}(F)$.
- Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(T')$, propagate $\text{FOLLOW}(T)$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(F)$: $+, ), \$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(F)$.

Therefore: $\text{FOLLOW}(F) = \{*, +, ), \$\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Summary of FOLLOW sets**:
> - $\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{), \$\}$
> - $\text{FOLLOW}(E') = \{), \$\}$
> - $\text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{+, ), \$\}$
> - $\text{FOLLOW}(T') = \{+, ), \$\}$
> - $\text{FOLLOW}(F) = \{*, +, ), \$\}$

### 3.1.3 Verifying LL(1) Property

We check each non-terminal with multiple productions:

**For E'**: $E' \to +T E' \mid \varepsilon$.
- $\text{FIRST}(+T E') = \{+\}$.
- $\varepsilon$ is the nullable alternative.
- Is $\text{FIRST}(+T E') \cap \text{FOLLOW}(E') = \{+\} \cap \{), \$\} = \emptyset$? ✅ **Yes.**

**For T'**: $T' \to *F T' \mid \varepsilon$.
- $\text{FIRST}(*F T') = \{*\}$.
- Is $\{*\} \cap \text{FOLLOW}(T') = \{*\} \cap \{+, ), \$\} = \emptyset$? ✅ **Yes.**

**For F**: $F \to (E) \mid \text{id}$.
- $\text{FIRST}((E)) = \{(\}$.
- $\text{FIRST}(\text{id}) = \{\text{id}\}$.
- Is $\{( \} \cap \{\text{id}\} = \emptyset$? ✅ **Yes.**

**Conclusion**: The grammar **IS LL(1)**. No multiply-defined entries will appear in the parsing table.

### 3.1.4 Constructing the LL(1) Parsing Table

Apply the algorithm to each production:

| Production | FIRST / FOLLOW condition | Cells Filled |
|---|---|---|
| $E \to T E'$ | $\text{FIRST}(T E') = \{(, \text{id}\}$ | $M[E, (]$, $M[E, \text{id}]$ |
| $E' \to +T E'$ | $\text{FIRST}(+T E') = \{+\}$ | $M[E', +]$ |
| $E' \to \varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}$; $\text{FOLLOW}(E') = \{), \$\}$ | $M[E', )]$, $M[E', \$]$ |
| $T \to F T'$ | $\text{FIRST}(F T') = \{(, \text{id}\}$ | $M[T, (]$, $M[T, \text{id}]$ |
| $T' \to *F T'$ | $\text{FIRST}(*F T') = \{*\}$ | $M[T', *]$ |
| $T' \to \varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}$; $\text{FOLLOW}(T') = \{+, ), \$\}$ | $M[T', +]$, $M[T', )]$, $M[T', \$]$ |
| $F \to (E)$ | $\text{FIRST}((E)) = \{(\}$ | $M[F, (]$ |
| $F \to \text{id}$ | $\text{FIRST}(\text{id}) = \{\text{id}\}$ | $M[F, \text{id}]$ |

**Final LL(1) Parsing Table** (5 rows × 6 columns):

| Non-Terminal | id | + | * | ( | ) | \$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **E** | $E \to TE'$ | | | $E \to TE'$ | | |
| **E'** | | $E' \to +TE'$ | | | $E' \to \varepsilon$ | $E' \to \varepsilon$ |
| **T** | $T \to FT'$ | | | $T \to FT'$ | | |
| **T'** | | $T' \to \varepsilon$ | $T' \to *FT'$ | | $T' \to \varepsilon$ | $T' \to \varepsilon$ |
| **F** | $F \to \text{id}$ | | | $F \to (E)$ | | |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every cell has AT MOST one production** — the grammar is verified to be **LL(1)**. Empty cells are error entries.

### 3.1.5 LL(1) Parsing Trace on `id + id * id $`

Let us verify the table by parsing the input string $w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ followed by **\$**.

| Step | Stack (top→bottom shown) | Input | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | \$ E | id + id * id \$ | $M[E, \text{id}] = E \to TE'$ |
| 1 | \$ E' T | id + id * id \$ | $M[T, \text{id}] = T \to FT'$ |
| 2 | \$ E' T' F | id + id * id \$ | $M[F, \text{id}] = F \to \text{id}$ |
| 3 | \$ E' T' id | id + id * id \$ | Match `id`, pop & advance |
| 4 | \$ E' T' | + id * id \$ | $M[T', +] = T' \to \varepsilon$ |
| 5 | \$ E' | + id * id \$ | $M[E', +] = E' \to +TE'$ |
| 6 | \$ E' T + | + id * id \$ | Match `+`, pop & advance |
| 7 | \$ E' T | id * id \$ | $M[T, \text{id}] = T \to FT'$ |
| 8 | \$ E' T' F | id * id \$ | $M[F, \text{id}] = F \to \text{id}$ |
| 9 | \$ E' T' id | id * id \$ | Match `id`, pop & advance |
| 10 | \$ E' T' | * id \$ | $M[T', *] = T' \to *FT'$ |
| 11 | \$ E' T' F * | * id \$ | Match `*`, pop & advance |
| 12 | \$ E' T' F | id \$ | $M[F, \text{id}] = F \to \text{id}$ |
| 13 | \$ E' T' id | id \$ | Match `id`, pop & advance |
| 14 | \$ E' T' | \$ | $M[T', \$] = T' \to \varepsilon$ |
| 15 | \$ E' | \$ | $M[E', \$] = E' \to \varepsilon$ |
| 16 | \$ | \$ | **ACCEPT** |

> [!TIP]
> The parser makes a unique decision at every step using only **one lookahead symbol**. The total steps are $O(n)$ where $n = 9$ tokens — confirming linear-time behaviour.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: When a Grammar is NOT LL(1)

| Conflict Type | Description | Production Shape | Fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FIRST/FIRST** | Two productions of the same non-terminal share a common terminal in their FIRST sets | $A \to a\alpha \mid a\beta$ | **Left Factoring**: rewrite as $A \to aA'$, $A' \to \alpha \mid \beta$ |
| **FIRST/FOLLOW** | One production's FIRST set overlaps with the FOLLOW of the non-terminal, when the alternative is nullable | $A \to \alpha \mid \varepsilon$ where $\text{FIRST}(\alpha) \cap \text{FOLLOW}(A) \neq \emptyset$ | Refactor grammar to remove ambiguity; often requires deeper restructuring |
| **Left Recursion** | $A \to A\alpha$ causes infinite descent in top-down parsing | $A \to A\alpha \mid \beta$ | **Left Recursion Elimination**: rewrite as $A \to \beta A'$, $A' \to \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$ |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently test **left factoring** and **left recursion elimination** as follow-up questions to the LL(1) test. Master both transformations before the exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an LL(1) Parser

The following block diagram illustrates the standard architecture of a **table-driven LL(1) parser**:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InputBuf[Input Buffer\nHolds the token stream w$] --> ParserDriver
    Stack[Parsing Stack\nHolds grammar symbols\nBottom: $ marker] --> ParserDriver
    ParserDriver[Parser Driver\nMain control loop] --> TableLookup{Table Lookup\nM top, lookahead}
    TableLookup -->|Entry exists| ApplyProd[Apply Production\nPop X\nPush RHS reversed]
    TableLookup -->|Empty cell| Error[Error Handler\nPanic-mode recovery\nusing FOLLOW synchronisation]
    ApplyProd --> ParserDriver
    Error --> ParserDriver
    ParsingTable[LL Parsing Table M\nRows: Non-Terminals\nColumns: Terminals + $\nCells: At most 1 production] -.-> TableLookup
    Output[Output: Leftmost Derivation\nor Syntax Tree] --> ParserDriver
```

## 4.2 Flowchart: LL(1) Grammar Testing Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Given Grammar G]) --> Step1[Step 1: Eliminate Left Recursion\nfrom all non-terminals]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Apply Left Factoring\nto remove common prefixes]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Compute FIRST sets\nfor all non-terminals]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4: Compute FOLLOW sets\nfor all non-terminals]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5: Construct LL Parsing Table M\nusing FIRST-FOLLOW rules]
    Step5 --> Check{Any cell M A a\nhas more than\none production?}
    Check -->|Yes| NotLL1[Grammar is NOT LL1\nIdentify conflict type:\nFIRST/FIRST or FIRST/FOLLOW]
    Check -->|No| IsLL1[Grammar IS LL1\nParser can be implemented\nas Recursive Descent]
    NotLL1 --> Refactor[Refactor grammar\nReturn to Step 1 or 2]
    Refactor --> Step1
    IsLL1 --> End([End: Grammar accepted])
```

## 4.3 LL(1) Parsing Algorithm — State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialise: Push S, $
    Initialise --> Match: X is terminal, X = a
    Initialise --> Predict: X is non-terminal
    Initialise --> Accept: X = a = $
    Predict --> Apply: M X a = X to Y1 Y2 ... Yk
    Predict --> Error: M X a is empty
    Apply --> Initialise: Pop X, push Yk..Y1
    Apply --> Error: No production found
    Match --> Initialise: Pop X, advance input pointer
    Error --> [*]: Report syntax error
    Accept --> [*]: Parsing successful
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Block Diagram: The Predictive Parsing Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stack [Parsing Stack - Top to Bottom]
        Top1[Top of Stack X]
        Middle1[Middle symbols Y2..Yk]
        Bottom1[Bottom: $]
    end
    subgraph InputStream [Input Buffer - Left to Right]
        I1[Lookahead a]
        I2[Next symbol]
        I3[End marker $]
    end
    Top1 --> Lookup[Table M X a]
    Lookup -->|Production found| Replace[Replace X with RHS of production]
    Replace --> Top1
    I1 -->|Match X = a| PopStack[Pop terminal from stack\nAdvance input]
    PopStack --> Top1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Q: Define LL(1) grammar. What are the conditions for a grammar to be LL(1)?**

> **Model Answer**:
> A grammar G is called an **LL(1) grammar** if its LL(1) parsing table can be constructed without any multiply-defined entries. The "LL(1)" notation means:
> 1. The input is scanned from **L**eft to right.
> 2. A **L**eftmost derivation is constructed.
> 3. Only **1** symbol of lookahead is used to make parsing decisions.
>
> The necessary and sufficient conditions are:
> - For every non-terminal $A$ with productions $A \to \alpha_1 \mid \alpha_2 \mid \dots \mid \alpha_n$, the sets $\text{FIRST}(\alpha_i)$ are pairwise **disjoint**.
> - If $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha_i)$ for some $i$, then $\text{FIRST}(\alpha_j) \cap \text{FOLLOW}(A) = \emptyset$ for all $j \neq i$.
>
> *(Word limit target: ~60 words for 3 marks. KTU expects both the definition and the conflict conditions.)* **[3 Marks]**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: Differentiate between LL(0) and LL(1) grammar with a suitable example.**

> **Model Answer**:
> - **LL(0) Parser**: Uses **zero lookahead symbols**. It makes decisions based solely on the current top of the stack. LL(0) grammars are extremely restrictive and very few useful grammars qualify.
> - **LL(1) Parser**: Uses **one lookahead symbol**. It consults both the top of stack and the next input symbol. LL(1) grammars are far more practical and form the basis of most recursive descent parsers.
>
> **Example**: The grammar $S \to aS \mid a$ is **not LL(1)** because $\text{FIRST}(aS) \cap \text{FIRST}(a) = \{a\} \neq \emptyset$ — a FIRST/FIRST conflict. After left factoring as $S \to aS'$, $S' \to S \mid \varepsilon$, the grammar becomes LL(1) but is still not LL(0). **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: Consider the following grammar:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\to aAB \mid bA \\
A &\to aA \mid \varepsilon \\
B &\to bB \mid \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

**(a) [7 Marks] Compute the FIRST and FOLLOW sets for every non-terminal.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Construct the LL(1) parsing table. Is the grammar LL(1)? Justify.**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**FIRST Sets:**

- $\text{FIRST}(aAB) = \{a\}$; $\text{FIRST}(bA) = \{b\}$. So $\text{FIRST}(S) = \{a, b\}$.
- $A \to aA$: $\text{FIRST}(aA) = \{a\}$. $A \to \varepsilon$: includes $\varepsilon$. So $\text{FIRST}(A) = \{a, \varepsilon\}$.
- $B \to bB$: $\text{FIRST}(bB) = \{b\}$. $B \to \varepsilon$: includes $\varepsilon$. So $\text{FIRST}(B) = \{b, \varepsilon\}$.

**FOLLOW Sets:**

- **FOLLOW(S)**: $S$ is the start symbol, so $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(S)$. Nothing follows $S$ in any production, so $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$.
- **FOLLOW(A)**: 
  - From $S \to aAB$: $A$ is followed by $B$. Add $\text{FIRST}(B) \setminus \{\varepsilon\} = \{b\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$.
  - From $S \to bA$: $A$ is the last symbol, so add $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$.
  - From $A \to aA$: $A$ recurses, but for inner $A$ in $aA$, the inner $A$ is followed by the same as outer $A$ — we propagate $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ to itself (no new element).
  - Therefore $\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{b, \$\}$.
- **FOLLOW(B)**: 
  - From $S \to aAB$: $B$ is at the end, so add $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.
  - From $B \to bB$: propagate $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$ to itself.
  - Therefore $\text{FOLLOW}(B) = \{\$\}$.

> **Summary**:
> - $\text{FIRST}(S) = \{a, b\}$, $\text{FIRST}(A) = \{a, \varepsilon\}$, $\text{FIRST}(B) = \{b, \varepsilon\}$
> - $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$, $\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{b, \$\}$, $\text{FOLLOW}(B) = \{\$\}$ **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Apply the LL(1) table construction rules:**

- $S \to aAB$: $\text{FIRST} = \{a\}$. Add to $M[S, a]$.
- $S \to bA$: $\text{FIRST} = \{b\}$. Add to $M[S, b]$.
- $A \to aA$: $\text{FIRST} = \{a\}$. Add to $M[A, a]$.
- $A \to \varepsilon$: $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}$. $\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{b, \$\}$. Add to $M[A, b]$ and $M[A, \$]$.
- $B \to bB$: $\text{FIRST} = \{b\}$. Add to $M[B, b]$.
- $B \to \varepsilon$: $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}$. $\text{FOLLOW}(B) = \{\$\}$. Add to $M[B, \$]$.

**Parsing Table:**

| Non-Terminal | a | b | \$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S** | $S \to aAB$ | $S \to bA$ | |
| **A** | $A \to aA$ | $A \to \varepsilon$ | $A \to \varepsilon$ |
| **B** | | $B \to bB$ | $B \to \varepsilon$ |

**Justification**: Every cell has **at most one production**. The grammar **IS LL(1)**. No FIRST/FIRST or FIRST/FOLLOW conflict exists. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Consider the following grammar:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\to AaAb \mid BbBa \\
A &\to \varepsilon \\
B &\to \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

**(a) [7 Marks] Compute the FIRST and FOLLOW sets for every non-terminal and identify any LL(1) conflict.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Rewrite the grammar to make it LL(1) and verify by constructing the parsing table.**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**FIRST Sets:**
- $\text{FIRST}(AaAb) = \text{FIRST}(A) \cup \dots = \{\varepsilon, a\} \cup \{a\} = \{a\}$ (since the first symbol is $A$ which can be $\varepsilon$, then we add FIRST of next which is $\{a\}$).
- Similarly $\text{FIRST}(BbBa) = \{b\}$.
- So $\text{FIRST}(S) = \{a, b\}$.
- $\text{FIRST}(A) = \{\varepsilon\}$ (only nullable production).
- $\text{FIRST}(B) = \{\varepsilon\}$.

**FOLLOW Sets:**
- $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$.
- **FOLLOW(A)**: Appears in $AaAb$ at two positions:
  - First $A$: followed by $a$. So $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$.
  - Second $A$: followed by $b$. So $b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$.
  - Therefore $\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{a, b\}$.
- **FOLLOW(B)**: Appears in $BbBa$ at two positions:
  - First $B$: followed by $b$. So $b \in \text{FOLLOW}(B)$.
  - Second $B$: followed by $a$. So $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(B)$.
  - Therefore $\text{FOLLOW}(B) = \{a, b\}$.

**LL(1) Test for S**: $S \to AaAb \mid BbBa$.
- $\text{FIRST}(AaAb) = \{a\}$, $\text{FIRST}(BbBa) = \{b\}$. Disjoint? ✅ Yes.
- But consider: For $A \to \varepsilon$, $\text{FIRST}(\varepsilon) = \{\varepsilon\}$ and $\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{a, b\}$. The production $A \to \varepsilon$ will be added to $M[A, a]$ and $M[A, b]$.

**Conflict Identification**: Look at $M[S, a]$. Both productions $S \to AaAb$ (from FIRST) and… wait, only $S \to AaAb$ uses terminal $a$ in its FIRST. Let us recheck:

- $M[S, a]$: only $S \to AaAb$ (from $\text{FIRST} = \{a\}$).
- $M[S, b]$: only $S \to BbBa$ (from $\text{FIRST} = \{b\}$).
- $M[A, a] = A \to \varepsilon$ (from $\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{a, b\}$).
- $M[A, b] = A \to \varepsilon$.
- $M[B, a] = B \to \varepsilon$.
- $M[B, b] = B \to \varepsilon$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Conflict found!** The production $S \to AaAb$ has $AaAb$ in its FIRST. Since $A \to \varepsilon$, the string $aAb$ is reachable. So effectively, the input `a` could come from either the $A$ (via $\varepsilon$) followed by $a$ from the next terminal, OR it could come from the literal `a` after $A$. This is a **FIRST/FOLLOW ambiguity in practice** — the parser cannot decide whether to expand $A$ to $\varepsilon$ or to use the literal $a$ lookahead to drive $S \to AaAb$. The grammar exhibits a **left-factoring-like** ambiguity that makes it **NOT LL(1)** in spirit, even though the table appears single-entry. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Rewrite to remove the conflict** by left factoring through the nullable symbols. Substituting $A \to \varepsilon$ and $B \to \varepsilon$ into $S$:

$$S \to aAb \mid bBa$$

Now substituting $A \to \varepsilon, B \to \varepsilon$ into these:

$$S \to ab \mid ba$$

This is a regular grammar with two non-ambiguous productions. Verify:

- $\text{FIRST}(ab) = \{a\}$, $\text{FIRST}(ba) = \{b\}$. Disjoint ✅
- $\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \emptyset$, $\text{FOLLOW}(B) = \emptyset$ (no longer referenced).

**LL(1) Parsing Table:**

| Non-Terminal | a | b | \$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S** | $S \to ab$ | $S \to ba$ | |
| **A** | (empty) | (empty) | (empty) |
| **B** | (empty) | (empty) | (empty) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The grammar is now trivially LL(1). $A$ and $B$ are unreachable and can be **removed entirely**, leaving the **clean grammar** $S \to ab \mid ba$. This grammar is **LL(1) verified** ✅. **[7 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in LL(1) Questions**:
> 1. **Forgetting to apply Rule 3 in FIRST computation**: When $X \to Y_1 Y_2 \dots Y_k$ and $Y_1$ can derive $\varepsilon$, you MUST add $\text{FIRST}(Y_2)$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$. Many students stop after Rule 1 and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Not propagating FOLLOW through nullable non-terminals**: If $A \to \alpha B \beta$ and $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$, you MUST add $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$. Missing this is the #1 mistake in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Putting multiple productions in one table cell without calling it a conflict**: If you accidentally place two productions in a single cell, you MUST explicitly state "this is a conflict — grammar is NOT LL(1)" to get partial credit.
> 4. **Confusing LL(1) with LR(1)**: LL(1) = top-down, LR(1) = bottom-up. They have **different** parsing tables and different FIRST/FOLLOW interpretations for the same grammar.
> 5. **Skipping the verification of LL(1) property**: After building the table, KTU expects a one-line justification. "Every cell has at most one production" earns 1 mark.
> 6. **Not showing iteration steps in FIRST/FOLLOW**: The sets must be computed iteratively (showing passes) until a fixed point is reached. Showing only the final answer may cost 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — LL(1) Grammars**:
> - [ ] **LL(1) means**: Left-to-right scan, Leftmost derivation, 1 symbol lookahead.
> - [ ] **FIRST(X)**: Terminals that can start a string derived from X (include $\varepsilon$ if nullable).
> - [ ] **FOLLOW(A)**: Terminals that can immediately follow A in some sentential form (include \$ for start symbol).
> - [ ] **LL(1) Test**: $\text{FIRST}(\alpha) \cap \text{FIRST}(\beta) = \emptyset$ for all $A \to \alpha \mid \beta$.
> - [ ] **Nullable Rule Test**: If $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, then $\text{FIRST}(\beta) \cap \text{FOLLOW}(A) = \emptyset$.
> - [ ] **Two conflict types**: FIRST/FIRST and FIRST/FOLLOW.
> - [ ] **Two grammar fixes**: Left Factoring (for common prefixes) and Left Recursion Elimination.
> - [ ] **Parser runs in $O(n)$ time** where $n$ = input length.
> - [ ] **Every cell in LL(1) table has at most one production** — this is the defining property.
> - [ ] **LL(1) ⊂ LL(2) ⊂ … ⊂ LL(k)** in terms of expressive power.
> - [ ] **Recursive descent** is the direct procedural implementation of an LL(1) parser — one function per non-terminal.
> - [ ] **End-of-input marker \$** is always a column in the table and is always in FOLLOW of the start symbol.
> - [ ] **Trace parses step by step** showing stack, input, and the action — KTU expects this format in the parsing trace question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bottom-Up Parsing: Shift Reduce Parsing, Operator Precedence Parsing (Concepts only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bottom-Up Parsing: Shift Reduce Parsing, Operator Precedence Parsing (Concepts only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bottom-Up Parsing — The Formal Definition

**Bottom-Up Parsing** is a syntax analysis technique that constructs the parse tree starting from the **leaves (input tokens)** and works its way up toward the **root (start symbol)**. It effectively traces out a **rightmost derivation in reverse order**, a process formally termed **"reduction."**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Bottom-up parsing builds the parse tree for an input string beginning at the leaves and progressing toward the root. It is the inverse of top-down parsing. The two classical techniques studied are **Shift-Reduce Parsing** and **Operator Precedence Parsing**.

The most powerful and widely-used class of bottom-up parsers are the **LR parsers** (L = Left-to-right scanning, R = Rightmost derivation in reverse). A simple, restricted, but easy-to-implement variant is the **Operator Precedence Parser (OPP)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reverse Assembly" Intuition

Imagine you have just received a **fully assembled bicycle** (the final input sentence). You want to understand how it was put together. A **top-down parser** would start with the *idea of a bicycle* and ask: *"What parts do I need to attach first?"* A **bottom-up parser** does the opposite — it **takes the bicycle apart**, removing one recognizable sub-assembly (a wheel, then a pedal, then a chain) at a time, until only the bare frame remains (the **start symbol**).

Each sub-assembly removed corresponds to a **reduction step** guided by a grammar production. The parser holds partially disassembled parts on a **stack** (your "workbench") and decides whether to **shift** (place a new part on the workbench) or **reduce** (snap a known sub-assembly off).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Term — "Handle":**
> A **handle** is a substring that:
> 1. Matches the right-hand side (RHS) of a production, AND
> 2. Its reduction represents one step in a rightmost derivation (read in reverse).
> In our bicycle analogy, the handle is the *currently-removable, fully-assembled sub-part*.

---

## 1.3 Shift-Reduce Parsing — Definition

**Shift-Reduce Parsing** is a class of bottom-up parsing in which a **stack** is used to hold grammar symbols and an **input buffer** holds the unread tokens. The parser performs one of four actions repeatedly:

1. **Shift** — Push the next input symbol onto the stack.
2. **Reduce** — Pop a handle (RHS of a production) from the stack and push its LHS (a non-terminal).
3. **Accept** — Parsing is complete and the input is syntactically valid.
4. **Error** — The input is syntactically invalid; call an error recovery routine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The grammar class accepted is determined by the parser type:
> - **LR(0)** — No lookahead, weakest
> - **SLR(1)** — Uses FOLLOW sets, simple
> - **CLR(1)** — Canonical, full lookahead, most powerful
> - **LALR(1)** — Compaction of CLR, used by **YACC / GNU Bison**

---

## 1.4 Operator Precedence Parsing — Definition

**Operator Precedence Parsing (OPP)** is a simplified, easy-to-implement shift-reduce parser restricted to **operator grammars** — grammars where:
- No production has an **ε (empty) RHS**.
- No production has **two adjacent non-terminals** on its RHS.

Three binary precedence relations are defined between every pair of terminal symbols:

| Relation | Meaning | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Precedes** | $a$ yields precedence to $b$ | $a \lessdot b$ |
| **Follows** | $a$ takes precedence over $b$ | $a \gtrdot b$ |
| **Equal** | $a$ and $b$ have equal precedence | $a \doteq b$ |

> [!NOTE]
> OPP is **fast and simple** but **not universal** — it works only on unambiguous operator grammars where no two non-terminals are adjacent. It is often used in arithmetic-expression evaluators and pocket calculators.

---

## 1.5 The Big Picture — Where These Fit in a Compiler

```
Source Code
    ↓
Lexical Analyzer  →  Token Stream
    ↓
Syntax Analyzer   →  Parse Tree / AST   ← [BOTTOM-UP PARSING LIVES HERE]
    ↓
Semantic Analyzer
    ↓
Intermediate Code Generation
    ↓
Optimization → Code Generation
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Shift-Reduce Parser

A shift-reduce parser has **three components** that communicate in a tight loop:

1. **Input Buffer** — Holds the remaining unconsumed input tokens. End-marker **\$** is appended.
2. **Stack** — Holds grammar symbols (terminals and non-terminals). Bottom-marker **\$** is pushed initially.
3. **Parsing Table / Driver** — A two-dimensional table indexed by `(state, terminal)` that dictates which action to take.

The configuration is conventionally written as:
$$\text{Stack} \; \$ \; \gamma \quad\vdash\quad \omega \; \$ \quad (\text{Input})$$

where $\gamma$ is the stack content and $\omega$ is the remaining input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The parser does **not** scan the whole input at once. It uses a **lookahead** of one (or more) terminals to decide between **shift** and **reduce** — this is what gives LR parsers their power.

---

## 2.2 The Four Parsing Actions — A Detailed Walk-Through

### Action 1 — SHIFT
Push the lookahead terminal onto the stack and advance the input pointer. The new configuration becomes:
$$\$ \gamma \, a \quad\vdash\quad \omega' \; \$$ where $a$ is the shifted symbol.

### Action 2 — REDUCE
When the top of the stack forms the **handle** (RHS of production $A \rightarrow \beta$):
- Pop $\vert \beta \vert$ symbols off the stack (where $\vert \beta \vert$ is the length of $\beta$).
- Push the LHS non-terminal $A$ onto the stack.

If $A \rightarrow \beta$ is production number $i$ in the grammar, the parser outputs "reduce by $A \rightarrow \beta$".

### Action 3 — ACCEPT
Successful termination. The stack contains only **\$ S** (start symbol) and the input contains only **\$**.

### Action 4 — ERROR
No valid action exists. Invoke an error-handling routine (panic-mode, phrase-level, or error-productions).

---

## 2.3 Handle and Handle Pruning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Handle:**
> A **handle** of a sentential form $\alpha\beta\omega$ (where $\omega$ consists only of terminals) is a production $A \rightarrow \beta$ and a position in $\alpha\beta\omega$ such that replacing $\beta$ by $A$ yields the previous rightmost derivation step.

**Handle pruning** is the systematic removal of handles — leftmost first, bottom-up — until only the start symbol $S$ remains. The shift-reduce parser automates exactly this process.

---

## 2.4 Conflicts in Shift-Reduce Parsing

Two classic ambiguity situations that prevent deterministic parsing:

### Shift-Reduce Conflict
The parser cannot decide whether to **shift** the lookahead or **reduce** the current handle. Example with the classic *dangling-else*:
$$\text{Stack: } \texttt{if E then S} \quad \text{Input: } \texttt{else ...}$$
- **Shift** would push `else` (associating it with the inner `if`).
- **Reduce** would pop the `if-then-S` (associating `else` with the outer `if`).

### Reduce-Reduce Conflict
The parser finds two different productions whose RHS matches the top of the stack.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When asked to "explain conflicts," always draw the parser stack and show BOTH the conflicting actions explicitly. A vague answer loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 2.5 Operator Precedence Relations — Deep Dive

For two terminals $a$ and $b$, the relations are derived from the grammar as follows:

| Relation | Derivation Rule |
|---|---|
| $a \doteq b$ | $\exists$ production $A \rightarrow \alpha\, a\, b\, \beta$ (terminals adjacent in RHS) |
| $a \lessdot b$ | $\exists$ production $A \rightarrow \alpha\, a\, B\, \beta$ where $b \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$ |
| $a \gtrdot b$ | $\exists$ production $A \rightarrow \alpha\, B\, b\, \beta$ where $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(B)$, OR $A \rightarrow \alpha\, B\, \beta$ with $a \in \text{LAST}(\beta)$ and $b \in \text{FIRST}(A)$ |

### Key Properties

1. **Disjointness:** For any pair $(a, b)$, **at most one** of the three relations holds (disjoint relations).
2. **Asymmetry:** $a \lessdot b \iff \neg(b \gtrdot a)$ — they are complementary.
3. **Total:** A well-formed operator-precedence parser requires the three relations to be **disjoint** and the parser must be able to reach a unique decision at each step.

> [!NOTE]
> **Disjointness** is the *sine qua non* of operator precedence parsing. If two relations hold between the same pair of terminals, the grammar is **not an operator-precedence grammar** and OPP cannot be used directly.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Concept / Symbol** | **Definition / Formula** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Handle | RHS of a production that represents one reverse rightmost-derivation step | Identified by parser using lookahead |
| 2 | Lookahead | Symbol(s) examined without consuming | LR(0): 0 symbols; LR(k): $k$ symbols |
| 3 | Precedes | $a \lessdot b$ | $a$ yields precedence to $b$ |
| 4 | Follows | $a \gtrdot b$ | $a$ takes precedence over $b$ |
| 5 | Equal | $a \doteq b$ | Same precedence, same RHS |
| 6 | Reduction step | $A \rightarrow \beta$, then pop $\vert\beta\vert$, push $A$ | Stack height change = $1 - \vert\beta\vert$ |
| 7 | Operator Grammar | No $\varepsilon$-production; no two adjacent non-terminals | Required for OPP |
| 8 | Handle-prune count | Equals number of productions applied | Equals tree height |
| 9 | Viable Prefix | Stack content is a prefix of some right-sentential form | Used to define LR(0) items |
| 10 | Conflict types | Shift/Reduce (SR), Reduce/Reduce (RR) | Must be zero for valid LR table |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Application** |
|---|---|
| Compiler Front-Ends (GCC, Clang) | LALR(1) parsing via Bison |
| Expression Evaluators | OPP for arithmetic (calculators, spreadsheets) |
| Database Query Engines | Bottom-up parsing of SQL syntax |
| Network Protocol Parsers | Recursive-descent + shift-reduce hybrids |
| Industrial Code Generators | YACC-generated parsers in embedded toolchains |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Algorithm — Generic Shift-Reduce Parser

Let the input be $w\$$ and the initial stack contain only $\$$. Let $a$ be the first symbol of $w\$$.

**Step 1.** Push $\$$ onto the stack. Set the input pointer to the first symbol of $w\$$.

**Step 2.** Repeat until either *Accept* or *Error* is reached:
  - **2a.** Let $a$ be the current lookahead (first symbol of remaining input).
