# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Canonical LR(1) Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Start: Augmented Grammar G prime with S prime arrow S"] --> A1["Seed Item: S prime arrow dot S lookahead dollar"]
    A1 --> A2["Compute CLOSURE to form State I0"]
    A2 --> A3{"For every symbol X, compute GOTO I0 X"}
    A3 --> B0["GOTO on S gives I1"]
    A3 --> B1["GOTO on C gives I2"]
    A3 --> B2["GOTO on c gives I3"]
    A3 --> B3["GOTO on d gives I4"]
    B0 --> C0["I1 contains S prime arrow S dot, dollar"]
    B1 --> C1["I2 contains S arrow C dot C, dollar"]
    B2 --> C2["I3 contains C arrow c dot C, c and d"]
    B3 --> C3["I4 contains C arrow d dot, c and d"]
    C0 --> D0["I1 has no GOTO: dead end"]
    C1 --> D1["From I2: GOTO on C, c, d yield I5, I3, I7"]
    C2 --> D2["From I3: GOTO on C, c, d yield I8, I3, I4"]
    C3 --> D3["I4 has no GOTO: dead end"]
    D1 --> E0["I5: S arrow CC dot, dollar"]
    D1 --> E1["I7: C arrow d dot, dollar"]
    D2 --> E2["I8: C arrow cC dot, c and d"]
    E0 --> F0["Add to canonical collection C"]
    E1 --> F0
    E2 --> F0
    F0 --> G0["Deduplicate states with identical items"]
    G0 --> H0["Final CLR 1 collection ready"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — The CLR(1) DFA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0["I0\nS prime dot S\nS dot CC\nC dot cC\nC dot d"] -->|S| S1["I1\nS prime S dot\nACCEPT on dollar"]
    S0 -->|C| S2["I2\nS C dot C\nC dot cC\nC dot d"]
    S0 -->|c| S3["I3\nC c dot C\nC dot cC\nC dot d"]
    S0 -->|d| S4["I4\nC d dot"]
    S2 -->|C| S5["I5\nS CC dot\nREDUCE S arrow CC"]
    S2 -->|c| S3
    S2 -->|d| S7["I7\nC d dot\nREDUCE C arrow d"]
    S3 -->|C| S8["I8\nC cC dot\nREDUCE C arrow cC"]
    S3 -->|c| S3
    S3 -->|d| S4
    S4 -->|REDUCE on c,d| R3["r3: C arrow d"]
    S5 -->|REDUCE on dollar| R1["r1: S arrow CC"]
    S7 -->|REDUCE on dollar| R3
    S8 -->|REDUCE on c,d| R2["r2: C arrow cC"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — LALR State Merging (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CLR["Canonical LR1 Collection - 10 States"]
        C0["I0 - S prime dot S"]
        C1["I1 - S prime S dot"]
        C2["I2 - S C dot C"]
        C3["I3 - C c dot C - la c,d"]
        C4["I4 - C d dot - la c,d"]
        C5["I5 - S CC dot"]
        C6["I6 - C c dot C - la dollar"]
        C7["I7 - C d dot - la dollar"]
        C8["I8 - C cC dot - la c,d"]
        C11["I11 - C cC dot - la dollar"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["Core Extraction Module"]
        K1["Extract LR0 cores from each state"]
    end

    subgraph MERGE["Merge Module"]
        M1["Group I3 with I6 - core C c dot C"]
        M2["Group I4 with I7 - core C d dot"]
        M3["Group I8 with I11 - core C cC dot"]
        M4["Union the lookaheads of merged states"]
    end

    subgraph LALR["LALR1 Collection - 7 States"]
        L0["I0: S prime dot S"]
        L1["I1: S prime S dot"]
        L2["I2: S C dot C"]
        L3["I3: C c dot C - la c,d,dollar"]
        L4["I4: C d dot - la c,d,dollar"]
        L5["I5: S CC dot"]
        L8["I8: C cC dot - la c,d,dollar"]
    end

    CLR --> CORE --> MERGE --> LALR
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Table View — ACTION/GOTO Cell Population Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Parse Item Set I_i"] --> P1{"Is item of form A arrow alpha dot a beta with lookahead L?"}
    P1 -->|Yes| P2["GOTO I_i a = I_j"]
    P2 --> P3["ACTION i,a = shift j"]
    P1 -->|No| P4{"Is item of form A arrow alpha dot with lookahead a and A is not S prime?"}
    P4 -->|Yes| P5["ACTION i,a = reduce by production A arrow alpha"]
    P4 -->|No| P6{"Is item S prime arrow S dot with lookahead dollar?"}
    P6 -->|Yes| P7["ACTION i,dollar = accept"]
    P6 -->|No| P8["ACTION i,x = error for terminal x"]
    P3 --> P9["Check for conflict in ACTION cell i,a"]
    P5 --> P9
    P7 --> P9
    P9 --> P10{"Multiple actions in same cell?"}
    P10 -->|Yes| P11["CONFLICT: Grammar not LR1"]
    P10 -->|No| P12["Cell is valid"]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Conceptual Map — When to Use Each LR Variant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Given a Grammar G, which LR parser?"] --> Q2{"Need maximum language coverage?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3["Use Canonical LR1 - most powerful"]
    Q2 -->|No| Q4{"Production compiler required?"}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q5["Use LALR1 - YACC, Bison standard"]
    Q4 -->|No| Q6{"Is grammar already LR0?"}
    Q6 -->|Yes| Q7["Use SLR1 or LR0 - simplest tables"]
    Q6 -->|No| Q5
    Q3 --> R1["Cost: huge tables, slow construction"]
    Q5 --> R2["Cost: medium tables, fast construction, near-CLR power"]
    Q7 --> R3["Cost: smallest tables, may fail on real grammars"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU 2024 Question Bank

### Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, KTU University Exam - Jun 2024 pattern]**

**Q1. What is an LR(1) item? How does it differ from an LR(0) item?**

**Model Answer:**
An **LR(1) item** is a pair `[A → α·β, a]` where `A → αβ` is a production, `·` indicates the parsing progress, and `a ∈ T ∪ {$}` is the **lookahead** terminal. The lookahead specifies which input symbols can legally follow the handle `α` in a viable derivation.
An **LR(0) item** `[A → α·β]` is the same structure **without** the lookahead. The extra lookahead component gives LR(1) parsers strictly more parsing power and enables resolution of conflicts that LR(0) cannot handle.
*For 3 marks: definition + 1 distinguishing line is sufficient; KTU expects this precise wording.*

**Q2. Define the LALR(1) parser. State any two differences between LALR(1) and CLR(1) parsers.**

**Model Answer:**
The **LALR(1) parser** is constructed from the canonical LR(1) collection by **merging any two states that have the same core (identical LR(0) items)** and **unioning their lookahead sets**.

**Differences (any two):**
1. **State Count:** CLR(1) may have up to 10× more states than LALR(1); LALR(1) has exactly as many states as LR(0)/SLR(1).
2. **Conflicts:** CLR(1) detects the *fewest* conflicts; LALR(1) can introduce **spurious reduce-reduce conflicts** that CLR(1) does not have (it never introduces new shift-reduce conflicts).
3. **Power:** Every LALR(1) grammar is CLR(1), but not vice versa.
4. **Industry use:** LALR(1) is used in YACC/Bison; CLR(1) is rarely built directly.

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper pattern, ESE Module 2]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Full CLR(1) Construction

Consider the augmented grammar:

```
S' → S
S  → A A
A  → a A
A  → b
```

**(a)** Construct the **canonical collection of LR(1) item sets** for this grammar. Show all CLOSURE and GOTO operations clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Build the **canonical LR(1) parsing table** (ACTION and GOTO). Indicate any conflicts, if present. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Step 1: Initial State I₀**
Start with `[S' → ·S, $]`. CLOSURE expansion:
- For `[S' → ·S, $]`: β = ε, lookahead = $ → add `[S → ·AA, $]`.
- For `[S → ·AA, $]`: dot before A, β = A, a = $ → FIRST(A$) = {a, b}. Add `[A → ·aA, a]`, `[A → ·aA, b]`, `[A → ·b, a]`, `[A → ·b, b]`.

$$I_0 = \{[S' \rightarrow \cdot S, \$], [S \rightarrow \cdot AA, \$], [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, a], [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, b], [A \rightarrow \cdot b, a], [A \rightarrow \cdot b, b]\}$$

**Step 2: GOTO from I₀**

- **GOTO(I₀, S):** Items with `·S` advanced → `[S' → S·, $]`. No further closure.
  $$I_1 = \{[S' \rightarrow S\cdot, \$]\}$$

- **GOTO(I₀, A):** Items with `·A` advanced → `[S → A·A, $]`. Closure: dot before A, β = ε, lookahead = $ → add `[A → ·aA, $]`, `[A → ·b, $]`.
  $$I_2 = \{[S \rightarrow A\cdot A, \$], [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, \$], [A \rightarrow \cdot b, \$]\}$$

- **GOTO(I₀, a):** Items with `·a` advanced → `[A → a·A, a]`, `[A → a·A, b]`. Closure: dot before A, β = ε, lookahead preserved.
  $$I_3 = \{[A \rightarrow a\cdot A, a], [A \rightarrow a\cdot A, b], [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, a], [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, b], [A \rightarrow \cdot b, a], [A \rightarrow \cdot b, b]\}$$

- **GOTO(I₀, b):** Items with `·b` advanced → `[A → b·, a]`, `[A → b·, b]`.
  $$I_4 = \{[A \rightarrow b\cdot, a], [A \rightarrow b\cdot, b]\}$$

**Step 3: Continue GOTO exploration**

- **GOTO(I₂, A):** → `[S → AA·, $]`. So $I_5 = \{[S \rightarrow AA\cdot, \$]\}$.
- **GOTO(I₂, a):** → `[A → a·A, $]`. Closure same pattern with lookahead $ only.
  $$I_6 = \{[A \rightarrow a\cdot A, \$], [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, \$], [A \rightarrow \cdot b, \$]\}$$
- **GOTO(I₂, b):** → `[A → b·, $]`. So $I_7 = \{[A \rightarrow b\cdot, \$]\}$.
- **GOTO(I₃, A):** → `[A → aA·, a]`, `[A → aA·, b]`. So $I_8 = \{[A \rightarrow aA\cdot, a], [A \rightarrow aA\cdot, b]\}$.
- **GOTO(I₃, a):** → mirrors I₃'s structure (same lookaheads) → re-enter I₃ (self-loop).
- **GOTO(I₃, b):** → mirrors I₄'s structure → re-enter I₄.
- **GOTO(I₆, A):** → `[A → aA·, $]`. New state $I_9 = \{[A \rightarrow aA\cdot, \$]\}$.
- All others are dead-ends or self-loops.

**Final CLR(1) Collection:** C = {I₀, I₁, I₂, I₃, I₄, I₅, I₆, I₇, I₈, I₉} — **10 states**.

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**GOTO Sub-Table:**

| State | S | A |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 2 |
| 1 | — | — |
| 2 | — | 5 |
| 3 | — | 8 |
| 4 | — | — |
| 5 | — | — |
| 6 | — | 9 |
| 7 | — | — |
| 8 | — | — |
| 9 | — | — |

**ACTION Sub-Table:**

| State | a | b | $ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | s3 | s4 | — |
| 1 | — | — | acc |
| 2 | s6 | s7 | — |
| 3 | s3 | s4 | — |
| 4 | r4 | r4 | — |
| 5 | — | — | r1 |
| 6 | s6 | s7 | — |
| 7 | — | — | r4 |
| 8 | r3 | r3 | — |
| 9 | — | — | r3 |

**Production Numbering:** r1: S→AA, r3: A→aA, r4: A→b.

**Conflict Check:** Every cell has at most one action → **No conflicts → Grammar is CLR(1).**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — LALR Table Construction by Core Merging

Consider the same grammar as Question A. **(a)** Identify the pairs of LR(1) item sets that have **identical cores** and show the resulting **LALR(1) item sets** with merged lookaheads. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Construct the **LALR(1) parsing table** and compare it with the CLR(1) table. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Step 1: Extract Core of Each CLR(1) State**

| CLR State | Core Items |
|---|---|
| I₀ | S'→·S, S→·AA, A→·aA, A→·b |
| I₁ | S'→S· |
| I₂ | S→A·A, A→·aA, A→·b |
| I₃ | A→a·A, A→·aA, A→·b |
| I₄ | A→b· |
| I₅ | S→AA· |
| I₆ | A→a·A, A→·aA, A→·b |
| I₇ | A→b· |
| I₈ | A→aA· |
| I₉ | A→aA· |

**Step 2: Group by Core**
- I₃ and I₆ → core: {A→a·A, A→·aA, A→·b} → **merge**
- I₄ and I₇ → core: {A→b·} → **merge**
- I₈ and I₉ → core: {A→aA·} → **merge**

**Step 3: Union Lookaheads**

**Merged I₃₆ = I₃ ∪ I₆:**
- From I₃: lookaheads are {a, b} on each item
- From I₆: lookaheads are {$} on each item
- **Union: {a, b, $}** for all items

$$I_{36} = \{[A \rightarrow a\cdot A, a/b/\$], [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, a/b/\$], [A \rightarrow \cdot b, a/b/\$]\}$$

**Merged I₄₇ = I₄ ∪ I₇:**
- From I₄: lookaheads {a, b}
- From I₇: lookahead {$}
- **Union: {a, b, $}**

$$I_{47} = \{[A \rightarrow b\cdot, a/b/\$]\}$$

**Merged I₈₉ = I₈ ∪ I₉:**
- From I₈: lookaheads {a, b}
- From I₉: lookahead {$}
- **Union: {a, b, $}**

$$I_{89} = \{[A \rightarrow aA\cdot, a/b/\$]\}$$

**Step 4: Renumber LALR States**

| LALR State | Items | Source |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | S'→·S, S→·AA, A→·aA, A→·b | I₀ |
| 1 | S'→S· | I₁ |
| 2 | S→A·A, A→·aA, A→·b | I₂ |
| 3 | A→a·A, A→·aA, A→·b (la: a/b/$) | **I₃ ∪ I₆** |
| 4 | A→b· (la: a/b/$) | **I₄ ∪ I₇** |
| 5 | S→AA· | I₅ |
| 6 | A→aA· (la: a/b/$) | **I₈ ∪ I₉** |

**Total: 7 LALR states** (vs. 10 CLR states — 30% reduction).

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**LALR(1) ACTION Table:**

| State | a | b | $ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | s3 | s3 | — |
| 1 | — | — | acc |
| 2 | s3 | s3 | — |
| 3 | s3 | s3 | — |
| **4** | **r4** | **r4** | **r4** |
| 5 | — | — | r1 |
| **6** | **r3** | **r3** | **r3** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Compare with CLR(1) table:** State 4 (merged from I₄ and I₇) now reduces on `a`, `b`, **and** `$`, whereas in CLR(1) the corresponding cells were separate. Similarly, state 6 (merged from I₈ and I₉) reduces on `a`, `b`, **and** `$`.

**LALR(1) GOTO Table:**

| State | S | A |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 2 |
| 1 | — | — |
| 2 | — | 5 |
| 3 | — | 6 |
| 4 | — | — |
| 5 | — | — |
| 6 | — | — |

**Comparison with CLR(1):**

| Aspect | CLR(1) | LALR(1) |
|---|---|---|
| States | 10 | 7 |
| Reduce entries in state 4 (formerly split) | 2 | 3 |
| Conflicts | 0 | 0 (this grammar is LALR-friendly) |
| Construction time | Higher | Lower |
| Recognition power | Maximum | Slightly less |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** No spurious conflicts were introduced. The grammar is LALR(1) as well as CLR(1). The LALR table is **more compact** and **equivalently correct** for this grammar.

---

## 5.2 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU CLR/LALR Construction Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the augmented production `S' → S`.** The initial state *must* start from `[S' → ·S, $]`. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks immediately.
>
> 2. **Mis-computing `FIRST(βa)` during CLOSURE.** If the item is `[A → α·Bβ, a]`, the new lookaheads are `FIRST(βa)`, **not** `FIRST(Ba)`. β is the *suffix after B*, not B itself.
>
> 3. **Replacing lookaheads instead of unioning them when merging LALR states.** Always take the **set union** of lookaheads from the merged CLR(1) states. Overwriting silently creates an incorrect (and possibly conflicting) LALR table.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the `$` end-marker.** In KTU problems, the augmented start item's lookahead is **always** `$`. The accept action is **always** on `$` for the item `[S' → S·, $]`.
>
> 5. **Failing to detect self-loops.** If `GOTO(I_i, X) = I_i` (i.e., a state references itself), record it but **do not re-add** it to the collection. The algorithm terminates correctly.
>
> 6. **Writing reduce actions for the start symbol.** `[S' → ·S, $]` cannot appear as a reduce action — only the accept action is valid for it. Putting `r0: S' → S` is a classic error.
>
> 7. **Confusing SLR(1) and LALR(1) reductions.** SLR(1) uses `FOLLOW(A)`; LALR(1) uses the merged lookahead of items in *each individual state*. They coincide only for simple grammars.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — CLR(1) and LALR(1) Construction:**

- [x] **CLR(1) item** = LR(0) item + 1-symbol lookahead terminal.
- [x] **Augmented grammar** `S' → S` is mandatory; initial state always `[S' → ·S, $]`.
- [x] **CLOSURE rule:** for `[A → α·Bβ, a]`, add `[B → ·γ, b]` for **every** `b ∈ FIRST(βa)`.
- [x] **GOTO rule:** advance the dot past `X`, then take CLOSURE.
- [x] **ACTION[i, a] = shift j** when `[A → α·aβ, b] ∈ I_i` and `GOTO(I_i, a) = I_j`.
- [x] **ACTION[i, a] = reduce by A → α** when `[A → α·, a] ∈ I_i` and `A ≠ S'`.
- [x] **ACTION[i, $] = accept** only for item `[S' → S·, $]`.
- [x] **Conflict** (cell with two actions) ⇒ grammar not LR(1).
- [x] **LALR(1)** = CLR(1) with **core-equivalent states merged** and **lookaheads unioned**.
- [x] **LALR state count** = **LR(0) state count** (same as SLR).
- [x] **LALR cannot introduce new shift-reduce conflicts**, but **can introduce new reduce-reduce conflicts** ("spurious" RR conflicts).
- [x] **Industry use:** YACC, GNU Bison, JavaCUP all generate LALR(1) tables.
- [x] **Key trade-off:** CLR(1) = most powerful but huge tables; LALR(1) = nearly as powerful with compact tables; SLR(1) = smallest but weakest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Mantra for KTU 2024:**
> *Build the full CLR(1) collection → Extract cores → Group identical cores → Union lookaheads → Repopulate ACTION/GOTO → Check for spurious conflicts → Done.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Syntax Directed Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT751 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Syntax Directed Definitions # 📘 MODULE 3 — SYNTAX DIRECTED DEFINITIONS (SDD) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):** > A **Syntax Directed Definition (SDD)** is a context-free grammar augmented with a set of **semantic rules**, where each grammar symbol is associated with a collection of **attributes**, and each production is attached with a set of semantic rules that compute the values of attributes of the symbols appearing in that production. In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, an SDD sits at the heart of the **Semantic Analysis phase** of a compiler. While the parser verifies the *syntactic* structure of the source program, it cannot understand *meaning* — for example, whether a variable has been declared, what its type is, or what an arithmetic expression evaluates to. The SDD is the formal mechanism that allows the compiler to **attach meaning to syntax**. ### 🔑 Key Components of an SDD (Examiner's Hot-List) | Component | Description | |-----------|-------------| | **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** | The underlying syntactic backbone (productions like $E \to E + T$). | | **Attributes** | Values associated with each grammar symbol $X$ (denoted as $X.a$, $X.b$, etc.). | | **Semantic Rules** | Equations attached to each production to compute attributes. | | **Production** | A CFG rule of the form $X \to Y_1 Y_2 \dots Y_n$. | | **Terminal / Non-terminal** | Both may carry attributes. | ### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe Card with Side Notes" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine a *recipe* for making Biryani. The recipe (CFG) tells you the **sequence of steps** — boil rice, fry onions, add spices, mix. But a *real chef's recipe card* also has **handwritten notes in the margin** — "rice should be 70% done", "fry till golden", "add 2 spoons of salt". These marginal notes are the **semantic rules** attached to each step. The *ingredients* (rice, onions, spices) are the **grammar symbols**, and their *properties* (amount, temperature, doneness) are the **attributes**. The whole annotated card is a **Syntax Directed Definition**. The parser reads the recipe in order, and as it executes each step, it consults the marginal notes to compute and pass along the values (e.g., total cooking time, spice level). This is *exactly* what an SDD does during compilation: as the parser builds the parse tree, it simultaneously executes the semantic rules to compute attribute values at each node. ### 🎯 Why SDD is Indispensable in Compiler Design - **Type Checking** — Ensures operands of `$+$` are integers or compatible types. - **Intermediate Code Generation** — Computes addresses, temporaries, and three-address code. - **Symbol Table Population** — Stores type, scope, and memory location of identifiers. - **Error Reporting** — Detects semantic errors (e.g., type mismatch, undeclared variable). > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** The examiner expects you to know that an SDD is a **specification** (declarative), not an implementation. The *implementation* is called a **Syntax Directed Translation (SDT)**, which embeds semantic actions directly into the parser. This distinction is a frequently asked 2-mark question. ### 📐 Formal Notation Recap For a production of the form: $$A \to B \, C$$ A typical semantic rule attached might be: $$A.a = f(B.b, C.c)$$ Here, $A.a$, $B.b$, and $C.c$ are **attributes**, and $f$ is a function combining them. The rule says: *compute the attribute `a` of the left-hand non-terminal `A` using attributes `b` and `c` of the right-hand side symbols `B` and `C`.* # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Pillars — Synthesized and Inherited Attributes The single most important theoretical concept in SDD is the **classification of attributes** based on the *direction of information flow* in the parse tree. This determines how, and in what order, attributes can be evaluated. > [!IMPORTANT] > **SYNTHESIZED ATTRIBUTES (Bottom-Up):** > A synthesized attribute of a non-terminal $A$ at a parse-tree node $N$ is defined **solely in terms of attribute values of the children of $N$** (or of $N$ itself). Information flows from **leaves → root** (bottom-up). > > **INHERITED ATTRIBUTES (Top-Down / Lateral):** > An inherited attribute of a non-terminal $B$ at a parse-tree node $N$ is defined **in terms of attribute values of the parent of $N$, the siblings of $N$, or $N$ itself**. Information flows from **root → leaves** (top-down) or **left-sibling → right-sibling** (lateral). ### 🔬 Comparative Analysis Table | Property | **Synthesized Attribute** | **Inherited Attribute** | |---|---|---| | **Symbol convention** | Often denoted $val$, $code$, $type$ | Often denoted $inh$, $scope$, $env$ | | **Information flow** | Bottom-up (children → parent) | Top-down / lateral (parent/sibling → node) | | **Dependency** | Depends *only* on children's attributes | May depend on parent, left-siblings, or self | | **Evaluation order** | Post-order traversal of parse tree | Any order respecting dependencies | | **Ease of evaluation** | Trivial — natural for bottom-up parsers (LR) | Requires care — natural for top-down parsers (LL) | | **KTU classic example** | Computing the value of an arithmetic expression | Tracking the type/scope of a variable during declaration | ### 2.2 S-Attributed Definitions — The Bottom-Up Special Case > [!NOTE] > An **S-attributed definition** is an SDD that uses **only synthesized attributes**. Every semantic rule computes an attribute of the **left-hand side** non-terminal from attributes of the **right-hand side** symbols. **Why it matters:** S-attributed SDDs are evaluated during a **post-order (bottom-up) traversal** of the parse tree, which corresponds **exactly** to the sequence in which a shift-reduce (LR) parser reduces a production. This means an S-attributed SDD can be **implemented directly on an LR parser stack** with no additional machinery — a huge practical advantage. ### 2.3 L-Attributed Definitions — The Top-Down Generalization > [!IMPORTANT] > An **L-attributed definition** is an SDD in which, for each production $A \to X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$, the **inherited attribute of $X_i$** depends only on: > 1. The **inherited attributes of $A$** (parent). > 2. The **attributes (inherited or synthesized) of $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_{i-1}$** (left siblings). > 3. **Any attributes of $X_i$ itself** (self-dependence, permitted in some definitions). > > *Note: KTU often uses the slightly stricter version where dependence on $X_i$'s own other attributes is excluded — both are accepted in standard textbooks.* **L-attributed SDDs can be evaluated by a left-to-right depth-first traversal** of the parse tree, matching naturally with **LL (top-down) parsers**. ### 2.4 Dependency Graphs and Evaluation Order > [!NOTE] > A **dependency graph** has: > - One **node** for each attribute of each parse-tree node. > - One **directed edge** $b \to c$ if the semantic rule for attribute $c$ uses the value of attribute $b$. > > A **valid topological sort** of the dependency graph gives a legal evaluation order. The **KTU Theorem** (high-yield): > *An SDD is well-defined (has a unique attribute value at every node) if and only if there exists at least one topological sort of the dependency graph for every parse tree — equivalently, the graph is acyclic.* ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!TIP] > The following table is the **master cheat-sheet** for SDD problems in KTU ESE. Memorize it. | **Concept** | **Symbolic Form / Rule** | **Direction of Flow** | **Evaluator** | |---|---|---|---| | Synthesized attribute of $A$ | $A.a = f(\text{children's attrs})$ | Bottom-up | Post-order DFS | | Inherited attribute of $X_i$ | $X_i.inh = g(A.inh, X_1.\text{attrs}, \dots, X_{i-1}.\text{attrs})$ | Top-down / lateral | Any topological order | | S-attributed SDD | Only synthesized attrs used | Pure bottom-up | LR parser stack (one synth attr per non-terminal) | | L-attributed SDD | Inherited attrs depend only on parent's or left-siblings' attrs | Top-down + bottom-up | LL parser, DFS, or recursive descent | | Dependency edge | $X_i.b \to X_j.c$ if $X_j.c$ uses $X_i.b$ | Edge in dep graph | Topological sort | | Well-defined SDD | Dependency graph is **acyclic** (DAG) for **every** parse tree | — | Required for compilation | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of SDD | **Application** | **Attribute Computed** | **Phase of Compiler** | |---|---|---| | Expression evaluation | $E.val$ (numeric value) | Constant folding / optimization | | Type checking | $E.type$ (int, float, etc.) | Semantic analysis | | Three-address code gen | $E.code$ (TAC string) | Intermediate code gen | | Symbol table lookup | $id.entry$ (pointer to table) | Front-end analysis | | Address generation | $E.addr$ (memory location of result) | Back-end / code emission | | Scope analysis | $id.scope$ (lexical depth) | Semantic analysis | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is to **confuse the SDD with the SDT**. An SDD is a *specification* — it does NOT prescribe *when* rules are evaluated; it only states *what* is computed. An SDT (Syntax Directed Translation) is an *implementation* that specifies the order. A given SDD can be implemented by *several* SDTs, but not every SDD can be evaluated in every order — only those consistent with a topological sort of the dependency graph. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Building an Annotated Parse Tree (Synthesized) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** Given the SDD below, construct the annotated parse tree for the input **`5 + 3 * 2`** and compute all attributes. **SDD Rules (S-attributed):** $$E \to E_1 + T \quad \Rightarrow \quad E.val = E_1.val + T.val$$ $$E \to T \quad \Rightarrow \quad E.val = T.val$$ $$T \to T_1 * F \quad \Rightarrow \quad T.val = T_1.val \times F.val$$ $$T \to F \quad \Rightarrow \quad T.val = F.val$$ $$F \to \text{digit} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F.val = \text{digit.lexval}$$ **Step 1 — Draw the (unannotated) parse tree for `5 + 3 * 2`:** The grammar enforces standard arithmetic precedence (multiplication before addition). The parse tree has the structure: ``` E / \ E + /| | E T T | \ / \ T F T | | |\ F 5 F * | | | digit digit 3 digit 2 5 ``` *(Rendered cleanly with the proper shape)* **Step 2 — Annotate bottom-up (post-order):** We evaluate leaves first, then propagate upward. **Leaves (digit productions):** - $F \to \text{digit}$ where digit = `5` → $F.val = 5$ - $F \to \text{digit}$ where digit = `3` → $F.val = 3$ - $F \to \text{digit}$ where digit = `2` → $F.val = 2$ **Level 1 (T from F):** Using $T \to F$: $T.val = F.val$ - The $T$ above digit `5`: $T.val = 5$ - The $T$ above digit `3`: $T.val = 3$ - The $T$ above digit `2`: $T.val = 2$ **Level 2 (Multiplication):** Using $T \to T_1 * F$: $T.val = T_1.val \times F.val$ - Inner $T$ (covering `3 * 2`): $$T.val = 3 \times 2 = 6$$ **Level 3 (Left E from T):** Using $E \to T$: $E.val = T.val$ - The $E$ above the inner $T$: $E.val = 6$ **Level 4 (Addition):** Using $E \to E_1 + T$: $E.val = E_1.val + T.val$ - Root $E$ (covering `5 + (3*2)`): $$E.val = 5 + 6 = 11$$ **Final Annotated Result:** $$E.val = 11, \quad E_1.val = 5, \quad T.val_{inner} = 6, \quad T.val_{left} = 5, \quad F_1.val = 5, \quad F_2.val = 3, \quad F_3.val = 2$$ This bottom-up flow is **typical of S-attributed definitions** and matches a post-order DFS of the parse tree. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — L-Attributed Definition (Inherited Attribute) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** Consider the following L-attributed SDD for **type declaration statements** in a Pascal-like language. The inherited attribute `dtype` carries the declared type down the tree, and `id.entry` stores the symbol-table pointer. **SDD Rules (L-attributed):** $$D \to T \, L \quad \Rightarrow \quad L.inh = T.type$$ $$T \to \text{int} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T.type = \text{integer}$$ $$T \to \text{real} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T.type = \text{real}$$ $$L \to L_1, \, id \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_1.inh = L.inh, \quad id.entry = \text{addtype}(id.name, L.inh)$$ $$L \to id \quad \Rightarrow \quad id.entry = \text{addtype}(id.name, L.inh)$$ **Input:** `real x, y, z` **Step 1 — Parse Tree Shape:** ``` D / \ T L | /|\ real L , id /|\ | L , id z | | id y | x ``` **Step 2 — Compute attributes following the L-attributed order (DFS left-to-right, pre-order style for inherited, post-order for synthesized).** **Rule $T \to \text{real}$:** $$T.type = \text{real}$$ **Rule $D \to T \, L$:** $$L.inh = T.type = \text{real}$$ **Rule $L \to L_1, \, id$ (outermost list, with `id` = `z`):** $$L_1.inh = L.inh = \text{real}$$ $$z.entry = \text{addtype}(z, \text{real}) \rightarrow \text{Symbol table: } (z, \text{real})$$ **Rule $L \to L_1, \, id$ (middle list, with `id` = `y`):** $$L_1.inh = L.inh = \text{real}$$ $$y.entry = \text{addtype}(y, \text{real}) \rightarrow \text{Symbol table: } (y, \text{real})$$ **Rule $L \to id$ (innermost, with `id` = `x`):** $$x.entry = \text{addtype}(x, \text{real}) \rightarrow \text{Symbol table: } (x, \text{real})$$ **Final Symbol Table Output:** | Identifier | Type | |---|---| | $x$ | real | | $y$ | real | | $z$ | real | **Verification of L-Attributed Property:** For production $L \to L_1, \, id$, the inherited attribute $L_1.inh$ depends only on $L.inh$ (its left-sibling's parent / its own parent's side). This satisfies the L-attribute definition constraint — *no right-sibling dependency exists*. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Dependency Graph Construction > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** Construct the dependency graph for the SDD given below, for the input string `aabb`. **SDD:** $$S \to a \, S_1 \, A \quad \Rightarrow \quad A.inh = S.inh, \quad S.syn = S_1.syn \oplus A.syn$$ $$A \to b \, B \quad \Rightarrow \quad B.inh = A.inh + 1, \quad A.syn = B.syn$$ $$A \to b \quad \Rightarrow \quad A.syn = A.inh \times 2$$ $$B \to b \quad \Rightarrow \quad B.syn = B.inh$$ *(Here $\oplus$ denotes concatenation, and we use a simple counting system.)* **Annotation Walkthrough:** - Start: $S.inh = 0$ (initial, root attribute, often given). - Apply $S \to a S_1 A$: $A.inh = 0$, $S.syn = S_1.syn \oplus A.syn$. - Apply $A \to b B$: $B.inh = 0 + 1 = 1$, $A.syn = B.syn$. - Apply $B \to b$: $B.syn = 1$. **Edges in Dependency Graph:** $$S.inh \to A.inh \quad (\text{via } A.inh = S.inh)$$ $$A.inh \to B.inh \quad (\text{via } B.inh = A.inh + 1)$$ $$B.inh \to B.syn \quad (\text{via } B.syn = B.inh)$$ Topological order: $S.inh \to A.inh \to B.inh \to B.syn \to A.syn \to S.syn$. ✓ Acyclic — well-defined. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "How to Approach an SDD Problem" > [!TIP] > When given an SDD in a KTU exam, follow this decision matrix: | **If you see...** | **Then classify as...** | **Evaluation strategy** | |---|---|---| | All rules have the form $A.a = f(\text{children's attrs})$ | **S-attributed** | Post-order DFS / bottom-up; fits LR parser | | An inherited attribute depends on parent or left-sibling | **L-attributed** | DFS left-to-right (pre-order for inh, post for synth); fits LL parser | | An inherited attribute depends on a **right-sibling** | **NOT L-attributed** | Requires separate evaluation / attribute grammar translation | | Rule has circular dependency ($A.a = f(A.a, \dots)$) | **Not well-defined** | Need to eliminate via translation; may not be evaluable | | Asked to "draw annotated parse tree" | Compute synth bottom-up, then inh top-down (if any) | Label every node with its attribute values | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SDD — Conceptual Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD CFG["Context-Free Grammar
Productions"]:::core Attrs["Attribute Set
X.a, X.b, X.type, X.val, X.code"]:::core SemRules["Semantic Rules
e.g., A.a = f(children's attrs)"]:::core SDD["SYNTAX DIRECTED DEFINITION
Combined Specification"]:::main CFG --> SDD Attrs --> SDD SemRules --> SDD SDD --> ParseTree["Annotated Parse Tree
Each node holds attribute values"]:::out SDD --> DepGraph["Dependency Graph
Directed acyclic edges"]:::out SDD --> TopoSort["Topological Evaluation Order"]:::out ParseTree --> SynthEval["Synthesized: bottom-up
post-order DFS"]:::route ParseTree --> InhEval["Inherited: top-down/lateral
any topo-sorted order"]:::route DepGraph --> TopoSort TopoSort --> CodeGen["Intermediate Code /
Type Info / Symbol Table"]:::out classDef core fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350f classDef main fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:3px,color:#1e3a8a classDef out fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:2px,color:#064e3b classDef route fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843 ``` ## 4.2 Attribute Classification Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR Attr["Attribute X.a
of grammar symbol X"]:::node Q1{"Defined using only
children's attributes?"}:::q Q2{"Depends on parent or
left siblings?"}:::q Synth["SYNTHESIZED ATTRIBUTE
Bottom-up flow"]:::syn Inh["INHERITED ATTRIBUTE
Top-down / lateral flow"]:::inh SATD["S-attributed SDD
Only synthesized attrs"]:::satd LATD["L-attributed SDD
Inh depends on parent/
left-siblings only"]:::latd NonL["Non-L-attributed SDD
Requires special handling"]:::nonl Attr --> Q1 Q1 -- "YES" --> Synth Q1 -- "NO" --> Q2 Q2 -- "YES" --> Inh Q2 -- "NO" --> NonL Synth --> SATD Inh --> LATD classDef node fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca,stroke-width:2px,color:#312e81 classDef q fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:2px,color:#713f12 classDef syn fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532d classDef inh fill:#ffe4e6,stroke:#be123c,stroke-width:2px,color:#881337 classDef satd fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532d classDef latd fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843 classDef nonl fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#7f1d1d ``` ## 4.3 SDD Evaluation Pipeline (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Step 1: Parse Input
Build parse tree using CFG"]:::step S2["Step 2: Attach Attributes
Annotate every tree node with attr slots"]:::step S3["Step 3: Write Semantic Rules
One per production"]:::step S4["Step 4: Build Dependency Graph
Edge X.b to Y.c if rule uses X.b for Y.c"]:::step S5["Step 5: Topological Sort
Produce linear evaluation order"]:::step S6["Step 6: Evaluate Attributes
Apply rules in sorted order"]:::step S7["Step 7: Output / Use Results
Type table / TAC / Symbol table"]:::step S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 classDef step fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#1e3a8a ``` ## 4.4 Attribute Information Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph "Synthesized (Bottom-Up)" direction BT leaf1["F.val = 5"]:::leaf leaf2["F.val = 3"]:::leaf leaf3["F.val = 2"]:::leaf t1["T.val = 5"]:::mid t2["T.val = 3"]:::mid t3["T.val = 2"]:::mid tmult["T.val = 6"]:::mid e1["E.val = 6"]:::high eroot["E.val = 11"]:::high end leaf1 --> t1 leaf2 --> t2 leaf3 --> t3 t2 --> tmult t3 --> tmult t1 --> e1 tmult --> e1 e1 --> eroot classDef leaf fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:2px,color:#713f12 classDef mid fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#c2410c,stroke-width:2px,color:#7c2d12 classDef high fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532d ``` ## 4.5 Compiler Phase Placement ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Source Code] --> B[Lexical Analysis] --> C[Syntax Analysis
Parser] --> D["Semantic Analysis
SDD executes here"]:::sd --> E[Intermediate Code Gen] --> F[Optimization] --> G[Target Code] --> H[Executable] C -.Builds.-> T1[Parse Tree] D -.Annotates.-> T2[Annotated Parse Tree] T1 --> T2 T2 -.Feeds.-> E classDef sd fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:3px,color:#1e3a8a ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** > **Q1.** Define **Syntax Directed Definition (SDD)**. Differentiate between **synthesized** and **inherited** attributes with a suitable example. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A *Syntax Directed Definition* (SDD) is a context-free grammar $G = (N, T, P, S)$ augmented with: - A set of **attributes** $A(X)$ for each grammar symbol $X$. - For each production $p \in P$, a set of **semantic rules** that define certain attributes of the symbols in $p$ in terms of other attributes. **Difference:** | Aspect | **Synthesized** | **Inherited** | |---|---|---| | **Computation** | $A.a = f(\text{children's attributes})$ | $B.inh = g(\text{parent, left-siblings, self})$ | | **Direction** | Bottom-up (post-order) | Top-down / lateral | | **Example** | $E.val = E_1.val + T.val$ | $L.inh = T.type$ (type passed down declaration list) | *(Mark split: 1 mark definition + 2 marks difference table/example.)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > **Q2.** What is an **S-attributed definition**? Why is it preferred for **LR parsing**? **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **S-attributed definition** is a special case of SDD in which **only synthesized attributes** are used — every semantic rule computes an attribute of the **left-hand side** non-terminal from attributes of the **right-hand side** symbols. **Why preferred for LR parsing (2 marks):** 1. The **actions** of a bottom-up LR parser execute at the moment of **reduction**, in a strict **post-order** sequence. 2. An S-attributed SDD computes values in **exactly this same post-order** when reducing. 3. Therefore, the SDD can be implemented **directly on the parser stack** — each stack entry for a non-terminal holds its synthesized attribute, and the reduction pops the children's values, applies the rule, and pushes the parent's new value. No extra data structures are needed. --- ## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice) --- ### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Full Module 3 Coverage **[KTU University Exam — Sample Pattern, ESE May 2024]** > **Q3 (a)** [7 Marks] For the following S-attributed **SDD** that evaluates arithmetic expressions involving `+` and `*`: > > $$E \to E_1 + T \quad \Rightarrow \quad E.val = E_1.val + T.val$$ > $$E \to T \quad \Rightarrow \quad E.val = T.val$$ > $$T \to T_1 * F \quad \Rightarrow \quad T.val = T_1.val \times F.val$$ > $$T \to F \quad \Rightarrow \quad T.val = F.val$$ > $$F \to (E) \quad \Rightarrow \quad F.val = E.val$$ > $$F \to \text{digit} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F.val = \text{digit.lexval}$$ > > **(i)** Construct the **annotated parse tree** for the input string **`(4 + 5) * 3`**. > **(ii)** Show the **post-order evaluation sequence** of all attribute computations. > **(iii)** State whether the SDD is **S-attributed** or **L-attributed** and justify. **Model Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Parse Tree Construction (3 marks):** Apply standard grammar parsing — the input ` (4+5)*3 ` is tokenized as `(`, `4`, `+`, `5`, `)`, `*`, `3`. Following operator precedence, `*` is the root operation, left operand is `(4+5)` (an F subtree), right operand is `3` (a digit → F → T). Tree structure (top-down): ``` E | T / \ T F | | F digit | 3 (E) /|\ E + T | | T F | | F digit | 5 digit 4 ``` **(ii) Annotated Evaluation — Post-Order (3 marks):** Step 1 (leaves): $F.val = 4$, $F.val = 5$, $F.val = 3$ Step 2 ($T \to F$): $T.val = 4$, $T.val = 5$, $T.val = 3$ Step 3 ($E \to E_1 + T$): $E.val = 4 + 5 = 9$ Step 4 ($F \to (E)$): $F.val = 9$ Step 5 ($T \to T_1 * F$): $T.val = 9 \times 3 = 27$ Step 6 ($E \to T$): $E.val = 27$ **Final answer: $E.val = 27$** **(iii) Classification (1 mark):** The SDD is **S-attributed** because: - All attributes (`val`) are **synthesized** — every rule computes the attribute of the **left-hand side** from attributes of the **right-hand side**. - No inherited attributes are present. - This also automatically makes it L-attributed (since S-attributed ⊂ L-attributed). --- > **Q3 (b)** [7 Marks] Consider the following **L-attributed SDD** for a simple **variable declaration**: > > $$D \to TL$$ > $$T \to \text{int} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T.type = \text{integer}$$ > $$T \to \text{float} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T.type = \text{float}$$ > $$L \to L_1, id \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_1.inh = L.inh, \; \text{addtype}(id.name, L.inh)$$ > $$L \to id \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{addtype}(id.name, L.inh)$$ > > **(i)** Construct the annotated parse tree for the input **`float a, b, c`**. > **(ii)** Show the **dependency graph** at the root production. > **(iii)** State why this SDD is **L-attributed** but **not S-attributed**. **Model Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Annotated Parse Tree (3 marks):** ``` D / \ T L | /|\ float L , id /|\ | L , id c | | id b | id a ``` **Attribute values:** - $T.type = \text{float}$ - $L.inh = \text{float}$ (at root L, by rule $D \to TL$) - $L_1.inh = L.inh = \text{float}$ (for outer list, with $id = c$) - $L_1.inh = L.inh = \text{float}$ (for middle list, with $id = b$) - $id = a$: base case $L \to id$, with $L.inh = \text{float}$ **Symbol table after evaluation:** `a → float`, `b → float`, `c → float`. **(ii) Dependency Graph (2 marks):** Edges (for outermost $L \to L_1, id$): - $L.inh \to L_1.inh$ - $L.inh \to \text{addtype}$ (via the rule for $id$) Edges (for $D \to TL$): - $T.type \to L.inh$ The graph is a **DAG** — no cycles — so the SDD is **well-defined**. **(iii) Classification Justification (2 marks):** - **L-attributed:** For production $L \to L_1, id$, the inherited attribute $L_1.inh$ depends only on $L.inh$ (which is either the parent's inherited attribute or the left-sibling's chain). It **never** depends on any right-sibling's attribute. Hence, it satisfies the L-attributed definition. - **Not S-attributed:** The attribute $L.inh$ is **inherited**, not synthesized. S-attributed definitions **prohibit** the use of any inherited attribute. Since $L.inh$ is essential for passing the type down, this SDD cannot be S-attributed. --- ### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Full Module 3 Coverage **[KTU University Exam — Sample Pattern, ESE Dec 2023]** > **Q4 (a)** [7 Marks] Define **dependency graph** for an SDD. For the following SDD, **draw the dependency graph** for the input string **`abc`**, and state whether the SDD is **well-defined**: > > $$S \to aS \quad \Rightarrow \quad S.syn = 1$$ > $$S \to aB \quad \Rightarrow \quad S.syn = 2$$ > $$B \to bC \quad \Rightarrow \quad B.inh = 1, \; C.syn = 2$$ > $$C \to c \quad \Rightarrow \quad C.syn = 3$$ **Model Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Dependency Graph Definition (2 marks):** A *dependency graph* is a directed graph where: - Each **node** represents an **attribute instance** of a parse-tree node. - A **directed edge** $X.b \to Y.c$ exists if and only if the semantic rule computing $Y.c$ **uses** the value of $X.b$. - A **topological sort** of the graph yields a valid evaluation order. - The SDD is *well-defined* if the graph is **acyclic** for **every** valid parse tree. **(ii) Parse Tree for `abc` (2 marks):** The input `abc` has two possible derivations. Let's pick: - $S \to aB$ (using the second rule) - $B \to bC$ - $C \to c$ ``` S /|\ a B /|\ b C | c ``` **(iii) Dependency Graph Construction (2 marks):** Attribute nodes and edges: - $B.inh = 1$ → no incoming edge (constant). - $B.inh \to C.syn$? **No** — the rule $C \to c$ defines $C.syn = 3$, independent of $B.inh$. - $S.syn = 2$ → no dependency on $B$ or $C$ (rule just gives constant 2). So the nodes are: $\{B.inh, C.syn, S.syn\}$ and the edges are: **none** (all rules produce constants or independent values). **Graph is acyclic → SDD is well-defined for this parse tree.** ✓ **(iv) General Well-Definedness Check (1 mark):** Since the SDD contains no rule of the form $X.a = f(X.a, \dots)$ and no circular references, the dependency graph is acyclic for **any** parse tree. The SDD is well-defined in general. --- > **Q4 (b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **classification of attributes** in SDD. For the SDD below, identify each attribute as **synthesized** or **inherited**, and state whether the SDD is **S-attributed**, **L-attributed**, or **neither**: > > $$A \to BC$$ > $$A.s = B.b + C.c \quad \text{(Rule 1)}$$ > $$B.i = A.s \times 2 \quad \text{(Rule 2)}$$ > $$C.s = B.b - 1 \quad \text{(Rule 3)}$$ **Model Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Attribute Classification Theory (2 marks):** - **Synthesized attribute** of non-terminal $X$: computed from attributes of its **children** (or itself). Bottom-up flow. - **Inherited attribute** of non-terminal $X$: computed from attributes of its **parent** and/or **left-siblings** (or itself). Top-down or lateral flow. **(ii) Identification for the Given SDD (3 marks):** | Attribute | Defined By | Depends On | Classification | |---|---|---|---| | $A.s$ | Rule 1 | $B.b, C.c$ (children) | **Synthesized** | | $B.i$ | Rule 2 | $A.s$ (parent) | **Inherited** | | $C.s$ | Rule 3 | $B.b$ (left-sibling) | **Synthesized** | **Justification:** - $A.s$ depends only on the children's attributes $B.b$ and $C.c$ → **synthesized**. - $B.i$ depends on the parent's attribute $A.s$ → **inherited**. - $C.s$ depends on the left-sibling's attribute $B.b$ → **synthesized** (synthesized can take from siblings too; the key is it's not an inherited attribute of $C$). **(iii) SDD Classification (2 marks):** - **Not S-attributed** — because $B.i$ is an inherited attribute. - **L-attributed** — let's verify. In production $A \to BC$: - $B.i$ (inherited) depends on $A.s$ (parent's attribute) ✓ — allowed in L-attributed. - $A.s$ (synthesized) depends on $B.b, C.c$ ✓ — synthesized always allowed. - $C.s$ (synthesized) depends on $B.b$ (left-sibling) ✓ — synthesized always allowed. - All inherited attribute dependencies in the SDD depend only on **parent's attributes** (and not right-sibling). Therefore, the SDD is **L-attributed**. --- > [!WARNING] > ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > > 1. **Confusing "synthesized" with "left-sibling dependency":** Synthesized attributes **can** use left-sibling attributes (e.g., $C.s = B.b - 1$). Students wrongly mark $C.s$ as inherited. The classification is about *where the attribute is defined*, not *who provides its value*. > > 2. **Assuming S-attributed ⊂ L-attributed (true) and that L ⊂ S is also true (FALSE):** Many L-attributed SDDs are NOT S-attributed. Always check for the presence of any inherited attribute. > > 3. **Forgetting to compute root's initial attribute:** Many KTU questions assume a specific initial value for the root's inherited attribute (e.g., $S.inh = 0$). Missing this gives 0 marks on the entire problem. > > 4. **Drawing parse trees upside-down:** KTU examiners deduct 1 mark consistently for parse trees with the root at the bottom. Always put the root on **top**. > > 5. **Skipping topological sort justification:** A dependency graph alone is **not enough** for a 7-mark question — you **must** show the topological order and state that the graph is acyclic for full credit. > > 6. **Mixing up SDD and SDT in definitions:** "SDD is a specification" vs. "SDT is an implementation" — a 1-marker casualty every year. > > 7. **Forgetting the `addtype()` side-effect:** In declaration-list SDDs, the `addtype` call updates the symbol table. Students often write only $id.entry = \dots$ but omit that the **symbol table** is the actual output. --- ## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!TIP] > **Final High-Density Summary — Print & Pin This!** - 🔹 An **SDD** = CFG + **attributes** + **semantic rules**. - 🔹 **Synthesized attribute** = defined from **children**; evaluated **bottom-up (post-order)**. - 🔹 **Inherited attribute** = defined from **parent / left-sibling**; evaluated **top-down or lateral**. - 🔹 **S-attributed SDD** uses **only** synthesized attributes; trivially evaluated by an **LR parser** during reduction. - 🔹 **L-attributed SDD** allows inherited attributes **only** when they depend on **parent's** or **left-siblings'** attributes; evaluable by a **left-to-right DFS** (LL-friendly). - 🔹 **Dependency graph** = DAG of attribute nodes with edges following semantic-rule uses. - 🔹 An SDD is **well-defined** ⟺ dependency graph is **acyclic for every parse tree**. - 🔹 **Topological sort** of the dependency graph gives a **legal evaluation order**. - 🔹 **SDD vs. SDT**: SDD = declarative *specification*; SDT = imperative *implementation* with embedded actions. - 🔹 **Every S-attributed SDD is L-attributed**, but not vice versa. - 🔹 Non-L-attributed SDDs (those with **right-sibling** inherited dependencies) require **translation** into L-attributed form for top-down parsing. - 🔹 **Classic applications**: type checking, intermediate code generation, symbol table population, address assignment, scope analysis. - 🔹 **KTU-favorite productions**: $E \to E + T$ (synthesized $val$), $D \to T\,L$ (inherited $type$), $L \to L, id$ (inherited $inh$ chain). - 🔹 **Exam mantra**: *Identify attribute type → write rule in standard form → annotate tree post-order (synth) or pre-order (inh) → state SDD class → verify acyclicity.*

topics.title: Bottom-up Evaluation of S-Attributed Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bottom-up Evaluation of S-Attributed Definitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD)** is called **S-attributed** if every attribute in it is a *synthesized* attribute. Synthesized attributes are those whose values are computed solely from the attribute values of the **children** (or constant attributes on terminals) of the node in the parse tree. In other words, an SDD is S-attributed if and only if it contains **no inherited attributes**.

A bottom-up parser can evaluate any S-attributed definition **on the fly** during reductions, provided the semantic actions are placed at the **right end** (postfix position) of each production. This is because:

- Bottom-up parsing reduces the children of a node *before* the parent.
- Therefore, when the parent is reduced, all synthesized attributes of its children have already been computed.
- The action placed at the end of the production is fired exactly at this moment, computing the parent's attribute and making it available for further reductions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** An S-attributed definition can always be implemented in a bottom-up, shift-reduce parser by maintaining a parallel semantic stack (often called the **val stack**) that mirrors the parse stack and stores synthesized attribute values.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Postfix Calculator

Imagine a stack-based postfix (Reverse Polish) calculator sitting beside a stack of wooden blocks being assembled:

- Each block (terminal or nonterminal) has a **number stamped on it**.
- When the builder picks up children blocks and glues them into a parent block, the parent block's number is computed **only from numbers already stamped on the children**.
- The calculator (val stack) mirrors the block stack. Every time a new block is added, its number is pushed. Every time a composite block is glued together (a reduction), the calculator pops the children's numbers, performs the production's formula, and pushes the parent's number.

Because the children's numbers are *always* available right at the moment of gluing (reduction), no information is lost. The numbers flow strictly **upward** in the parse tree — this is precisely the meaning of "synthesized."

> [!NOTE]
> **Key terminology to memorize for KTU:**
> - **S-attributed SDD** – all attributes synthesized; data flows bottom-up.
> - **Bottom-up evaluation** – performed during LR shift-reduce parsing via a val stack.
> - **Translation scheme** – SDD rewritten with semantic actions placed inside the production body.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Steps — How Bottom-Up Evaluation Works

The bottom-up evaluator maintains two parallel structures during parsing:

1. **Parse Stack** – holds grammar symbols (terminals and nonterminals).
2. **Value (val) Stack** – holds the synthesized attribute value associated with each symbol on the parse stack.

The evaluation loop proceeds as follows:

- **Shift step:** When a terminal `t` with attribute `t.attr` is shifted onto the parse stack, the value `t.attr` is simultaneously pushed onto the val stack.
- **Reduce step:** When a production `A → X₁ X₂ … Xₙ` is used for reduction:
  - The top `n` entries of the val stack (corresponding to `X₁ … Xₙ`) are accessed as `$1, $2, …, $n`.
  - The semantic action computes `$$` (the value of `A`).
  - The top `n` entries are popped and replaced by the single value `$$`.
  - Simultaneously, the top `n` symbols on the parse stack are replaced by `A`.
- **Accept step:** At the end of parsing, the value left on top of the val stack is the attribute of the start symbol (e.g., the value of the expression, or the type of the program).

## 2.2 The Postfix Action Rule

A translation scheme implements an S-attributed SDD in bottom-up parsing **iff** all semantic actions appear at the *rightmost position* of each production. Actions embedded *inside* the RHS cannot be executed at the right moment in a shift-reduce parser without additional machinery.

If an S-attributed SDD has an action that depends on the values of symbols appearing *before* a yet-to-be-reduced symbol, we must restructure the SDD:

- Introduce a fresh **marker nonterminal** `M` between the symbols.
- Give `M` a synthesized attribute equal to the value we wish to "remember."
- Replace the action by a second production `M → ε` whose action copies the inherited value into a synthesized one.

This transformation converts an otherwise L-attributed construction into one implementable in a bottom-up parser.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why postfix placement?** In a shift-reduce parser, the parser never has direct access to a symbol *inside* the RHS until it has already shifted every symbol to its right. Hence, only the rightmost position guarantees that *all* RHS symbols have their values on the val stack at the moment of reduction.

## 2.3 The yacc Convention

The widely-used parser generator `yacc` adopts the following convention for the right-hand side of a production:

| Symbol in Production | Semantic Reference | Meaning |
| :-- | :-- | :-- |
| `$$` | Left-hand side nonterminal | The synthesized attribute being computed |
| `$1` | First symbol on RHS | Value of `X₁` |
| `$2` | Second symbol on RHS | Value of `X₂` |
| `$n` | n-th symbol on RHS | Value of `Xₙ` |

The default action `{ $$ = $1; }` is automatically inserted when a production has no explicit action — useful for pass-through rules such as `T → F`.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Reference Table

| Concept | Mathematical / Logical Form | Used For |
| :-- | :-- | :-- |
| S-attributed condition | $\forall A.a,\; A.a \text{ is synthesized}$ | Identifying whether an SDD is S-attributed |
| Value stack update on shift | $\text{val\_stack.push}(t.\text{attr})$ | When terminal `t` is shifted |
| Value stack update on reduce | $\text{val\_stack}[ \text{top} - n + 1 ] = f(\$1,\$2,\dots,\$n)$ | When `A → X₁ … Xₙ` is reduced |
| Postfix action requirement | $\text{action at index} = \lvert \text{RHS} \rvert$ | Direct bottom-up implementation |
| Marker nonterminal synthesis | $M.syn = M.inh$ (via $M \rightarrow \varepsilon$) | Preserving values for non-postfix actions |
| Result location after parsing | $\text{val\_stack.top()}$ | Final attribute of the start symbol |
| Reduction count relation | $\text{shifts} + \text{reductions} = \lvert \text{tokens} \rvert + \text{productions used}$ | Sanity-checking parser traces |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical bar notation $\lvert \text{RHS} \rvert$ denotes the cardinality (number of symbols) on the right-hand side — avoid using plain $\vert$ inside markdown tables to preserve table rendering.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bottom-up evaluation of S-attributed definitions underpins virtually every production compiler front-end:

- **Expression evaluation** in interpreters and JIT compilers (arithmetic, boolean, bitwise).
- **Type checking** of declarations and expressions (the type is a synthesized attribute).
- **Intermediate code generation** for stack machines (three-address code, P-code).
- **Constant folding** and **strength reduction** optimization passes.
- **Symbol-table construction** for languages with block-free scopes.
- **Pretty-printing** and **syntax highlighting** tools that rely on AST metadata computed bottom-up.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Evaluating `3 * 5 + 4`

### Grammar (S-attributed SDD)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\rightarrow E + T \quad \{ E.val = E_1.val + T.val \} \\
E &\rightarrow T \quad\quad\;\; \{ E.val = T.val \} \\
T &\rightarrow T * F \quad \{ T.val = T_1.val * F.val \} \\
T &\rightarrow F \quad\quad\;\; \{ T.val = F.val \} \\
F &\rightarrow ( E ) \quad\; \{ F.val = E.val \} \\
F &\rightarrow \textbf{id} \quad \{ F.val = \textbf{id}.lexval \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Input

The token stream with lexical values:

$$
\textbf{id}_1 \, * \, \textbf{id}_2 \, + \, \textbf{id}_3 \quad\text{where}\quad \textbf{id}_1.lexval = 3,\; \textbf{id}_2.lexval = 5,\; \textbf{id}_3.lexval = 4
$$

### Stack Trace

The two parallel stacks evolve as follows during a bottom-up (LR) parse. The **left column** is the parse stack (top on the right); the **right column** is the val stack (top on the right).

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1: Initial state} \\
&\text{Parse:} \; \$ \\
&\text{Val:} \quad\;\; \$ \\
\\
&\textbf{Step 2: Shift } \textbf{id}_1 \text{ (lexval 3)} \\
&\text{Parse:} \; \$ \; \textbf{id} \\
&\text{Val:} \quad\;\; \$ \; 3 \\
\\
&\textbf{Step 3: Reduce } F \rightarrow \textbf{id} \quad (F.val = \textbf{id}.lexval = 3) \\
&\text{Parse:} \; \$ \; F \\
&\text{Val:} \quad\;\; \$ \; 3 \\
\\
&\textbf{Step 4: Reduce } T \rightarrow F \quad (T.val = F.val = 3) \\
&\text{Parse:} \; \$ \; T \\
&\text{Val:} \quad\;\; \$ \; 3 \\
\\
&\textbf{Step 5: Shift } * \\
&\text{Parse:} \; \$ \; T \; * \\
&\text{Val:} \quad\;\; \$ \; 3 \; - \\
\\
&\textbf{Step 6: Shift } \textbf{id}_2 \text{ (lexval 5)} \\
&\text{Parse:} \; \$ \; T \; * \; \textbf{id} \\
&\text{Val:} \quad\;\; \$ \; 3 \; - \; 5 \\
\\
&\textbf{Step 7: Reduce } F \rightarrow \textbf{id} \quad (F.val = 5) \\
&\text{Parse:} \; \$ \; T \; * \; F \\
&\text{Val:} \quad\;\; \$ \; 3 \; - \; 5 \\
\\
&\textbf{Step 8: Reduce } T \rightarrow T * F \quad (T.val = 3 * 5 = 15) \\
&\text{Parse:} \; \$ \; T \\
&\text{Val:} \quad\;\; \$ \; 15 \\
\\
&\textbf{Step 9: Reduce } E \rightarrow T \quad (E.val = 15) \\
&\text{Parse:} \; \$ \; E \\
&\text{Val:} \quad\;\; \$ \; 15 \\
\\
&\textbf{Step 10: Shift } + \\
&\text{Parse:} \; \$ \; E \; + \\
&\text{Val:} \quad\;\; \$ \; 15 \; - \\
\\
&\textbf{Step 11: Shift } \textbf{id}_3 \text{ (lexval 4)} \\
&\text{Parse:} \; \$ \; E \; + \; \textbf{id} \\
&\text{Val:} \quad\;\; \$ \; 15 \; - \; 4 \\
\\
&\textbf{Step 12: Reduce } F \rightarrow \textbf{id} \quad (F.val = 4) \\
&\text{Parse:} \; \$ \; E \; + \; F \\
&\text{Val:} \quad\;\; \$ \; 15 \; - \; 4 \\
\\
&\textbf{Step 13: Reduce } T \rightarrow F \quad (T.val = 4) \\
&\text{Parse:} \; \$ \; E \; + \; T \\
&\text{Val:} \quad\;\; \$ \; 15 \; - \; 4 \\
\\
&\textbf{Step 14: Reduce } E \rightarrow E + T \quad (E.val = 15 + 4 = 19) \\
&\text{Parse:} \; \$ \; E \\
&\text{Val:} \quad\;\; \$ \; 19 \\
\\
&\textbf{Step 15: Accept — final result is } 19
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer Extraction

$$
E.val = 19 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 3 * 5 + 4 = 19 \;\;\checkmark
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Marker Nonterminal for Repeated Symbols

### Problem Setup

Consider a grammar where we want to **print the value of the left operand** at the moment the `+` operator is consumed, before the right operand has been parsed.

$$
E \rightarrow E \; + \; T \quad \{\, \text{print}(E_1.val); \; E.val = E_1.val + T.val \,\}
$$

This action is **not** in postfix position — the `print` must fire when `+` is shifted, but at that moment `T` has not yet been reduced. Placing it mid-RHS breaks the bottom-up implementation.

### Transformation Using a Marker Nonterminal

Introduce a fresh nonterminal `M` and an ε-production:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\rightarrow E \; + \; M \; T \\
M &\rightarrow \varepsilon \quad \{\, M.val = \text{stack}[E] \,\}
\end{aligned}
$$

When the parser has reduced the left `E` and shifted `+`, the marker `M` is reduced next via `M → ε`. At this moment, the val stack top is precisely `E_1.val`, so the action of `M → ε` copies it into `M.val`. The value is now safely "stashed" at the top of the val stack. When `T` is finally reduced and `E → E + M T` fires, both `M.val` (= `E_1.val`) and `T.val` are still on the stack, allowing the print and addition to proceed correctly.

### Numerical Trace Fragment

Suppose left `E.val = 7` and right `T.val = 4`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{After reducing left } E &: \; \text{val\_stack top} = 7 \\
\text{After shift } + &: \; \text{val\_stack top} = \text{(nothing useful)} \\
\text{After reduce } M \rightarrow \varepsilon &: \; M.val = 7 \;\text{(copied from below)} \\
\text{After reducing } T &: \; \text{val\_stack top} = 4 \\
\text{After reduce } E \rightarrow E + M T &: \; \text{print}(7); \; E.val = 7 + 4 = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

### Why This Works

The marker nonterminal acts as a **deferred scratchpad** — it has no children, but it is reduced at the precise moment a mid-RHS action was supposed to fire, and its ε-rule action transfers the needed value to the top of the val stack.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Reduction Action Forms

| Production | Reduction Action in `$$` form | When the val stack pops | What is pushed |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| $F \rightarrow \textbf{id}$ | `$$ = id.lexval` | 1 value (`id.lexval`) | $F.val$ |
| $T \rightarrow F$ | `$$ = $1` | 1 value ($F.val$) | $T.val$ |
| $T \rightarrow T * F$ | `$$ = $1 * $3` | 3 values ($T_1.val, *, F.val$) | $T.val$ |
| $E \rightarrow E + T$ | `$$ = $1 + $3` | 3 values ($E_1.val, +, T.val$) | $E.val$ |
| $M \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | `$$ = stack[below]` | 0 values | $M.val$ (preserved) |

> [!NOTE]
> The `*` and `+` terminals have **no synthesized semantic value** (their lexval is irrelevant). They still occupy a slot on the val stack — typically a dummy or unused entry — which is why `$2` is skipped in actions like `$$ = $1 + $3`.

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — yacc-style Bottom-Up Translator

The following diagram captures the major functional blocks of a bottom-up translator implementing an S-attributed definition, plus their information flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    src["Source Program"] --> lex["Lexical Analyzer"]
    lex --> tstream["Token Stream with lexval"]
    tstream --> lrsim["LR Shift-Reduce Parser Engine"]
    pstack["Parse Stack (Grammar Symbols)"] --> lrsim
    vstack["Val Stack (Synthesized Attributes)"] --> lrsim
    lrsim -->|"Shift: push symbol + value"| pstack
    lrsim -->|"Shift: push value"| vstack
    lrsim -->|"Reduce: pop n symbols"| pstack
    lrsim -->|"Reduce: pop n values, compute $$"| vstack
    action["Semantic Action Library (yacc)"] --> lrsim
    lookup["Production Table (LHS, RHS length)"] --> lrsim
    final["Final Attribute on val stack top"] --> output["Translated Output (e.g., 3-addr code)"]
    lrsim -->|"Accept"| final
```

**Interpretation of the flow:**

- The lexical analyzer hands the parser tokens paired with their `lexval`.
- The LR engine consults the production table to decide shifts and reductions.
- On every shift, the parser pushes both the grammar symbol onto the parse stack and its synthesized value onto the val stack.
- On every reduction, the engine pops the correct number of entries from both stacks, invokes the corresponding semantic action from the action library, computes `$$`, and pushes `$$` onto the val stack.
- On acceptance, the topmost value on the val stack is the start symbol's attribute, which is the final translation result.

---

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Parse Stack vs. Val Stack During Evaluation of `id * id + id`

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Input as Token Stream
    participant PS as Parse Stack
    participant VS as Val Stack
    participant Action as Semantic Action

    Note over PS,VS: Initial: PS=[\$] VS=[\$]
    Input->>PS: shift id (3)
    PS->>VS: push 3
    Note over PS,VS: PS=[\$,id] VS=[\$,3]
    PS->>Action: reduce F -> id
    Action->>VS: pop 3, push F.val=3
    Note over PS,VS: PS=[\$,F] VS=[\$,3]
    PS->>Action: reduce T -> F
    Action->>VS: replace 3 with T.val=3
    Note over PS,VS: PS=[\$,T] VS=[\$,3]
    Input->>PS: shift *
    Note over PS,VS: PS=[\$,T,*] VS=[\$,3,dash]
    Input->>PS: shift id (5)
    PS->>VS: push 5
    Note over PS,VS: PS=[\$,T,*,id] VS=[\$,3,dash,5]
    PS->>Action: reduce F -> id
    Action->>VS: pop 5, push F.val=5
    Note over PS,VS: PS=[\$,T,*,F] VS=[\$,3,dash,5]
    PS->>Action: reduce T -> T*F
    Action->>VS: pop 3,dash,5; push T.val=15
    Note over PS,VS: PS=[\$,T] VS=[\$,15]
    PS->>Action: reduce E -> T
    Action->>VS: push E.val=15
    Note over PS,VS: PS=[\$,E] VS=[\$,15]
    Input->>PS: shift +
    Input->>PS: shift id (4)
    PS->>VS: push 4
    PS->>Action: reduce F -> id
    PS->>Action: reduce T -> F
    PS->>Action: reduce E -> E+T
    Action->>VS: pop 15,dash,4; push E.val=19
    Note over PS,VS: PS=[\$,E] VS=[\$,19]
    Action->>Input: ACCEPT
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dash `-` in the val stack represents a dummy entry pushed when `*` or `+` is shifted — terminals with no meaningful synthesized attribute. This dummy keeps stack heights synchronized between the parse stack and val stack.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Marker Nonterminal Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Original (broken in bottom-up)"
        O1[E] --> O2[+] --> O3[T]
        Omid["print action fires here -- T not yet reduced!"]
    end

    subgraph "Augmented (works in bottom-up)"
        L1[E] --> L2[+] --> LM[M marker] --> L3[T]
        LR1["M -> epsilon"] -->|"copies E.val to M.val"| LM
        LR2["E -> E + M T fires at end"] -->|"prints M.val and adds T.val"| L1
    end
```

The augmentation converts an L-attributed construction into one whose actions all sit in postfix position — making it implementable in a pure bottom-up parser.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define an S-attributed definition. Why is it said that any S-attributed definition can be evaluated during bottom-up parsing?**

**Model Answer:**

An **S-attributed definition** is a syntax-directed definition in which every attribute associated with every nonterminal is a *synthesized* attribute. Synthesized attributes have values that depend only on the attribute values of the **children** of the corresponding parse-tree node (and on terminal attributes).

It can be evaluated during bottom-up parsing because:

1. In a bottom-up (shift-reduce) parser, the children of any node are always reduced **before** the parent.
2. Hence, by the time a reduction fires the production's action, all required child attribute values are already available.
3. A parallel **val stack** holds the synthesized values; on each reduction, the top `n` values are popped, the action computes `$$`, and the new value is pushed.
4. Therefore, no inherited attributes or mid-RHS actions are needed — pure bottom-up traversal suffices.

**(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**What is the role of the val (value) stack in bottom-up evaluation of S-attributed definitions?**

**Model Answer:**

The **val stack** is a stack maintained in parallel with the parse stack. It stores the synthesized attribute value of every grammar symbol currently on the parse stack. Its roles are:

1. **Synchronization:** It mirrors the parse stack one-to-one, ensuring that the `i`-th entry from the top corresponds to the `i`-th symbol on the parse stack's RHS during a reduction.
2. **Action evaluation:** When a production is reduced, the action accesses `$1, $2, …, $n` from the val stack (top `n` entries), computes `$$`, and replaces those `n` entries with `$$`.
3. **Result storage:** After a complete parse, the top of the val stack holds the synthesized attribute of the start symbol, which is the final translated output.

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Consider the following S-attributed grammar for a desk calculator:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &\rightarrow E \, \textbf{n} \quad \{ L.val = E.val \} \\
E &\rightarrow E_1 + T \quad \{ E.val = E_1.val + T.val \} \\
E &\rightarrow T \quad\quad\quad \{ E.val = T.val \} \\
T &\rightarrow T_1 * F \quad \{ T.val = T_1.val \times F.val \} \\
T &\rightarrow F \quad\quad\quad \{ T.val = F.val \} \\
F &\rightarrow ( E ) \quad\quad\; \{ F.val = E.val \} \\
F &\rightarrow \textbf{digit} \quad \{ F.val = \textbf{digit}.lexval \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trace the bottom-up evaluation of the input `3 * 5 + 4 n` showing the parse stack, val stack, and the action fired at every reduction.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The grammar is S-attributed — every attribute is synthesized, and all actions are at the right end of their productions.

| Step | Action | Parse Stack | Val Stack |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| 0 | Initial | `$` | `$` |
| 1 | Shift `digit` (3) | `$ digit` | `$ 3` |
| 2 | Reduce $F \to \textbf{digit}$ | `$ F` | `$ 3` |
| 3 | Reduce $T \to F$ | `$ T` | `$ 3` |
| 4 | Shift `*` | `$ T *` | `$ 3 -` |
| 5 | Shift `digit` (5) | `$ T * digit` | `$ 3 - 5` |
| 6 | Reduce $F \to \textbf{digit}$ | `$ T * F` | `$ 3 - 5` |
| 7 | Reduce $T \to T * F$ ($3 \times 5$) | `$ T` | `$ 15` |
| 8 | Reduce $E \to T$ | `$ E` | `$ 15` |
| 9 | Shift `+` | `$ E +` | `$ 15 -` |
| 10 | Shift `digit` (4) | `$ E + digit` | `$ 15 - 4` |
| 11 | Reduce $F \to \textbf{digit}$ | `$ E + F` | `$ 15 - 4` |
| 12 | Reduce $T \to F$ | `$ E + T` | `$ 15 - 4` |
| 13 | Reduce $E \to E + T$ ($15+4$) | `$ E` | `$ 19` |
| 14 | Shift `n` | `$ E n` | `$ 19 -` |
| 15 | Reduce $L \to E \, \textbf{n}$ | `$ L` | `$ 19` |

**Final result:** $L.val = 19$, which the calculator would print.

**(7 marks — 1 mark per major reduction step, plus marks for correct initialization and final result)**

---

#### (b) Explain the concept of marker nonterminals. Why are they needed, and demonstrate with an example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (2 marks):** A *marker nonterminal* is a freshly-introduced ε-production nonterminal (typically named `M`) inserted into the body of an existing production. It carries a synthesized attribute that *remembers* a value needed by a mid-RHS semantic action. By forcing the marker to be reduced at exactly the point where the action was supposed to fire, the value is "captured" onto the top of the val stack.

**Need (2 marks):** A bottom-up parser cannot execute semantic actions that lie *inside* the RHS of a production — by the time the parser has shifted the rightmost symbol, the inner position has already been "passed over." To preserve the L-attributed construction, we transform it into an equivalent S-attributed one by adding a marker nonterminal. This converts actions placed mid-RHS into ε-productions whose actions are now in postfix position relative to the marker.

**Example (3 marks):** Suppose we want to print the value of the left `E` at the moment `+` is shifted, in a grammar $E \to E + T$:

- **Original (not directly bottom-up):**
  $$
  E \rightarrow E \; + \; T \quad \{\, \text{print}(E_1.val); \; E.val = E_1.val + T.val \,\}
  $$

- **Augmented using marker `M`:**
  $$
  \begin{aligned}
  E &\rightarrow E \; + \; M \; T \\
  M &\rightarrow \varepsilon \quad \{\, M.val = \text{captured}(E_1.val); \; \text{print}(M.val) \,\} \\
  &\text{(parent production action at the end: } E.val = M.val + T.val \text{)}
  \end{aligned}
  $$

When the left `E` is reduced, the val stack holds its value. Shifting `+` does not pop that value. When `M → ε` is reduced next, the action captures the value of `E_1` (currently just below the top of the val stack) into `M.val`, prints it, and pushes `M.val` to the top. When `T` is later reduced and finally `E → E + M T` fires, both `M.val` and `T.val` are available — the addition is performed and `E.val` is computed and pushed.

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### (a) Design an S-attributed definition that computes the **height of a binary tree** represented by the grammar:

$$
S \rightarrow ( L ) \quad \vert \quad \textbf{id} \\
L \rightarrow L \, , \, S \quad \vert \quad S
$$

**Show how the height is computed during a bottom-up parse of the input `( id , ( id , id ) )`.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**S-attributed SDD (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow ( \, L \, ) \quad \{ S.h = L.h + 1 \} \\
S &\rightarrow \textbf{id} \quad\quad \{ S.h = 1 \} \\
L &\rightarrow L_1 \, , \, S \quad \{ L.h = \max(L_1.h, S.h) \} \\
L &\rightarrow S \quad\quad\quad\;\; \{ L.h = S.h \}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here every attribute (`h`) is synthesized — the SDD is S-attributed.

**Trace (4 marks):**

Input: `( id , ( id , id ) )` — a tree of height 3.

| Step | Action | Parse Stack (top right) | Val Stack (top right) |
| :-- | :-- | :-- | :-- |
| 0 | Initial | `$` | `$` |
| 1 | Shift `id` | `$ id` | `$ 1` |
| 2 | Reduce $S \to \textbf{id}$ ($S.h=1$) | `$ S` | `$ 1` |
| 3 | Reduce $L \to S$ ($L.h=1$) | `$ L` | `$ 1` |
| 4 | Shift `,` | `$ L ,` | `$ 1 -` |
| 5 | Shift `(` | `$ L , (` | `$ 1 - -` |
| 6 | Shift `id` | `$ L , ( id` | `$ 1 - - 1` |
| 7 | Reduce $S \to \textbf{id}$ | `$ L , ( S` | `$ 1 - - 1` |
| 8 | Reduce $L \to S$ ($L.h=1$) | `$ L , ( L` | `$ 1 - - 1` |
| 9 | Shift `,` | `$ L , ( L ,` | `$ 1 - - 1 -` |
| 10 | Shift `id` | `$ L , ( L , id` | `$ 1 - - 1 - 1` |
| 11 | Reduce $S \to \textbf{id}$ | `$ L , ( L , S` | `$ 1 - - 1 - 1` |
| 12 | Reduce $L \to L \, , \, S$ ($\max(1,1)=1$) | `$ L , ( L` | `$ 1 - - 1` |
| 13 | Shift `)` | `$ L , ( L )` | `$ 1 - - 1 -` |
| 14 | Reduce $S \to (L)$ ($S.h=1+1=2$) | `$ L , S` | `$ 1 - 2` |
| 15 | Reduce $L \to L \, , \, S$ ($\max(1,2)=2$) | `$ L` | `$ 2` |
| 16 | Shift `)` | `$ L )` | `$ 2 -` |
| 17 | Reduce $S \to (L)$ ($S.h=2+1=3$) | `$ S` | `$ 3` |

**Final result:** $S.h = 3$ — the height of the binary tree.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### (b) Discuss how the `yacc` parser-generator implements S-attributed definitions. Explain the semantics of `$$`, `$1`, `$2`, … and describe how the val stack is updated. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**yacc overview (2 marks):** `yacc` (Yet Another Compiler-Compiler) accepts a grammar annotated with semantic actions in C. It generates an LALR(1) bottom-up parser that, at runtime, maintains the parse stack and a parallel value stack. When a production `A : X1 X2 … Xn` is reduced, yacc-generated code:

1. Pops `n` entries from the value stack (these become accessible as `$1, $2, …, $n`).
2. Executes the user-supplied C action, in which `$$` denotes the LHS value being computed.
3. Pushes `$$` onto the value stack.

**Semantics of positional references (3 marks):**

- **`$$`** — the synthesized attribute value of the LHS nonterminal `A`. It is computed by the action and pushed as the new top of the val stack.
- **`$1, $2, …, $n`** — the synthesized attribute values of the symbols `X₁, X₂, …, Xₙ` on the RHS. They correspond to the topmost `n` entries of the val stack just before the reduction. `$i` is the `i`-th value from the top.
- **Terminals without semantic values** — e.g., `+`, `*`, `(`, `)` — still occupy a slot on the val stack (typically a dummy/ignored entry) so that indexing is consistent.

**Val stack update algorithm (2 marks):** During shifting, yacc pushes the terminal's semantic value (often `yylval`, set by `lex`). During reduction, the code pops `n` values, executes the action, and pushes the resulting `$$`. A simple C-code skeleton is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{case production } A \rightarrow X_1 \dots X_n: \\
&\quad \text{pop } n \text{ values from val\_stack into } \$1, \dots, \$n \\
&\quad \text{execute user action computing } \$\$ \\
&\quad \text{push } \$ \$ \text{ onto val\_stack} \\
&\quad \text{push } A \text{ onto parse stack}
\end{aligned}
$$

This automatic stack management is why the programmer never has to write explicit stack manipulation code — yacc handles it, making S-attributed definitions extremely easy to implement in practice.

**(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes KTU examiners penalize:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the val stack parallel to the parse stack.** Many students describe only the parse stack and skip showing attribute values — *expect to lose 2–3 marks per question*.
> 2. **Confusing `$1` indices.** `$1` is the **first** symbol on the RHS, not the LHS. In `E → E + T`, `$$ = $1 + $3`, **not** `$$ = $2 + $3`.
> 3. **Placing actions mid-RHS without justification.** If you write an action between nonterminals, you must explain the marker nonterminal mechanism — otherwise the action cannot fire in a bottom-up parser.
> 4. **Skipping dummy entries.** When a terminal like `+` or `*` is shifted, a corresponding entry is pushed on the val stack (often unused). Omitting it from the trace shows a misunderstanding of stack synchronization.
> 5. **Calling inherited attributes "synthesized."** If even one inherited attribute appears in an SDD, it is **not** S-attributed. Examiners dock full marks if the definition and example contradict each other.
> 6. **Not showing the final accept step.** Always explicitly state the final value left on the val stack as the answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- An **S-attributed SDD** uses only synthesized attributes; data flows strictly bottom-up in the parse tree.
- Bottom-up evaluation is performed during **shift-reduce parsing** using a parallel **val (value) stack**.
- On a **shift**, the terminal's attribute is pushed onto the val stack alongside the symbol onto the parse stack.
- On a **reduce** by `A → X₁ … Xₙ`, the top `n` val-stack entries are popped, the action computes `$$`, and `$$` is pushed back.
- All actions must be in **postfix** position (rightmost end) of their production for direct bottom-up implementation.
- In **yacc**, the action uses `$$`, `$1`, `$2`, … — yacc generates the stack manipulation code automatically.
- A **marker nonterminal** is an ε-production nonterminal inserted to capture and preserve a value across reductions, enabling mid-RHS-style computations in bottom-up parsers.
- After parsing, the **top of the val stack** holds the synthesized attribute of the start symbol — the translation result.
- The number of **reductions** equals the number of internal parse-tree nodes; each reduction corresponds to exactly one semantic action firing.
- S-attributed definitions are the **most efficient** SDD class for bottom-up parsers — no extra stack machinery, no inherited-attribute propagation.
- Practical applications include arithmetic evaluation, type checking, intermediate code generation, and AST metadata construction.
- KTU exam questions on this topic almost always ask for: (1) the definition, (2) a stack-trace example, and (3) the role of the val stack or the marker nonterminal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: L-Attributed Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** L-Attributed Definitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
