An **L-Attributed Definition** is a specialized class of **Syntax-Directed Definitions (SDD)** in which each attribute of a non-terminal on the **right-hand side of a production** depends either on the **inherited attributes of that same non-terminal** or on the **attributes (inherited or synthesized) of the symbols appearing to its left in the production body**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD) is called **L-Attributed** if, for every production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$ and for every inherited attribute $X_i.inh$ (where $1 \le i \le n$), the attribute can be computed using only the attributes of the parent $A$ and the attributes of $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_{i-1}$ — i.e., the symbols **to its left** in the production.

In essence, the dependency graph formed by the SDD edges never flows from **right-to-left**; information always flows **top-down** (from parent to children) or **left-to-right** (among siblings).

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Assembly Line" Analogy

Imagine a **car assembly line** where the chassis moves from **left to right**, and at each station, workers add parts:

- Workers at later stations can **only use parts installed by earlier stations**.
- A worker cannot reach **backwards** in time to grab a part that hasn't been built yet.

This is precisely how an **L-attributed definition behaves**: when evaluating attributes for a symbol $X_i$, we may only draw information from symbols $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_{i-1}$ that have **already been "assembled"** to the left. The "**L**" literally stands for **"Left"** — information flows leftward-to-rightward within a production.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Every **S-Attributed Definition is automatically an L-Attributed Definition**, but the converse is **not true**. L-attributed is a **broader class** that permits some inherited attributes.

## 1.3 Why L-Attributed Definitions Matter in Compiler Design

L-attributed definitions are the **cornerstone** that allows syntax-directed translation to be performed during a **single left-to-right traversal** of the parse tree. This property is what enables their implementation during:

- **Top-down parsing** (Recursive Descent, LL(1) parsers)
- **Bottom-up parsing** (LALR(1), SLR parsers) — with proper ordering

The **KTU 2024 Scheme** places significant emphasis on L-attributed definitions because they represent the **most general class of SDDs** that can be evaluated in conjunction with an **LL or LR parser** without requiring multiple passes over the parse tree.

---

## 1.4 Formal Mathematical Characterization

For any production of the form:

$$A \rightarrow X_1 \, X_2 \, X_3 \, \dots \, X_n$$

An SDD is **L-attributed** if and only if for each **inherited attribute** $X_i.a$ (where $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$), the equation defining $X_i.a$ involves **only** the following attributes:

1. **Inherited attributes** of $A$ (the head non-terminal).
2. **Either inherited or synthesized attributes** of $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_{i-1}$ (symbols strictly to the left of $X_i$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Rule:** $X_i$ itself **cannot depend on synthesized attributes of symbols to its right** ($X_{i+1}, X_{i+2}, \dots$), as these violate the left-to-right dependency principle.

---

## 1.5 Symbolic Attribute Notations

The KTU board typically uses the following notation conventions:

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $X.syn$ or $X.val$ | Synthesized attribute of $X$ |
| $X.inh$ | Inherited attribute of $X$ |
| $A.a$ | Attribute $a$ of non-terminal $A$ |
| $X_i$ | The $i$-th symbol on the RHS of a production |

The implicit **sub-script convention** $X_i$ refers to the $i$-th grammar symbol on the right-hand side.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Foundational Rules of L-Attributed Definitions

For an SDD to qualify as **L-attributed**, the following rules must hold **for every production** in the grammar:

### Rule 1: Start Symbol Initialization

Each synthesized attribute of the **start symbol** (typically $S$) must be initialized to a valid value before evaluation begins. This is a precondition enforced by the compiler writer.

### Rule 2: Inherited Attribute Dependency Constraint

For every production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$ and every inherited attribute $X_i.inh$ where $1 \le i \le n$:

$$X_i.inh = f(\,A.inh,\, X_1.syn,\, X_1.inh,\, X_2.syn,\, X_2.inh,\, \dots,\, X_{i-1}.syn,\, X_{i-1}.inh\,)$$

That is, $X_i.inh$ may depend **only** on:

- **Inherited attributes of $A$** (the LHS non-terminal).
- **Attributes (synthesized or inherited) of $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_{i-1}$** — the symbols to the **left** of $X_i$.

### Rule 3: Synthesized Attribute Standard Form

For every production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$, each **synthesized attribute** of $A$ must be computed from attributes of the **symbols in the production body** — this is the standard rule of any SDD.

---

## 2.2 Why "L" Stands for "Left" — The Ordering Principle

The "**L**" in L-attributed literally refers to **"Left-to-right"** evaluation order. When we traverse a parse tree:

1. We visit the parse tree **depth-first, left-to-right**.
2. When we reach symbol $X_i$, all symbols $X_1, \dots, X_{i-1}$ have **already been fully processed**.
3. Therefore, **any attribute of $X_i$ that depends only on these leftward symbols** can be safely computed at this moment.

This principle is what allows L-attributed definitions to be evaluated during parsing **without any backtracking**.

---

## 2.3 Comparison: L-Attributed vs S-Attributed Definitions

| Property | **S-Attributed Definition** | **L-Attributed Definition** |
|----------|----------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Attribute Type** | Only **synthesized** attributes allowed | **Synthesized + Inherited** attributes allowed |
| **Dependency Direction** | Strictly **bottom-up** (child to parent) | **Top-down** and **left-to-right** |
| **Parser Compatibility** | Easy with **bottom-up (LR)** parsers | Compatible with both **top-down (LL)** and **bottom-up (LR)** parsers |
| **Subset Relation** | A subset of L-attributed definitions | A superset of S-attributed definitions |
| **Translation Scheme** | **Postfix notation** only | Allows **infix translation schemes** |
| **Class Size** | Smaller class | Larger, more expressive class |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question:** "Show that every S-attributed definition is L-attributed, but the converse is not true." This is a frequently asked 7-mark question in Part B of the ESE.

---

## 2.4 Translation Schemes for L-Attributed Definitions

A **Translation Scheme** is a context-free grammar in which semantic actions are **embedded within the right-hand side of productions**, enclosed within curly braces `{ ... }`. For an L-attributed SDD, we can convert it into a translation scheme by:

1. **Inlining the semantic actions** at the point where the attribute is computed.
2. **Preserving left-to-right evaluation order** so that all required attributes are available before their consumers.

### Example Translation Scheme Pattern

For the production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 X_3$ with $X_2.inh = f(X_1.syn)$, the corresponding L-attributed translation scheme is:

$$A \rightarrow X_1 \; \{\text{compute } X_2.inh\} \; X_2 \; X_3$$

The action is placed **between** $X_1$ and $X_2$ because $X_2.inh$ depends on $X_1.syn$, which must be computed first.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of L-Attributed Definitions

L-attributed definitions are extensively used in:

- **Type Checking** during semantic analysis (passing type information downward through the parse tree).
- **Intermediate Code Generation** (e.g., three-address code for expressions).
- **Symbol Table Management** (inheriting scope information from enclosing blocks).
- **Static Analysis Tools** (data-flow analysis, optimization passes).

In modern compilers like **GCC** and **LLVM**, intermediate representations are constructed using techniques inspired by L-attributed translations.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Constraint** |
|-------------|---------------------|----------------|
| L-Attributed Rule | $X_i.inh = f(A.inh, X_1.\alpha, \dots, X_{i-1}.\alpha)$ | $1 \le i \le n$; $\alpha \in \{syn, inh\}$ |
| S-Attributed Rule | $A.syn = f(X_1.syn, \dots, X_n.syn)$ | No inherited attributes |
| Subset Relation | $\text{S-Attributed} \subset \text{L-Attributed}$ | Always true |
| Production Format | $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$ | Standard CFG form |
| Attribute Evaluation | **Depth-first, left-to-right** traversal | Required for L-attributed SDDs |
| Semantic Action Placement | Between $X_{i-1}$ and $X_i$ | After dependencies of $X_i$ are resolved |
| Top-Down Parsing | Fully compatible | LL(1) parsers |
| Bottom-Up Parsing | Compatible with rewriting | LALR(1) parsers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition:** The index $i$ for an inherited attribute must satisfy $1 \le i \le n$. When $i = 1$ (the leftmost symbol), $X_i.inh$ can only depend on inherited attributes of $A$ since there are no $X_j$ with $j < i$.

---

## 2.7 Algorithm to Test L-Attributed Property

To test whether an SDD is L-attributed, apply this systematic procedure:

**Input:** An SDD with set of productions $P$.

**Output:** Boolean (True if L-attributed, False otherwise).

1. For each production $p = A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$ in $P$:
   a. For each inherited attribute $X_i.inh$ defined in $p$:
      i. Identify the set of attributes it depends on, denoted $D$.
      ii. If $D$ contains any attribute of $X_j$ where $j > i$ → **Return False**.
      iii. If $D$ contains any attribute of $A$ that is **not inherited** → **Return False**.
   b. If all checks pass, continue to next production.
2. If all productions pass → **Return True**.

This algorithm has a time complexity of $O(\vert P \vert \times n \times k)$, where $k$ is the average number of attributes per symbol.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Verifying L-Attributed Property

**Given Grammar and SDD:**

Consider the following production rules with attribute definitions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Production 1:} \quad & D \rightarrow T \, L \\
& L.inh = T.type \\
\\
\text{Production 2:} \quad & T \rightarrow \text{int} \\
& T.type = \text{integer} \\
\\
\text{Production 3:} \quad & T \rightarrow \text{float} \\
& T.type = \text{float} \\
\\
\text{Production 4:} \quad & L \rightarrow L_1 \, \text{comma} \, id \\
& L_1.inh = L.inh \\
& L.syn = \text{append}(L_1.syn, \text{id.entry}) \\
\\
\text{Production 5:} \quad & L \rightarrow \text{id} \\
& L.syn = \text{id.entry}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Identify all inherited attributes.

The inherited attributes are:
- $L.inh$ in Production 1
- $L_1.inh$ in Production 4

**Step 2:** Check Production 1: $D \rightarrow T \, L$

For $L.inh = T.type$:
- $T.type$ is a **synthesized attribute** of $T$, and $T$ is **to the left** of $L$ in the production.
- $T$ is at position $i = 1$, $L$ is at position $i = 2$.
- Since $T$ is to the **left** of $L$, the constraint $j < i$ where $i = 2$ is satisfied for $j = 1$.
- ✅ **Passes the L-attributed test.**

**Step 3:** Check Production 4: $L \rightarrow L_1 \, \text{comma} \, id$

For $L_1.inh = L.inh$:
- $L.inh$ is an **inherited attribute** of the LHS non-terminal $L$.
- $L_1$ is at position $i = 1$, and $L$ is the parent.
- ✅ **Passes the L-attributed test.**

**Step 4:** All other productions contain only synthesized attributes, which trivially satisfy the L-attributed property.

**Conclusion:** The given SDD is **L-attributed**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Proving an SDD is NOT L-Attributed

**Given SDD:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Production:} \quad & A \rightarrow B \, C \\
& C.inh = B.syn \\
\\
\text{Production:} \quad & B \rightarrow b \\
& B.syn = 1 \\
\\
\text{Production:} \quad & C \rightarrow c \\
& C.syn = C.inh + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:**

For the production $A \rightarrow B \, C$:
- $B$ is at position $i = 1$, $C$ is at position $i = 2$.
- $C.inh = B.syn$: $C.inh$ depends on $B.syn$.
- $B$ is **to the left** of $C$ (position $j = 1 < i = 2$).
- ✅ **This appears to satisfy L-attributed property...**

However, let us consider a different SDD:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Production:} \quad & A \rightarrow B \, C \\
& B.inh = C.syn \quad \text{(Right-to-Left dependency!)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:**

- $B$ is at position $i = 1$, $C$ is at position $i = 2$.
- $B.inh$ depends on $C.syn$.
- $C$ is at position $j = 2$, which is **to the right** of $B$ ($j > i = 1$).
- ❌ **Violates the L-attributed property.**

**Conclusion:** This SDD is **NOT L-attributed** because attribute information flows from **right to left**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Converting an L-Attributed SDD into a Translation Scheme

**Given L-Attributed SDD:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\rightarrow B \, C \\
&\text{where } C.inh = B.syn + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Identify the position where $C.inh$ must be computed.

$C.inh$ depends on $B.syn$. Therefore, the action must be placed **after** $B$ is fully processed and **before** $C$ is processed.

**Step 2:** Construct the translation scheme:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \rightarrow B \; \{C.inh = B.syn + 1\} \; C
\end{aligned}
$$

The semantic action `{C.inh = B.syn + 1}` is embedded between $B$ and $C$.

**Step 3:** Verify correctness.

- When the parser processes $B$ first, it computes $B.syn$.
- Immediately afterward, the semantic action executes, computing $C.inh = B.syn + 1$.
- When $C$ is later processed, $C.inh$ is already available.
- ✅ **Valid translation scheme for an L-attributed SDD.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Dependency Analysis for Common Production Patterns

The following table presents a comprehensive dependency analysis for various production patterns:

| **Production Pattern** | **Attribute Definition** | **L-Attributed?** | **Reasoning** |
|------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------|---------------|
| $A \rightarrow B \, C$ | $C.inh = B.syn$ | ✅ Yes | $B$ is to the left of $C$ |
| $A \rightarrow B \, C$ | $B.inh = C.syn$ | ❌ No | $C$ is to the right of $B$ |
| $A \rightarrow B \, C$ | $A.syn = B.syn + C.syn$ | ✅ Yes | Synthesized attribute of head |
| $A \rightarrow \epsilon$ | (no attributes) | ✅ Yes | Trivially satisfied |
| $A \rightarrow B$ | $B.inh = A.inh$ | ✅ Yes | Depends only on parent's inherited attr |
| $A \rightarrow B \, C \, D$ | $D.inh = B.syn$ | ✅ Yes | Skipping $C$ is allowed |
| $A \rightarrow B \, C \, D$ | $C.inh = D.syn$ | ❌ No | $D$ is to the right of $C$ |
| $A \rightarrow B \, C \, D$ | $C.inh = A.inh + B.syn$ | ✅ Yes | $A$'s inherited + $B$'s attribute |
| $S \rightarrow A \, B$ | $A.inh = S.inh$ | ✅ Yes | Parent's inherited attribute |
| $A \rightarrow B \, C$ | $B.inh = C.inh$ | ❌ No | $C$ is to the right of $B$ |

---

## 3.5 Detailed Worked Example: Type Declaration Grammar

**Problem:** Consider the following grammar for processing type declarations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &\rightarrow T \, L \\
T &\rightarrow \text{int} \mid \text{float} \\
L &\rightarrow L_1 \, \text{comma} \, id \mid id
\end{aligned}
$$

with the following SDD:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &\rightarrow T \, L : \quad L.inh = T.type \\
T &\rightarrow \text{int} : \quad T.type = \text{integer} \\
T &\rightarrow \text{float} : \quad T.type = \text{float} \\
L &\rightarrow L_1 \, \text{comma} \, id : \quad L_1.inh = L.inh \\
L &\rightarrow L_1 \, \text{comma} \, id : \quad L.syn = \text{append}(L_1.syn, id.entry) \\
L &\rightarrow id : \quad L.syn = id.entry
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-Step Evaluation on Input: `int a, b, c`**

**Parse Tree:**
```
D
├── T (int)
│     └── T.type = integer
└── L
      ├── L (innermost)
      │     ├── id (c)
      │     └── L.syn = c.entry
      ├── comma
      └── L (middle)
            ├── id (b)
            └── L.syn = append(middle.syn_left, b.entry)
      ├── comma
      └── id (a)
      └── L.syn = append(outer.syn_left, a.entry)
```

**Evaluation Order (Left-to-Right, Depth-First):**

1. Visit $D$. No inherited attributes for the start symbol initially.
2. Visit $T \rightarrow \text{int}$. Compute $T.type = \text{integer}$.
3. Return to $D$. Compute $L.inh = T.type = \text{integer}$.
4. Visit innermost $L \rightarrow id$. Compute innermost $L.syn = c.entry$.
5. Visit middle $L$. Inherited: $L.inh = \text{integer}$. Visit $id(b)$: $L.syn = \text{append}(c.entry, b.entry)$.
6. Visit outermost $L$. Inherited: $L.inh = \text{integer}$. Compute $L.syn = \text{append}([b,c], a.entry)$.

**Final $L.syn$:** A list containing $\{a, b, c\}$ all of type **integer**.

This evaluation was possible **without multiple passes** over the parse tree because the SDD is L-attributed.

---

## 3.6 Mathematical Proof: Every S-Attributed Definition is L-Attributed

**Theorem:** If an SDD is S-attributed, then it is also L-attributed.

**Proof:**

By definition, an S-attributed SDD has **no inherited attributes**. Therefore, the only constraints to verify are for synthesized attributes of the LHS non-terminal $A$ in each production.

For any production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$ in an S-attributed SDD:

- The synthesized attribute $A.syn$ depends only on attributes of $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$.
- Since there are **no inherited attributes**, the constraint "inherited attributes of $X_i$ can only depend on symbols to the left" is **vacuously satisfied**.

Therefore, every S-attributed SDD trivially satisfies the L-attributed property. ∎

**Corollary:** The class of S-attributed definitions is a **proper subset** of L-attributed definitions:

$$\text{S-Attributed Definitions} \subsetneq \text{L-Attributed Definitions}$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define L-Attributed Definition. State the conditions that must be satisfied for a Syntax-Directed Definition to be L-attributed.**

#### Model Answer:

An **L-Attributed Definition** is a Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD) in which every inherited attribute of a symbol on the right-hand side of a production depends only on the inherited attributes of the production head and the attributes (synthesized or inherited) of the symbols appearing **to its left** in the production.

**Conditions:**

For every production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$ and each inherited attribute $X_i.inh$:

1. $X_i.inh$ can depend on **inherited attributes of $A$**.
2. $X_i.inh$ can depend on **attributes (synthesized or inherited) of $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_{i-1}$**.
3. $X_i.inh$ **cannot** depend on attributes of $X_{i+1}, \dots, X_n$ (right-side symbols).
4. $X_i.inh$ **cannot** depend on synthesized attributes of $A$.

When all productions satisfy these conditions, the SDD is L-attributed.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Differentiate between S-Attributed and L-Attributed Definitions. Why is every S-attributed definition also L-attributed?**

#### Model Answer:

| **S-Attributed Definition** | **L-Attributed Definition** |
|----------------------------|------------------------------|
| Only synthesized attributes allowed | Both synthesized and inherited attributes allowed |
| Information flows bottom-up only | Information flows top-down and left-to-right |
| Evaluated during post-order traversal | Evaluated during depth-first left-to-right traversal |
| Easy with bottom-up parsers | Compatible with both LL and LR parsers |

**Reason for subset relation:** An S-attributed SDD has **no inherited attributes**, so the constraint that "inherited attributes must depend only on leftward symbols" is **vacuously true**. Hence, every S-attributed definition trivially satisfies the L-attributed property, making S-attributed a **proper subset** of L-attributed.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a)** Consider the following grammar with the associated SDD. Verify whether the SDD is L-attributed. **(7 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow A \, B \\
&\quad A.inh = S.val \\
&\quad B.inh = A.syn + 1 \\
&\quad S.val = B.syn \\
\\
A &\rightarrow a \\
&\quad A.syn = 5 \\
\\
B &\rightarrow b \\
&\quad B.syn = B.inh \times 2
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify all productions with inherited attributes.

The productions defining inherited attributes are:
- Production 1: $S \rightarrow A \, B$ defines $A.inh$ and $B.inh$.

**Step 2:** Check $A.inh = S.val$:

- $A$ is at position $i = 1$.
- $A.inh$ depends on $S.val$.
- $S.val$ is an inherited attribute (from the start symbol's perspective — though $S.val$ is the start symbol's attribute, we consider it as the parent's attribute).
- There are no symbols to the left of $A$ (since $A$ is the first symbol).
- ✅ **Passes the L-attributed constraint.**

**Step 3:** Check $B.inh = A.syn + 1$:

- $B$ is at position $i = 2$.
- $B.inh$ depends on $A.syn$.
- $A$ is at position $j = 1 < i = 2$ (to the left of $B$).
- ✅ **Passes the L-attributed constraint.**

**Step 4:** Check $S.val = B.syn$:

- $S.val$ is the synthesized attribute of the LHS non-terminal.
- The LHS attribute depends on attributes of RHS symbols, which is allowed.
- ✅ **Standard SDD rule, passes.**

**Step 5:** Productions for $A \rightarrow a$ and $B \rightarrow b$ involve only synthesized attributes and trivially pass.

**Conclusion:** The given SDD is **L-Attributed**.

---

**b)** Convert the above L-attributed SDD into a Translation Scheme and show the step-by-step evaluation for the input string $a \, b$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Construct the Translation Scheme.**

For the production $S \rightarrow A \, B$:

- $A.inh = S.val$ must be computed before $A$ is processed.
- $B.inh = A.syn + 1$ must be computed after $A.syn$ is available.

The translation scheme is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow \{A.inh = S.val\} \; A \; \{B.inh = A.syn + 1\} \; B \; \{S.val = B.syn\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Evaluate on Input `a b`.**

The parse tree for input `a b` is:

```
S
├── A
│     └── a
└── B
      └── b
```

**Evaluation Steps (Depth-First, Left-to-Right):**

1. Initialize: $S.val = 0$ (or any initial value, often a designated entry point).
2. Visit S's first action: Compute $A.inh = S.val = 0$.
3. Visit A, apply production $A \rightarrow a$: Compute $A.syn = 5$.
4. Visit S's second action: Compute $B.inh = A.syn + 1 = 5 + 1 = 6$.
5. Visit B, apply production $B \rightarrow b$: Compute $B.syn = B.inh \times 2 = 6 \times 2 = 12$.
6. Visit S's third action: Compute $S.val = B.syn = 12$.

**Final Result:** $S.val = 12$.

This demonstrates that the translation scheme successfully evaluates the SDD in a **single left-to-right pass**, confirming its compatibility with predictive parsing.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**a)** Consider the following SDD and determine whether it is L-attributed. Justify your answer with a detailed dependency analysis. **(7 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Production 1:} \quad & E \rightarrow E_1 + T \\
& E_1.inh = E.inh \\
& T.inh = E_1.syn + E.inh \\
& E.syn = T.syn \\
\\
\text{Production 2:} \quad & E \rightarrow T \\
& T.inh = E.inh \\
& E.syn = T.syn \\
\\
\text{Production 3:} \quad & T \rightarrow T_1 \times F \\
& T_1.inh = T.inh \\
& F.inh = T_1.syn \times T.inh \\
& T.syn = F.syn \\
\\
\text{Production 4:} \quad & T \rightarrow F \\
& F.inh = T.inh \\
& T.syn = F.syn \\
\\
\text{Production 5:} \quad & F \rightarrow (E) \\
& E.inh = F.inh \\
& F.syn = E.syn \\
\\
\text{Production 6:} \quad & F \rightarrow \text{digit} \\
& F.syn = \text{digit.lexval}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Analyze Production 1: $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$**

- $E_1.inh = E.inh$: Depends on $E$'s inherited attribute. Since $E_1$ is the first symbol ($i = 1$), there are no leftward symbols. Depends only on parent's inherited attribute. ✅ **L-attributed OK.**
- $T.inh = E_1.syn + E.inh$: Depends on $E_1.syn$ (a leftward symbol) and $E.inh$ (parent). $T$ is at position $i = 3$, $E_1$ at $i = 1 < 3$. ✅ **L-attributed OK.**
- $E.syn = T.syn$: Synthesized attribute of LHS. ✅ **Standard rule, OK.**

**Step 2: Analyze Production 2: $E \rightarrow T$**

- $T.inh = E.inh$: Depends on parent's inherited attribute. ✅ **OK.**
- $E.syn = T.syn$: Synthesized attribute. ✅ **OK.**

**Step 3: Analyze Production 3: $T \rightarrow T_1 \times F$**

- $T_1.inh = T.inh$: Depends on parent's inherited attribute. ✅ **OK.**
- $F.inh = T_1.syn \times T.inh$: Depends on $T_1.syn$ (leftward symbol) and $T.inh$ (parent). $F$ at $i = 3$, $T_1$ at $i = 1$. ✅ **L-attributed OK.**
- $T.syn = F.syn$: Synthesized. ✅ **OK.**

**Step 4: Analyze Production 4: $T \rightarrow F$**

- $F.inh = T.inh$: Depends on parent's inherited. ✅ **OK.**
- $T.syn = F.syn$: Synthesized. ✅ **OK.**

**Step 5: Analyze Production 5: $F \rightarrow (E)$**

- $E.inh = F.inh$: Depends on parent's inherited attribute. ✅ **OK.**
- $F.syn = E.syn$: Synthesized. ✅ **OK.**

**Step 6: Analyze Production 6: $F \rightarrow \text{digit}$**

- $F.syn = \text{digit.lexval}$: Synthesized from token value. ✅ **OK.**

**Conclusion:** All productions satisfy the L-attributed property. **The SDD is L-attributed.**

---

**b)** Construct the annotated parse tree for the input expression `3 × 4 + 5` using the above L-attributed SDD. Assume $E.inh = 0$ initially. Show all intermediate attribute values. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Parse Tree Structure:**

```
E (inh=0)
├── E1 (inh=0)
│     └── T (inh=0)
│           ├── T1 (inh=0)
│           │     └── F (inh=0)
│           │           └── digit 3 [syn=3]
│           └── × (token)
│           └── F (inh=0 × 3=0)
│                 └── digit 4 [syn=4]
└── + (token)
└── T (inh=E1.syn + E.inh = 3 + 0 = 3)
      └── F (inh=3)
            └── digit 5 [syn=5]
```

**Detailed Step-by-Step Evaluation:**

**Subtree: `3 × 4`**

1. Start: $E.inh = 0$.
2. Production $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$: Compute $E_1.inh = E.inh = 0$.
3. Production $E_1 \rightarrow T$: Compute $T.inh = E_1.inh = 0$.
4. Production $T \rightarrow T_1 \times F$: Compute $T_1.inh = T.inh = 0$.
5. Production $T_1 \rightarrow F$: Compute $F.inh = T_1.inh = 0$.
6. Production $F \rightarrow \text{digit}$: Compute $F.syn = 3$.
7. Back in $T \rightarrow T_1 \times F$: Compute $F.inh = T_1.syn \times T.inh = 3 \times 0 = 0$.
8. Production $F \rightarrow \text{digit}$: Compute $F.syn = 4$.
9. Back in $T \rightarrow T_1 \times F$: Compute $T.syn = F.syn = 4$.
10. Back in $E_1 \rightarrow T$: Compute $E_1.syn = T.syn = 4$.

**Subtree: `+ 5`**

11. Back in $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$: Compute $T.inh = E_1.syn + E.inh = 4 + 0 = 4$.

**Correction based on revised values:**

Let me recalculate with the proper evaluation order:

- $T_1 \rightarrow F$ where $F \rightarrow \text{digit 3}$: $F.syn = 3$, so $T_1.syn = 3$.
- For $F$ in $T_1 \times F$: $F.inh = T_1.syn \times T.inh = 3 \times 0 = 0$.
- $F \rightarrow \text{digit 4}$: $F.syn = 4$.
- $T.syn = 4$ (for the multiplication subtree).
- $E_1.syn = 4$.
- For outer $T$: $T.inh = E_1.syn + E.inh = 4 + 0 = 4$.
- $T \rightarrow F$: $F.inh = 4$.
- $F \rightarrow \text{digit 5}$: $F.syn = 5$.
- $T.syn = 5$ (for the right subtree).
- $E.syn = T.syn = 5$.

**Final Annotated Tree (Simplified):**

| **Node** | **inh** | **syn** |
|----------|---------|---------|
| E (root) | 0 | 5 |
| E1 (left of +) | 0 | 4 |
| T (under E1) | 0 | 4 |
| T1 (left of ×) | 0 | 3 |
| F (digit 3) | 0 | 3 |
| F (digit 4) | 0 | 4 |
| T (right of +) | 4 | 5 |
| F (digit 5) | 4 | 5 |

**Final Answer:** $E.syn = 5$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes Identified by KTU Examiners:**
>
> 1. **Mistake #1: Confusing L-attributed with S-attributed.** Students often state that L-attributed definitions have "no inherited attributes." This is **wrong** — L-attributed definitions **permit** inherited attributes, as long as dependencies flow left-to-right.
>
> 2. **Mistake #2: Forgetting the start symbol initialization.** When asked to evaluate an L-attributed SDD, students frequently omit the initialization of the start symbol's inherited attribute. The KTU board deducts **1-2 marks** for this oversight.
>
> 3. **Mistake #3: Incorrect semantic action placement in translation schemes.** Actions must be placed **after** all dependencies of the consumed attributes are computed. Placing an action too early will cause compilation errors and lose marks.
>
> 4. **Mistake #4: Failing to justify the L-attributed property.** Simply stating "yes, it is L-attributed" without showing the **dependency analysis** for each production loses significant marks (typically **3-4 marks out of 7**).
>
> 5. **Mistake #5: Writing `C.inh = B.syn` style rules without specifying positions.** Always mention the **positional indices** ($i$ and $j$) when explaining why a dependency is leftward.
>
> 6. **Mistake #6: Confusing top-down with bottom-up evaluation.** L-attributed SDDs are evaluated **depth-first, left-to-right**, which is **different** from the post-order evaluation used for S-attributed SDDs.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — L-Attributed Definitions**

- **Definition:** An SDD is L-attributed if every inherited attribute of a symbol $X_i$ on the RHS depends only on inherited attributes of the LHS non-terminal and attributes of symbols to the **left** of $X_i$.

- **The "L" stands for "Left"** — information flows leftward-to-rightward within a production.

- **Subset Relation:** S-Attributed $\subset$ L-Attributed (every S-attributed SDD is also L-attributed, but not vice versa).

- **Parser Compatibility:** L-attributed SDDs work with both **top-down (LL)** and **bottom-up (LR)** parsers — making them the most practical class for compiler implementations.

- **Key Constraint Formula:**
$$X_i.inh = f(A.inh, X_1.\alpha, X_2.\alpha, \dots, X_{i-1}.\alpha)$$
where $\alpha \in \{syn, inh\}$ and the indices satisfy $j < i$.

- **Translation Scheme Conversion:** Embed semantic actions between grammar symbols at positions where all required dependencies are satisfied.

- **Start Symbol:** Initialize inherited attributes of the start symbol before evaluation begins.

- **Right-to-Left Dependency = Violation:** Any inherited attribute depending on a symbol to its right violates the L-attributed property.

- **Evaluation Order:** Depth-first, left-to-right traversal of the parse tree.

- **Real-World Use:** Type checking, intermediate code generation, symbol table management in production compilers (GCC, LLVM).

- **Boundary Cases:** When $i = 1$ (first symbol), inherited attributes can only depend on the LHS inherited attributes. When $i = n$ (last symbol), all leftward symbols are available as dependencies.

- **Empty Productions:** Trivially satisfy the L-attributed property.

- **Verification Algorithm:** Check each production; for each inherited attribute, ensure no rightward or parent-synthesized dependencies exist.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bottom-up Evaluation of Inherited Attributes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bottom-up Evaluation of Inherited Attributes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bottom-up Evaluation of Inherited Attributes** is a syntax-directed translation technique in which the inherited attributes of a non-terminal are pre-computed and stored as *synthesized* attributes of inserted **marker (chain) non-terminals**, so that a single **bottom-up (LR) parse** can evaluate the *entire* annotated parse tree without requiring a separate top-down pass.

In formal terms, given an **L-attributed definition (LAD)** over a grammar $G$, we construct an equivalent **S-attributed definition** over a modified grammar $G^{\prime}$ such that:

$$ \text{LAD}(G) \;\equiv\; \text{SAD}(G^{\prime}) \quad \text{with} \quad L(G) = L(G^{\prime}) $$

where the only language-preserving change is the introduction of **epsilon-producing marker non-terminals** of the form $M \to \varepsilon$ that act as "value-carrier" placeholders.

### Key Terminology Quick-Reference

| Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
|---|---|
| **Synthesized Attribute** | Value computed from children, evaluated **bottom-up** along with parsing. |
| **Inherited Attribute** | Value copied from parent/left-siblings, traditionally evaluated **top-down/pre-order**. |
| **L-Attributed Definition (LAD)** | Definition where every inherited attribute of $X_j$ depends *only* on attributes of $A$ (parent), $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_{j-1}$ (left siblings). |
| **S-Attributed Definition (SAD)** | Definition containing **only** synthesized attributes. |
| **Marker Non-Terminal** | A new symbol $M_i$ that derives $\varepsilon$ and carries a synthesized "copy" of the inherited value. |
| **Chain / Epsilon Production** | A production of the form $M \to \varepsilon$, used purely as a vehicle for attribute transfer. |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy 🏗️)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The "Note-Passing Relay" in a Construction Site**

Imagine a **construction site** where workers are laying bricks from the **ground floor up** (bottom-up). The foreman on each floor needs to know the **floor plan** (an inherited attribute) from the architect above *before* he starts laying bricks. Normally, the architect would have to walk down each floor shouting instructions (top-down evaluation — slow, error-prone).

**Bottom-up evaluation** solves this elegantly: the architect writes the instructions on **sticky notes** and places them on each floor's doorway **before** the workers arrive. As the workers (LR parser reductions) progress upward, they simply **pick up the sticky note** at each floor's door and follow it — no separate top-down pass needed.

The "sticky notes" are the **marker non-terminals** $M \to \varepsilon$; the "instruction" is the synthesized attribute that *encodes* the inherited value.

### Why Does KTU Care About This?

- **LR parsers (YACC/Bison)** are the industrial standard — they work **bottom-up**.
- A naive LR parser **cannot natively** evaluate inherited attributes, because by the time a production is *reduced*, its parent has *not yet been seen*.
- This transformation unlocks the power of LR parsing for **all** L-attributed schemes (including type checking, code generation, etc.).

### Physical / Standard Metrics (highlighted)

- **Grammar size growth**: In the worst case, transformations can increase the number of non-terminals by a factor of **$O(n \cdot k)$**, where $n$ is the number of non-terminals and $k$ is the average number of inherited attributes per RHS symbol.
- **Parsing-table growth**: The number of LR(1) states may increase by a constant multiplicative factor, typically **2× to 4×** in practical cases.
- **Evaluation order**: Strictly **post-order traversal** of the parse tree, matching LR reduction order.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why & How

### The Core Problem

Consider the production:
$$ A \to X_1 \, X_2 \, X_3 $$

with inherited attributes $X_2.in$ and $X_3.in$. In a **bottom-up parse**, the parser reduces $X_2$ and $X_3$ *before* $A$ is even on top of the stack. Therefore, when $X_2.in$ is needed *during* the reduction of $X_2$, the value of $A$'s attributes (the natural source) is **not yet known**.

### The Solution Strategy

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** If we can *pre-compute* $X_j.in$ using only information available *before* $X_j$ is processed in a left-to-right scan, we can "bake" this value into a synthesized attribute of a marker that appears just before $X_j$.

For an **L-attributed definition**, this pre-computation is *always possible* because:
- $X_j.in$ depends only on $A.in$ (a constant for the parse subtree) and attributes of $X_1, \dots, X_{j-1}$ (already processed).

### 2.2 The Transformation Algorithm (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Algorithm: Transforming an LAD into an SAD via marker insertion**

**Input:** An L-attributed definition with a base grammar $G$.

**Output:** A modified grammar $G^{\prime}$ with an equivalent S-attributed definition.

**Procedure:**

1. **Verify L-Attributedness.** Inspect every semantic rule $X_j.a := f(\dots)$. If any inherited attribute depends on a *right sibling* ($X_{j+1}, X_{j+2}, \dots$), the definition is **not L-attributed** — abort and rewrite the grammar.

2. **For each production** $A \to X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$ in $G$:
   - Initialize $i = 1$.
   - For each RHS symbol $X_i$ that has **inherited attributes**:
     - Insert a fresh marker $M_i$ immediately *before* $X_i$.
     - Add the production $M_i \to \varepsilon$ to $G^{\prime}$.
     - Attach a synthesized attribute $M_i.syn$ to $M_i$ whose value equals the desired inherited attribute of $X_i$.

3. **Replace inherited-assignments** in semantic rules with **copying-from-marker** rules:
   $$ X_i.in \;:=\; M_i.syn $$

4. **Attach the marker-action** to the production $M_i \to \varepsilon$:
   $$ M_i.syn := f(\text{available attributes from } A, X_1, \dots, X_{i-1}) $$

5. **Verify no new LR conflicts** are introduced. If conflicts arise, consider left-factoring or rewriting the original grammar.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Property Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All entries below are KTU-exam-ready, with mathematical rigor.

| # | Property / Formula | Statement | Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Equivalence of Languages** | $L(G) = L(G^{\prime})$ | $M \to \varepsilon$ preserves language. |
| 2 | **Attribute Equivalence** | $X_i.in_{(G)} \equiv M_i.syn_{(G^{\prime})}$ | By construction. |
| 3 | **Marker Count Bound** | $\sum_{A \to \alpha} \sum_{i: X_i \text{ has inherited attrs}} 1$ | Upper bound on markers added. |
| 4 | **L-Attributed Test** | For all $X_j.a := f(\dots)$, $\text{args}(f) \subseteq \{A\text{ attrs}\} \cup \{X_i \text{ attrs} \mid i < j\}$ | Necessary & sufficient. |
| 5 | **Bottom-up Safety Theorem** | $G^{\prime}$ admits a single LR-driven annotation pass | Holds *iff* base $G$ is LAD. |
| 6 | **State-count Multiplier** | $\vert \text{States}(G^{\prime}) \vert \le C \cdot \vert \text{States}(G) \vert$ for some constant $C$ | $C$ depends on inherited-attribute density. |
| 7 | **Reduction Sequence** | For $M \to \varepsilon$: $M.syn$ is computed *at* reduction | Uses top-of-stack values. |
| 8 | **Independence from Order** | Result of bottom-up pass = result of hypothetical top-down pass | For LADs only. |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Compiler Construction (YACC/Bison)** | Enables full type-checking and code-gen during shift-reduce parsing. |
| **IDE Static Analysis** | Real-time type inference and scope resolution in language servers. |
| **DSL Design** | Embedding business rules into parsers (financial DSLs, query languages). |
| **Verilog/VHDL Synthesis** | Hardware description languages need inherited attributes (bit-widths) during a single pass. |
| **Database Query Compilers** | SQL parsers evaluate schema-dependent (inherited) info in one bottom-up sweep. |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example (Aho-Sethi-Ullman Style)

### Original Grammar (L-Attributed)

Consider the following fragment for a typed declaration list (typical Pascal-style):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &\to T \; L \\
T &\to \text{int} \;\mid\; \text{real} \\
L &\to L_1 , \text{id} \\
L &\to \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Semantic Rules (LAD):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& L.in := T.type \\
& L_1.in := L.in \\
& \text{addtype}(\text{id}.entry, L.in) \quad \text{(at each } L \text{ rule)} \\
& T.type := \text{integer} \quad \text{(for } T \to \text{int)} \\
& T.type := \text{real} \quad \text{(for } T \to \text{real)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** $L.in$ is *inherited*, depending on $T.type$ (a synthesized attribute of the *left sibling*). LR parsers reduce $L$ **before** $T$ is fully reduced to $D$. We must pass the type *forward*.

---

### 3.2 Transformation Walkthrough — Line by Line

**Step 1: Identify inherited attributes on RHS**

In the production $D \to T \; L$, the symbol $L$ has an inherited attribute $L.in$. Therefore we need a marker **between** $T$ and $L$.

In the production $L \to L_1 , \text{id}$, the symbol $L_1$ has an inherited attribute $L_1.in$. Therefore we need a marker **before** $L_1$.

**Step 2: Insert marker non-terminals**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &\to T \; M_1 \; L \\
L &\to M_2 \; L_1 , \; M_3 \; \text{id} \\
L &\to M_4 \; \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Add marker productions**

$$
M_1 \to \varepsilon, \quad M_2 \to \varepsilon, \quad M_3 \to \varepsilon, \quad M_4 \to \varepsilon
$$

**Step 4: Attach synthesized "copy" attributes to each marker**

For $M_1$: at the moment of reducing $M_1 \to \varepsilon$, the value of $T.type$ is on top of the stack. Therefore:

$$ M_1.syn \;:=\; T.type $$

For $M_2$: at reduction time, $L.in$ is available (because $M_2$ appears *after* $L$ has begun processing but *before* $L_1$). Hence:

$$ M_2.syn \;:=\; L.in $$

For $M_3$: this marker appears *after* $L_1$ has been reduced, so $L_1.in$ is available:

$$ M_3.syn \;:=\; L_1.in $$

For $M_4$: similar reasoning to $M_2$:

$$ M_4.syn \;:=\; L.in $$

**Step 5: Rewrite the original semantic rules to read from markers**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L.in &:= M_1.syn \\
L_1.in &:= M_2.syn \\
\text{addtype}(\text{id}.entry, M_3.syn) & \quad \text{(replaces the old rule at } L \to L_1 , \text{id)} \\
\text{addtype}(\text{id}.entry, M_4.syn) & \quad \text{(replaces the old rule at } L \to \text{id)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Final Transformed Grammar (S-Attributed)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &\to T \; M_1 \; L \qquad \{L.in := M_1.syn\} \\
L &\to M_2 \; L_1 , \; M_3 \; \text{id} \qquad \{L_1.in := M_2.syn;\; \text{addtype}(\text{id}.entry, M_3.syn)\} \\
L &\to M_4 \; \text{id} \qquad \{\text{addtype}(\text{id}.entry, M_4.syn)\} \\
T &\to \text{int} \qquad \{T.type := \text{integer}\} \\
T &\to \text{real} \qquad \{T.type := \text{real}\} \\
M_1 &\to \varepsilon \qquad \{M_1.syn := T.type\} \\
M_2 &\to \varepsilon \qquad \{M_2.syn := L.in\} \\
M_3 &\to \varepsilon \qquad \{M_3.syn := L_1.in\} \\
M_4 &\to \varepsilon \qquad \{M_4.syn := L.in\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Every attribute is now **synthesized** — they are all computed from children or already-computed siblings. A single LR parse can evaluate everything in **post-order**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Reduction Sequence for Input `int id1 , id2`

| Step | Stack (top on right) | Action | Attribute Computed |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `int` | shift | — |
| 2 | `int T` | reduce $T \to \text{int}$ | $T.type = \text{integer}$ |
| 3 | `int T M₁` | reduce $M_1 \to \varepsilon$ | $M_1.syn = T.type = \text{integer}$ |
| 4 | `int T M₁ id` | shift | — |
| 5 | `int T M₁ id M₄` | reduce $M_4 \to \varepsilon$ | $M_4.syn = L.in$ (from $M_1.syn$ on stack) = `integer` |
| 6 | `int T M₁ L` | reduce $L \to M_4 \text{id}$ | `addtype(id1.entry, integer)` |
| 7 | `int T M₁ L ,` | shift | — |
| 8 | `int T M₁ L , id` | shift | — |
| 9 | `int T M₁ L , id M₃` | reduce $M_3 \to \varepsilon$ | $M_3.syn = L_1.in$ (from stack) = `integer` |
| 10 | `int T M₁ L` | reduce $L \to M_2 L_1 , M_3 \text{id}$ | `addtype(id2.entry, integer)` |
| 11 | `int T M₁ L D` | reduce $D \to T M_1 L$ | $L.in = M_1.syn = \text{integer}$ (carried forward) |
| 12 | Accept | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> Every inherited lookup was replaced by a *direct read* of a synthesized marker attribute. **No second pass is needed.**

---

### 3.5 Generalization (Formal Statement)

For an arbitrary production $A \to X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$ with $k$ inherited-attribute-bearing symbols on the RHS, the transformed production is:

$$
A \to M_1 \, X_1 \, M_2 \, X_2 \, \dots \, M_n \, X_n \quad (M_j \to \varepsilon)
$$

The number of **new symbols** added is $\sum_{p \in P} \text{(inherited-attribute count of RHS symbols of } p\text{)}$, and the number of **new productions** equals this same sum (each marker gets one $\varepsilon$-production).

$$
\begin{aligned}
|\Sigma_{G^{\prime}}| &\le |\Sigma_G| + \sum_{p \in P} \text{IAR}(p) \\
|P_{G^{\prime}}| &\le |P_G| + \sum_{p \in P} \text{IAR}(p)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\text{IAR}(p)$ = *inherited-attribute requirement* of production $p$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow: LAD → SAD Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input L-Attributed Definition LAD] --> B[Verify L-Attributed Property]
    B --> C{Is L-Attributed?}
    C -->|No| D[Stop: Rewrite Grammar First]
    C -->|Yes| E[Scan All Productions for Inherited Attributes on RHS]
    E --> F[For Each Production, Identify Inherited-Attribute Symbols]
    F --> G[Insert Marker Non-Terminals M sub i before Each Such Symbol]
    G --> H[Add Epsilon Productions M sub i to epsilon]
    H --> I[Attach Synthesized Copy Attributes to Markers]
    I --> J[Rewrite Inherited Assignments as Marker Reads]
    J --> K[Check for New LR Conflicts]
    K --> L{Conflicts Detected?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Apply Left-Factoring or Grammar Refinement]
    M --> E
    L -->|No| N[Output Equivalent S-Attributed Definition SAD]
    N --> O[Use Single Bottom-Up LR Pass for Evaluation]
```

## 4.2 Attribute-Value Flow During a Reduction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Parent A attributes] --> M1[Marker M1]
    M1 -->|M1.syn inherited value| X1[Symbol X1]
    X1 -->|X1.syn synthesized output| M2[Marker M2]
    M2 -->|M2.syn inherited value| X2[Symbol X2]
    X2 -->|X2.syn synthesized output| RED[Reduction A to X1 X2]
    RED -->|Final synthesized result| TOP[Top of Stack]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: YACC/Bison Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[Source Code Tokens] --> LEX[Lexical Analyzer]
    LEX --> TOK[Token Stream]
    TOK --> PARSE[LR Parser Engine]
    PARSE --> STK[Parser Stack with Attribute Slots]
    STK --> RED[Reduction Actions Invoke Semantic Rules]
    RED --> MARK[Marker Productions M to epsilon Execute]
    MARK --> SYN[Synthesized Attributes Computed]
    SYN --> STK
    SYN --> OUT[Intermediate Code or AST]
    OUT --> NEXT[Subsequent Compilation Phases]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix: Top-Down vs. Bottom-Up Inherited-Attribute Handling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TD[Traditional Top-Down Pass]
        T1[Build Tree] --> T2[Annotate Top-Down Pre-Order]
        T2 --> T3[Annotate Bottom-Up Post-Order]
    end
    subgraph BU[Marker-Based Bottom-Up Pass]
        B1[LR Parse with Built-In Annotation] --> B2[Single Post-Order Sweep]
    end
    TD -.->|Two Passes| OUT[Result]
    BU -.->|One Pass| OUT
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q:** Define an **L-attributed definition**. Why is the L-attributed property a prerequisite for bottom-up evaluation of inherited attributes?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **L-attributed definition** is a syntax-directed definition in which every inherited attribute of a symbol $X_j$ on the right side of a production $A \to X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$ depends *only* on:
1. The (inherited or synthesized) attributes of $A$ (the parent), and
2. The attributes of $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_{j-1}$ (siblings to the left of $X_j$).

The L-attributed property is a prerequisite for bottom-up evaluation because an LR parser processes the input strictly **left-to-right** and reduces children before parents. Therefore, any inherited attribute must be computable from information that is *already on the parsing stack* when the symbol is processed. This is precisely what the L-attributed restriction guarantees — no inherited attribute ever needs a right sibling's value. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q:** What is a **marker non-terminal** in the context of bottom-up attribute evaluation? Give an example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **marker non-terminal** is a new symbol $M$ added to the grammar that produces the empty string ($M \to \varepsilon$) and carries a **synthesized attribute** whose value is the inherited attribute that needs to be passed forward. Markers do not change the language of the grammar but allow inherited values to be "materialized" on the parser stack before the corresponding non-terminal is reduced.

**Example:** For production $D \to T \; L$ with semantic rule $L.in := T.type$, we transform it into:
$$ D \to T \; M \; L, \quad M \to \varepsilon \;\; \{\, M.syn := T.type \,\}, \quad \{L.in := M.syn\} $$
The marker $M$ acts as a "buffer" that holds $T.type$ until $L$ is processed. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU ESE pattern: Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice is provided.

---

### Question B1 (A) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the procedure for evaluating inherited attributes during a bottom-up parse. Clearly state the preconditions and the role of marker non-terminals.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Precondition Check (1 mark):** The grammar must be **L-attributed**. Formally, for every production $A \to X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$ and every inherited attribute $X_j.a$, the defining expression $f(\dots)$ may reference only:
- Attributes of $A$ (the parent).
- Attributes of $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_{j-1}$ (left siblings).

If this fails, the transformation is not directly applicable; the grammar must be rewritten.

**Step 2 — Identify Inherited Attributes on RHS (1 mark):** Scan all productions and for each RHS symbol $X_j$ that possesses an inherited attribute, flag the position just before $X_j$ for marker insertion.

**Step 3 — Insert Marker Non-Terminals (2 marks):** For each flagged position, create a fresh non-terminal $M_j$ and rewrite the production as $A \to \dots M_j X_j \dots$. Add the production $M_j \to \varepsilon$ to the grammar. Since $M_j$ derives $\varepsilon$, the language $L(G)$ is preserved.

**Step 4 — Attach Synthesized "Copy" Attributes (2 marks):** To each marker $M_j$, attach a synthesized attribute $M_j.syn$ that is computed within the semantic action of $M_j \to \varepsilon$. The action references whatever attributes of $A$ and $X_1, \dots, X_{j-1}$ are available on the parser stack at reduction time. The value of $M_j.syn$ is *exactly* the value that the original semantic rule would have assigned to $X_j.in$.

**Step 5 — Rewrite Inherited-Assignment Rules (1 mark):** Replace each original rule $X_j.in := \dots$ with $X_j.in := M_j.syn$. All other semantic rules remain unchanged.

The result is an S-attributed definition that a single LR parse can evaluate in one bottom-up sweep. **(Total: 7 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the following grammar for variable declarations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& S \to D \\
& D \to T \; L \\
& T \to \text{real} \;\mid\; \text{int} \\
& L \to L , \text{id} \;\mid\; \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

with semantic rules: $L.in := T.type$, and `addtype(id.entry, L.in)` at each L-production. Show the **complete transformation** for bottom-up evaluation.

**Model Solution:**

**Original semantic actions:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \{L.in := T.type\} \quad \text{in } D \to T \, L \\
& \{\text{addtype}(\text{id}.entry, L.in)\} \quad \text{in } L \to \text{id} \\
& \{L_1.in := L.in;\; \text{addtype}(\text{id}.entry, L_1.in)\} \quad \text{in } L \to L_1 , \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Transformed grammar (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& S \to D \\
& D \to T \; M_1 \; L \qquad \{L.in := M_1.syn\} \\
& L \to M_2 \; L_1 , \; M_3 \; \text{id} \qquad \{L_1.in := M_2.syn;\; \text{addtype}(\text{id}.entry, M_3.syn)\} \\
& L \to M_4 \; \text{id} \qquad \{\text{addtype}(\text{id}.entry, M_4.syn)\} \\
& T \to \text{real} \qquad \{T.type := \text{real}\} \\
& T \to \text{int} \qquad \{T.type := \text{int}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Marker productions and their actions (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& M_1 \to \varepsilon \qquad \{M_1.syn := T.type\} \\
& M_2 \to \varepsilon \qquad \{M_2.syn := L.in\} \\
& M_3 \to \varepsilon \qquad \{M_3.syn := L_1.in\} \\
& M_4 \to \varepsilon \qquad \{M_4.syn := L.in\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Justification of correctness (2 marks):** At the moment $M_1$ is reduced (after $T$ but before $L$), the value of $T.type$ is on the top of the parser stack. The action $\{M_1.syn := T.type\}$ therefore captures the type and "carries" it forward as a synthesized attribute. The subsequent rule $\{L.in := M_1.syn\}$ makes this value the inherited attribute of $L$, exactly as the original L-attributed rule intended. The same logic applies to $M_2, M_3, M_4$. The resulting grammar is purely S-attributed and admits a single LR-driven evaluation. **(Total: 7 marks)**

---

### Question B1 (B) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **S-attributed** and **L-attributed** definitions. Under what conditions can an L-attributed definition be evaluated during a bottom-up parse, and what role do **marker non-terminals** play in this process?

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Property | S-Attributed | L-Attributed |
|---|---|---|
| Attribute Types Allowed | Only synthesized | Synthesized + Inherited (with restrictions) |
| Evaluation Order | Post-order (bottom-up) | Pre-order then post-order (in general) |
| Compatibility with LR Parsers | Direct, no transformation | Requires transformation via markers |
| Expressive Power | Subset of L-attributed | Strictly more general |
| Typical Use | Expression type-checking, code emission | Variable declarations, scope handling |

**Conditions for Bottom-up Evaluation (2 marks):** An L-attributed definition can be evaluated during a single bottom-up pass *if and only if* it is possible to insert marker non-terminals such that (a) the language is preserved (only $\varepsilon$-productions added) and (b) the resulting grammar has no LR conflicts. The L-attributed property is a *necessary* condition because the LR parser processes tokens left-to-right, and an inherited attribute depending on a right sibling's value would not yet be available on the stack.

**Role of Marker Non-Terminals (2 marks):** Marker non-terminals serve as *value-carrier placeholders* that:
- Derive the empty string (preserving the language).
- Carry a synthesized attribute whose value is the inherited attribute needed by the next non-terminal.
- Trigger their semantic action at the *exact* moment the inherited value becomes computable from the stack.

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given the production $A \to B \, C \, D$ with the following L-attributed semantic rules:
- $B.in := A.s$
- $C.in := B.val + A.s$
- $D.in := C.val$

Show the **transformation** for bottom-up evaluation. Write down the final S-attributed grammar.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify markers needed (1 mark):** $B$, $C$, and $D$ each have an inherited attribute, so we need three markers $M_B, M_C, M_D$ placed before $B$, $C$, and $D$ respectively.

**Step 2 — Insert markers and rewrite production (2 marks):**

$$ A \to M_B \; B \; M_C \; C \; M_D \; D $$

**Step 3 — Add marker productions and actions (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_B &\to \varepsilon \qquad \{M_B.syn := A.s\} \\
M_C &\to \varepsilon \qquad \{M_C.syn := B.val + A.s\} \\
M_D &\to \varepsilon \qquad \{M_D.syn := C.val\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Rewrite inherited assignments (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B.in &:= M_B.syn \\
C.in &:= M_C.syn \\
D.in &:= M_D.syn
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final S-attributed grammar (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\to M_B \; B \; M_C \; C \; M_D \; D \\
B.in &:= M_B.syn, \quad B.val := \dots \\
C.in &:= M_C.syn, \quad C.val := \dots \\
D.in &:= M_D.syn, \quad D.val := \dots \\
A.s &:= \text{(some expression over children)} \\
M_B &\to \varepsilon \quad \{M_B.syn := A.s\} \\
M_C &\to \varepsilon \quad \{M_C.syn := B.val + A.s\} \\
M_D &\to \varepsilon \quad \{M_D.syn := C.val\}
\end{aligned}
$$

All attributes are now synthesized; a single LR pass suffices. **(Total: 7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the L-Attributedness Check (–2 to –3 marks):** Many students jump straight to marker insertion without verifying that the definition is L-attributed. If the definition is *not* L-attributed, the transformation is **invalid** — examiners deduct heavily.
>
> 2. **Confusing Marker Position (–1 to –2 marks):** The marker $M_i$ must be placed *immediately before* the symbol $X_i$ whose inherited attribute it carries, not *after*. Placing it after means the inherited value is read too late.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the $\varepsilon$-Production (–1 mark):** The marker is useless without its production $M_i \to \varepsilon$. Forgetting to add it breaks the grammar.
>
> 4. **Mixing Up Attribute Direction (–2 marks):** Writing $M_i.syn := X_i.in$ instead of $X_i.in := M_i.syn$ reverses the data flow. The marker must *supply* the inherited value, not consume it.
>
> 5. **Ignoring LR Conflicts (–1 to –2 marks):** The transformation may introduce shift-reduce or reduce-reduce conflicts. If unaddressed, the grammar may no longer be LR(1), and the bottom-up strategy collapses.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Bottom-up evaluation of inherited attributes = L-attributed definition + marker non-terminal transformation → S-attributed definition.**
- ✅ **Marker non-terminal $M \to \varepsilon$** is the cornerstone; it carries a synthesized "copy" of the inherited value.
- ✅ **Precondition (Mandatory):** The definition must be **L-attributed** — inherited attributes may *only* depend on the parent and **left** siblings.
- ✅ **Language Preservation:** Inserting $M \to \varepsilon$ does not change $L(G)$.
- ✅ **Parsing Conflicts:** Marker insertion may introduce LR(1) conflicts — verify with an LR-parser generator (YACC/Bison).
- ✅ **Resulting Definition Type:** The transformed definition is **S-attributed**, fully compatible with a single bottom-up (LR) pass.
- ✅ **Algorithm Steps (memorize):** Check LAD → Identify inherited attrs on RHS → Insert markers → Add $\varepsilon$-productions → Attach synthesized copy actions → Rewrite inherited assignments as reads.
- ✅ **Key Formulas:**
  - $L(G) = L(G^{\prime})$
  - $X_i.in \equiv M_i.syn$ after transformation
  - $|P_{G^{\prime}}| \le |P_G| + \sum_p \text{IAR}(p)$
- ✅ **Common KTU Traps:** Forgetting the L-attributed check, misplacing markers, omitting the $\varepsilon$-production, reversing attribute flow direction.
- ✅ **Engineering Tools:** YACC, Bison, ANTLR (with adapted strategy) all rely on this principle for industrial compiler construction.
- ✅ **Counter-example to remember:** A rule like $X_2.in := f(X_3.syn)$ is **NOT** L-attributed and cannot be transformed by this technique.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Type Checking: Type Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Type Checking: Type Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type System (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **type system** is a formal, mathematically tractable framework used in programming language theory and compiler design to define, classify, and enforce the data types of program constructs (variables, expressions, functions, modules). It is a collection of rules that assigns a **type attribute** to every program construct (syntactic phrase) and verifies that the program obeys the language's **type compatibility** and **type safety** rules without violating any **well-typedness** constraints.

The **Type Checker** is the phase of the compiler (typically a sub-phase of **Semantic Analysis**) that implements the type system. It traverses the **Syntax Tree (AST)** produced by the parser, applies type rules, infers types where they are not explicitly declared, detects **type errors**, and either accepts the program as **well-typed** or rejects it with a diagnostic message.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Exam Favorite):**
> - **Type System** → The *abstract rules* (mathematical/logical specification).
> - **Type Checker** → The *concrete algorithm* (implementation) that enforces those rules.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **pharmaceutical prescription system** at a hospital:

1. Every drug is assigned a **class** (Antibiotic, Analgesic, Antiviral) — this is the **type**.
2. The pharmacy database contains **rules**: "Antibiotics cannot be mixed with Anticoagulants without doctor override" — these are the **type rules**.
3. When a doctor writes a prescription, the **pharmacy validation software** automatically checks the combination — this is the **type checker**.
4. If a forbidden combination appears, the prescription is **rejected** — this is a **type error**.

In a programming language:
- **Variables** are like drug containers — each has a type (`int`, `float`, `string`).
- **Operators and function calls** are like prescriptions — they consume and produce specific types.
- **Type rules** define what combinations are valid.
- **Type checker** is the compiler component that enforces them.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Students:** *"A type system is the **grammar of meaning** — it tells the compiler what combinations of values make semantic sense."*

---

## 1.3 Core Components of a Type System (KTU Exam High-Yield)

| Component | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Base Types** | Primitive, indivisible types built into the language | `int`, `float`, `char`, `bool` |
| **Type Constructors** | Mechanisms to build complex types from simpler ones | Arrays, Pointers, Records, Functions |
| **Type Rules** | Logical inference rules defining valid type usage | `int + int → int` |
| **Type Environment (Γ)** | A mapping from identifiers to their types | `Γ ⊢ x : int` |
| **Type Equality / Compatibility** | When two types are considered the same | `int ≡ int`, structural vs nominal |
| **Type Inference** | Algorithm to deduce the type without explicit declaration | ML, Haskell style: `let x = 5 → x : int` |

---

## 1.4 Static vs Dynamic Type Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Type Checking:** Performed at **compile time** without executing the program. Examples: C, C++, Java, Rust, TypeScript.
> **Dynamic Type Checking:** Performed at **runtime** as the program executes. Examples: Python, Ruby, JavaScript (in some modes), PHP.

KTU 2024 strongly emphasizes **static type checking** since it falls in the compiler's semantic analysis phase.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formal Specification of a Type System

A type system is formally defined as a tuple:

$$ \mathcal{T} = \langle \text{Types}, \text{Rules}, \text{Judgments}, \text{Environment} \rangle $$

Where:
- **Types** = the set of all type expressions (e.g., `int`, `bool`, `int → int`).
- **Rules** = inference rules of the form: $\dfrac{\text{premise}_1 \quad \text{premise}_2}{\text{conclusion}}$.
- **Judgments** = statements of the form `Γ ⊢ e : τ` ("In environment Γ, expression `e` has type τ").
- **Environment (Γ)** = a type mapping (symbol table context).

---

## 2.2 Type Expressions

A **type expression** is a denotation of a type. The grammar for type expressions:

$$ \tau \rightarrow \text{type-id} \mid \text{array}(\tau, \text{num}) \mid \text{record}((\text{id}:\tau)^*) \mid \text{pointer}(\tau) \mid \tau \rightarrow \tau $$

For example:
- `int` → atomic type-id
- `array(int, 10)` → integer array of size 10
- `pointer(float)` → pointer to float
- `int → bool` → function taking int, returning bool

---

## 2.3 Type Rules — The Heart of the System

Type rules follow a logical pattern:

$$ \dfrac{\text{premises (above the line)}}{\text{conclusion (below the line)}} $$

### Standard Rules (Intuition-Building)

**1. Variable Rule (lookup):**

$$ \dfrac{x : \tau \in \Gamma}{\Gamma \vdash x : \tau} \quad \text{(If x is declared with type τ in Γ, then x has type τ)} $$

**2. Addition Rule:**

$$ \dfrac{\Gamma \vdash e_1 : \text{int} \quad \Gamma \vdash e_2 : \text{int}}{\Gamma \vdash e_1 + e_2 : \text{int}} $$

**3. Function Application:**

$$ \dfrac{\Gamma \vdash e_1 : \tau_1 \rightarrow \tau_2 \quad \Gamma \vdash e_2 : \tau_1}{\Gamma \vdash e_1(e_2) : \tau_2} $$

> [!TIP]
> **"Why" and "How" Intuition:**
> The "why" of type rules is to mathematically *prove* that a program cannot go wrong at runtime due to type mismatches. The "how" is by recursively traversing the AST and applying these rules bottom-up (post-order traversal), accumulating types until the root gives the type of the entire program.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formal Notation / Rule | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Type Judgment** | $\Gamma \vdash e : \tau$ | "Under environment Γ, expression e has type τ" |
| 2 | **Type Environment Extension** | $\Gamma, x : \tau$ | Extend Γ with new binding x → τ |
| 3 | **Function Type** | $\tau_1 \rightarrow \tau_2$ | Maps τ₁ inputs to τ₂ outputs |
| 4 | **Product / Record** | $\tau_1 \times \tau_2$ | Tuple / struct of two types |
| 5 | **Sum / Variant** | $\tau_1 + \tau_2$ | Discriminated union type |
| 6 | **Subtype Rule** | $\dfrac{\tau_1 \le \tau_2}{\Gamma \vdash e : \tau_2}$ | If τ₁ is a subtype of τ₂, value of type τ₁ can be used as τ₂ |
| 7 | **Polymorphic Instantiation** | $\dfrac{\Gamma \vdash e : \forall \alpha. \tau}{\Gamma \vdash e : \tau[\tau'/\alpha]}$ | Substitute type τ' for universal type variable α |
| 8 | **Hindley-Milner Unification** | $\text{unify}(\tau_1, \tau_2) = \sigma$ | Find substitution σ making τ₁ and τ₂ equal |
| 9 | **Worst-Cast Inference Complexity** | $O(n)$ to $O(n^3)$ | Linear for simple, cubic for full Hindley-Milner |
| 10 | **Type Equivalence** | $\text{struct\_equiv}(\tau_1, \tau_2) = \text{true}$ | Structural (name-free) equality check |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Pipe Character Rule:** Whenever you see notation like $\vert x \vert$ in formulas, use `\vert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking the markdown table. For example: $\vert \Gamma \vert$ denotes the size of the environment.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Type Systems |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Systems (Automotive/Aerospace)** | MISRA-C type rules prevent entire classes of runtime faults; type-safe kernels in seL4. |
| **Database Engines** | Strong type checking in SQL optimizers prevents implicit conversion bugs. |
| **Compilers & JIT (LLVM, CLR)** | LLVM's typed IR (`i32`, `float`, `ptr`) enables aggressive optimization and safety proofs. |
| **Network Protocols** | Protocol Buffers / Cap'n Proto enforce type contracts across language boundaries. |
| **Formal Verification** | Languages like Coq, Idris, F* use dependent types to mathematically prove program correctness. |
| **Web Security** | TypeScript prevents injection attacks by catching unsafe type usage at build time. |

---

## 2.6 Classification of Type Systems (Hierarchical)

1. **Simple / Monomorphic** — Each variable has exactly one type. (e.g., early C, Pascal)
2. **Polymorphic** — One name can have many types.
   - *Parametric* (generics) — e.g., `List<T>` in Java, C++ templates.
   - *Ad-hoc (Overloading)* — Same name, different implementations per type. e.g., `+` for int vs float.
   - *Subtype* — Subclass can be used where supertype is expected.
3. **Type Inference-based** — Types deduced automatically (Hindley-Milner in ML/Haskell).
4. **Dependent Type Systems** — Types can depend on values (Idris, Agda, Coq).
5. **Gradual Typing** — Mix of static and dynamic (TypeScript, Python with hints).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Type Checking an Expression Tree

Consider the expression: `(x + y) * z`

**Given Type Environment:**
- `x : int`
- `y : int`
- `z : float`

**Goal:** Determine the type of the entire expression.

---

### Step 1 — Represent the Expression as an AST

The abstract syntax tree is structured as:

```
         *
        / \
       +   z
      / \
     x   y
```

Where:
- Leaf nodes `x`, `y`, `z` are identifier nodes.
- Internal node `+` is the addition operator.
- Internal node `*` is the multiplication operator.

---

### Step 2 — Recursive Type Inference (Post-Order Traversal)

We apply type rules in a bottom-up fashion. The derivation proceeds as:

**Step 2a — Type-check `x`:**

$$
\Gamma \vdash x : \text{int} \quad \text{(by variable lookup rule)}
$$

**Step 2b — Type-check `y`:**

$$
\Gamma \vdash y : \text{int} \quad \text{(by variable lookup rule)}
$$

**Step 2c — Type-check `x + y`:**

$$
\dfrac{\Gamma \vdash x : \text{int} \quad \Gamma \vdash y : \text{int}}{\Gamma \vdash x + y : \text{int}} \quad \text{(by integer addition rule)}
$$

**Step 2d — Type-check `z`:**

$$
\Gamma \vdash z : \text{float} \quad \text{(by variable lookup rule)}
$$

**Step 2e — Attempt to type-check `(x + y) * z`:**

We need a rule of the form:

$$
\dfrac{\Gamma \vdash e_1 : \tau_1 \quad \Gamma \vdash e_2 : \tau_2}{\Gamma \vdash e_1 * e_2 : \tau_3}
$$

For a strongly-typed language with **no implicit conversion**, the rule typically requires $\tau_1 = \tau_2$:

$$
\dfrac{\Gamma \vdash e_1 : \text{int} \quad \Gamma \vdash e_2 : \text{int}}{\Gamma \vdash e_1 * e_2 : \text{int}} \quad \text{(homogeneous multiplication rule)}
$$

But here $\tau_1 = \text{int}$ and $\tau_2 = \text{float}$. They are **not equal** under strict typing.

---

### Step 3 — Conclusion: Type Error Detected

$$
\dfrac{\Gamma \vdash (x + y) : \text{int} \quad \Gamma \vdash z : \text{float} \quad \text{int} \neq \text{float}}{\text{Type Error: incompatible operands of '*'}}
$$

The type checker emits an error such as:

> **"ERROR: Operator '*' has incompatible operand types: 'int' and 'float'."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic in Words:**
> Step 1 builds the AST (data structure). Step 2 walks the tree from leaves to root, applying the most specific inference rule whose premises match the child types. Step 3 identifies a failed unification — a *mismatch* — and reports it. This is exactly the Hindley-Milner unification algorithm in action.

---

## 3.2 Algorithmic Type Inference (Hindley-Milner Unification)

The Hindley-Milner (HM) algorithm performs type inference in **three logical steps**. Let us derive them.

### Step 1 — Generate Type Equations

Walk the AST and produce a set of **equations** between type variables. For our example:

- Assign fresh type variables: $x \to \alpha_1$, $y \to \alpha_2$, $z \to \alpha_3$.
- For `+` between $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$, generate: $\alpha_1 = \alpha_2$.
- For `*` between $(\alpha_1 = \alpha_2)$ and $\alpha_3$, generate: $\alpha_1 = \alpha_3$ (if homogeneous required).

This gives the equation set:

$$
S = \{ \alpha_1 = \alpha_2, \quad \alpha_1 = \alpha_3 \}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Unification

The **unify** function solves the system by finding a substitution $\sigma$ that satisfies all equations.

**Unification Algorithm (Robinson, 1965):**

$$
\text{unify}(S) = 
\begin{cases}
\emptyset & \text{if } S = \emptyset \quad \text{(success — no constraints left)} \\
\text{unify}(S \setminus \{ \tau = \tau \}) & \text{(rule 1: trivial equation, remove)} \\
\text{let } \sigma = [\tau' / \alpha] \text{ in unify}(S\sigma \cup \{ \alpha = \tau' \}) & \text{(rule 2: variable binding)} \\
\text{FAIL} & \text{(occurs-check violation or type mismatch)}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Substitution to Get Concrete Types

If unify succeeds, we get $\sigma = \{ \alpha_1 \mapsto \text{int}, \alpha_2 \mapsto \text{int}, \alpha_3 \mapsto \text{float} \}$.

Then check: $\sigma(\alpha_1) = \sigma(\alpha_3)$? We get `int` vs `float` — **mismatch** → type error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Complexity Note:** Robinson's unification runs in $O(n \cdot \alpha(n))$ where $\alpha$ is the inverse Ackermann function — nearly linear in practice. However, the worst-case occurs-check is $O(n)$. The full HM algorithm, with let-polymorphism, runs in $O(n^3)$ in the worst case.

---

## 3.3 Type Compatibility — Two Major Equivalences

> [!TIP]
> This is a classic KTU 2-mark question.

### A. Name Equivalence (Nominal Typing)

Two types are equivalent **if and only if** they have the **same declared name** in the source.

Example: In C, `typedef int MY_INT;` and `int` are **name-equivalent** only if explicitly declared so, but in most C compilers, `MY_INT` and `int` are interchangeable because `typedef` creates an *alias*, not a *new type*.

### B. Structure Equivalence (Structural Typing)

Two types are equivalent **if their internal structure is identical**, regardless of name.

Example:

$$
\text{struct } \{ \text{int } a; \text{ float } b; \} \equiv \text{struct } \{ \text{int } a; \text{ float } b; \}
$$

even if declared under different names in different modules.

| Aspect | Name Equivalence | Structure Equivalence |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Faster (hash-table lookup) | Slower (recursive comparison) |
| Used In | C++, Java, C# | ML, Haskell, Go (interfaces), TypeScript |
| Pros | Clear, prevents accidental matches | Flexible, supports structural typing |
| Cons | Verbose, less reusable | Surprising matches possible |

---

## 3.4 Conversion Logic: How an Inference Rule Becomes Code

The formal rule:

$$
\dfrac{\Gamma \vdash e_1 : \text{int} \quad \Gamma \vdash e_2 : \text{int}}{\Gamma \vdash e_1 + e_2 : \text{int}}
$$

Becomes the following recursive function (conceptual, not for KTU coding questions):

```
function typeOf(e, Γ):
    if e is x: return Γ.lookup(x)
    if e is e1 + e2:
        t1 = typeOf(e1, Γ)
        t2 = typeOf(e2, Γ)
        if t1 == int and t2 == int: return int
        else: error("Type mismatch in +")
    if e is e1 * e2: similar
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation of Logic:** The recursive structure of the function *mirrors* the recursive structure of the inference rule. Each rule becomes one branch of the function. The pattern "premises above, conclusion below" maps directly to "check children, return result."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Type Checking Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This diagram shows where **Type Checking** sits in the compiler and how it interacts with the symbol table, AST, and downstream phases.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Code .c file] --> B[Lexical Analyzer]
    B --> C[Token Stream]
    C --> D[Syntax Analyzer Parser]
    D --> E[Abstract Syntax Tree AST]
    E --> F[Semantic Analyzer]
    F --> G[Type Checker]
    H[Symbol Table] --> G
    G --> I{Type Error?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Error Recovery and Diagnostics]
    I -->|No| K[Annotated AST with Types]
    J --> L[Report to User]
    K --> M[Intermediate Code Generator]
    M --> N[Three Address Code TAC]
    N --> O[Optimizer]
    O --> P[Code Generator]
    P --> Q[Target Machine Code]
```

**Node Descriptions:**
- `Lexical Analyzer` — Tokenizes the source.
- `Syntax Analyzer Parser` — Builds the AST.
- `Semantic Analyzer` — Hosts the type checker.
- `Symbol Table` — Provides the type environment Γ.
- `Type Checker` — Applies inference rules.
- `Annotated AST` — AST with `τ` annotations on every node.
- `Intermediate Code Generator` — Next phase (Module 4 in KTU syllabus).

---

## 4.2 Type Inference Flowchart (Hindley-Milner Algorithm)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: AST node e, Environment Γ]) --> A{Is e a variable?}
    A -->|Yes| B[Look up in Γ]
    B --> C[Return its type τ]
    A -->|No| D{Is e a literal?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Return base type int, bool, etc]
    D -->|No| F{Is e an application f arg?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Infer t1 = typeOf f]
    G --> H[Infer t2 = typeOf arg]
    H --> I[t1 must be of form a to b]
    I --> J[Unify a with t2]
    J --> K{Unify succeeds?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Return b]
    K -->|No| M[Type Error]
    F -->|No| N{Is e a lambda x dot body?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Introduce fresh type var a for x]
    O --> P[Augment Γ with x to a]
    P --> Q[Infer t = typeOf body]
    Q --> R[Return a to t]
    N -->|No| S{Is e a let binding?}
    S -->|Yes| T[Infer t for rhs]
    T --> U[Generalize t to scheme]
    U --> V[Infer body type with new Γ]
    V --> W[Return body type]
```

---

## 4.3 Type System Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Type System] --> S1[Static]
    Root --> S2[Dynamic]
    S1 --> M1[Monomorphic]
    S1 --> M2[Polymorphic]
    M2 --> P1[Parametric Generics]
    M2 --> P2[Ad hoc Overloading]
    M2 --> P3[Subtype Inclusion]
    S1 --> M3[Dependent]
    S1 --> M4[Gradual]
    S1 --> M5[Inferred HM]
```

**Description of Hierarchy:**
- **Static** types are checked at compile time.
- **Dynamic** types are checked at runtime.
- **Polymorphic** has three sub-flavors (parametric, ad-hoc, subtype).
- **Dependent** types are the most expressive (e.g., Coq, Idris).
- **Gradual** types allow mixing static and dynamic (e.g., TypeScript).
- **Inferred (HM)** means the compiler deduces types (Haskell, ML).

---

## 4.4 Concrete Worked Example — Inferred Types for a Mini-Language

For the following program:

```
let id = fun x -> x in
let a = id 5 in
let b = id true in
a + b
```

The HM algorithm infers:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[let id = fun x to x] --> B[id has type a to a polymorphic]
    B --> C[Apply id to 5]
    C --> D[a unifies with int]
    D --> E[a becomes int]
    B --> F[Apply id to true]
    F --> G[a unifies with bool]
    G --> H[CONFLICT int vs bool]
    H --> I[Type Error: cannot unify int with bool]
```

**Conversion Logic in Words:**
- First, `id` is given a polymorphic type $\forall \alpha. \alpha \to \alpha$.
- Using it with `5` constrains $\alpha = \text{int}$.
- Reusing the *same* `id` with `true` would force $\alpha = \text{bool}$.
- The HM algorithm treats each use of `id` as a *fresh instantiation*, so it can give `id` two different concrete types in two different contexts.
- However, the final expression `a + b` is `int + bool`, which is a type error since `+` requires both operands to be numeric.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** *Define a type system. List any four components of a type system.* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key Pattern):**

A **type system** is a collection of rules used by a compiler to assign types to program constructs (variables, expressions, functions) and to verify that the program obeys type compatibility rules. It enforces type safety.

**Four components:**
1. **Base Types** — primitive types like `int`, `float`, `char`, `bool`.
