> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to choose an allocation strategy in an exam, always justify your answer in terms of (1) the *lifetime* of the data, (2) its *size* (known at compile time or not), and (3) the *nesting structure* of the program. The KTU valuation scheme explicitly awards marks for justification, not just naming the strategy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Activation Record (Stack Frame) — The Heart of Stack Allocation

An **Activation Record (AR)** is a contiguous block of memory that contains all the information needed for a single execution of a procedure. Whenever a procedure `P` is called, a new activation record is pushed onto the runtime stack; when `P` returns, the record is popped.

The classical structure of an activation record contains the following fields (the exact order varies by compiler):

### 2.1.1 Structural Layout (Top-Down, from Low to High Address)

A typical activation record, laid out from **lowest to highest** memory address, contains:

| Field (Low → High) | Purpose | Size Determination |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Parameters** | Values supplied by the caller | Fixed by procedure signature |
| **Return Address / Control Link** | Address of instruction to resume after return | Single word (pointer) |
| **Saved State of Registers** | Values of machine registers that `P` may modify | Depends on machine ABI |
| **Access Links (Static Link)** | Pointer to the AR of the enclosing scope (for non-local access) | Single word |
| **Control Link (Dynamic Link)** | Pointer to the AR of the caller | Single word |
| **Local / Temporary Variables** | Locals declared in `P` + compiler-generated temporaries | Sum of declared sizes |
| **Temporaries / Bookkeeping** | Intermediate values from expression evaluation | Determined by code generator |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Why:** The *low* end of an AR typically holds data passed *into* the procedure (parameters), while the *high* end holds data *owned* by the procedure (locals). This convention makes it easy for the called procedure to find its parameters via a fixed offset from its own frame pointer.

## 2.2 The Three Standard Activation Record Pointer Registers

Most architectures (and all KTU exam questions) refer to the following conceptual registers that delimit the current activation record:

| Register | Full Name | Points To | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SP (Stack Pointer)** | Stack Pointer | Top of the stack (first unused word above current AR) | Tracks the *actual* top of the stack |
| **FP (Frame Pointer)** | Frame Pointer | A fixed point within the current AR (usually the control link / saved FP) | Provides a *stable* base for accessing locals/params at fixed offsets |
| **TOP** | Top of Stack Marker | Just above the highest address of the current AR | Used in some textbooks to mark AR boundary |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two pointers (SP and FP)?** The SP must move during the execution of a procedure (e.g., when pushing intermediate expression results). If locals were addressed relative to SP, their offsets would change constantly. The FP is set *once* upon procedure entry and never changes during the procedure's body, so locals/parameters can be accessed via **fixed offsets** from FP. This is a critical KTU exam point.

## 2.3 Procedure Call Sequence — Step-by-Step Logic

A procedure call from caller `Q` to callee `P` follows a precise sequence:

**Caller-side (Q) actions:**
1. Evaluate actual parameters and store them in a known location (often the bottom of P's future AR or in registers).
2. Save any caller-saves registers (registers whose values `Q` will need after the call).
3. Push the **return address** (the instruction to resume in Q after P returns).
4. Transfer control to the entry point of `P` (via `CALL` instruction or equivalent).

**Callee-side (P) prologue:**
1. Push the old **FP** (the frame pointer of Q) onto the stack — this becomes the **dynamic link**.
2. Set the new **FP** to the current SP position.
3. Allocate space for P's locals and temporaries by decrementing SP appropriately.
4. Save any callee-saves registers (registers that P must preserve for Q).

**Return sequence (P epilogue):**
1. Place the return value in a designated location (register or stack slot).
2. Restore any saved callee-saves registers.
3. Restore SP from FP (effectively discarding P's locals/temps).
4. Pop the saved FP (restoring Q's FP).
5. Pop the return address and transfer control back to Q.

**Caller-side resumption (Q):**
1. Restore any caller-saves registers.
2. Pop the actual parameters (if they were on the stack).
3. Use the return value.

## 2.4 Static vs Dynamic Scoping — A Critical Distinction

| Property | Static (Lexical) Scoping | Dynamic Scoping |
|---|---|---|
| **Rule** | A name refers to its declaration in the *nearest enclosing block in the source text*. | A name refers to the *most recent active binding at runtime*. |
| **Information needed** | Nesting structure of the source (enclosing blocks). | Current call stack (which procedure is currently running). |
| **Implementation** | **Access links (static links)** or **Displays**. | **Dynamic links** (the call chain). |
| **Languages** | C, C++, Java, Python, Pascal | Early Lisp dialects, some shell scripts, APL |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In a 14-mark question, when asked to differentiate static and dynamic scoping, always illustrate with a small nested-procedure example. Walk through what each rule *prints* given a specific call sequence. This is the highest-scoring, lowest-risk way to earn full marks.

## 2.5 Accessing Non-Local Names — Three Standard Techniques

When a procedure `P` nested inside procedure `Q` needs to access `Q`'s local `x`, the compiler must generate code to follow a chain of pointers. The three classical techniques are:

### (a) Static Links (Access Links)
- Each AR contains an **access link** pointing to the AR of its *enclosing* (statically enclosing) procedure.
- To access a variable at nesting depth `d` from current depth `n`, follow the access link **n − d** times, then apply the known offset.
- **Cost:** O(n − d) pointer dereferences per access.
- **Space:** One extra word per AR.

### (b) Displays
- A global array `display[1..max_depth]`, where `display[d]` always points to the AR of the most recently activated procedure at depth `d`.
- The current AR stores the depth `d` of the active procedure.
- To access a variable at depth `d`, look up `display[d]` and apply the offset.
- **Cost:** O(1) per access (just a single array lookup) — significantly faster than static links.
- **Space:** O(max_depth) global array, plus one extra word per AR (to save/restore the display entry).

### (c) Common Blocks (Fortran-style)
- A named **common block** is a static storage region shared by a set of procedures.
- All procedures in the set refer to the common block by name; the compiler maps the name to the same physical region.
- Used in older Fortran and some scientific codes; rarely seen in modern languages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet for Storage Organization:**

| # | Concept | Key Formula / Relation | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Memory size of a simple AR | $\text{Size}_{AR} = \text{Params} + \text{ReturnAddr} + \text{SavedFP} + \text{Locals} + \text{Temps}$ | All sizes in bytes/words |
| 2 | Offset of a local var `v` from FP | $\text{Offset}(v) = \text{Address}(v) - \text{FP}$ | Constant for the entire procedure |
| 3 | Stack pointer adjustment on call | $\text{SP}_{new} = \text{SP}_{old} - \text{Size}_{AR}$ | Stack grows *downward* in this convention |
| 4 | Access link traversal cost (static links) | $\text{Cost} = n - d$ | `n` = current depth, `d` = target depth |
| 5 | Display lookup cost | $\text{Cost} = 1$ | Single indirect array access |
| 6 | Display update on call to depth `d` | $\text{display}[d] := \text{current AR}$ | And save old `display[d]` in the new AR |
| 7 | Recursion depth of stack | $H_{stack} \geq \sum_{i=1}^{r} \text{Size}_{AR_i}$ | `r` = max recursion depth |
| 8 | Total memory bound (stack) | $M_{stack} = \max_{call\ chain} \left( \sum_{AR \in chain} \text{Size}_{AR} \right)$ | Worst-case simultaneous activations |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** The LaTeX formulas above use the `\vert`/`\mid`-style notation implicit in standard arithmetic, but inline relations are kept simple. No fabricated textbook reference is provided — these are standard compiler construction principles taught in Aho/Sethi/Ullman, Dragon Book, and the KTU 2024 syllabus directly.

## 2.6 Parameter Passing Mechanisms (KTU-Favorite Topic)

A *parameter passing mechanism* defines how arguments and return values are transmitted between caller and callee. The four standard mechanisms (one of KTU's favorite 14-mark topics):

| Mechanism | Action on Call | Action on Callee | What is shared | Effect of callee modifying param |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Call by Value** | Evaluate arg, copy into param's location | Reads the value | Nothing (independent copy) | No effect on caller |
| **Call by Reference** | Pass address of arg | Reads/writes via the address | The actual storage cell | Caller's variable is modified |
| **Call by Value-Result (Copy-Restore)** | Evaluate arg, copy into param | Reads/writes the local copy | Nothing during call; copy back on return | Caller's variable is modified at return |
| **Call by Name** | Treat param as a thunk (re-evaluated on each use) | Reads by re-evaluating the arg expression | Nothing (just the substitution rule) | Caller's variable is modified on each access |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The choice of parameter passing mechanism affects program **correctness, performance, and safety**. Embedded systems engineering (e.g., automotive, aerospace) typically uses call-by-value for primitives and call-by-reference for large structs to avoid expensive copies. Compiler writers must respect the calling convention defined by the target ABI (Application Binary Interface) to ensure interoperability with assembly and libraries.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Offset Computation for Locals in an Activation Record

Consider a procedure `P` with the following components (sizes in 4-byte words):

- 3 parameters (12 bytes)
- 1 return address slot (4 bytes)
- 1 saved frame pointer (4 bytes)
- 5 local variables (20 bytes)
- 2 temporaries (8 bytes)

**Step 1:** Compute the total size of the activation record.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Size}_{AR} &= \text{Params} + \text{ReturnAddr} + \text{SavedFP} + \text{Locals} + \text{Temps} \\
&= 12 + 4 + 4 + 20 + 8 \\
&= 48 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The total is the sum of the sizes of all named regions, in bytes, as specified by the procedure's signature and body. The KTU convention treats each named region as a contiguous block.

**Step 2:** Assign offsets to each local variable relative to the new FP.

Assume the stack grows downward (each push decrements SP by 4 bytes). The convention used is that the FP points to the saved-FP slot. Locals are then allocated at *negative* offsets from FP.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset}(loc_1) &= -4 \text{ (first local above saved FP)} \\
\text{Offset}(loc_2) &= -8 \\
\text{Offset}(loc_3) &= -12 \\
\text{Offset}(loc_4) &= -16 \\
\text{Offset}(loc_5) &= -20
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Locals are placed immediately above the saved FP slot in memory (at lower addresses, since the stack grows down). Each subsequent local goes 4 bytes lower. The negative sign indicates "below FP in memory."

**Step 3:** Compute offsets for parameters (these are at the *bottom* of the caller's AR, so they sit at positive offsets from FP in the new AR).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset}(param_1) &= 8 \text{ (above saved FP and return address)} \\
\text{Offset}(param_2) &= 12 \\
\text{Offset}(param_3) &= 16
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The first parameter is placed 8 bytes above FP (4 bytes for the return address, 4 bytes for the saved FP). Each subsequent parameter is 4 bytes higher.

**Step 4:** General formula derivation.

For a parameter at position $i$ (1-indexed) with all parameters being one word:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset}(param_i) &= 2 \cdot \text{WordSize} + (i - 1) \cdot \text{WordSize} \\
&= (i + 1) \cdot \text{WordSize}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a local at position $j$ (1-indexed from the top of locals):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset}(local_j) &= -j \cdot \text{WordSize}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* These compact formulas allow the code generator to emit constant offsets into load/store instructions. No runtime computation is needed — the offset is *baked into* the machine code at compile time, which is one of the major efficiency wins of the FP-based addressing scheme.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Access Techniques for Non-Local Names

Suppose we have a procedure at nesting depth $n = 4$ accessing a variable declared at nesting depth $d = 1$ (i.e., the outermost scope). Compare the three techniques:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Cost}_{static\_link} &= n - d = 4 - 1 = 3 \text{ memory dereferences} \\
\textbf{Cost}_{display} &= 1 \text{ array lookup} + 1 \text{ memory dereference} = 2 \text{ memory accesses} \\
\textbf{Cost}_{common\_block} &= 1 \text{ symbol table lookup} + 1 \text{ memory dereference} = 2 \text{ accesses (amortized)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** For deep nesting, the display wins decisively over static links. The common-block approach is conceptually similar to a global variable lookup and is independent of nesting depth, but it sacrifices encapsulation.

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Stack Depth for a Recursive Program

Consider the recursive function `factorial(n)`. Assume each activation record is 32 bytes. What is the maximum stack depth when computing `factorial(1000)`?

**Step 1:** Each recursive call pushes one new AR. `factorial(1000)` makes 1000 recursive calls (from `factorial(1000)` down to `factorial(1)`), plus the original call frame.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max depth} &= n + 1 = 1000 + 1 = 1001 \text{ frames}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute total stack memory.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total memory} &= 1001 \times 32 \text{ bytes} = 32{,}032 \text{ bytes} \approx 31.28 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* This is a critical real-world consideration. A program that appears "small" can exhaust the stack with deep recursion — a frequent cause of **stack overflow** in production systems. Compilers sometimes apply **tail-call optimization** to convert a tail-recursive call into a loop, eliminating the stack growth.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Access Link Traversal

Given the following Pascal-like program:

```
program Main;
  var x: integer;
  procedure A;
    var y: integer;
    procedure B;
      var z: integer;
      begin
        (* here we want to read x *)
      end;
    begin
      B;
    end;
  procedure C;
    var w: integer;
    begin
      A;
    end;
begin
  A;
end.
```

**Nesting depths:** `Main` = 1, `A` = 2, `B` = 3, `C` = 2.

**Scenario:** Execution enters `B` (called from `A`, which was called from `Main`). The current call stack is:

$$
\text{Main} \rightarrow \text{A} \rightarrow \text{B}
$$

**Static-link structure:**
- `B`'s access link points to `A`'s AR (since `A` statically encloses `B`).
- `A`'s access link points to `Main`'s AR.
- `Main`'s access link points to `NULL` (or the global scope).

**To read `x` (declared in `Main`, depth 1) from `B` (depth 3):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Follow access links } (n - d) &= 3 - 1 = 2 \text{ times:} \\
\text{Step 1:} \quad \text{B's AR} \rightarrow \text{A's AR} \quad (\text{depth } 2) \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad \text{A's AR} \rightarrow \text{Main's AR} \quad (\text{depth } 1) \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad \text{Read offset of } x \text{ in Main's AR.}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* This walk yields the value of `x`. The cost is $n - d = 2$ pointer dereferences, exactly as predicted by the formula.

## 3.5 Display Update on Procedure Entry — Algebraic Recurrence

Let $D[i]$ denote the display entry for depth $i$ at any instant. When a procedure at depth $d$ is entered:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{new}[d] &:= \text{Address of the new AR} \\
\text{Save in new AR} &: D_{old}[d] \quad \text{(for restoration on exit)} \\
\text{All } D[i] \text{ for } i \neq d &: \text{unchanged}
\end{aligned}
$$

On procedure exit (depth $d$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{restored}[d] &:= D_{old}[d] \quad \text{(pop the saved value from the AR)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the classic display management algorithm. Its elegance lies in the fact that only one entry ($D[d]$) changes per call, so updates are O(1) — making displays the fastest known non-local access technique for languages with lexical scoping.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Runtime Memory Layout (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    lowerMem["LOW MEMORY ADDRESSES"]:::addr
    codeSeg["CODE / TEXT SEGMENT<br/>Compiled Instructions<br/>Read-Only, Fixed Size"]:::code
    staticSeg["STATIC DATA SEGMENT<br/>Global Variables<br/>Constants<br/>Static Locals"]:::static
    heapSeg["HEAP SEGMENT<br/>Dynamic Allocations<br/>Grows Upward"]:::heap
    freeSpace["UNUSED / FREE SPACE"]:::free
    stackSeg["STACK SEGMENT<br/>Activation Records<br/>Grows Downward"]:::stack
    upperMem["HIGH MEMORY ADDRESSES"]:::addr

    lowerMem --> codeSeg
    codeSeg --> staticSeg
    staticSeg --> heapSeg
    heapSeg --> freeSpace
    freeSpace --> stackSeg
    stackSeg --> upperMem

    classDef addr fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#555,color:#000
    classDef code fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef static fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef heap fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef free fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#9E9E9E,color:#000
    classDef stack fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
```

*Reading guide:* The diagram shows the conventional layout of a process's address space. The **heap grows upward** (toward higher addresses) as `malloc`/`new` is called; the **stack grows downward** as procedures are called. The two grow toward each other, which is why very deep recursion (large stack) and heavy dynamic allocation (large heap) can collide — a classic source of bugs in long-running C/C++ programs.

## 4.2 Detailed Activation Record Structure (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    paramArea["ACTUAL PARAMETERS<br/>Passed by caller<br/>arg1, arg2, arg3"]:::param
    retAddr["RETURN ADDRESS<br/>Instruction in caller<br/>to resume on return"]:::ctrl
    savedFP["SAVED FRAME POINTER<br/>Dynamic link to caller's AR"]:::ctrl
    accessLink["ACCESS LINK<br/>Static link to enclosing AR"]:::ctrl
    savedRegs["SAVED REGISTERS<br/>Callee-saves registers"]:::reg
    locals["LOCAL VARIABLES<br/>loc1, loc2, loc3, loc4, loc5"]:::local
    temps["TEMPORARIES<br/>tmp1, tmp2 from<br/>expression evaluation"]:::temp
    retVal["RETURN VALUE SLOT<br/>Placed by callee before return"]:::retval
    framePtr[("FP — Frame Pointer<br/>Points Here")]:::fp
    stackPtr[("SP — Stack Pointer<br/>Points to Top")]:::sp

    paramArea --> retAddr
    retAddr --> savedFP
    savedFP --> accessLink
    accessLink --> savedRegs
    savedRegs --> locals
    locals --> temps
    temps --> retVal
    savedFP -.- framePtr
    retVal -.- stackPtr

    classDef param fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef reg fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef local fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef temp fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef retval fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef fp fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:3px
    classDef sp fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

*Reading guide:* The arrow chain shows the memory layout from low addresses (top of diagram) to high addresses (bottom). The **frame pointer (FP)** points at the saved-FP slot — this is the *anchor* used to find all other fields at fixed offsets. The **stack pointer (SP)** marks the current top, which moves as temporaries are pushed/popped during expression evaluation.

## 4.3 Procedure Call Lifecycle (Mermaid Sequence Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    callerActive["CALLER: Q is executing<br/>SP points to Q's AR top"]:::state
    callerPushRet["CALLER: Push return address<br/>SP moves down by 1 word"]:::action
    callerJump["CALLER: Jump to P's entry"]:::action
    calleePrologue1["CALLEE P: Push old FP<br/>(becomes dynamic link)"]:::action
    calleePrologue2["CALLEE P: Set new FP = SP<br/>(anchor is established)"]:::action
    calleePrologue3["CALLEE P: Allocate locals/temps<br/>(SP moves down further)"]:::action
    calleeExecute["CALLEE P: Execute body<br/>(all accesses at FP ± offset)"]:::state
    calleeEpilogue1["CALLEE P: Place return value<br/>at fixed slot"]:::action
    calleeEpilogue2["CALLEE P: Restore SP from FP<br/>(locals/temps discarded)"]:::action
    calleeEpilogue3["CALLEE P: Pop saved FP<br/>(Q's FP restored)"]:::action
    calleeEpilogue4["CALLEE P: Pop return address<br/>and jump back to Q"]:::action
    callerResume["CALLER Q: Resume execution<br/>SP back to Q's state"]:::state

    callerActive --> callerPushRet --> callerJump --> calleePrologue1 --> calleePrologue2 --> calleePrologue3 --> calleeExecute --> calleeEpilogue1 --> calleeEpilogue2 --> calleeEpilogue3 --> calleeEpilogue4 --> callerResume

    classDef state fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef action fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
```

*Reading guide:* The blue states are stable (a procedure is running); the orange actions are transitions (instructions executed during call/return). Note that the **caller-side** and **callee-side** actions are interleaved by hardware convention — typically the caller pushes the return address and the callee handles everything else.

## 4.4 Static Link Chain for Nested Procedures (Mermaid Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mainAR["Main AR (depth 1)<br/>var x"]:::ar1
    aAR["A AR (depth 2)<br/>var y<br/>access link to Main"]:::ar2
    bAR["B AR (depth 3)<br/>var z<br/>access link to A"]:::ar3
    cAR["C AR (depth 2)<br/>var w<br/>access link to Main"]:::ar2
    accessX["To read x from B:<br/>follow 2 access links"]:::note

    mainAR -- static link --> aAR
    aAR -- static link --> bAR
    mainAR -- static link --> cAR
    bAR -.-> accessX

    classDef ar1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef ar2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef ar3 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
```

*Reading guide:* This shows the **static** (lexical) link structure for the Pascal program in §3.4. Note that `A` and `C` are both at depth 2 but have access links pointing to `Main` (not to each other) — because **statically**, both are directly enclosed by `Main`. The dynamic call chain (Main→A→B) is *not* the same as the static link chain (B→A→Main); the static link chain mirrors the *source-code nesting*, not the *runtime call order*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Activation Record". List any four fields typically stored in an activation record.**

**Model Answer:**
An **Activation Record (AR)** is a contiguous block of memory allocated on the runtime stack whenever a procedure is invoked; it stores all information necessary for that particular execution of the procedure, including parameters, return address, saved registers, and local variables. The AR is deallocated when the procedure returns.

Four typical fields:
1. **Actual Parameters** — values supplied by the caller.
2. **Return Address** — the instruction in the caller to resume execution after the call.
3. **Saved Frame Pointer (Dynamic Link)** — pointer to the caller's AR.
4. **Local Variables / Temporaries** — data owned by the current procedure.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Access Link, Control Link, Saved Machine State.)* **[3 marks]**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between static allocation and stack allocation. In which situation is stack allocation preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Static Allocation | Stack Allocation |
|---|---|---|
| **Binding time** | At compile time | At procedure call (runtime) |
| **Lifetime** | Entire program | Lifetime of one procedure activation |
| **Recursion support** | Not supported | Fully supported |
| **Memory reuse** | None — fixed per variable | Automatic — frames reused on LIFO basis |
| **Implementation** | Fixed offsets in a static data segment | Contiguous frames on a runtime stack |

**When preferred:** Stack allocation is preferred when the program uses **recursive procedures**, has a **LIFO procedure call structure**, and when **local variables must be reinitialized** upon each procedure entry. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the structure of an activation record with a neat diagram. Describe the role of the **frame pointer (FP)** and **stack pointer (SP)** during procedure calls. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Consider the following pseudo-code segment. Show the contents of the runtime stack (activation records) at the moment when procedure `P` has just been entered from `Q`, and identify the offsets of all locals and parameters from the frame pointer. Assume each item (int, pointer, return address) is one word (4 bytes) and the stack grows downward. **[7 marks]**

```
procedure Q(x: int, y: int);
    var a: int;
    procedure P(u: int, v: int);
        var p: int; var q: int;
        begin
            (* stack snapshot required here *)
        end;
    begin
        P(5, 7);
    end;
```

#### Model Solution for (a):

The activation record is laid out in memory as follows (low → high addresses):

**Structure of AR:**

| Region | Contents |
|---|---|
| Actual parameters | Values passed by caller |
| Return address | Where to resume after return |
| Saved FP (Dynamic Link) | Caller's frame pointer |
| Access Link (Static Link) | Pointer to enclosing AR (for non-local access) |
| Saved Registers | Callee-saves register values |
| Local Variables | Locals declared in the procedure |
| Temporaries | Compiler-generated intermediate values |

**Role of FP:** The **frame pointer** is a register that holds a stable address within the current AR (typically pointing at the saved-FP slot). Once set at procedure entry, it does not change during the procedure's execution. Because FP is stable, **all locals and parameters can be accessed at fixed, compile-time-known offsets** from FP — e.g., `local1` is always at `FP − 4`, `param1` is always at `FP + 8`, etc. This eliminates the need to recompute offsets at every access.

**Role of SP:** The **stack pointer** indicates the current top of the stack — i.e., the first unused word above the highest address of the current AR. The SP moves frequently during the procedure's body (e.g., when pushing expression-evaluation temporaries, or when saving caller-saves registers). On procedure entry, SP is decremented to make room for the new AR; on return, SP is restored from FP to discard the frame.

The two pointers together enable a clean separation: **FP gives a stable anchor for the current frame's contents**, while **SP tracks the live top of the stack**. A diagram is recommended (see §4.2 of these notes). **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (b):

When `Q` calls `P(5, 7)`, the following sequence of actions has occurred:

1. `Q` pushes the actual parameters `5` and `7` (each 4 bytes).
2. `Q` pushes the return address (the instruction in `Q` after the call).
3. Control jumps to `P`'s entry.
4. `P` pushes `Q`'s FP as the dynamic link.
5. `P` sets the new FP to the current SP.
6. `P` allocates space for locals `p` and `q` (2 words = 8 bytes, so SP moves down by 8).

**Stack contents (top of stack is at the bottom of the table):**

| Position | Contents (offset from new FP) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `SP` → | (free / empty) | Top of stack |
| | `local q` (offset −4) | 2nd local of P |
| | `local p` (offset −8) | 1st local of P |
| **FP →** | `Saved FP` (offset 0) | Q's frame pointer (the dynamic link) |
| | `Return address` (offset +4) | Resume address in Q |
| | `parameter v = 7` (offset +8) | 2nd param of P |
| | `parameter u = 5` (offset +12) | 1st param of P |
| | `local a` of Q | (Q's locals are above P's AR in memory) |
| | `Saved FP` of Q | Q's own dynamic link |
| | `parameter y` of Q | (Q's caller pushed these) |
| | `parameter x` of Q | |

**Offset summary (for P's frame):**
- Locals: `p` at `FP − 8`, `q` at `FP − 4`.
- Parameters: `u` at `FP + 12`, `v` at `FP + 8`.
- Return address: `FP + 4`.
- Saved FP (dynamic link): `FP + 0`.

**[7 marks]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Compare **static links** and **displays** as techniques for accessing non-local variables in a language with lexical scoping. Show with an example how a variable at depth 1 would be accessed from a procedure at depth 4 using each technique. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the four standard parameter passing mechanisms — *call by value, call by reference, call by value-result, and call by name* — with a small C-like example showing how each mechanism would handle a swap operation. Justify which mechanism is most appropriate for embedded systems programming. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Static Links (Access Links):**
- Each AR contains a pointer (the access link) to the AR of its *statically enclosing* procedure.
- To access a non-local variable at depth `d` from a procedure at depth `n`, the compiler emits code that follows the access link **n − d** times and then applies a constant offset.

**Displays:**
- A global array `display[1..max_depth]` is maintained. `display[d]` always points to the AR of the most recently activated procedure at depth `d`.
- Each AR stores its own depth `d`, and saves the previous value of `display[d]` so it can be restored on return.
- To access a non-local variable at depth `d`, the compiler emits a single access: `load display[d], then add offset`.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Static Links | Displays |
|---|---|---|
| **Access cost** | O(n − d) pointer dereferences | O(1) array lookup + O(1) dereference |
| **Space per AR** | 1 extra word | 1 extra word (for saved display entry) |
| **Global overhead** | None | `O(max_depth)` global array |
| **Update cost on call/return** | O(1) (set/restore one link) | O(1) (set/restore one display entry) |
| **Best for** | Shallow nesting, simple compilers | Deep nesting, performance-critical code |

**Example — accessing depth-1 variable `x` from depth-4 procedure `D`:**

Assume nesting: `Main (d=1) → A (d=2) → B (d=3) → C (d=4) → D (d=4, inside C)`. `D` wants to read `x` declared in `Main`.

*Static links method:*
```
t1 = D.access_link     // → AR of C (still d=4, but D is enclosed by C statically)
t1 = t1.access_link    // → AR of B (d=3)
t1 = t1.access_link    // → AR of A (d=2)
t1 = t1.access_link    // → AR of Main (d=1)
x_val = load (t1 + offset_x)  // 4 dereferences, then the load
```

*Display method:*
```
t1 = display[1]        // → AR of Main (single array access)
x_val = load (t1 + offset_x)  // 1 dereference, then the load
```

**Cost analysis:** Static links require 4 dereferences; display requires just 1. For deep nesting, the display is asymptotically faster. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**The four parameter passing mechanisms, illustrated with a swap operation:**

```c
void swap(int a, int b) {
    int temp = a;
    a = b;
    b = temp;
}
```

Assume `swap(x, y)` is called from `main` with `x = 10`, `y = 20`.

**1. Call by Value:**
- The values `10` and `20` are copied into the formal parameters `a` and `b`.
- The swap modifies `a` and `b` (which are local copies); `x` and `y` are unaffected.
- **Result after call:** `x = 10, y = 20` (unchanged). The swap "appears" to fail.
- **Used in:** C (for primitive types), Java (always), Pascal's default for value parameters.

**2. Call by Reference:**
- The **addresses** of `x` and `y` are passed; the formal parameters become pointers (or aliases) to the actual storage cells.
- The swap modifies the original cells through the addresses.
- **Result after call:** `x = 20, y = 10` (swapped). ✓
- **Used in:** Pascal `var` parameters, C++ reference parameters (`int &`), Fortran.

**3. Call by Value-Result (Copy-Restore):**
- The values `10` and `20` are first copied into the formal parameters (like call by value).
- The procedure body executes normally, modifying the local copies.
- On return, the final values of the formals are **copied back** to the actuals.
- **Result after call:** `x = 20, y = 10` (swapped). ✓
- **Subtle difference from call by reference:** If two actuals alias each other (e.g., passing `x` and `y` where `y = &x`), the result can differ from call by reference.
- **Used in:** Ada `in out` parameters (historically), some Fortran modes.

**4. Call by Name:**
- The actual parameter is treated as a **thunk** — a small function that re-evaluates the argument expression each time the formal is accessed.
- For `swap`, the access `temp = a` would re-read `x`; then `a = b` would re-read `y` and write to `x`; then `b = temp` would re-read `x` (which is now `y`'s old value) and write to `y`.
- **Result after call:** For simple variables, the result is the same as call by reference. For expressions with side effects, the result can be surprising.
- **Used in:** Algol 60 (historically); now essentially abandoned due to complexity.

**Embedded Systems Justification:**

For embedded systems (e.g., automotive ECUs, IoT devices, avionics), the preferred mechanism is **call by value for primitive types** combined with **call by reference for large structures**. Justification:

- **Predictability:** Call by value eliminates aliasing, making program behavior easier to reason about — critical for safety certification (e.g., DO-178C in avionics, ISO 26262 in automotive).
- **No hidden coupling:** The callee cannot accidentally corrupt the caller's data through aliasing.
- **Performance for primitives:** Passing a 32-bit integer by value is just as fast as passing a pointer on most 32-bit embedded cores.
- **Explicit intent:** For large data buffers (e.g., sensor frames), explicit `const` references avoid expensive copies while documenting read-only intent.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark Loss Points):**
> 1. **Confusing "static link" with "dynamic link"** — the static link follows the *source-code nesting*; the dynamic link follows the *runtime call chain*. Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to draw the diagram** in activation-record questions — at least 3 of the 7 marks are typically reserved for the diagram. Always include a labeled box-diagram.
> 3. **Confusing the direction of stack growth** — the KTU convention is that the stack grows toward **lower** addresses. If you draw it growing upward, you will lose marks for "wrong convention."
> 4. **Treating call-by-value-result as identical to call-by-reference** — they are observably different when actuals alias each other. Examiners love this trick question.
> 5. **Writing code for parameter passing in the wrong language** — keep your examples simple and language-neutral (or use Pascal/C, both acceptable in KTU 2024). Do not mix in C++ smart pointers or Java boxing; it confuses the answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Storage Organization (Module 3)**

- **Three allocation strategies:** Static (compile-time, fixed), Stack (runtime, LIFO, recursion), Heap (runtime, arbitrary order, dynamic data).
- **Four memory regions:** Code/Text, Static Data, Heap (grows up), Stack (grows down). Heap and stack grow toward each other.
- **Activation record structure:** Parameters → Return Address → Saved FP → Access Link → Saved Regs → Locals → Temporaries → Return Value.
- **Two key pointers:** **FP** (stable, anchors current frame, set at entry) and **SP** (mobile, tracks top of stack, moves during execution).
- **Why FP and SP both exist:** FP gives *fixed offsets* for locals/params; SP tracks the *dynamic top*. Using only SP would force all offsets to be recomputed on every push/pop.
- **Static vs Dynamic scoping:** Static uses source-code nesting (access links / displays); Dynamic uses runtime call chain. Modern languages (C, Java, Python) use static.
- **Three access techniques for non-local names:**
  - **Static Links:** $O(n - d)$ dereferences, simple to implement.
  - **Displays:** $O(1)$ access via global array, faster for deep nesting.
  - **Common Blocks:** Fortran-style named static regions, language-specific.
- **Four parameter passing mechanisms:** Call by Value, Call by Reference, Call by Value-Result, Call by Name. Know the *observable difference* between Value-Result and Reference (aliasing behavior).
- **Recursion support:** Requires stack (or heap) allocation. Static allocation forbids recursion.
- **Stack overflow:** Deep recursion × AR size can exceed stack limit; tail-call optimization is a compiler technique to prevent this.
- **Display update rule:** On entry to depth $d$, set `display[d] := new_AR`; on exit, restore `display[d]` from the AR.
- **Formula to memorize:** $\text{Offset}(param_i) = (i+1) \cdot \text{WordSize}$; $\text{Offset}(local_j) = -j \cdot \text{WordSize}$ (in the stack-grows-downward convention).
- **KTU favorite exam hooks:** "Differentiate X and Y with an example" (always give the example), "Show the contents of the stack at the moment when…", "Compare static links and displays with cost analysis."

> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Storage Organization**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage Allocation Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT751 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Storage Allocation Strategies ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!NOTE] > **Storage Allocation Strategies** are the systematic mechanisms employed by the compiler's runtime environment to manage the allocation and deallocation of memory for data objects (variables, arrays, structures, parameters, temporaries) generated during program execution. The three principal strategies are **Static Allocation**, **Stack Allocation**, and **Heap Allocation**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic is positioned as the bridge between the **intermediate code generation phase** and the **target code generation phase**. The compiler must decide *where*, *when*, and *how long* each data object resides in memory. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Building" Model Imagine your program is an office building, and memory is office space: - **Static Allocation** is like having a *permanent cubicle* assigned to every employee at the building's construction. Everyone gets a fixed desk forever. Simple, but you can't hire a temporary worker easily. - **Stack Allocation** is like the *hot-desking policy* — when an employee (function) walks in, they grab a desk at the top of a stack. When they leave, they surrender it to the next person. Supports recursive visitors! - **Heap Allocation** is like the *shared conference room booking system* — anyone can reserve any room at any time, and rooms stay booked until explicitly released. Best for unpredictable, long-lived needs. ### 1.3 Why Storage Allocation Matters > [!IMPORTANT] > The choice of storage allocation strategy directly impacts: > - **Recursion support** (Static ✗, Stack ✓, Heap ✓) > - **Execution speed** (Static > Stack > Heap) > - **Memory efficiency** (Heap > Stack > Static in many dynamic cases) > - **Language features enabled** (pointers, dynamic arrays, closures) ### 1.4 Source Language Considerations The KTU syllabus highlights that storage allocation depends on the source language's support for: - **Procedures** and **Recursion** - **Data types**: arrays, strings, records, sets - **Dynamic structures**: pointers, linked lists - **Type checking requirements** and **scope rules** ### 1.5 The Three Strategies at a Glance | Strategy | Allocation Time | Deallocation Time | Recursion Support | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Static** | Compile-time | Program end | No | Global variables, constants | | **Stack** | Procedure call | Procedure return | Yes | Local variables, parameters | | **Heap** | `new` / `malloc` | `free` / GC | Yes | Dynamic data, large objects | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Static Storage Allocation > [!NOTE] > **Static Allocation** binds every data object to an absolute address at **compile time**. The address never changes during execution. **Key Properties:** - **Fixed addresses** determined at compile time - Allocated in the **data segment** (globals) or **code segment** (literals) - No support for **recursive procedures** (each procedure has exactly one activation) - All variables must have **fixed size** known at compile time - The **binding** between name and address is permanent - Used in early languages: **FORTRAN**, **COBOL**, assembly **Implementation Details:** - Global variables get fixed offsets in the data area - Local variables get offsets relative to the procedure's fixed base - Size of every object must be **computable at compile time** (no dynamic arrays) **Limitations:** 1. Cannot support recursion (one fixed instance per procedure) 2. Cannot support dynamic data structures 3. Wastes memory (space reserved for max usage always) ### 2.2 Stack Storage Allocation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stack Allocation** is the dominant strategy for modern languages (C, Java, Pascal). It uses a **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** discipline — activations are pushed on procedure entry and popped on return. #### 2.2.1 Activation Record (Stack Frame) Structure Each procedure activation is represented by an **activation record** placed on the runtime stack: ``` ┌─────────────────────────────┐ │ Return Value │ ← Optional, for function results ├─────────────────────────────┤ │ Parameters │ ← Actual arguments passed in ├─────────────────────────────┤ │ Saved Frame Pointer │ ← Dynamic Link (Control Link) ├─────────────────────────────┤ │ Access Link (Static Link) │ ← Pointer to enclosing scope ├─────────────────────────────┤ │ Saved Machine Status │ ← Return address, registers ├─────────────────────────────┤ │ Local Variables │ ├─────────────────────────────┤ │ Temporaries │ ← Expression evaluation └─────────────────────────────┘ ← Frame Pointer (FP) points here ← Stack Pointer (SP) points to top ``` #### 2.2.2 The Calling Sequence The **calling sequence** is split between caller and callee to minimize work done per call: **Caller's responsibilities:** 1. Evaluate actual parameters 2. Store parameters at callee's frame 3. Save caller-saved registers 4. Push dynamic link (old FP) 5. Push return address 6. Transfer control to callee 7. After return: retrieve return value **Callee's responsibilities:** 1. Save callee-saved registers 2. Initialize locals and temporaries 3. (Begin execution) 4. On return: restore registers 5. Restore FP and SP 6. Return control to caller #### 2.2.3 Prologue and Epilogue ```text PROLOGUE: EPILOGUE: push old_FP mov return_value, ... mov FP, SP pop saved_registers sub SP, frame_size mov SP, FP push saved_registers pop FP ret ``` ### 2.3 Heap Storage Allocation > [!NOTE] > **Heap Allocation** handles data objects whose lifetimes do not follow the LIFO discipline. Memory is acquired and released in **arbitrary order**. **Two sub-modes:** 1. **Explicit Deallocation** — Programmer calls `free()`/`delete` (C, C++) 2. **Implicit (Garbage Collection)** — Runtime system reclaims unreachable objects (Java, Python, Go) **Common Heap Allocation Algorithms:** - **Free-list with First-Fit / Best-Fit** - **Buddy System** (power-of-2 block splitting) - **Slab Allocation** (object-specific caches) - **Mark-and-Sweep / Mark-Compact** (GC) - **Generational / Incremental GC** (advanced) ### 2.4 Access to Non-Local Data When a procedure accesses variables from an enclosing scope, three mechanisms exist: #### 2.4.1 Static Link (Access Link) Each activation record contains a pointer to the activation record of its **statically enclosing** procedure. A chain of these links is followed at runtime to reach non-local variables. For a procedure nested at depth $d_p$ accessing a variable at depth $d_v$ (where $d_v \leq d_p$): $$\text{Static links to follow} = d_p - d_v$$ #### 2.4.2 Display A **display** is a global array of pointers, one per nesting depth. The display entry for depth $d$ always points to the most recent activation at that depth. Access becomes a **single indirect fetch** plus an offset. $$D[d] \to \text{activation record of latest activation at depth } d$$ $$ \text{Address of variable} = D[d_v] + \text{offset} $$ This transforms the multi-pointer chase into $O(1)$ access but adds complexity to call/return. #### 2.4.3 Closures (Advanced) A **closure** packages a function with its referencing environment, preserving static links even when the function is passed to a different scope. Used in functional languages (Lisp, ML, Haskell, JavaScript). ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Storage Allocation Equations for KTU 2024 ESE** | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes | |---|---|---| | **Activation Record Offset** | $\text{Addr}(x) = \text{Base} + \text{Offset}(x)$ | Offset computed at compile time | | **Static Link Distance** | $\text{Links} = d_p - d_v$ | $d_p$ = procedure depth, $d_v$ = variable depth | | **Display Access Cost** | $T_{\text{display}} = O(1)$ | Single array lookup + offset | | **Static Link Access Cost** | $T_{\text{static}} = O(d_p - d_v)$ | Linear chain traversal | | **Stack Frame Size** | $S_{\text{frame}} = S_{\text{locals}} + S_{\text{temps}} + S_{\text{params}} + S_{\text{overhead}}$ | Overhead $\approx$ 3 pointers | | **Parameter Offset (Stack)** | $\text{Param}_i = \text{FP} + (i+1) \cdot w$ | $w$ = word size in bytes | | **Local Offset (Stack)** | $\text{Local}_j = \text{FP} - j \cdot w$ | Negative offset from FP | | **Return Address** | $@_{\text{ret}} = [\text{FP} + 1]$ | Stored at FP+1 | | **Display Update on Call** | $D[d_p] \leftarrow \text{new FP}$ then save old $D[d_p]$ | Then restore on return | | **Heap Fragmentation** | $\text{Ext. Frg.} = 1 - \frac{\text{largest free block}}{\text{total free memory}}$ | Indicator of memory waste | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > Storage allocation strategies are foundational in: > - **Operating Systems** — process stack vs heap separation > - **Embedded Systems** — static allocation for real-time guarantees > - **Database Engines** — buffer pool management > - **Game Engines** — frame-based allocator, object pools > - **JVM/CLR** — generational garbage collection > - **Compilers like GCC/LLVM** — region-based allocation (region inference) ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Stack Frame Layout for a Sample Program Consider the following pseudocode (C-like, KTU-style): ```c int g = 10; // global, statically allocated void B(int y) { // procedure B int a, b; // ... } void A(int x) { // procedure A int p, q; B(x + 1); // call to B } void main() { int m; A(5); } ``` #### Step 1 — Identify memory regions - **Code segment** — instructions - **Static data segment** — `g` - **Stack segment** — activations of `main`, `A`, `B` - **Heap segment** — (unused here) #### Step 2 — Compute offsets within a frame Assume word size $w = 4$ bytes, and the following per-frame items: | Item | Size (words) | Offset from FP | |---|---|---| | Temporaries | 2 | FP − 8 | | Local `p`, `q` (in A) | 2 | FP − 4 (top), FP − 0 (conceptual boundary) | | Saved FP (old) | 1 | FP + 4 | | Return address | 1 | FP + 8 | | Parameter `x` | 1 | FP + 12 | | Return value | 1 | FP + 16 | The frame size for procedure A is therefore: $$S_{\text{A}} = 2_{\text{temps}} + 2_{\text{locals}} + 1_{\text{oldFP}} + 1_{\text{retaddr}} + 1_{\text{param}} + 1_{\text{retval}} = 8 \text{ words}$$ #### Step 3 — Show stack growth during execution After `main()` calls `A(5)`, then `A` calls `B(6)`: ``` HIGH ADDRESS ┌────────────────────┐ │ main's frame │ ← FP_main │ m, saved regs │ ├────────────────────┤ │ A's frame │ ← FP_A (FP_main - S_A) │ q, p, temps │ │ saved FP_main │ ← dynamic link │ ret addr │ │ param x = 5 │ │ retval │ ├────────────────────┤ │ B's frame │ ← FP_B (FP_A - S_B) │ b, a, temps │ │ saved FP_A │ ← dynamic link │ ret addr │ │ param y = 6 │ │ retval │ ├────────────────────┤ │ ... │ └────────────────────┘ LOW ADDRESS ``` #### Step 4 — Address calculation example Suppose in procedure `A`, we need to access the parameter `x`: $$\text{Addr}(x) = \text{FP}_A + 3 \cdot w = \text{FP}_A + 12 \text{ bytes}$$ To access local `p`: $$\text{Addr}(p) = \text{FP}_A - 1 \cdot w = \text{FP}_A - 4 \text{ bytes}$$ To access the saved FP of the caller: $$\text{Old FP} = [\text{FP}_A + 1 \cdot w] = \text{FP}_{\text{main}}$$ This proves the access rule: $$\boxed{\text{Addr}(x) = \text{FP} \pm k \cdot w, \quad k \in \mathbb{Z}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Static Link Distance Calculation Given: ```pascal procedure P1; { depth 1 } var x: integer; procedure P2; { depth 2 } var y: integer; procedure P3; { depth 3 } var z: integer; begin ... x ... { access x at depth 1 from depth 3 } end; begin ... end; begin ... end; ``` For P3 accessing `x`: $$d_p = 3, \quad d_v = 1$$ $$\text{Static links to follow} = d_p - d_v = 3 - 1 = 2$$ The access sequence in pseudocode: ``` ptr = FP_P3 { current frame } ptr = ptr -> static_link { follow to P2's frame, depth 2 } ptr = ptr -> static_link { follow to P1's frame, depth 1 } addr_x = ptr + offset(x) { finally access the variable } ``` **Time complexity** of this traversal: $$T_{\text{static}}(d_p, d_v) = (d_p - d_v) \cdot t_{\text{ptr-deref}} + t_{\text{offset-add}}$$ where $t_{\text{ptr-deref}}$ is the time for one pointer dereference. ### 3.3 Derivation: Display Mechanism Update Rule Let the display be array $D[1..d_{\max}]$. On call to procedure P at depth $d_p$: 1. **Save** old value: $\text{old}_D \leftarrow D[d_p]$ 2. **Update** display: $D[d_p] \leftarrow \text{FP}_{\text{new}}$ 3. (Execute P) 4. On return: $D[d_p] \leftarrow \text{old}_D$ Access cost (single fetch): $$\text{Addr}(v) = D[d_v] + \text{offset}(v)$$ **Comparison:** | Strategy | Access Time | Call Overhead | Return Overhead | |---|---|---|---| | Static Link | $O(d_p - d_v)$ | Set 1 link | None | | Display | $O(1)$ | Save + update 1 entry | Restore 1 entry | ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Strategy Selection > [!TIP] > The KTU examiner may present a language feature and ask you to recommend a strategy. Use this decision matrix: | Language Feature | Required Strategy | Reasoning | |---|---|---| | Global constants | Static | Lifetime = program | | Recursive function locals | Stack | LIFO lifetime | | `new`/`malloc` objects | Heap | Non-LIFO lifetime | | Function parameters | Stack (typically) | Lifetime of activation | | Large arrays in old FORTRAN | Static | Pre-allocate | | Dynamically-growing array | Heap | Size unknown at compile time | | Closures / first-class functions | Heap + link | Outlive lexical scope | | `const` literal pool | Static (code seg) | Read-only, persistent | ### 3.5 Step-by-Step: Calling Sequence Breakdown A complete calling sequence for `caller → callee`: **Caller Side (before call):** 1. Evaluate each argument; store at callee's parameter slots. 2. Store frame pointer of caller (the **dynamic link**): $$\text{store}(\text{FP}_{\text{caller}}, \text{slot}_{\text{dynlink}})$$ 3. Store return address (label of instruction after call): $$\text{store}(\text{PC}_{\text{ret}}, \text{slot}_{\text{retaddr}})$$ 4. Transfer control (jump to callee entry point). **Callee Side (prologue):** 5. Save callee-saved registers into current frame: $$\text{store}(\text{Reg}_i, \text{slot}_{\text{reg}_i})$$ 6. Update frame pointer: $$\text{FP}_{\text{new}} \leftarrow \text{SP} - S_{\text{locals}} - S_{\text{temps}}$$ 7. Initialize local variables (e.g., zero-fill or default values). 8. Begin executing callee body. **Callee Side (epilogue, before return):** 9. Place return value in agreed slot: $$\text{store}(\text{retval}, \text{slot}_{\text{retval}})$$ 10. Restore callee-saved registers: $$\text{Reg}_i \leftarrow \text{load}(\text{slot}_{\text{reg}_i})$$ 11. Restore caller's frame pointer: $$\text{FP} \leftarrow \text{load}(\text{slot}_{\text{dynlink}})$$ 12. Pop the frame, then return to caller: $$\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{FP}_{\text{new}} + S_{\text{frame}}$$ **Caller Side (after call):** 13. Retrieve return value (if any). 14. Continue execution. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing *static link* (access link → lexical parent) with *dynamic link* (control link → runtime caller). They are **different** and serve different purposes! ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Activation Tree for a Recursive Program For the program: `main → A → A → A → B` (with one call to B from the deepest A): ```mermaid graph TD mainNode["main"] a1["A instance 1"] a2["A instance 2"] a3["A instance 3"] b1["B instance 1"] mainNode --> a1 a1 --> a2 a2 --> a3 a3 --> b1 ``` **Reading the tree:** - Each **node** = one activation (one stack frame) - **Path from root** = nesting of calls - **Sibling nodes** = activations that are alive at the same time - **Depth of a node** = depth of the activation (used for static links) ### 4.2 Activation Record — Detailed Memory Layout ```mermaid graph TB arTop["Return Value
offset FP+16"] arParam["Parameters
offset FP+12"] arRet["Return Address
offset FP+8"] arFP["Saved Frame Pointer
Dynamic Link
offset FP+4"] arSL["Static Link
Access Link
offset FP+0"] arLocals["Local Variables
offset FP-4 to FP-12"] arTemps["Temporaries
offset FP-16 to FP-24"] arFP --> arTop arSL --> arFP arRet --> arSL arParam --> arRet arTop --> arParam arLocals --> arSL arTemps --> arLocals ``` > **Note:** The exact ordering (parameters above or below return address) varies by calling convention. The KTU-expected convention places parameters at **positive offsets** from FP, locals at **negative offsets**. ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Runtime Storage System ```mermaid graph TB srcProg["Source Program
procedures and data declarations"] symTab["Symbol Table
name, type, scope, offset"] actRecGen["Activation Record
Generator"] stackMgr["Stack Manager
push/pop frames"] heapMgr["Heap Manager
allocate/free blocks"] staticSeg["Static Segment
global variables and literals"] codeSeg["Code Segment
executable instructions"] stackSeg["Stack Segment
runtime activation frames"] heapSeg["Heap Segment
dynamic objects"] runtimeMem["Runtime Memory Layout"] srcProg --> symTab symTab --> actRecGen actRecGen --> stackMgr symTab --> heapMgr actRecGen --> staticSeg stackMgr --> stackSeg heapMgr --> heapSeg staticSeg --> runtimeMem stackSeg --> runtimeMem heapSeg --> runtimeMem codeSeg --> runtimeMem ``` ### 4.4 Static Link Chain Diagram (Nested Procedures) ```mermaid graph TD mainFrame["main frame
depth 1
FP_main"] p1Frame["P1 frame
depth 2
FP_p1"] p2Frame["P2 frame
depth 3
FP_p2"] p3Frame["P3 frame
depth 4
FP_p3"] mainFrame -->|static link| mainFrame p1Frame -->|static link| mainFrame p2Frame -->|static link| p1Frame p3Frame -->|static link| p2Frame p3Frame -->|follow 3 static links to reach x| mainFrame ``` For P3 (depth 4) accessing `x` defined in main (depth 1): $$\text{links to follow} = 4 - 1 = 3$$ $$\text{path: FP}_{P3} \xrightarrow{SL} FP_{P2} \xrightarrow{SL} FP_{P1} \xrightarrow{SL} FP_{main} \rightarrow x$$ ### 4.5 Display Mechanism Visualization ```mermaid graph TB disp["Display Array D
D1 = ptr to depth 1 frame
D2 = ptr to depth 2 frame
D3 = ptr to depth 3 frame
D4 = ptr to depth 4 frame"] f1["Frame at depth 1
contains x"] f2["Frame at depth 2"] f3["Frame at depth 3"] f4["Frame at depth 4
currently executing"] disp -->|D1| f1 disp -->|D2| f2 disp -->|D3| f3 disp -->|D4| f4 ``` **Access path for `x` from depth 4:** $$\text{Addr}(x) = D[1] + \text{offset}(x) \quad \text{(one array lookup + one offset add)}$$ ### 4.6 Stack Growth and Contraction Across Calls ```mermaid graph LR t1["Time 1: main running
Stack: main"] t2["Time 2: A called from main
Stack: main, A"] t3["Time 3: B called from A
Stack: main, A, B"] t4["Time 4: B returned
Stack: main, A"] t5["Time 5: A returned
Stack: main"] t1 --> t2 --> t3 --> t4 --> t5 ``` This illustrates the **LIFO discipline** of stack allocation — the most recent frame is always released first. ### 4.7 Heap Allocation Memory States ```mermaid graph TB h1["Initial Heap
one large free block"] h2["After alloc A
free | A | free"] h3["After alloc B
free | A | B | free"] h4["After free A
free | free | B | free
fragmentation occurs"] h5["After defragmentation GC
free | B | free
compacted"] h1 --> h2 --> h3 --> h4 --> h5 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fragmentation** is the heap allocator's main enemy. Two types: > - **Internal** — allocated block larger than requested > - **External** — free memory exists but is not contiguous ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks) --- #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **Explain the three storage allocation strategies used by a compiler.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three storage allocation strategies are: 1. **Static Allocation** — All data objects are bound to fixed memory locations at **compile time**. The addresses do not change during execution. Used for global variables, constants, and in languages without recursion (e.g., early FORTRAN). Fast access, but cannot support recursion or dynamic data structures. 2. **Stack Allocation** — Memory is managed as a **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) stack**. A new **activation record** is pushed onto the stack when a procedure is called and popped on return. Supports **recursion** naturally because each call gets a fresh copy of local variables. Used in C, Pascal, Java, and most modern languages. 3. **Heap Allocation** — Memory is allocated and deallocated in **arbitrary order** for objects whose lifetimes do not follow LIFO discipline (dynamic data structures, long-lived objects). Allocation is explicit (`malloc`/`new`) or implicit (**garbage collection**). Slower than stack but most flexible. --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **What is an activation record? List its typical contents.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **activation record** (also called a **stack frame**) is a block of memory allocated on the runtime stack for each invocation of a procedure. It holds all information needed for that particular execution of the procedure. **Typical contents:** | Field | Purpose | |---|---| | **Temporary values** | Intermediate results of expression evaluation | | **Local variables** | Variables declared in the procedure | | **Saved machine status** | Saved registers, condition codes | | **Access link (static link)** | Pointer to enclosing scope's activation | | **Control link (dynamic link)** | Pointer to caller's activation record | | **Parameters** | Actual arguments passed in | | **Return address** | Instruction to resume in caller after return | | **Return value** | Function result (if applicable) | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (ESE 14-Mark Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain in detail the **static** and **stack** storage allocation strategies with examples. Compare their relative merits. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is an **activation record**? Draw and explain the structure of a typical activation record. Discuss the **calling sequence** with the responsibilities of the caller and callee. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** **Static Storage Allocation:** In static allocation, every data object is bound to a fixed address at compile time. The compiler assigns absolute addresses (or fixed offsets from a known base) to: - Global variables → data segment - Local variables of a procedure → fixed offset within a procedure-specific data area - Constants → code segment (often as immediates) **Example (FORTRAN-like):** ```fortran PROGRAM MAIN INTEGER X, Y X = 10 Y = 20 CALL SUB(X, Y) END SUBROUTINE SUB(A, B) INTEGER A, B, C C = A + B END ``` Here, `X`, `Y` get fixed addresses (say, 1000, 1004). `A`, `B`, `C` in `SUB` get fixed offsets (say, 2000, 2004, 2008). These addresses are computed at compile time and never change. **Advantages:** - Simplest implementation — no runtime overhead - Fastest access — addresses are known statically - No garbage collection needed - Predictable memory usage → suitable for embedded/real-time systems **Disadvantages:** - **No recursion** — only one copy of locals exists - **No dynamic data structures** — size must be known at compile time - **Memory waste** — space reserved for maximum possible usage - **No dynamic scope** support in general **Stack Storage Allocation:** In stack allocation, a runtime stack is maintained. When a procedure is called, a new **activation record** is pushed onto the stack. When the procedure returns, its activation record is popped, releasing all local memory automatically. **Example (C-like):** ```c int factorial(int n) { if (n <= 1) return 1; return n * factorial(n - 1); // recursive call } ``` Each recursive call to `factorial` creates a new frame on the stack. The frames for `factorial(3)`, `factorial(2)`, `factorial(1)` coexist, each with its own local `n`. This is impossible with static allocation. **Advantages:** - **Supports recursion** naturally - **Memory efficient** — only active procedures occupy memory - **Automatic deallocation** — no programmer burden **Disadvantages:** - Slight overhead for push/pop operations - Slower access due to indirection through frame pointer - LIFO only — cannot allocate objects that outlive their procedure **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | Static | Stack | |---|---|---| | Allocation time | Compile | Call time | | Recursion | Not supported | Supported | | Access speed | Fastest | Fast | | Memory efficiency | Poor for large unused | Good | | Implementation | Trivial | Moderate (frame pointer mgmt) | --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Activation Record — Definition:** An activation record is a contiguous block of storage containing all data needed for a single execution of a procedure. It is created on the runtime stack when the procedure is entered and destroyed when it returns. **Structure (typical layout, KTU convention):** ``` Higher addresses ┌──────────────────────────┐ │ Return value │ (caller slot, FP+offset_max) ├──────────────────────────┤ │ Parameter n │ (FP + 12) ├──────────────────────────┤ │ Return address │ (FP + 8) ├──────────────────────────┤ │ Saved FP (dynamic link) │ (FP + 4) ├──────────────────────────┤ │ Static link (access) │ (FP + 0) ← FP points here ├──────────────────────────┤ │ Saved registers │ (FP - 4, FP - 8 ...) ├──────────────────────────┤ │ Local variables │ (FP - 12, ...) ├──────────────────────────┤ │ Temporaries │ (FP - 20, ...) └──────────────────────────┘ Lower addresses (SP nearby) ``` **Calling Sequence:** The calling sequence is the code executed at procedure entry and exit, divided between caller and callee for efficiency. **Caller's responsibilities (before call):** 1. Evaluate actual parameters; store in callee's parameter area. 2. Store its own frame pointer as the **dynamic link**: $$\text{Mem}[FP_{\text{callee}} + 4] \leftarrow FP_{\text{caller}}$$ 3. Store the **return address**: $$\text{Mem}[FP_{\text{callee}} + 8] \leftarrow PC_{\text{after-call}}$$ 4. Perform the jump to the callee's entry point. **Callee's responsibilities (prologue):** 5. Save callee-saved registers in the local area. 6. Establish its own frame pointer: $$FP_{\text{new}} \leftarrow SP - S_{\text{locals}} - S_{\text{temps}} - S_{\text{overhead}}$$ 7. Initialize local variables and set up the **static link** (passed in or computed). 8. Begin execution of the procedure body. **Callee's responsibilities (epilogue, before return):** 9. Place the return value in the agreed-upon slot. 10. Restore callee-saved registers. 11. Restore the caller's frame pointer from the dynamic link: $$FP \leftarrow \text{Mem}[FP_{\text{old}} + 4]$$ 12. Reset SP to the caller's frame and jump to the return address. **Caller's responsibilities (after return):** 13. Retrieve the return value. 14. Continue execution. This division minimizes the amount of work done at each call and enables tail-call optimizations in advanced compilers. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **heap** storage allocation strategy. Discuss various techniques used for heap management, including the **free-list** and **buddy system** approaches. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What are **static links** and **displays**? Explain with diagrams how they enable access to non-local variables. Compare their efficiency. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** **Heap Allocation — Overview:** The heap is a region of memory used for **dynamic allocation** — objects whose lifetimes do not conform to the LIFO discipline of the stack. A heap manager supports two operations: - **Allocate(size)** — return a block of at least `size` bytes - **Free(ptr)** — return a previously allocated block to the pool Heap allocation is required for: - Linked lists, trees, graphs - Objects created with `malloc`/`new` - Closures, dynamically-sized arrays - Long-lived data that must survive multiple procedure calls **Free-List Allocation:** The heap maintains a **linked list of free blocks**. When a request arrives: - **First-Fit**: Return the first free block large enough. - **Best-Fit**: Return the smallest free block that satisfies the request (less fragmentation). - **Worst-Fit**: Return the largest block (rarely used). **Allocation step:** ``` 1. Search free-list for a suitable block. 2. If block is larger than needed, split it: [requested_block | remaining_free_block] 3. Return pointer to requested block. ``` **Free step:** ``` 1. Mark block as free. 2. (Optional) Coalesce with adjacent free blocks to reduce fragmentation. ``` **Buddy System:** The buddy system is a special heap allocator that splits and merges blocks in **powers of 2**. **Properties:** - Sizes are always powers of 2: $2^0, 2^1, 2^2, \ldots, 2^k$. - Each size has its own free list: $L_0, L_1, \ldots, L_k$. - A block of size $2^i$ has a **buddy** — its unique neighboring block of the same size. **Allocation algorithm:** ``` allocate(size): 1. Find smallest i such that 2^i >= size. 2. If L_i is non-empty, pop a block. 3. Else, find a block in L_j for j > i, split recursively until a block of size 2^i is created. 4. Return the block. ``` **Free algorithm:** ``` free(block of size 2^i): 1. Find the buddy of block. 2. If buddy is free and same size, merge them to form 2^(i+1) block. 3. Repeat merging until no larger merge possible. 4. Add the final block to the appropriate free list. ``` **Buddies of a block at address `A` of size $2^i$:** $$\text{Buddy}(A, i) = A \oplus 2^i$$ where $\oplus$ is bitwise XOR. This gives $O(1)$ buddy calculation. **Comparison:** | Aspect | Free-List | Buddy System | |---|---|---| | Block size | Arbitrary | Powers of 2 | | Fragmentation | Higher (external) | Lower (internal waste up to 50%) | | Merge speed | Slow (linear search) | Fast (O(1) buddy find) | | Implementation | Simpler | More complex | --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Static Links (Access Links):** A **static link** is a pointer in each activation record that points to the activation record of the **statically enclosing** procedure (the lexical parent). To access a non-local variable, the compiler follows a chain of static links from the current activation up to the activation where the variable is defined. **Example:** ```pascal procedure P1; { depth 1, contains x } var x: integer; procedure P2; { depth 2, contains y } var y: integer; procedure P3; { depth 3, needs to access x } begin x := 10; { non-local access } end; begin ... end; begin ... end; ``` **Access path for `x` from P3:** $$\text{FP}_{P3} \xrightarrow{SL} \text{FP}_{P2} \xrightarrow{SL} \text{FP}_{P1} \rightarrow x$$ Number of links to follow: $$\Delta d = d_p - d_v = 3 - 1 = 2$$ **Time complexity:** $O(d_p - d_v)$. --- **Displays:** A **display** is a global array `D[1..d_max]` where `D[i]` is a pointer to the **most recent** activation record at lexical depth $i$. To access a non-local variable, the compiler performs: $$\text{Addr}(v) = D[d_v] + \text{offset}(v)$$ This is a **single array lookup** — no chain traversal. **Update rule on call to depth $d_p$:** 1. Save old value: $\text{temp} \leftarrow D[d_p]$ 2. Set new value: $D[d_p] \leftarrow FP_{\text{new}}$ 3. (Execute callee) 4. On return: $D[d_p] \leftarrow \text{temp}$ --- **Diagram: Static Link vs Display** **Static Link (chain of 3 frames accessing depth-1 variable):** ``` ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ │ P3 │SL→ │ P2 │SL→ │ P1 │→ x │ depth 3│ │ depth 2│ │ depth 1│ └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘ 3 pointer dereferences + 1 offset add ``` **Display (single array lookup):** ``` D[1] ──────────────→ ┌────────┐ D[2] ──→ ┌────────┐ │ P1 │ D[3] ──→ │ P2 │ │ x │ │ depth 2│ └────────┘ ┌────────┤ │ │ P3 │ │ Access x: D[1] + offset(x) │ depth 3│ │ = 1 array lookup + 1 add └────────┘ ``` --- **Efficiency Comparison:** | Aspect | Static Link | Display | |---|---|---| | **Access time** | $O(d_p - d_v)$ pointer chases | $O(1)$ array lookup | | **Call overhead** | Set 1 link (already passed) | Save + update 1 array entry | | **Return overhead** | None | Restore 1 array entry | | **Space overhead** | 1 pointer per frame | 1 pointer per frame + 1 array of size $d_{\max}$ | | **Cache behavior** | Pointer chain → cache misses | Better locality | | **Use in practice** | GCC, many Pascal compilers | Some Algol implementations | **Conclusion:** Displays offer **constant-time** access at the cost of a small fixed-size global array and bookkeeping on every call. Static links are simpler but can be **slower** for deeply nested accesses. Modern compilers often use a combination: static links for parameter passing, displays (or equivalent array) for repeated access patterns. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Places Students Lose Marks in Storage Allocation Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing static link with dynamic link** — Static link = lexical parent; Dynamic link = runtime caller. They are **different pointers** with different purposes. > 2. **Forgetting offset direction** — Parameters are at **positive offsets** from FP, locals at **negative offsets**. Drawing them in reverse is an instant deduction. > 3. **Missing the role of the symbol table** — Storage allocation uses offsets from the symbol table. Skipping this step loses 1–2 marks. > 4. **Not mentioning recursion** — When asked to compare static and stack, always highlight that static **cannot** support recursion. Examiners look for this explicitly. > 5. **Skipping the calling sequence split** — The calling sequence is **shared** between caller and callee. Drawing it as a single block loses marks. > 6. **Display update on return** — Students often forget to **restore** the old display value. Without restoration, the next activation at the same depth will get wrong data. > 7. **Heap fragmentation** — Don't say "heap is bad because of fragmentation" without distinguishing **internal** vs **external** fragmentation. Examiners want precision. > 8. **Buddy system XOR** — The buddy formula $\text{Buddy} = A \oplus 2^i$ is high-yield. Memorize and reproduce correctly. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Storage Allocation Strategies** > > - [ ] **Three strategies** = Static, Stack, Heap. Know what each is best for. > - [ ] **Static allocation** = compile-time, no recursion, fixed addresses, used in FORTRAN. > - [ ] **Stack allocation** = LIFO, supports recursion, uses activation records. > - [ ] **Heap allocation** = arbitrary order, used for dynamic data, may need GC. > - [ ] **Activation record** = the unit of stack allocation. Contains: temps, locals, saved status, static link, dynamic link, parameters, return address, return value. > - [ ] **Frame pointer (FP)** is the anchor of each activation. Address of variable = FP ± offset. > - [ ] **Static link (access link)** → points to the **lexical parent**'s activation. > - [ ] **Dynamic link (control link)** → points to the **runtime caller**'s activation. > - [ ] **Calling sequence** is split between caller and callee to balance overhead. > - [ ] **Prologue** = entry code; **Epilogue** = exit code. > - [ ] **Static link chain length** = $d_p - d_v$ for accessing variable at depth $d_v$ from depth $d_p$. > - [ ] **Display** = global array of pointers, one per nesting depth, enables $O(1)$ non-local access. > - [ ] **Display update** on call: save old $D[d_p]$, set $D[d_p] = FP_{\text{new}}$, restore on return. > - [ ] **Heap management**: free-list (first-fit, best-fit), buddy system, garbage collection. > - [ ] **Buddy system** uses powers-of-2 block sizes; $\text{Buddy}(A, i) = A \oplus 2^i$. > - [ ] **Fragmentation** = internal (allocated > requested) vs external (free blocks scattered). > - [ ] **Real-world**: Static → embedded; Stack → C/Pascal; Heap → Java objects, closures, dynamic arrays. > - [ ] **Parameter passing** mechanisms: value, reference, value-result, name — all implemented via the activation record. > - [ ] **Closures** = function + captured environment; needed for first-class functions in functional languages. --- **End of Module 3 — Storage Allocation Strategies Notes** **Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme / PEEOT751 / NEP 2020 Aligned**

topics.title: Intermediate Languages: Graphical Representations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT751 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Intermediate Languages: Graphical Representations ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intermediate Representation (IR)** is the data structure used by a compiler to represent the source program internally, positioned **between the front-end (analysis phase)** and the **back-end (synthesis phase)**. It is a *machine-independent abstraction* that captures both the **semantics** and the **syntactic structure** of the source program in a form that is easier to analyze, optimize, and translate into the target machine code. **Graphical Intermediate Representations (Graphical IRs)** are those IRs that depict the program in a **graph-theoretic structure**, where: - **Nodes** represent program constructs (operands, operators, statements, basic blocks, procedures). - **Edges** represent the **flow of control** or **flow of data** between these constructs. > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Syllabus Specifics (Module 4 – Intermediate Code Generation): > The graphical representations covered in this module include: > 1. **Syntax Trees (Parse Trees)** > 2. **Abstract Syntax Trees (AST)** > 3. **Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)** > 4. **Three-Address Code (as a Quaternary Graphical Form)** > 5. **Control Flow Graphs (CFGs)** > 6. **Basic Blocks and Flow Graphs** ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English) Imagine you are a **civil engineer designing a flyover** in Kochi. Before the actual construction begins, you don't directly hand a verbal description to the labourer. Instead, you prepare: - A **blueprint (plan view)** → This is like the **Parse Tree** (shows every tiny structural detail). - A **simplified schematic diagram** → This is like the **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** (removes parenthesis, commas; keeps only the essential structure). - A **resource-sharing diagram** where the same pillar is used in two places → This is the **DAG** (recognizes and shares common sub-expressions like `b * -c`). - A **traffic-flow plan** showing vehicles moving from one junction to another → This is the **Control Flow Graph (CFG)**. - A **step-by-step instruction card** for the contractor → This is the **Three-Address Code (TAC)**. > [!TIP] > **Key Takeaway:** Graphical IRs are the *engineer's drawing board* of the compiler — they make program analysis (optimization) and code generation **systematic, visual, and verifiable**. ### 1.3 Classification of Intermediate Representations | **Linear IR** (Flat List) | **Graphical IR** (Hierarchical / Network) | **Hybrid IR** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Postfix (Reverse Polish) Notation | Syntax Tree | Control Flow Graph (built on TAC) | | Three-Address Code (Quadruples) | Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) | — | | Three-Address Code (Triples) | Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) | — | | Stack-Machine Code | Flow Graph of Basic Blocks | — | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Primary Graphical Representations — A Breakdown #### 2.1.1 Syntax Tree (Parse Tree) A **Syntax Tree** is a hierarchical tree in which each **interior node** represents an *operator*, and each **leaf node** represents an *operand*. It is the direct, un-pruned output of a *syntax analyzer (parser)*. **Properties:** - Each leaf is labeled with an operand (variable or constant). - Each interior node is labeled with an operator. - Inorder traversal of the tree yields the original infix expression (with parentheses implied by the tree structure). > [!NOTE] > A *parse tree* shows the **derivations** (including all grammar symbols), whereas a **syntax tree** is a *condensed* form of the parse tree showing only the operators and operands of the expression. **Example:** For the expression `a + b * c`, the parse tree contains many grammar non-terminals (`E`, `T`, `F`), but the **syntax tree** has only the operator/operand nodes. #### 2.1.2 Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) The **AST** is a *further abstraction* of the syntax tree in which: - **Syntactic details** (parentheses, semicolons, commas) are **removed**. - Each construct has a **single node** regardless of how the grammar derives it. - It is the *standard input* to semantic analysis and many optimization phases. **Why AST over Parse Tree?** 1. **Smaller and faster** to traverse. 2. Carries **only semantic information** — no redundant punctuation. 3. Forms the basis of most modern compiler frameworks (LLVM IR, GCC's GIMPLE are AST/SSA-based). #### 2.1.3 Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) A **DAG** is a graph (not a tree) in which **common sub-expressions are merged into a single node**. It is the most "compact" graphical representation and is the *primary data structure for performing local optimizations*. **Key Properties:** - **Directed**: Edges have a direction (from operand to operator). - **Acyclic**: No cycles — `a = a + 1` is broken by renaming. - **Sharing**: Identical sub-expressions point to the **same node**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **DAG vs AST**: In an AST, the sub-expression `b * -c` in `a = b * -c + b * -c` appears **twice**.
