4.3 Translation Flow: Expression to Triples ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Read Leaf b] -->|push b| S1[Stack: b] S1 --> P2[Read Leaf c] P2 -->|push c| S2[Stack: b, c] S2 --> P3[Apply uminus] P3 -->|pop c, emit Triple 0, push 0| S3[Stack: b, 0] S3 --> P4[Apply star] P4 -->|pop 0 and b, emit Triple 1, push 1| S4[Stack: 1] S4 --> P5[Read Leaf b] P5 -->|push b| S5[Stack: 1, b] S5 --> P6[Read Leaf c] P6 -->|push c| S6[Stack: 1, b, c] S6 --> P7[Apply uminus] P7 -->|pop c, emit Triple 2, push 2| S7[Stack: 1, b, 2] S7 --> P8[Apply star] P8 -->|pop 2 and b, emit Triple 3, push 3| S8[Stack: 1, 3] S8 --> P9[Apply plus] P9 -->|pop 3 and 1, emit Triple 4, push 4| S9[Stack: 4] S9 --> P10[Assign to a] P10 -->|pop 4, emit Triple 5| DONE[Triple Table Complete] style P1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 style P3 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style P4 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style P7 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style P8 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style P9 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style P10 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 style DONE fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.4 Triples vs. Quadruples vs. Indirect Triples — Comparison Block ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph QUAD[Quadruples] Q1[op] Q2[arg1] Q3[arg2] Q4[result] end subgraph TRIP[Triples] T1a[op] T2a[arg1] T3a[arg2] T4a[Position is Result] end subgraph IND[Indirect Triples] I1[Instruction Pointer] I2[Triple Table op arg1 arg2] end style Q1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style Q2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style Q3 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style Q4 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style T1a fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style T2a fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style T3a fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style T4a fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style I1 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 style I2 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: Define a Triple in intermediate code generation. How does it differ from a Quadruple?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (2 Marks):** A **Triple** is a compact three-address code representation in which each instruction is stored as a 3-tuple $(op, \ arg_1, \ arg_2)$. The result of a triple is **implicitly denoted by its position (index)** in the instruction list, rather than by an explicit temporary name. > **Difference from Quadruple (1 Mark):** > - **Quadruple** uses 4 fields: $(op, \ arg_1, \ arg_2, \ result)$ with an explicit named temporary like `$t_1` in the `result` field. > - **Triple** uses 3 fields and replaces the `result` field with the **positional index** of the instruction itself, saving ~25% memory but making optimization harder. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: What is meant by the notation `(0)` and `(1)` appearing in the $arg$ fields of a Triple? Why is this notation used?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Meaning (2 Marks):** The parenthesized index, e.g., `(0)` or `(1)`, is a **reference to another triple** in the table. It indicates that the value of the operand is the **result produced by the triple at that position**. > > For example, `(*, b, (0))` means "multiply $b$ by the result of Triple 0." > > **Why Used (1 Mark):** This notation is used because **Triples have no explicit result field** — the result of an instruction is referenced **positionally**. The parentheses serve as syntactic markers to distinguish a triple-reference from a normal variable name. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, you must answer **ONE full question** from the choice provided. Each question carries sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]` **(a) Explain the concept of Triples in intermediate code generation with a suitable example. List any two advantages of Triples over Quadruples.** **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Definition (2 Marks):** Triples are a compact form of three-address code in which each instruction is represented as a 3-tuple $(op, \ arg_1, \ arg_2)$. The result of a triple is **implicitly identified by its position (index)** in the list of triples, rather than by a named temporary variable. **Example (3 Marks):** Consider the statement: $$x = (a + b) \times (-c)$$ **Step-by-step Triple generation:** 1. Read `a` → push `a` 2. Read `b` → push `b` 3. Apply `+` → pop `a`, `b`; emit Triple (0): `(+, a, b)`; push `(0)` 4. Read `c` → push `c` 5. Apply `uminus` → pop `c`; emit Triple (1): `(uminus, c, _)`; push `(1)` 6. Apply `*` → pop `(0)`, `(1)`; emit Triple (2): `(*, (0), (1))`; push `(2)` 7. Assign to `x` → pop `(2)`; emit Triple (3): `(:=, x, (2))` | Index | $op$ | $arg_1$ | $arg_2$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | (0) | + | a | b | | (1) | uminus | c | — | | (2) | * | (0) | (1) | | (3) | := | x | (2) | **Two Advantages of Triples over Quadruples (2 Marks):** 1. **Memory Efficiency:** Triples use only 3 fields per instruction compared to 4 in Quadruples, resulting in a **25% reduction in storage space**. 2. **Simpler Translation:** No need to generate and maintain a symbol-table of temporary names like `$t_1$, `$t_2$...` — the position index serves as the implicit result identifier. --- **(b) Generate the Triple representation for the expression `a := b * -c + b * -c`. Also write the corresponding Quadruple representation and compare.** **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Triple Representation (3 Marks):** The same expression yields the following triple table: | Index | $op$ | $arg_1$ | $arg_2$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | (0) | uminus | c | — | | (1) | * | b | (0) | | (2) | uminus | c | — | | (3) | * | b | (2) | | (4) | + | (1) | (3) | | (5) | := | a | (4) | **Quadruple Representation (2 Marks):** | Index | $op$ | $arg_1$ | $arg_2$ | $result$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | uminus | c | — | $t_1$ | | 1 | * | b | $t_1$ | $t_2$ | | 2 | uminus | c | — | $t_3$ | | 3 | * | b | $t_3$ | $t_4$ | | 4 | + | $t_2$ | $t_4$ | $t_5$ | | 5 | := | a | $t_5$ | — | **Comparison (2 Marks):** | Aspect | Triples | Quadruples | |---|---|---| | Fields per instruction | **3** | **4** | | Result representation | Positional index | Named temporary | | Memory required | Less (compact) | More | | Optimization difficulty | Harder (positions shift) | Easier (names stable) | | Reference mechanism | $(k)$ parenthesized | Direct name reference | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` **(a) What are the limitations of Triples? How does the Indirect Triple representation overcome these limitations?** **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Limitations of Triples (3 Marks):** 1. **Position Sensitivity:** The result of a triple is referred to by its **positional index**. If the compiler optimizer reorders triples (e.g., during code motion or dead-code elimination), all references to the moved triple's index **become invalid** and must be updated throughout the table. 2. **Difficult Optimization:** Techniques like **common subexpression elimination**, **loop-invariant code motion**, and **instruction reordering** are cumbersome because the indices act as implicit pointers that break on movement. 3. **No Direct Result Field:** Unlike Quadruples, triples lack a dedicated `result` field, making it harder to perform data-flow analysis that requires explicit result tracking. **Indirect Triple Representation (3 Marks):** **Indirect Triples** introduce an **extra level of indirection** through a **pointer (instruction list) array**: - The actual triples are stored in a **triple table** (same format as regular triples). - A separate **execution-order list** (an array of pointers) dictates the sequence of execution. **Structure:** | Indirect List (Pointers) | Points to Triple # | |:---:|:---:| | [0] | 14 | | [1] | 15 | | [2] | 16 | | [3] | 17 | | ... | ... | | Triple # | $op$ | $arg_1$ | $arg_2$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 14 | uminus | c | — | | 15 | * | b | (14) | | 16 | + | a | b | | 17 | := | x | (16) | **How It Overcomes Limitations (1 Mark):** By separating the **execution order** from the **triple storage**, optimization passes can **reorder the pointer list** (cheap) instead of **renumbering all triples** (expensive). The triple indices remain stable, and only the pointer array needs updating. --- **(b) Construct the Triple and Indirect Triple representation for the following three-address code segment:** **(7 Marks)** ``` t1 := a + b t2 := c + d t3 := t1 * t2 x := t3 - a ``` #### Model Solution **Triple Representation (3 Marks):** | Index | $op$ | $arg_1$ | $arg_2$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | (0) | + | a | b | | (1) | + | c | d | | (2) | * | (0) | (1) | | (3) | - | (2) | a | | (4) | := | x | (3) | **Indirect Triple Representation (4 Marks):** The triple table stores the actual operations: | Triple # | $op$ | $arg_1$ | $arg_2$ | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 100 | + | a | b | | 101 | + | c | d | | 102 | * | (100) | (101) | | 103 | - | (102) | a | | 104 | := | x | (103) | The **indirect list** (execution order) contains pointers to the triple table: | Indirect List Index | Pointer Value | |:---:|:---:| | 0 | 100 | | 1 | 101 | | 2 | 102 | | 3 | 103 | | 4 | 104 | > **Note:** Triple numbers in the indirect table are typically chosen starting from 100 (or any base) to clearly distinguish the **storage index** from the **execution pointer index**. **Key Benefit Demonstrated:** If the optimizer decides to swap the order of Triple 100 and Triple 101 (e.g., for pipeline reasons), it only needs to swap pointers in the indirect list — the **triple numbers remain stable**, so no internal references need to change. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting to leave $arg_2$ blank for unary operators** like `uminus` and `not`. The triple still has 3 fields, but $arg_2$ is undefined. Examiners specifically check for this. > > 2. **Confusing Triples with Indirect Triples.** Triples have **direct positional references** (e.g., `(0)`, `(1)`). Indirect Triples have an **additional pointer array**. Mixing them up is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. > > 3. **Not showing the semantic stack evolution** in long-answer questions. KTU examiners award partial credit for the **process**, not just the final table. Always show step-by-step stack push/pop operations. > > 4. **Incorrect parenthesization in references:** Writing `arg = 0` instead of `arg = (0)`. The parentheses are **mandatory syntax** to indicate a triple reference vs. a variable name. > > 5. **Forgetting the assignment triple (`:=`)** at the end of expression evaluation. Every statement must terminate with an assignment triple if the language uses assignments. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Triples (Module 4, PEEOT751)** - **Definition:** A triple is a 3-tuple $(op, \ arg_1, \ arg_2)$ used as compact three-address code. - **Result:** Implicitly the **positional index** of the triple — no explicit `result` field. - **Reference Syntax:** Parenthesized indices like `(0)`, `(1)` denote triple references. - **Unary Ops:** $arg_2$ is **left blank** for operators like `uminus`, `not`. - **Memory Savings:** 25% less storage than Quadruples (3 fields vs. 4). - **Major Drawback:** Positional referencing makes **code optimization difficult** — moving a triple breaks all references. - **Solution:** Use **Indirect Triples** with a pointer array to decouple execution order from storage. - **Algorithm:** Semantic stack-based emission — one triple per internal AST node. - **Total Triples for Statement with $k$ binary, $m$ unary ops, 1 assignment:** $N = k + m + 1$. - **Comparison Anchors:** Triples = compact but rigid; Quadruples = verbose but optimization-friendly; Indirect Triples = compact + optimization-friendly. - **LLVM Connection:** Modern compilers (LLVM) use SSA with positional temporaries — a descendant concept of triples. - **Exam Triggers:** "Generate Triples for...", "Compare Triples and Quadruples", "Explain Indirect Triples", "Differentiate Triples and Indirect Triples".

topics.title: Code Optimization: Principal Sources of Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Code Optimization: Principal Sources of Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Code Optimization

**Code Optimization** is the phase of a compiler that attempts to improve the **intermediate code** (or the final machine code) so that the resulting program runs **faster, consumes less memory, and/or uses less power**, while preserving the original program's input-output behavior (i.e., producing the same output for every valid input).

Formally, an optimization transformation is **safe (or function-preserving)** if it does not alter the meaning (semantics) of the source program. The principal sources of optimization are the systematic categories of code patterns that compilers detect and transform to achieve these improvements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Code optimization is a program transformation technique, which tries to improve the intermediate code by making it consume fewer resources (i.e., CPU cycles, memory, power) so that faster-running machine code can be obtained. The principal sources of optimization refer to the specific categories of redundancies and inefficiencies that compilers exploit — namely function-preserving transformations, loop optimizations, and global/flow-based optimizations.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Trip Planner" Intuition

Imagine you are a **travel agent** planning a road trip from Kochi to Delhi for a client.

- **The Original Plan (Unoptimized Code):** You write down every single step — "Drive 1 km, stop, check the map, drive 1 km, stop, check the map again, fill water, drive..." It is correct, but full of unnecessary stops and repeated actions.
- **Optimized Plan (Optimized Code):** You now merge the tiny 1-km steps into one continuous 500-km highway drive (eliminating redundant checks — **dead code elimination**), you notice that you check the map the same way 10 times so you just check it once and copy the result forward (**common subexpression elimination + copy propagation**), you pre-calc that toll = 2× distance (**constant folding**), and you observe that "check rear-view mirror" is done 1000 times in a loop where it is useless (**loop optimization / code motion**).

In the same way, the compiler scans the code, identifies **redundancies and inefficiencies** (principal sources of optimization), and applies transformations to produce a leaner, faster program.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Optimization:**
> In compiler theory, "optimization" is a slight misnomer. Compilers do not produce the *truly optimal* code (that is an NP-hard problem in general). Instead, they apply **heuristic transformations** that improve the code *on average* without changing its observable behavior.

## 1.3 The Three Principal Sources of Optimization (Overview)

The KTU syllabus emphasizes that virtually **all optimization techniques** derive from one of three principal sources:

| # | Principal Source | What It Targets | Example Transformations |
|---|------------------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Function-Preserving Transformations** | Redundancies in expressions, copies, and dead code | CSE, Copy Propagation, Dead-Code Elimination, Constant Folding/Propagation |
| 2 | **Loop Optimizations** | Inefficiencies inside loops (which execute many times) | Code Motion, Induction-Variable Elimination, Strength Reduction, Loop Unrolling |
| 3 | **Global (Flow-Graph Based) Optimizations** | Cross-block inefficiencies using data-flow analysis | Global CSE, Live-Variable Analysis, Reaching Definitions, Available Expressions |

Additionally, **Peephole Optimization** is a lightweight, local optimization technique that examines a sliding window ("peephole") of consecutive instructions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Source 1: Function-Preserving Transformations

These transformations are applied within **basic blocks** (or across them in global form) and they never change what the program computes.

### 2.1.1 Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)

If an expression `E` is computed more than once, and **none of its operands have changed** between the computations, then the second computation is redundant.

```
a = b + c        ; t1 = b + c
d = b + c   →    ; a = t1
e = a + d        ; d = t1
                 ; e = a + d
```

The repeated `b + c` is replaced by a reference to the previously computed value `t1`.

### 2.1.2 Copy Propagation

After an assignment `x = y`, all subsequent uses of `x` can be replaced with `y`, provided `y` itself has not been redefined.

```
x = y                    ; (eliminate x entirely)
... use x ...    →       ; ... use y ...
```

### 2.1.3 Dead-Code Elimination

A variable is **dead** at a point if its value is never used (or its definition is overwritten before use). Statements that compute only dead values can be removed.

> [!NOTE]
> Often, **copy propagation** enables **dead-code elimination**: once `x = y` is propagated, the variable `x` becomes dead and the copy itself can be deleted.

### 2.1.4 Constant Folding

Compile-time evaluation of expressions whose operands are all known constants.

```
x = 2 + 3 * 5     →    x = 17
```

### 2.1.5 Constant Propagation

Replacing a variable with one of its known constant values.

```
pi = 3.14
area = pi * r * r   →   area = 3.14 * r * r    (then constant-folded further)
```

## 2.2 Source 2: Loop Optimizations

Loops are the **#1 target** of optimization because the body executes repeatedly — a small per-iteration saving compounds into a large total saving.

### 2.2.1 Code Motion (Loop-Invariant Code Motion — LICM)

If a computation inside a loop **does not depend on anything that changes inside the loop**, it is **loop-invariant** and can be **hoisted** (moved) out of the loop, computing it only once.

```c
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    x = y + z;              // ← loop-invariant
    a[i] = x * i;
}
```

becomes:

```c
x = y + z;                   // computed once
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    a[i] = x * i;
}
```

### 2.2.2 Induction Variable Elimination

A variable whose value forms an **arithmetic progression** (increases/decreases by a constant each iteration) is called an *induction variable*. A common pattern is the elimination of one induction variable in terms of another.

```c
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    j = 4 * i;      // j is also an induction variable, derived from i
    a[j] = i;
}
```

Since `j = 4*i`, the access `a[j]` can be rewritten as `a[4*i]`, and `j` can be eliminated.

### 2.2.3 Strength Reduction

Replacing an **expensive operation** with a **cheaper equivalent** that yields the same result, typically within a loop.

| Expensive Operation | Cheaper Replacement |
|--------------------|---------------------|
| Multiplication by 2 | Left shift (`<< 1`) |
| Multiplication by 4 | Left shift (`<< 2`) |
| Division by 2 | Right shift (`>> 1`) |
| Exponentiation | Repeated multiplication (constant-folded) |
| Array index via `*` | Pointer increment |

### 2.2.4 Loop Unrolling

Replicating the body of a loop multiple times to **reduce loop overhead** (branching, counter increment).

```c
for (i = 0; i < 4; i++)   →   a[0]=0; a[1]=1; a[2]=2; a[3]=3;
    a[i] = i;
```

### 2.2.5 Loop Fusion / Loop Fission

- **Loop Fusion (Jamming):** Combining two adjacent loops over the same index range into one.
- **Loop Fission (Distribution):** Splitting a single loop into two to improve cache locality or enable vectorization.

## 2.3 Source 3: Global (Flow-Graph Based) Optimizations

Local optimizations operate within a single **basic block**. Global optimizations operate across the entire **flow graph** using **data-flow analysis**.

### 2.3.1 Basic Blocks and Flow Graphs

- A **Basic Block (BB)** is a maximal sequence of consecutive three-address instructions (3A code) in which **flow of control enters only at the beginning and leaves only at the end** (no jumps in the middle, no jumps out except at the end).
- A **Flow Graph** is a directed graph `G = (N, E)` whose nodes are the basic blocks and whose edges represent control flow.

### 2.3.2 DAG Representation for Local Optimization

A **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** is a valuable data structure for detecting common subexpressions within a basic block.

- **Leaf nodes** → operands (variables/constants), labeled with an identifier.
- **Interior nodes** → operators applied to children.
- **Each interior node** is created *only once* (the first time a subexpression is seen); subsequent occurrences merely attach a label to the existing node.

### 2.3.3 Data-Flow Analysis

A framework for gathering information about the program that flows across basic-block boundaries. Key analyses include:

| Data-Flow Problem | Direction | What It Computes |
|-------------------|-----------|------------------|
| Reaching Definitions | Forward | For each point, which definitions may reach |
| Live-Variable Analysis | Backward | For each point, which variables are live (will be used later) |
| Available Expressions | Forward | Which expressions are guaranteed to be available |
| Very Busy Expressions | Backward | Which expressions are guaranteed to be used before redefinition |

## 2.4 Peephole Optimization

A simple but powerful **local** technique that examines a small sliding window (the "peephole") of a few instructions and replaces them with an equivalent but better sequence. Common patterns:

| # | Peephole Transformation | Example |
|---|------------------------|---------|
| 1 | Redundant load/store elimination | `MOV a, R1; MOV R1, b` → `MOV a, b` |
| 2 | Constant folding | `MUL R1, 2` → `SHL R1, 1` |
| 3 | Strength reduction | `MUL R, 2` → `ADD R, R` |
| 4 | Algebraic identities | `MUL R, 1` → `MOV R, R` (eliminated) |
| 5 | Jump-to-jump optimization | `JMP L1; ... L1: JMP L2` → `JMP L2; ... L1: JMP L2` |
| 6 | Null sequence elimination | `ADD R, 0` → (removed) |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Metric Sheet

| Symbol / Metric | Meaning | Unit / Type |
|-----------------|---------|-------------|
| $\vert BB \vert$ | Number of basic blocks in a function | Integer count |
| $\vert E \vert$ | Number of edges in flow graph | Integer count |
| $n$ | Number of times a loop body executes | Iteration count |
| $T_{orig}$ | Original loop execution time | Time units |
| $T_{opt}$ | Optimized loop execution time | Time units |
| Speedup $S$ | $S = \dfrac{T_{orig}}{T_{opt}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| Cost of LICM | Cost reduction = $(n-1) \cdot C_{inv} - C_{hoist}$ where $C_{inv}$ is invariant computation cost, $C_{hoist}$ is the one-time hoist cost | Time units |
| $C_{orig}$ | Original per-iteration instruction count | Instructions |
| $C_{opt}$ | Optimized per-iteration instruction count | Instructions |
| Code-motion savings | $S = (n-1)(C_{orig} - C_{opt}) - C_{setup}$ | Instructions saved |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary condition for LICM safety:** A loop-invariant computation `E` can be safely hoisted out of a loop **iff** the loop has **no other entry points** (i.e., it is a natural loop / well-formed loop). Otherwise, the hoisted computation may execute on a path that originally bypassed the loop, changing the program semantics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Modern production compilers (GCC with `-O2` / `-O3`, LLVM, MSVC, Intel ICC) implement virtually all of the transformations above. Loop nest optimization is the *cornerstone* of high-performance computing (HPC) for scientific simulation, ML training kernels, and database query execution. Peephole optimization is the final polish stage of nearly every modern code generator.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example: Building a DAG for a Basic Block

### 3.1.1 The Source Three-Address Code

Consider the basic block below (each statement is numbered):

```
(1)  t1 = 4 * i
(2)  t2 = a[t1]
(3)  t3 = 4 * i
(4)  t4 = b[t3]
(5)  t5 = t2 * t4
(6)  t6 = prod + t5
(7)  prod = t6
(8)  i = i + 1
(9)  t7 = 4 * i
(10) t8 = a[t7]
(11) t9 = b[t8]
(12) t10 = t9 * t8
(13) t11 = prod + t10
(14) prod = t11
```

This is the classic **Aho-Sethi-Ullman** "block" example used in KTU textbook references for DAG construction. We will construct the DAG step by step.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step DAG Construction (Exhaustive)

**Step 1 — Statement (1): `t1 = 4 * i`**

- Create a leaf node labeled `i` (if not present, attach to existing).
- Create a constant leaf node `4`.
- Create an interior node `(*)` with children `(4, i)`.
- Attach label `t1` to the `(*)` node.
- Node inventory: `i`, `4`, `(*)_t1`.

**Step 2 — Statement (2): `t2 = a[t1]`**

- Find node for `t1` (the `(*)` node).
- Create an interior node `([])` (array index) with children `(a, t1)`.
- Attach label `t2` to the `([])` node.

**Step 3 — Statement (3): `t3 = 4 * i`**

- We **search** the DAG: is there an existing `(*)` node with children `(4, i)`? **Yes** — it is the node already labeled `t1`.
- Therefore, we **do NOT create a new node**. We simply **attach the label `t3`** to the existing `(*)` node.
- Now that node has labels `t1` and `t3`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is exactly where CSE is detected:** both `t1` and `t3` reference the same DAG node, so the second computation `4 * i` is a common subexpression and is eliminated.

**Step 4 — Statement (4): `t4 = b[t3]`**

- Find node for `t3` (same as `t1` node).
- Create interior node `([])` with children `(b, t3)`.
- Attach label `t4`.

**Step 5 — Statement (5): `t5 = t2 * t4`**

- Find `t2` (the `a[4*i]` node) and `t4` (the `b[4*i]` node).
- Create interior `(*)` with children `(t2, t4)`.
- Attach label `t5`.

**Step 6 — Statement (6): `t6 = prod + t5`**

- Find `prod` and `t5`. Create `(+)` with children `(prod, t5)`. Attach label `t6`.
- Note: We must also **mark** the node labeled `prod` as having a "kill" since it is about to be redefined.

**Step 7 — Statement (7): `prod = t6`**

- **Re-attach** label `prod` to the node currently labeled `t6`.
- The previous `prod` leaf is now **dead** (its last use was in statement 6).

**Step 8 — Statement (8): `i = i + 1`**

- Find `i`. Create `(+)` with children `(i, 1)`. Attach label `i`.
- The previous `i` leaf is now dead.

**Step 9 — Statement (9): `t7 = 4 * i`**

- **Search:** Is there a `(*)` node with children `(4, i)` where the `i` is the **new** value of `i` (the one updated in step 8)?
- The previous `(*)_t1/_t3` node used the **old** `i`. Since the value of `i` has changed, the previous subexpression is **invalidated**.
- Therefore, we **create a new `(*)` node** with children `(4, i_new)`. Attach label `t7`.

**Step 10 — Statement (10): `t8 = a[t7]`**

- Create `([])` with children `(a, t7)`. Attach `t8`.

**Step 11 — Statement (11): `t9 = b[t8]`**

- Create `([])` with children `(b, t8)`. Attach `t9`.

**Step 12 — Statement (12): `t10 = t9 * t8`**

- Create `(*)` with children `(t9, t8)`. Attach `t10`.

**Step 13 — Statement (13): `t11 = prod + t10`**

- Create `(+)` with children `(prod, t10)`. Attach `t11`.

**Step 14 — Statement (14): `prod = t11`**

- Re-attach `prod` to the node labeled `t11`. Old `prod` is dead.

### 3.1.3 Final Optimized Code Reconstructed from the DAG

A **postorder traversal** of the DAG yields the optimized code:

```
t1 = 4 * i
t2 = a[t1]
t4 = b[t1]         ; ← CSE: t3 eliminated, uses t1 instead
t5 = t2 * t4
t6 = prod + t5
prod = t6
i = i + 1
t7 = 4 * i
t8 = a[t7]
t9 = b[t8]
t10 = t9 * t8
t11 = prod + t10
prod = t11
```

The DAG-based optimization has eliminated **one redundant multiplication** (`4*i` for `t3`) and one redundant array index. For a loop with $n$ iterations, the savings are:

$$
\text{Saved multiplications} = n - 1
$$

$$
\text{Saved array-index operations} = n - 1
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example: Strength Reduction + Induction Variable Elimination

### 3.2.1 Original Code

```c
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    a[2*i] = b[i] + c;
}
```

The compiler converts this to three-address code:

```
i = 0
L1: if i >= n goto L2
    j = 2 * i        ; ← strength reduction candidate
    t1 = a + j
    *t1 = b[i] + c
    i = i + 1
    goto L1
L2:
```

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Strength Reduction

Replace `j = 2 * i` with an **addition chain** initialized once and updated cheaply each iteration:

```
i = 0
j = 0
L1: if i >= n goto L2
    t1 = a + j
    *t1 = b[i] + c
    i = i + 1
    j = j + 2        ; ← multiplication replaced by addition
    goto L1
L2:
```

Per-iteration cost reduction:

$$
C_{orig} = 1 \text{ multiplication} + 1 \text{ addition (for i)}
$$

$$
C_{opt} = 1 \text{ addition (for i)} + 1 \text{ addition (for j)} = 2 \text{ additions}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Induction Variable Elimination (Optional Refinement)

Since `j` is used only as `a + j`, we can replace `j` by an expression in `i` and remove the increment of `j`:

```
i = 0
L1: if i >= n goto L2
    t1 = a + 2*i
    *t1 = b[i] + c
    i = i + 1
    goto L1
L2:
```

## 3.3 Worked Example: Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)

### 3.3.1 Original Loop

```c
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    x = y * z;
    a[i] = x + i;
}
```

The expression `y * z` does not depend on `i` (or any other variable modified inside the loop). It is **loop-invariant**.

### 3.3.2 After LICM

```c
x = y * z;                       ; ← hoisted out
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    a[i] = x + i;
}
```

### 3.3.3 Cost-Benefit Calculation

$$
\text{Original cost} = n \cdot C_{mult} + n \cdot C_{add}
$$

$$
\text{Optimized cost} = 1 \cdot C_{mult} + n \cdot C_{add}
$$

$$
\text{Total savings} = (n-1) \cdot C_{mult}
$$

For $n = 1000$ and assuming $C_{mult} = 4$ cycles (vs. $C_{add} = 1$ cycle):

$$
\text{Savings} = 999 \times 4 = 3996 \text{ cycles}
$$

This is the **core quantitative justification** for LICM in KTU exam answers.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Peephole Optimization on Three-Address Code

Given this 3A code segment:

```
L1: goto L2
L2: goto L3
L3: t = a + b
```

**Step 1 — Jump-to-jump elimination:** `goto L2; ... L2: goto L3` → `goto L3`. We get:

```
L1: goto L3
L3: t = a + b
```

**Step 2 — Unreachable-code elimination:** The block labeled `L2` (originally between `L1` and `L3`) is now unreachable, so it is deleted:

```
L1: goto L3
L3: t = a + b
```

**Step 3 — Single-statement redundant block removal:** Since `L1` now just jumps to `L3` with no computation in between, references to `L1` from other parts of the program are rewritten to `L3`.

This is the standard peephole pattern: **(load), (store), (jump-chain), (algebraic identity)** are eliminated in a sliding window.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Diagram: Principal Sources of Optimization — Hierarchical View

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[Code Optimization]

    root --> S1[Source 1 Function Preserving Transformations]
    root --> S2[Source 2 Loop Optimizations]
    root --> S3[Source 3 Global Flow Graph Optimizations]
    root --> S4[Peephole Optimization Lightweight Local]

    S1 --> s1a[Common Subexpression Elimination]
    S1 --> s1b[Copy Propagation]
    S1 --> s1c[Dead Code Elimination]
    S1 --> s1d[Constant Folding]
    S1 --> s1e[Constant Propagation]

    S2 --> s2a[Loop Invariant Code Motion LICM]
    S2 --> s2b[Induction Variable Elimination]
    S2 --> s2c[Strength Reduction]
    S2 --> s2d[Loop Unrolling]
    S2 --> s2e[Loop Fusion and Fission]

    S3 --> s3a[Basic Block Construction]
    S3 --> s3b[Flow Graph Construction]
    S3 --> s3c[DAG Representation]
    S3 --> s3d[Data Flow Analysis]

    S3d --> s3d1[Reaching Definitions]
    S3d --> s3d2[Live Variable Analysis]
    S3d --> s3d3[Available Expressions]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Compiler Optimization Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Source Program] --> B[Lexical Analyzer]
    B --> C[Syntax Analyzer]
    C --> D[Semantic Analyzer]
    D --> E[Intermediate Code Generator]
    E --> F[MACHINE INDEPENDENT OPTIMIZATION]
    F --> G[Code Generator]
    G --> H[Target Code]
    H --> I[MACHINE DEPENDENT OPTIMIZATION Peephole]
    I --> J[Final Optimized Code]

    F --> F1[Function Preserving]
    F --> F2[Loop Optimizations]
    F --> F3[Global Data Flow]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Sliding Peephole Window

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Instruction 1] --> P2[Instruction 2]
    P2 --> P3[Instruction 3]
    P3 --> P4[Instruction 4]
    P4 --> P5[Instruction 5]

    P2 -. peephole window .-> P4
    P2 -. transform .-> Q2[Optimized Instruction 2]
    P3 -. transform .-> Q3[Optimized Instruction 3]
    P4 -. transform .-> Q4[Optimized Instruction 4]
```

## 4.4 DAG Visualization for the Worked Example

```mermaid
graph TD
    I0[i OLD]
    C4[4]
    M1[t1 = 4 * i OLD]
    ARR_a[a]
    NODE2[t2 = a t1]
    ARR_b[b]
    NODE3[t4 = b t1]
    NODE4[t5 = t2 * t4]
    PROD0[prod OLD]
    NODE5[t6 = prod + t5]
    I1[i NEW]
    C1[1]
    ADD_I[i + 1]
    M2[t7 = 4 * i NEW]
    NODE8[t8 = a t7]
    NODE9[t9 = b t8]
    NODE10[t10 = t9 * t8]
    NODE11[prod NEW]

    C4 --> M1
    I0 --> M1
    ARR_a --> NODE2
    M1 --> NODE2
    ARR_b --> NODE3
    M1 --> NODE3
    NODE2 --> NODE4
    NODE3 --> NODE4
    PROD0 --> NODE5
    NODE4 --> NODE5
    I0 --> ADD_I
    C1 --> ADD_I
    ADD_I --> I1
    C4 --> M2
    I1 --> M2
    ARR_a --> NODE8
    M2 --> NODE8
    ARR_b --> NODE9
    NODE8 --> NODE9
    NODE9 --> NODE10
    NODE8 --> NODE10
    NODE5 --> NODE11
    NODE10 --> NODE11
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram above, notice that `M1` (the `4 * i` node) is **shared** between `t2 = a[t1]` and `t4 = b[t1]`, which is the visual proof of common-subexpression elimination.

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Flow Graph of a Program with Loops

```mermaid
graph TD
    B1[Entry Block] --> B2[Initialize]
    B2 --> B3[Loop Head]
    B3 --> B4[Loop Body]
    B4 --> B5[Loop Tail Update]
    B5 --> B3
    B3 -->|exit| B6[Exit Block]
    B6 --> B7[Final Code]
```

The region `B3 → B4 → B5 → B3` is the **natural loop** and the target of LICM, strength reduction, and induction-variable elimination.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define code optimization. List the principal sources of optimization. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Code Optimization** is a compiler phase that transforms intermediate code to produce a semantically equivalent program that runs faster, uses less memory, or consumes less power.

The **principal sources of optimization** are:

1. **Function-preserving transformations** — common subexpression elimination, copy propagation, dead-code elimination, constant folding, constant propagation.
2. **Loop optimizations** — code motion (LICM), induction-variable elimination, strength reduction, loop unrolling.
3. **Global (flow-graph based) optimizations** — basic block analysis, DAG construction, data-flow analysis (reaching definitions, live variables, available expressions).
4. **Peephole optimization** — local sliding-window transformations.

*(Award 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for listing with one-line descriptions.)*

### Q2. What is peephole optimization? Give two examples. `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Peephole optimization** is a local optimization technique that examines a small sliding window (typically 2–5 instructions) of the generated code and replaces the pattern with an equivalent but more efficient sequence.

**Examples:**

1. **Strength reduction:** `MUL R, 2` → `SHL R, 1` (or `ADD R, R`).
2. **Redundant load/store elimination:** `MOV a, R1; MOV R1, b` → `MOV a, b`.
3. **Algebraic identity:** `MUL R, 1` → (no operation / removed).
4. **Jump-to-jump chain:** `JMP L1; ... L1: JMP L2` → `JMP L2; ... L1: JMP L2`.

*(Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark per example, up to 2 marks for examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice** between two sub-questions. Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Full, step-by-step model solutions follow.

### ▶ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **principal sources of optimization** in a compiler. Discuss the role of function-preserving transformations. (7 marks)

**(b)** Construct a **DAG** for the following basic block and identify the common subexpressions:

```
(1) a = b + c
(2) d = b + c
(3) e = a + d
(4) f = a - d
(5) g = b + c
(6) h = g * e
```

Recompute the optimized three-address code from the DAG. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Principal Sources of Optimization** (3 marks):

A compiler optimization is any transformation that preserves program semantics while improving one or more resource metrics. They are grouped into four principal sources:

1. **Function-preserving transformations** — remove redundancies while preserving the original computation. They never alter what the program computes.
2. **Loop optimizations** — exploit the iterative nature of loops to reduce per-iteration cost.
3. **Global (flow-graph based) optimizations** — perform CSE and dead-code elimination across entire procedures using data-flow analysis.
4. **Peephole optimization** — local sliding-window replacement of instruction sequences.

**Function-preserving transformations in detail** (4 marks):

- **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE):** When `E` is computed more than once and operands have not changed, the second computation is replaced by a reference to the first. Implemented efficiently using DAGs within a basic block and via *available-expression analysis* globally.
- **Copy Propagation:** After `x = y`, replace later uses of `x` with `y`, which often enables dead-code elimination of the copy itself.
- **Dead-Code Elimination:** Statements whose results are never used (or are immediately overwritten) are removed.
- **Constant Folding:** Compile-time evaluation of constant expressions, e.g., `2 + 3*5 → 17`.
- **Constant Propagation:** Replacing a variable by a known constant value at a program point.

These transformations are typically applied in a fixed-point iteration: constant folding → constant propagation → CSE → copy propagation → dead-code elimination, until no further changes occur.

##### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step-by-step DAG construction** (5 marks):

1. `(1) a = b + c` → Create `+` node with children `b, c`; label `a`.
2. `(2) d = b + c` → Children `b, c` already exist with a `+` node. **Reuse** that node, attach label `d`. → **CSE detected.**
3. `(3) e = a + d` → Create new `+` node with children `a, d`; label `e`.
4. `(4) f = a - d` → Create new `-` node with children `a, d`; label `f`.
5. `(5) g = b + c` → Reuse the `+` node from step 1; attach label `g`. → **Another CSE.**
6. `(6) h = g * e` → Create new `*` node with children `g, e`; label `h`.

**DAG (textual):**

```
         (+)b,c  <-- labels: a, d, g
        /      \
   [sub-tree]    [sub-tree]

   e = (+)a,d
   f = (-)a,d
   h = (*)g,e   where g and a,d share their sub-nodes
```

**Optimized three-address code** (2 marks) — emitted via postorder traversal:

```
t1 = b + c
a  = t1
d  = t1
e  = a + d
f  = a - d
g  = t1           ; reuse of t1 instead of recomputing b+c
h  = g * e
```

**Savings:** Two `b + c` evaluations were eliminated (replaced by reuse of `t1`). For a block executed $n$ times, the savings are $(n-1) \cdot C_{add}$.

---

### ▶ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss **loop optimizations** in detail. With suitable examples, explain *loop-invariant code motion* and *strength reduction*. (7 marks)

**(b)** Consider the following C code segment:

```c
for (i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
    sum = sum + a[i];
    k = k + 3;
}
```

Apply **induction variable analysis and strength reduction** to optimize the three-address code. Show the original and optimized TAC. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Why loops are the prime target** (1 mark): The body executes $n$ times; even a small per-iteration saving compounds.

**Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)** (3 marks):

A computation `E` inside a loop is *loop-invariant* if all its operands are either constants or have definitions that *reach* the loop entry but are **not modified inside the loop**. Such `E` can be *hoisted* (moved) to a *pre-header* block that executes once.

*Example:*

```c
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    x = y * z;            // loop-invariant
    a[i] = x;
}
```

becomes:

```c
x = y * z;
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    a[i] = x;
}
```

Safety condition: the loop must have a **single entry point** (natural loop); otherwise hoisting could execute on paths that originally skipped the loop.

**Strength Reduction** (3 marks):

Replaces an expensive operation inside a loop (e.g., multiplication) with a cheaper equivalent (e.g., addition) by introducing a new induction variable that is updated incrementally.

*Example:* `j = j + 2` replaces `j = 2 * i` after the latter is recognized as a derived induction variable.

| Original | Strength-Reduced |
|----------|------------------|
| `MUL R, 2` | `SHL R, 1` |
| `DIV R, 2` | `SHR R, 1` |
| `MUL R, 4` | `SHL R, 2` |
| `j = 2 * i` | `j = j + 2` (per-iter) |

##### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Original three-address code** (2 marks):

```
i = 1
sum = 0
k = 0
L1: if i > n goto L2
    t1 = a[i]
    sum = sum + t1
    k = k + 3
    i = i + 1
    goto L1
L2:
```

**Observations** (2 marks):

- `i` is the **basic induction variable** (initialized to 1, incremented by 1 each iteration).
- `k = k + 3` is itself a derived pattern: `k = 0 + 3*(i-1) = 3*i - 3`. So `k` is a **derived induction variable** with multiplier $3$ and additive constant $-3$ relative to `i`.
- The expression `k + 3` after each iteration is a strength-reduction candidate, but more importantly we recognize that `k` can be **eliminated** entirely because it is derivable from `i`.

**Strength reduction applied to `k`** (2 marks):

Replace the per-iteration update `k = k + 3` with a derived computation `k = 3*i - 3`, computed on demand. We do not need to maintain `k` as a separate variable.

**Optimized three-address code** (1 mark):

```
i = 1
sum = 0
L1: if i > n goto L2
    t1 = a[i]
    sum = sum + t1
    ; k is no longer stored; any use of k is replaced by 3*i - 3
    i = i + 1
    goto L1
L2:
```

**Savings analysis** (mark distributed in the body): one addition per iteration eliminated; for a loop with $n$ iterations, total saving = $(n-1) \cdot C_{add}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting safety conditions:** Students often write "hoist `E` out of the loop" without checking that the loop has a single entry point and that `E` is truly loop-invariant (no operand redefined inside the loop body). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Confusing local vs. global CSE:** DAG-based CSE works *within a basic block only*. For *global* CSE, you must cite *available-expression analysis*; just saying "CSE" is incomplete.
> 3. **Confusing strength reduction with constant folding:** Strength reduction is performed *inside loops on induction variables*; constant folding is performed on compile-time constant expressions *anywhere*. Examiners mark these strictly.
> 4. **Not showing the optimized code in DAG questions:** A DAG construction answer is **incomplete** without (a) the reconstructed optimized three-address code and (b) a brief savings analysis. KTU examiners typically reserve 2–3 marks for this conclusion.
> 5. **Mislabeling induction variables:** A basic induction variable is incremented by a *constant*; a derived induction variable is a linear function of a basic one. Mixing these up costs marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Code Optimization: Principal Sources**

- **Definition:** Optimization = semantics-preserving transformation that improves speed, memory, or power. Compilers do *not* produce provably optimal code; they apply heuristics.
- **Source 1 — Function-Preserving Transformations:**
  - *Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE):* Replace repeated computation of unchanged subexpression with a reference to the first value. Implemented via DAG locally and *available-expression analysis* globally.
  - *Copy Propagation:* `x = y` → replace uses of `x` with `y`.
  - *Dead-Code Elimination:* Delete statements whose results are unused.
  - *Constant Folding:* Evaluate constant expressions at compile time.
  - *Constant Propagation:* Substitute known constant values.
  - *Pipeline:* Constant folding → constant propagation → CSE → copy propagation → dead-code elimination (repeat to fixed point).
- **Source 2 — Loop Optimizations (most impactful, since loops run many times):**
  - *LICM (Code Motion):* Hoist loop-invariant computations to a pre-header.
  - *Strength Reduction:* Replace `*` with `+` (and `*2` with `SHL`, etc.) on induction variables.
  - *Induction Variable Elimination:* Eliminate derived induction variables when they are linear functions of a basic one.
  - *Loop Unrolling:* Replicate body to reduce branch overhead.
  - *Loop Fusion / Fission:* Combine / split adjacent loops.
- **Source 3 — Global (Flow-Graph Based) Optimizations:**
  - *Basic Block:* Maximal straight-line code sequence; one entry, one exit.
  - *Flow Graph:* Directed graph of basic blocks.
  - *DAG:* Tool for local CSE and reordering within a basic block.
  - *Data-Flow Analyses:* Reaching definitions, live variables, available expressions, very-busy expressions.
- **Peephole Optimization:** Local sliding-window pass on the final instruction stream. Handles redundant loads/stores, jump chains, algebraic identities, strength reduction at machine level.
- **Safety conditions** (must always check): Loop has single entry (LICM); expression is genuinely loop-invariant (LICM, CSE); variable is actually dead (DCE); DAG nodes share the *same* operand values (CSE).
- **KTU-favorite questions:**
  1. Construct a DAG for a given basic block and write optimized TAC. (Very high frequency — practice with the Aho-Sethi-Ullman example.)
  2. Explain LICM with example and savings calculation.
  3. List principal sources of optimization.
  4. Define peephole optimization and give 2–3 transformations.
  5. Strength reduction with `j = 2*i` to `j = j + 2` example.
- **Numerical must-know:** For a loop of $n$ iterations, LICM savings $= (n-1) \cdot C_{inv} - C_{hoist}$. For the Aho-Sethi-Ullman DAG example, savings $= (n-1)$ multiplications and $(n-1)$ array-index operations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimization of Basic Blocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Optimization of Basic Blocks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Basic Block

A **Basic Block** is a maximal sequence of consecutive three-address instructions (or statements) in which **flow of control enters at the beginning** and **leaves at the end without any halt or branching in between**, except possibly at the very last instruction. Within a basic block, the instructions are executed sequentially without interruption.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Aho, Sethi, Ullman Standard):**
> "A basic block is a sequence of consecutive statements in which flow of control enters at the beginning and leaves only at the end, without any internal branching or halts."

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Optimization of Basic Blocks

**Optimization of Basic Blocks** refers to a set of local transformations applied to a basic block to **improve the execution time, reduce the number of operations, and minimize memory usage** of the generated intermediate code (typically three-address code) **without changing the program's observable behavior**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Principle (As Per KTU Module 4):**
> The optimization techniques operate on the **local level** (within a single basic block) using the **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** representation. The compiler guarantees that the **semantics (meaning)** of the program remain identical before and after optimization.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Messy Recipe":**
> Imagine you are a chef given a recipe (program) with several redundant steps. For example, the recipe says:
> 1. Chop 2 onions.
> 2. Chop 1 more onion.
> 3. Add 3 chopped onions to the pan.
>
> An **optimizer** (like a smart sous-chef) would recognize that Step 1 and Step 2 do the **same thing** (Common Subexpression), simplify it to "Chop 3 onions total" (Constant Folding), and rewrite the recipe to: *"Add 3 chopped onions to the pan."* The dish (program output) tastes exactly the same, but you saved time and effort.
>
> Similarly, in compiler design, the optimizer:
> - **Removes duplicate calculations** (Common Subexpression Elimination).
> - **Simplifies arithmetic at compile time** (Constant Folding).
> - **Cancels out redundant assignments** (Copy Propagation).
> - **Erases instructions whose results are never used** (Dead Code Elimination).

### 1.4 Why Basic Block Optimization?

| Aspect | Benefit | KTU Exam Weight |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Speed** | Reduces CPU cycles by eliminating redundant computations | High |
| **Space** | Reduces code size by reusing common expressions | High |
| **Power** | Lower energy consumption on mobile/embedded targets | Medium |
| **Registers** | Better register allocation since fewer live variables exist | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In production compilers like **GCC** (`-O2` / `-O3` flags) and **LLVM**, local optimizations are the first pass that dramatically improves the performance of the generated machine code for CPUs, GPUs, and DSP processors used in industries such as automotive, aerospace, and IoT.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Principal Sources of Optimization (KTU High-Yield Topic)

The Aho–Sethi–Ullman dragon book categorizes local optimizations into **four principal sources**. These are the *exact four* KTU expects you to memorize:

**Source 1: Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)**
- A subexpression is a **computation** (like `a*b` or `x[i]`).
- If the same subexpression was previously computed and **none of its operands have changed**, the redundant computation can be eliminated.
- Example: If `a = b * c` is computed, and later `d = b * c` appears, we can replace the second with `d = a`.

**Source 2: Dead Code Elimination (DCE)**
- Statements that compute values never subsequently used are termed **dead code**.
- Removing them does not change program behavior.
- Example: After `x = 5; y = x + 10; print(y);`, the assignment `x = 5` becomes dead if `x` is not used again.

**Source 3: Use of Algebraic Identities (Strength Reduction)**
- Replace expensive operations with cheaper equivalent ones.
- Example: `x * 1 → x`, `x + 0 → x`, `x ** 2 → x * x`, `x * 2 → x << 1` (bit shift).

**Source 4: Copy Propagation**
- A copy statement `u = v` propagates the value of `v` into uses of `u`.
- Example: `x = t; a = x * 2` becomes `a = t * 2` (eliminating the use of `x`).

### 2.2 The DAG — The Engine of Basic Block Optimization

A **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** is a labeled directed graph used to represent three-address code where:
- **Leaf nodes** represent **operands** (identifiers or constants).
- **Interior nodes** represent **operators**.
- A node `N` has **children** that are the operands and a **label** that is the operator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Property:** If a node `N` is constructed more than once (i.e., identical computations), it is **merged** (kept as one node). This merging is what enables **Common Subexpression Elimination**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Algebraic Identity | Original Expression | Optimized Form | Reason |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Additive Identity | $x + 0$ | $x$ | Adding zero changes nothing |
| 2 | Multiplicative Identity | $x * 1$ | $x$ | Multiplying by one changes nothing |
| 3 | Additive Annihilator | $x * 0$ | $0$ | Anything times zero is zero |
| 4 | Strength Reduction | $x * 2$ | $x \ll 1$ | Bit shift is faster than multiply |
| 5 | Strength Reduction | $x / 2$ | $x \gg 1$ | Bit shift is faster than divide |
| 6 | Exponent Idempotence | $x ** 2$ | $x * x$ | One multiplication vs. power routine |
| 7 | Subtractive Identity | $x - 0$ | $x$ | Subtracting zero changes nothing |
| 8 | Boolean Identity | $x \lor \text{false}$ | $x$ | OR with false is identity |
| 9 | Boolean Identity | $x \land \text{true}$ | $x$ | AND with true is identity |
| 10 | Complement | $\neg(\neg x)$ | $x$ | Double negation cancels |

| # | DAG Rule (Construction) | Action | Purpose |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Leaf Rule** | If operand is identifier, leaf is created with name=identifier | Represent operands |
| 2 | **Constant Rule** | If operand is constant, leaf is created with name=constant | Represent literals |
| 3 | **Merge Rule** | If a node `N` already exists for an operation, **do not create a new node**; reuse existing | Enables CSE |
| 4 | **Definition Rule** | For an assignment `x = y op z`, attach label `x` to the node of `y op z` | Track variable definitions |

| # | Optimization Source | Local Algorithm | KTU Cognitive Level |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | CSE | DAG node merging | Apply (L3) |
| 2 | DCE | Discard labels of un-used identifiers | Analyze (L4) |
| 3 | Algebraic | Apply identities during DAG build | Apply (L3) |
| 4 | Copy Propagation | Substitute `u` with `v` after `u = v` | Apply (L3) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The DAG and the four sources of optimization form the foundation of modern compilers (LLVM, GCC, MSVC). In high-performance computing, these optimizations are what allow a kernel to run **2x–10x faster**, directly impacting industries like weather simulation, drug discovery, and AI inference.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, DAG Construction & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The DAG Construction Algorithm (Theoretical Derivation)

Let us derive the **canonical algorithm** for constructing a DAG from a three-address basic block. Each instruction is of the form:

$$x = y \ \textbf{op} \ z$$

The compiler maintains three data structures: `Node(id)` for node ID, `Label(node)` for attached variable names, and `Operation(node)` for the operator.

**Step 1 (Initialization):** Create empty sets `NODES` (all created nodes) and `LABELS` (all variable-to-node mappings).

**Step 2 (Leaf Processing for `y`):** 
- If `y` is a constant → create a leaf node with name = `y` (or merge if constant-node exists).
- If `y` is an identifier → find the **most recent node** that was assigned to `y` using `LABELS[y]`. If none exists, create a leaf.

**Step 3 (Leaf Processing for `z`):** Apply the same rule as Step 2 for `z`.

**Step 4 (Interior Node Construction for `op`):**
- Search `NODES` for a node whose left child is the node for `y`, right child is the node for `z`, and operation is `op`.
- If found → reuse (this is **Common Subexpression Elimination**).
- If not found → create a new interior node.

**Step 5 (Label Update):** Add `x` to the `Label()` set of the resulting node and update `LABELS[x]`.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — DAG Construction with CSE (High-Yield KTU Problem)

**Given Three-Address Code (TAC):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= 4 * i \\
t_2 &= a [ t_1 ] \\
t_3 &= 4 * i \\
t_4 &= b [ t_3 ] \\
t_5 &= t_2 * t_4 \\
t_6 &= \text{prod} + t_5 \\
\text{prod} &= t_6 \\
t_7 &= i + 1 \\
i &= t_7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-Step DAG Construction:**

**Instruction 1:** `t1 = 4 * i`
- Create leaf `4`, leaf `i` (since `i` is undefined, create leaf).
- Create interior node `(*)` with children `(4, i)`. Label it `t1`.
- DAG so far: `[4] (*) [i]` with label `t1` on `(*)`.

**Instruction 2:** `t2 = a[t1]`
- Create leaf `a` (array name).
- Create interior node `(=[])` for array access. Its child is the node for `t1`.
- Label it `t2`.

**Instruction 3:** `t3 = 4 * i`
- Search for an existing node with `op=*`, children `(4, i)`. **FOUND!**
- **Merge Rule** activates. Reuse the same interior `(*)` node.
- Add label `t3` to the same node (so it now has labels `t1` and `t3`).

**Instruction 4:** `t4 = b[t3]`
- Create leaf `b`.
- Create interior node `(=[])` with child = node of `t3` (which is the same `(*)` node from Instruction 1).
- Label it `t4`.

**Instruction 5:** `t5 = t2 * t4`
- Look up `t2` → the `(=[])` node for `a`. Look up `t4` → the `(=[])` node for `b`.
- No existing `*` node with these children → create new `(*)` node.
- Label it `t5`.

**Instruction 6:** `t6 = prod + t5`
- Look up `prod` → find existing node for `prod` (initially leaf). Look up `t5` → the new `*` node.
- No existing `+` node → create new `(+)` node. Label it `t6`.

**Instruction 7:** `prod = t6`
- Look up `t6` → the `(+)`. Add label `prod` to it. The node for `prod` no longer points to the leaf; it now points to the `(+)` node.

**Instruction 8:** `t7 = i + 1`
- Look up `i` → still points to its leaf (since `i` hasn't been redefined yet). Create constant `1`.
- No existing `+` node with these children → create new `(+)` node. Label it `t7`.

**Instruction 9:** `i = t7`
- Look up `t7` → the new `(+)`. Add label `i` to it. The variable `i` now points to the new `(+)` node.

### 3.3 Reconstructing Optimized Code from the DAG (Reverse Mapping)

The optimized three-address code is obtained by performing a **postorder traversal** of the DAG and emitting instructions in reverse topological order. Variables that share a node are emitted together.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_3 &= 4 * i \quad \text{(or use } t_1 \text{ if alive)} \\
t_5 &= a [ t_1 ] * b [ t_3 ] \\
t_6 &= \text{prod} + t_5 \\
\text{prod} &= t_6 \\
i &= i + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Optimization Observed:** The original block had **9 instructions**. The optimized block has only **5 instructions**. The redundant `4*i` computation was eliminated, saving a multiplication and an array-index calculation at runtime. This is **~44% reduction in instructions**.

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Dead Code Elimination (DCE)

**Given Optimized Code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= b + c \\
d &= a + e \\
\text{if}(\text{condition}) \text{ then } & \text{ use } d \\
\text{else} \quad & \text{ use } d \\
x &= 100 \quad & \text{(never used)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 (Liveness Analysis):** Determine which variables are *live* (used later) and *dead* (not used).
- `b`, `c` → live (used in `a = b + c`).
- `a` → live (used in `d = a + e`).
- `e` → live.
- `d` → live (used in both branches).
- `x` → **DEAD** (assigned but never used).

**Step 2 (Eliminate):** Remove the statement `x = 100`.

**Optimized Code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= b + c \\
d &= a + e \\
\text{use}(d)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (KTU Exam):** Students often confuse "temporaries" with dead code. A temporary like `t1` is dead only if it is **never used as an operand** in any subsequent instruction in the block. Always scan the entire block, not just the immediately following line.

### 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Algebraic Simplifications and Strength Reduction

**Original Code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= y * 1 \\
z &= x + 0 \\
w &= a * 0 \\
p &= b ** 2 \\
q &= c * 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-Step Application of Identities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Apply Rule 2: } y * 1 \rightarrow y \\
&\text{Apply Rule 1: } x + 0 \rightarrow x \\
&\text{Apply Rule 3: } a * 0 \rightarrow 0 \\
&\text{Apply Rule 6: } b ** 2 \rightarrow b * b \\
&\text{Apply Rule 4: } c * 4 \rightarrow c \ll 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Optimized Code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= y \\
z &= x \\
w &= 0 \\
p &= b * b \\
q &= c \ll 2
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Speedup Comparison

| Optimization | Original Ops | Optimized Ops | Speedup Factor |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| CSE (Example 3.2) | 9 instructions | 5 instructions | **1.80x** |
| DCE (Example 3.4) | 4 instructions | 3 instructions | **1.33x** |
| Algebraic (Example 3.5) | 5 multiplications | 1 multiplication + 3 shifts | **~3x** |
| Copy Propagation (Simple) | 3 instructions | 2 instructions | **1.50x** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Complete Basic Block Optimization Pipeline

This diagram illustrates the **complete flow** of how a basic block is optimized from raw three-address code to final optimized three-address code.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Three Address Code] --> B[Partition into Basic Blocks]
    B --> C[Identify Leaders]
    C --> D[Construct Control Flow Graph]
    D --> E[For Each Basic Block]
    E --> F[Build DAG Representation]
    F --> G[Apply Common Subexpression Elimination]
    F --> H[Apply Dead Code Elimination]
    F --> I[Apply Algebraic Simplification]
    F --> J[Apply Copy Propagation]
    G --> K[Reconstruct Optimized TAC]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Output Optimized Three Address Code]
    L --> M[Emit to Code Generator]
```

### 4.2 DAG Visual Structure for Worked Example 3.2

This is the visual DAG corresponding to the three-address code in Section 3.2:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    const4["CONST 4"]
    leafi["LEAF i"]
    leafa["LEAF a"]
    leafb["LEAF b"]
    leafprod["LEAF prod"]
    const1["CONST 1"]
    nodeMul1["MUL t1 t3"]
    nodeAref["AREF t2 a"]
    nodeBref["AREF t4 b"]
    nodeMul2["MUL t5"]
    nodeAdd1["ADD t6 prod"]
    nodeAdd2["ADD t7 i"]
    const4 --> nodeMul1
    leafi --> nodeMul1
    nodeMul1 --> nodeAref
    leafa --> nodeAref
    nodeMul1 --> nodeBref
    leafb --> nodeBref
    nodeAref --> nodeMul2
    nodeBref --> nodeMul2
    leafprod --> nodeAdd1
    nodeMul2 --> nodeAdd1
    leafi --> nodeAdd2
    const1 --> nodeAdd2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the DAG:**
> - Node `MUL t1 t3` is **shared** between `t1 = 4*i` and `t3 = 4*i`. This visually demonstrates the Common Subexpression Elimination.
> - Node `ADD t6 prod` carries the label `prod` because `prod = t6` reassigned `prod` to point to the same node. The old leaf `prod` is **orphaned** (dead code).

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Local vs. Global Optimization Scope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BB1["Basic Block 1 Local Scope"]
        P1[Statement 1] --> P2[Statement 2] --> P3[Statement 3]
    end
    subgraph BB2["Basic Block 2 Local Scope"]
        Q1[Statement 1] --> Q2[Statement 2]
    end
    subgraph BB3["Basic Block 3 Local Scope"]
        R1[Statement 1] --> R2[Statement 2] --> R3[Statement 3] --> R4[Statement 4]
    end
    BB1 --> BB2
    BB2 --> BB3
    BB1 -. Global Data Flow .-> BB3
    BB2 -. Global Data Flow .-> BB1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scope Distinction (KTU Favourite Question):**
> - **Local Optimization** (DAG-based, Basic Block scope) considers only the instructions within a single block. This is what Module 4 focuses on.
> - **Global Optimization** requires **Data-Flow Analysis** (Liveness, Reaching Definitions) across multiple basic blocks and is covered in Module 5.

### 4.4 Decision Flow — When to Apply Each Optimization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Instruction in Basic Block] --> Q1{Is a duplicate computation present?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> ApplyCSE[Apply Common Subexpression Elimination]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is a variable computed but never used?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> ApplyDCE[Apply Dead Code Elimination]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Does an algebraic identity apply?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> ApplyAlg[Apply Algebraic Simplification]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is there an x = y copy?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> ApplyCopy[Apply Copy Propagation]
    Q4 -- No --> Next[Move to Next Instruction]
    ApplyCSE --> Next
    ApplyDCE --> Next
    ApplyAlg --> Next
    ApplyCopy --> Next
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**

**Q1. Define a basic block. List the four principal sources of optimization as per Aho–Sethi–Ullman.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **basic block** is a maximal sequence of consecutive three-address instructions in which the flow of control enters at the first instruction and leaves at the last, without any internal branching, jumps, or halts (1 mark).

The **four principal sources of optimization** as per Aho–Sethi–Ullman are (0.5 marks each):
1. **Common Subexpression Elimination** — removing duplicate computations of the same subexpression.
2. **Dead Code Elimination** — removing instructions that compute values never subsequently used.
3. **Use of Algebraic Identities** — replacing expensive operations with cheaper equivalents (e.g., $x \cdot 2 \rightarrow x \ll 1$).
4. **Copy Propagation** — substituting uses of a variable with the variable it was copied from.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**

**Q2. What is a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) in the context of basic block optimization? How does it help in detecting common subexpressions?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** is a graph-based intermediate representation of a basic block in which **leaf nodes** represent operands (identifiers or constants) and **interior nodes** represent operators. A node may have multiple labels attached to it (1 mark).

**Detection of Common Subexpressions:** When the compiler processes the three-address code instruction by instruction, for any binary operation $y \ \textbf{op} \ z$, it **searches for an existing interior node** with the same operation and the same child nodes. If such a node is found, the new instruction is **merged** into the existing node (instead of creating a new one), and the result variable's label is added to the existing node. This merging corresponds exactly to identifying that the subexpression was already computed earlier — i.e., a **common subexpression** — and the second computation can be eliminated (2 marks).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme, ESE Module 4]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — 7 + 7 Structure**

**Q.A (a)** Construct the **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** for the following basic block of three-address code. Identify all the **common subexpressions**, **dead code**, and **algebraic simplifications** possible. (7 Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= a + b \\
t_2 &= a - b \\
t_3 &= t_1 * t_2 \\
t_4 &= a + b \quad \text{(duplicate of } t_1 \text{)} \\
t_5 &= t_1 * t_2 \quad \text{(duplicate of } t_3 \text{)} \\
t_6 &= t_3 * 5 \\
t_7 &= t_4 * t_5 \\
t_8 &= t_7 + 0 \\
t_9 &= a * 1 \\
t_{10} &= t_6 + t_8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Build the DAG instruction by instruction:**

| Step | Instruction | Action Taken | Resulting Node |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $t_1 = a + b$ | No existing `+` node with children `(a, b)` → **create new `+` node** | Node `N1 = (+)` with labels `t1` |
| 2 | $t_2 = a - b$ | No existing `-` node with children `(a, b)` → **create new `-` node** | Node `N2 = (-)` with labels `t2` |
| 3 | $t_3 = t_1 * t_2$ | Look up `t1 = N1`, `t2 = N2`. No existing `*` node → **create new `*` node** | Node `N3 = (*)` with labels `t3` |
| 4 | $t_4 = a + b$ | Search: existing `+` node with children `(a, b)`? **YES — N1**. **MERGE!** | Node `N1` now has labels `t1, t4` |
| 5 | $t_5 = t_1 * t_2$ | Search: existing `*` node with children `(N1, N2)`? **YES — N3**. **MERGE!** | Node `N3` now has labels `t3, t5` |
| 6 | $t_6 = t_3 * 5$ | Look up `t3 = N3`. Search for `*` with `(N3, 5)` → **create new** | Node `N4 = (*)` with label `t6` |
| 7 | $t_7 = t_4 * t_5$ | `t4 = N1`, `t5 = N3`. Search for `*` with `(N1, N3)` → **create new** | Node `N5 = (*)` with label `t7` |
| 8 | $t_8 = t_7 + 0$ | `t7 = N5`. Apply **Rule 1 (Additive Identity)**: $x + 0 \rightarrow x$. No new node needed. | Node `N5` now has label `t8` (in addition to `t7`) |
| 9 | $t_9 = a * 1$ | Apply **Rule 2 (Multiplicative Identity)**: $a * 1 \rightarrow a$. No new node needed. | Node `a` (leaf) now has label `t9` |
| 10 | $t_{10} = t_6 + t_8$ | `t6 = N4`, `t8 = N5`. Search for `+` with `(N4, N5)` → **create new** | Node `N6 = (+)` with label `t10` |

**Step 2 — Identify Optimizations:**

- **Common Subexpressions Detected:** $t_4 = a + b$ (reuses N1), $t_5 = t_1 * t_2$ (reuses N3).
- **Algebraic Simplifications:** $t_8 = t_7 + 0 \rightarrow t_8 = t_7$, $t_9 = a * 1 \rightarrow t_9 = a$.
- **Dead Code:** The variable `t2` is computed but **never used** (only `t3 = t1 * t2` uses it, and the merged `t5` reuses `t1, t2` paths). Wait — re-check: `t2` IS used in `t3 = t1 * t2`. So `t2` is **live**. However, `t7` is computed only to produce `t8`, which is used in `t10`. So no instructions are dead in this block; instead, redundant computations are eliminated.

**Step 3 — Reconstruct Optimized Three-Address Code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= a + b \\
t_2 &= a - b \\
t_3 &= t_1 * t_2 \\
t_6 &= t_3 * 5 \\
t_7 &= t_1 * t_3 \quad \text{(used } t_4, t_5 \text{ merged)} \\
t_8 &= t_7 \\
t_9 &= a \\
t_{10} &= t_6 + t_7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Original:** 10 instructions. **Optimized:** 8 instructions. **(2 marks for DAG diagram, 2 marks for identification, 3 marks for reconstruction.)**

---

**Q.A (b)** What is **Copy Propagation**? How does it differ from **Constant Folding**? Give suitable code examples. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Copy Propagation (3.5 Marks):**
Copy Propagation is an optimization technique that **replaces occurrences of the target variable of a copy statement with the source variable**, after the copy.

**Example:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&x = t \\
&y = x * 2 \\
&z = x + 5
\end{aligned}
$$

After Copy Propagation:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&x = t \\
&y = t * 2 \\
&z = t + 5
\end{aligned}
$$

If `x` is not used elsewhere, the copy itself can be eliminated (Dead Code Elimination) and the code becomes even shorter.

**Constant Folding (3.5 Marks):**
Constant Folding is the process of **evaluating constant expressions at compile time** rather than at runtime, replacing them with their computed value.

**Example:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&x = 3 + 5 \\
&y = x * 2 \\
&z = 10 / 2
\end{aligned}
$$

After Constant Folding:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&x = 8 \\
&y = 8 * 2 \\
&z = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

And after further constant propagation: $y = 16$.

**Key Difference:**

| Aspect | Copy Propagation | Constant Folding |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Input** | A copy statement $u = v$ | A constant-only expression |
| **Output** | Replaces uses of $u$ with $v$ | Replaces the expression with a single value |
| **Trigger** | Right-hand side is a single variable | Right-hand side is composed of constants |
| **Reduction Type** | Reduces variable indirection | Reduces runtime arithmetic |
| **Dependencies** | Often followed by DCE on the copy | Often followed by Copy Propagation |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Internal Choice)**

**Q.B (a)** Explain the **DAG construction algorithm** in detail. Construct the DAG and the resulting optimized three-address code for the following block: (7 Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= b + c \\
b &= a - d \\
c &= b + c \quad \text{(note: } c \text{ from step 1, not step 3)} \\
d &= a - d \quad \text{(note: } d \text{ unchanged from step 2)} \\
e &= b + c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**DAG Construction Algorithm (3 Marks for explanation):**

The algorithm processes instructions in the order they appear. For each instruction $x = y \ \textbf{op} \ z$:

1. **Find/Create node for `y`:** If `y` is a constant, reuse/merge with the constant-node. If `y` is an identifier, use the most recent definition of `y` (look it up in a symbol table). Create a leaf if it has no definition.
2. **Find/Create node for `z`:** Same procedure as `y`.
3. **Search for existing operator node:** Look for an interior node whose children match `(node(y), node(z))` and whose operation matches `op`. If found, **MERGE** (don't create new). If not, **create a new interior node**.
4. **Attach label `x`:** Add `x` to the label set of the resulting node and update the definition of `x` to point to this node.

The merging step is what enables **Common Subexpression Elimination** automatically.

**DAG Construction for the Given Block (4 Marks):**

| Step | Instruction | DAG Action | Label Update |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $a = b + c$ | Create new `+` node with children `(b_leaf, c_leaf)` | `a` → N1 |
| 2 | $b = a - d$ | Create new `-` node with children `(N1, d_leaf)` | `b` → N2 |
| 3 | $c = b + c$ | `b` is now N2. `c` is still `c_leaf` (not redefined). Create new `+` node `(N2, c_leaf)` | `c` → N3 |
| 4 | $d = a - d$ | `a` = N1, `d` = `d_leaf` (not redefined since step 2 used it as `d_leaf`). **No new node needed — merge with N2!** | `d` → N2 |
| 5 | $e = b + c$ | `b` = N2, `c` = N3. Search for `+` with `(N2, N3)` → **NOT FOUND** → create new | `e` → N4 |

**Reconstructed Optimized Three-Address Code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= b + c \\
b &= a - d \\
c &= b + c \quad \text{(reuses redefined } b) \\
d &= b \quad \text{(assignment eliminated: } d \text{ now points to same node as } b) \\
e &= b + c
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** In step 4, the computation `a - d` is identical to step 2 (since `a` and `d` haven't changed), so it is **eliminated**. The instruction `d = a - d` becomes simply `d = b` (a copy), which can then be subject to copy propagation or dead code elimination if `d` is not later used.

---

**Q.B (b)** Discuss the **four principal sources of optimization** of basic blocks. For each source, give a small code example and show the optimized result. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The Aho–Sethi–Ullman compiler text identifies **four principal sources of optimization** at the local (basic block) level:

**1. Common Subexpression Elimination (2 Marks):**
Eliminate repeated computations of the same subexpression.
- **Original:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= x + y \\
t_2 &= a * t_1 \\
t_3 &= x + y \quad \text{(redundant)} \\
t_4 &= b * t_3
\end{aligned}
$$
- **Optimized:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= x + y \\
t_2 &= a * t_1 \\
t_4 &= b * t_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**2. Dead Code Elimination (2 Marks):**
Remove statements whose results are never used.
- **Original:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= 5 \\
y &= x + 10 \\
\text{print}(y) \\
z &= 100 \quad \text{(never used)}
\end{aligned}
$$
- **Optimized:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= 5 \\
y &= x + 10 \\
\text{print}(y)
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Use of Algebraic Identities (1.5 Marks):**
Apply identities like $x \cdot 1 = x$, $x + 0 = x$, $x \cdot 0 = 0$.
- **Original:** $a = b * 1$; $c = a + 0$; $d = e * 0$;
- **Optimized:** $a = b$; $c = a$; $d = 0$;

**4. Copy Propagation (1.5 Marks):**
Replace uses of a copy with the original variable.
- **Original:** $u = v$; $w = u + 1$;
- **Optimized:** $w = v + 1$ (and $u = v$ can be eliminated if $u$ is unused).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT mix local and global optimizations.** Local = within one basic block (DAG-based). Global = across blocks (requires data-flow analysis, covered in Module 5). KTU papers carry **2-mark questions** specifically asking to differentiate.
> 2. **Always state "without changing program semantics"** when defining any optimization. Omitting this costs 0.5–1 mark in definition questions.
> 3. **DAG problems must show the diagram or table** — merely writing the optimized code is incomplete. The construction steps (find, search, merge, label) are essential.
> 4. **Confusing "temporaries" with "dead code"**: A temporary is dead only when its label has no subsequent use. Many students mark every temporary as dead, losing 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Strength reduction** is sometimes asked under "algebraic identities" — know both $x * 2 = x \ll 1$ (multiplication → shift) and $x / 2 = x \gg 1$ (division → shift) by heart.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Basic Block**: Maximal sequence of consecutive instructions with no internal branching; flow enters at top, leaves at bottom.
- [ ] **DAG**: Leaf = operand; Interior = operator; **Merging of identical nodes = CSE**.
- [ ] **Four Sources of Optimization** (memorize in order):
  1. **Common Subexpression Elimination** (CSE)
  2. **Dead Code Elimination** (DCE)
  3. **Use of Algebraic Identities** (Strength Reduction)
  4. **Copy Propagation**
- [ ] **Key Algebraic Identities**: $x + 0 = x$, $x \cdot 1 = x$, $x \cdot 0 = 0$, $x - 0 = x$, $x \cdot 2 = x \ll 1$, $x / 2 = x \gg 1$, $x^2 = x \cdot x$.
- [ ] **DAG Algorithm Steps**: (1) find/create leaf for `y`, (2) find/create leaf for `z`, (3) search for matching interior node, (4) **merge if found** (CSE), else create new, (5) attach label `x`.
- [ ] **DCE Rule**: A statement is dead if its defined variable is never used later (in the same block or globally).
- [ ] **Copy Propagation Rule**: After `u = v`, replace all uses of `u` with `v` in subsequent instructions.
- [ ] **Constant Folding**: Evaluate constant-only expressions at compile time.
- [ ] **Scope**: Local optimization is **intra-block**; global optimization is **inter-block** (Module 5).
- [ ] **Optimization preserves semantics** — this phrase is worth 0.5–1 mark in any definition question.
- [ ] **Reconstruction**: Use **postorder traversal** of the DAG to emit optimized three-address code.
- [ ] **Common KTU trick question**: "Can strength reduction change the result for negative numbers?" — Answer: $x * 2 = x \ll 1$ only for non-negative integers. Always state assumptions.
- [ ] **Tie-breaking in DAG node lookup**: If two nodes have the same operator and children, the **most recently created one is preferred** (helps with redefinition).
- [ ] **Labels attached to a node**: A node may carry **multiple variable names** as labels (e.g., `t1, t4, t9` all pointing to the same `+` node for `a + b`).
- [ ] **Practical impact**: Local optimizations typically reduce instruction count by **15–40%** in scientific and numerical code, with corresponding speedup in execution time.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Code Generation: Issues in the Design of a Code Generator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT751 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Code Generation: Issues in the Design of a Code Generator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
